topics.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence, Foundation and History of AI Agents and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Artificial Intelligence, Foundation and History of AI Agents and Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Artificial Intelligence?

### 🎯 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** is the branch of computer science concerned with the design and construction of intelligent agents — systems that can **perceive** their environment, **reason** over the data they gather, and **act** rationally to achieve specified goals.

Two classical formulations (Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*) are accepted as the **gold standard** in KTU examinations:

| Dimension | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Human-Centered** | AI is the art of creating machines that perform functions that require **intelligence when performed by humans** (Haugeland, 1985). |
| **Rational-Agent** | AI is the study of **agents** that receive **percepts** from the environment and perform **actions** that maximize their performance measure (Russell & Norvig, 1995). |

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Apprentice"

Imagine you hire a **bright apprentice** to help run a coffee shop. The apprentice:
- **Watches** the world (sensors = cameras, eyes, ears)
- **Thinks** before acting (reasoning = brain, processing)
- **Acts** using available tools (actuators = hands, legs, machines)
- **Improves** with experience (learning = feedback loop)

An AI system is **exactly** this apprentice — but silicon-based. It perceives, decides, acts, and learns. The only difference is that its "brain" is made of algorithms running on hardware, not biological neurons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly emphasizes the **Agent-Environment paradigm** as the unifying framework of AI. Every AI problem is modeled as an *agent* interacting with an *environment*. Memorize this framing — it is the spine of Module 1.

---

## 1.2 Intelligent Agents — The Heart of AI

### 🎯 Formal Definition

> An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that can be viewed as **perceiving its environment through sensors** and **acting upon that environment through actuators**.

Mathematically, an **agent function** is a mapping from the entire history of percepts to an action:

$$f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ = the set of all finite sequences of percepts
- $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of all possible actions

The **agent program** is the concrete internal implementation that runs on a specific **architecture** (physical platform).

$$\text{Agent} = \text{Architecture} + \text{Program}$$

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Thermostat"

A **room thermostat** is the simplest possible agent:
- **Sensor:** thermometer reads room temperature
- **Environment:** the room itself
- **Actuator:** switches the heater ON or OFF
- **Rule:** *If temperature < 20°C, turn heater ON; else OFF*

It is "intelligent" only in a narrow sense — but it is genuinely an agent. Scale this up with cameras, microphones, robotic arms, and reasoning algorithms, and you get **autonomous vehicles, chatbots, or surgical robots**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** Even simple devices (thermostats, automatic doors) qualify as agents. The sophistication of *reasoning* distinguishes a *reflex agent* from a *rational, deliberative agent*.

---

## 1.3 The Environment — Where the Agent Lives

### 🎯 Formal Definition

> The **Environment** is the external world with which the agent interacts. It provides **percepts** through the agent's sensors and is modified by the agent's **actions** through its actuators.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Chessboard"

For a **chess-playing AI**:
- The **board** (positions of pieces, opponent's moves) is the environment
- **Percepts** = the current board configuration
- **Actions** = legal moves
- The opponent is part of the environment (and possibly another agent — making it a *multi-agent* environment)

The environment may be **fully observable** (chess — you see the whole board) or **partially observable** (poker — you cannot see opponents' cards).

---

## 1.4 The PEAS Framework — A KTU-Favorite Tool

To formally specify an AI task, the KTU syllabus mandates the **PEAS** framework:

| Component | Meaning | Example (Self-Driving Car) |
|---|---|---|
| **P**erformance | How success is measured | Safety, speed, comfort, fuel efficiency |
| **E**nvironment | The world the agent operates in | Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians |
| **A**ctuators | How the agent acts | Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signals |
| **S**ensors | How the agent perceives | Cameras, LIDAR, GPS, radar, speedometer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEAS is a 3-mark favorite in KTU Module 1.** When asked "Specify a problem," always write PEAS in tabular form with concrete examples for the four components.

---

## 1.5 Strong AI vs Weak AI (Foundational Distinction)

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Weak AI (Narrow AI)** | Systems designed for a **specific task**; no real understanding. | Siri, chess engines, recommendation systems |
| **Strong AI (General AI / AGI)** | Hypothetical systems with **human-level general intelligence**, consciousness, and self-awareness. | Not yet achieved — remains a research goal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Rational Agent — KTU's Central Paradigm

A **rational agent** does the **right thing** — the action that maximizes its **performance measure**, given its percept sequence and built-in knowledge.

### 🔑 The Five Pillars of a Rational Agent

For each possible percept sequence, a rational agent must select an action that is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, where the expectation is computed over:

1. The **performance measure** defining the criterion of success
2. The agent's **prior knowledge** of the environment
3. The **actions** the agent can perform
4. The **percept sequence** to date (everything the agent has perceived)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rationality ≠ Omniscience.** A rational agent acts on what it *knows/perceives*, not on unknowable future events. It maximizes **expected** performance, not actual performance.

---

## 2.2 Types of Intelligent Agents (Russell & Norvig Taxonomy)

The KTU syllabus demands a clear understanding of the **five canonical agent types**, ordered by increasing sophistication.

### 🤖 Type 1: Simple Reflex Agent

- **Architecture:** Condition-Action Rules
- **Memory:** None — reacts to current percept only
- **Formula:** $\text{Action} \leftarrow \text{Interpret}(\text{Percept}) \rightarrow \text{Rule-Match} \rightarrow \text{Act}$

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal State | None |
| Best Suited For | Fully observable environments |
| Limitation | Fails in partially observable settings |
| Example | Thermostat, automatic door, simple vacuum cleaner |

### 🤖 Type 2: Model-Based Reflex Agent

- **Architecture:** Internal State + World Model
- **Memory:** Yes — tracks unobserved aspects of the world
- **Components:** *State*, *How the world evolves*, *How agent's actions affect the world*

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal State | Yes |
| Best Suited For | Partially observable environments |
| Limitation | No goal — just reacts based on model |
| Example | Robot vacuum that remembers where it has cleaned |

### 🤖 Type 3: Goal-Based Agent

- **Architecture:** Goal information + Search/Planning
- **Memory:** Yes — keeps track of goals and possible future states
- **Key Idea:** Considers "what will happen if I do action X?" to reach a goal

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal State | Yes |
| Best Suited For | Sequential decision-making |
| Limitation | Goals don't capture quality trade-offs |
| Example | GPS navigation, chess AI, path-planning robot |

### 🤖 Type 4: Utility-Based Agent

- **Architecture:** Utility Function over states
- **Key Idea:** When goals conflict or are uncertain, choose the action that **maximizes expected utility** (happiness/quality measure)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal State | Yes |
| Best Suited For | Trade-off scenarios |
| Limitation | Utility functions are hard to design |
| Example | Self-driving car balancing speed vs safety vs comfort |

### 🤖 Type 5: Learning Agent

- **Architecture:** Four conceptual components
  1. **Learning Element** — improves performance
  2. **Performance Element** — selects actions (the agent itself)
  3. **Critic** — provides feedback on how well the agent is doing
  4. **Problem Generator** — suggests exploratory actions

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Internal State | Yes |
| Best Suited For | Unknown, changing environments |
| Example | AlphaGo, recommendation systems, self-driving cars |

---

## 2.3 Properties of Environments (KTU 7-Mark Favorite)

The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Classify the following environment using all seven properties."* Memorize these:

| Property | Possible Values | Chess Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Observability** | Fully / Partially / Unobservable | Fully observable (you see the whole board) |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic / Stochastic | Deterministic (no randomness) |
| **Episodic** | Episodic / Sequential | Sequential (move affects all future) |
| **Static/Dynamic** | Static / Dynamic / Semi-dynamic | Static (board doesn't change while you think) |
| **Discrete/Continuous** | Discrete / Continuous | Discrete (finite states) |
| **Single/Multi-agent** | Single / Multi-agent | Multi-agent (you and the opponent) |
| **Known/Unknown** | Known / Unknown | Known (rules are clear) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** "**O-D-E-S-D-S-K**" = Observability, Determinism, Episodic, Static/Dynamic, Discrete/Continuous, Single/Multi-agent, Known/Unknown. This order is examiner-friendly.

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Conceptual Formula Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | Maps percept history to action |
| Agent Decomposition | $\text{Agent} = \text{Architecture} + \text{Program}$ | Hardware + Software |
| Rationality Condition | $\text{Action} = \arg\max_{a} E[\text{Performance} \mid \text{Percept History}, \text{Knowledge}]$ | Best expected action |
| Perception Pipeline | $\text{Percept} \xrightarrow{\text{Sensors}} \text{Agent} \xrightarrow{\text{Actuators}} \text{Action}$ | Sense-think-act loop |
| Learning Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \alpha \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ | Gradient-based parameter update (conceptual) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Agent Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Self-driving cars (Tesla, Waymo) | Utility-Based Learning Agent |
| **Healthcare** | Diagnostic systems (IBM Watson Oncology) | Goal-Based Agent |
| **Finance** | Algorithmic trading, fraud detection | Utility-Based Learning Agent |
| **Robotics** | Industrial assembly, surgery (Da Vinci) | Model-Based Reflex Agent |
| **Smart Cities** | Traffic light control, energy grids | Multi-agent Learning Systems |
| **Gaming** | AlphaGo, Stockfish, OpenAI Five | Goal/Utility-Based Learning Agent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Agent-Environment paradigm** is not academic — it is the **architectural blueprint** for every deployed AI system. Understanding it correctly is the difference between a passing grade and a distinction in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Agent Function from First Principles

### Step 1: Define the Set of Percepts

Let $\mathcal{P}$ be the set of all possible percepts that an agent can receive from its environment at a single instant.

$$\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, p_3, \dots, p_n\}$$

### Step 2: Construct the Set of Percept Sequences

A percept sequence is any finite sequence of percepts the agent has experienced from time $t = 0$ up to the current time $t$.

$$\mathcal{P}^* = \{(p_0, p_1, p_2, \dots, p_t) \mid t \geq 0\}$$

This includes the **empty sequence** () for an agent that has not yet perceived anything.

### Step 3: Define the Set of Actions

Let $\mathcal{A}$ be the set of all possible actions the agent can take. The agent may not be able to perform every action in every state — the **action function** is:

$$\text{Action}(s) \subseteq \mathcal{A}$$

where $s$ is the current world state.

### Step 4: Define the Agent Function

The agent function $f$ is a complete specification of how the agent responds to any percept sequence:

$$f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$$

In words: for *every possible* history of percepts, the function specifies exactly one action (or a probability distribution over actions, in stochastic policies).

### Step 5: Transition from Function to Program

The **agent program** is the concrete implementation of $f$ running on a specific physical **architecture** (CPU, memory, sensors, actuators).

$$\text{Agent} = \text{Architecture} + \text{Program}$$

$$\text{Program} \approx \text{Implementation of } f$$

### Step 6: Introduce Performance Measure

A **rational agent** chooses the action that maximizes its expected performance measure $V$, given percept history $\mathcal{H}$ and built-in knowledge $\mathcal{K}$:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; E\Big[\, V(s') \mid a, \mathcal{H}, \mathcal{K} \,\Big]$$

where $s'$ is the expected next world state. The expectation $E[\cdot]$ is taken over the stochastic transitions of the environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A common mistake is to confuse *rationality* with *perfection*. A rational agent chooses the **expected-best** action given what it knows — it does **not** predict the future with certainty. This nuance is often tested in 3-mark conceptual questions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Five Agent Types Side-by-Side

| Feature | Simple Reflex | Model-Based | Goal-Based | Utility-Based | Learning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Percept Used** | Current only | Current + history | Current + history | Current + history | Current + history + feedback |
| **Internal State** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **World Model** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Goal Information** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Implicit | ✅ Implicit |
| **Utility Function** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Optional |
| **Learning Component** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Handles Partial Observability** | ❌ Poorly | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Computational Cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Example** | Thermostat | Roomba (basic) | GPS route finder | Self-driving car | AlphaGo |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Hack:** When asked to "explain different types of agents," draw **all five block diagrams** with the four internal components labeled. This single visual habit can earn you 3–4 bonus marks out of 7.

---

## 3.3 Evolution of AI — Conceptual Derivation Timeline

The history of AI is best understood as a **progression of theoretical breakthroughs**, each unlocking new classes of problems.

### 🕰️ Phase 1: The Foundations (1943–1955)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1943** | McCulloch & Pitts publish the first mathematical model of an artificial neuron | Proves that logic can be implemented in networks of threshold devices |
| **1950** | Alan Turing publishes *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* and proposes the **Turing Test** | Establishes a behavioral criterion for machine intelligence |
| **1951** | Marvin Minsky & Dean Edmonds build SNARC — first neural network computer | Hardware feasibility demonstrated |
| **1955** | Allen Newell & Herbert Simon create the **Logic Theorist** | First AI program to mimic human problem-solving |

### 🕰️ Phase 2: The Birth of AI as a Field (1956)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1956** | **Dartmouth Conference** organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and Nathaniel Rochester | The term **"Artificial Intelligence"** is officially coined. AI is born as an academic discipline. |

### 🕰️ Phase 3: Early Enthusiasm & Symbolic AI (1956–1974)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1958** | John McCarthy invents **LISP**, the dominant AI programming language for decades | Provides the symbolic-manipulation toolset for AI research |
| **1959** | Arthur Samuel coins **"Machine Learning"** with his checkers-playing program | Establishes learning from experience as a research avenue |
| **1964–66** | Joseph Weizenbaum builds **ELIZA** — the first chatbot | Demonstrates natural language processing (and the limits of pattern matching) |
| **1969** | SRI's **Shakey** robot integrates perception, planning, and action | First mobile robot to reason about its own actions |

### 🕰️ Phase 4: The First AI Winter (1974–1980)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1973** | **Lighthill Report** (UK) criticizes AI for failing its grand promises | Funding slashed in the UK and US |
| **1974–80** | Reduced funding, scaled-back expectations | "AI Winter" — symbolic AI hits combinatorial-explosion walls |

### 🕰️ Phase 5: Expert Systems Boom (1980–1987)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1980** | **XCON** (Digital Equipment Corp) — first commercial expert system | Demonstrates commercial value of knowledge-based AI |
| **1980** | **MYCIN** (Stanford) — medical diagnosis expert system | Shows domain-specific reasoning can match expert humans |
| **1980s** | Japan announces the **Fifth Generation Computer** project | Sparks global AI investment |

### 🕰️ Phase 6: The Second AI Winter (1987–1993)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1987** | Expert systems prove brittle and expensive to maintain | Market collapses; AI Winter returns |
| **1988–93** | Funding cuts, retreat to mathematical foundations | Connectionism and probabilistic methods gain ground |

### 🕰️ Phase 7: Statistical & Probabilistic Renaissance (1993–2011)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **1997** | **IBM Deep Blue** defeats world chess champion Garry Kasparov | Symbolic search + hardware can defeat human intuition |
| **2006** | Geoffrey Hinton coins **"Deep Learning"**; neural networks revived | Algorithmic + data + GPU advances enable multi-layer learning |
| **2011** | **IBM Watson** defeats Jeopardy! champions | NLP + IR + ML combined into a unified system |

### 🕰️ Phase 8: The Modern Deep Learning Era (2012–Present)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **2012** | **AlexNet** (Krizhevsky et al.) wins ImageNet by a huge margin | CNNs + GPUs = image-recognition breakthrough |
| **2014** | **GANs** invented by Ian Goodfellow | Generative modeling becomes viable |
| **2016** | **AlphaGo** (DeepMind) defeats Lee Sedol 4–1 | Reinforcement learning triumphs in Go |
| **2017** | **Transformer** architecture (*Attention is All You Need*) | Foundational for GPT, BERT, T5 — the LLM era |
| **2020** | **GPT-3** released (175B parameters) | Few-shot language understanding emerges |
| **2022** | **ChatGPT** launched — Generative AI goes mainstream | Foundation models become products |
| **2023–24** | GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, Llama, open-source LLM explosion | Generative AI reshapes industry and education |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The examiner expects students to know **Turing (1950), Dartmouth (1956), AI Winters, Expert Systems, Deep Learning revival, and the Transformer era**. Linking milestones to the **agent-environment paradigm** is the highest-scoring strategy.

---

## 3.4 Four Approaches to AI (Russell & Norvig Categorization)

| Approach | Definition | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acting Humanly** | Systems that pass the Turing Test (mimic human behavior) | Intuitive benchmark | Cannot measure subjective "humanness" |
| **Thinking Humanly** | Systems that model actual human cognition (cognitive science) | Theoretically rich | Requires deep neuroscience/psychology |
| **Thinking Rationally** | Systems that use formal logic to reason | Mathematically rigorous | Hard to encode uncertainty |
| **Acting Rationally** | Systems that perform actions to maximize expected utility | Most general, modern AI standard | Utility may be hard to specify |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Acting Rationally** paradigm is the foundation of modern AI research and is the framing used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agent–Environment Interaction Block Diagram

This is the **single most important diagram in Module 1**. It appears in nearly every KTU Part B question on agents.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[ENVIRONMENT]
    SENS[SENSORS]
    AGENT[AGENT]
    ACT[ACTUATORS]
    PER[PERCEPT]
    ACT2[ACTION]

    ENV -->|Stimulus| SENS
    SENS -->|Input Signal| PER
    PER -->|Feed| AGENT
    AGENT -->|Output Signal| ACT2
    ACT2 -->|Drive| ACT
    ACT -->|Modify| ENV
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Environment** sends a stimulus to the **Sensors** of the agent.
- The Sensors produce a **Percept** — the agent's internal representation of the input.
- The Percept is fed into the **Agent** (the reasoning core), which decides what to do.
- The Agent issues an **Action** that drives the **Actuators**.
- The Actuators modify the **Environment**, which then produces a new stimulus, completing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Generic Intelligent Agent Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENS[Sensor Input]
    PERC[Perception Module]
    REAS[Reasoning and Decision Engine]
    KB[Knowledge Base]
    LEARN[Learning Module]
    ACT[Action Selection]
    OUT[Actuator Output]

    SENS --> PERC
    PERC -->|Current State| REAS
    KB -->|Stored Knowledge| REAS
    LEARN -->|Updated Rules| KB
    REAS -->|Decision| ACT
    ACT --> OUT
    LEARN -.Feedback.-> REAS
```

**Module roles:**
- **Perception Module:** Translates raw sensor data into meaningful state representation.
- **Reasoning and Decision Engine:** Performs inference, planning, or optimization.
- **Knowledge Base:** Stores facts, rules, or learned parameters.
- **Learning Module:** Updates the knowledge base from feedback or experience.
- **Action Selection:** Converts the decision into a concrete actuator command.

---

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Agent Types (Conceptual Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Simple Reflex Agent]
    A2[Model Based Reflex Agent]
    A3[Goal Based Agent]
    A4[Utility Based Agent]
    A5[Learning Agent]

    A1 -->|Add Internal State| A2
    A2 -->|Add Goal Information| A3
    A3 -->|Add Utility Function| A4
    A4 -->|Add Learning Element| A5
```

This ladder shows the **progressive enrichment** of agent architecture. Each higher rung subsumes the capabilities of all rungs below it. KTU examiners love this kind of "from simple to complex" walk-through.

---

## 4.4 PEAS Specification Flowchart for an Autonomous Taxi

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Performance Measure]
    E[Environment]
    A[Actuators]
    S[Sensors]

    P -->|Define Success| GOAL[GOAL Maximize Safety Speed Comfort Profit]
    E -->|Define World| WORLD[Roads Traffic Weather Pedestrians Customers]
    A -->|Define Actions| ACTS[Steering Brake Accelerator Signal Horn Display]
    S -->|Define Inputs| INPUTS[Camera LIDAR GPS Speedometer Microphone]
```

**Read this as:** "The autonomous taxi's **Performance** goal is to maximize safety/speed/comfort/profit. It operates in an **Environment** of roads, traffic, weather, etc. It acts via **Actuators** (steering, brake, etc.) and perceives via **Sensors** (cameras, LIDAR, GPS, etc.)."

---

## 4.5 Timeline of AI — Major Milestones (Visual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[1943 McCulloch Pitts Neural Model]
    N2[1950 Turing Test Proposed]
    N3[1956 Dartmouth Conference AI Coined]
    N4[1958 LISP Invented]
    N5[1969 Shakey Robot]
    N6[1973 Lighthill Report First AI Winter]
    N7[1980 Expert Systems Boom MYCIN XCON]
    N8[1987 Second AI Winter]
    N9[1997 Deep Blue Beats Kasparov]
    N10[2006 Deep Learning Revival Hinton]
    N11[2011 Watson Wins Jeopardy]
    N12[2012 AlexNet Wins ImageNet]
    N13[2016 AlphaGo Defeats Lee Sedol]
    N14[2017 Transformer Architecture]
    N15[2022 ChatGPT Released]
    N16[2024 Generative AI Era]

    N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4 --> N5 --> N6 --> N7 --> N8 --> N9 --> N10 --> N11 --> N12 --> N13 --> N14 --> N15 --> N16
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Memorize at least **8 milestones** with their years. The high-probability ones are *Turing Test (1950), Dartmouth (1956), LISP (1958), Expert Systems (1980), Deep Blue (1997), Deep Learning Revival (2006), AlphaGo (2016), and Transformer/ChatGPT (2017/2022)*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** Define Artificial Intelligence. Differentiate between Weak AI and Strong AI. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> Artificial Intelligence is the branch of computer science devoted to developing systems that can perform tasks that, when done by humans, require intelligence. A modern formulation (Russell & Norvig) defines AI as the study of *rational agents* — systems that perceive their environment and act to maximize a performance measure.

**Weak AI vs Strong AI (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Weak AI (Narrow AI) | Strong AI (General AI) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Designed for a **specific** task | Hypothetical; **human-level general** intelligence |
| Consciousness | No self-awareness | Possesses consciousness, sentience |
| Examples | Siri, Alexa, chess engines, spam filters | Not yet achieved — remains a research goal |
| Current Status | Commercially deployed | Theoretical / Philosophical |

> **Awarding Marks:** Definition = 1 mark. Table or clear distinction = 2 marks. Add 1 bonus mark if the student mentions *AGI* (Artificial General Intelligence) as a synonym for Strong AI.

---

### **Q2.** What is an intelligent agent? List and explain the four components of the PEAS framework with an example. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> An **intelligent agent** is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through **sensors** and acting upon that environment through **actuators**. A rational agent selects actions that maximize its expected performance.

**PEAS Components (2 Marks):**

| Component | Description | Example: Medical Diagnosis System |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Criterion of success | Correct diagnosis, patient recovery, low cost |
| **Environment** | The world in which the agent operates | Hospital, patient records, doctors, lab reports |
| **Actuators** | Means of acting | Display screen, alerts to physician, prescription printer |
| **Sensors** | Means of perceiving | Keyboard, microphone, database queries, lab instrument feeds |

> **Awarding Marks:** Definition = 1 mark. Any 2 PEAS components explained with example = 2 marks. Full 4 components in a table = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A.** *(14 Marks)*

#### **(a)** Explain the structure of an intelligent agent with a neat diagram. Discuss the concept of PEAS specification with a suitable example. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer

**Agent Definition (1 Mark):**
An intelligent agent is an entity that perceives its environment via sensors and acts upon it via actuators, with the goal of maximizing a performance measure.

**Generic Agent Architecture (3 Marks):**

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │               AGENT                          │
        │                                              │
ENV ─►  │  SENSORS ─► PERCEPT ─► AGENT FUNCTION f ─►   │  ─► ACTION
        │                          ▲                  │
        │                          │                  │
        │                     KNOWLEDGE /             │
        │                       LEARNING              │
        │                          ▲                  │
        │                          │                  │
        │                       FEEDBACK             │
        │                          │                  │
        └──────────────────────────┴──────────────────┘
```

The agent continuously executes the **sense–think–act loop**:
1. Sensors perceive the environment → produce percepts.
2. Percepts are mapped through the agent function to actions (using internal knowledge/learning).
3. Actuators execute the action → modify the environment.
4. Feedback updates the internal knowledge (in learning agents).

**PEAS Specification (3 Marks):**

> PEAS stands for **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — a structured way to define an AI task.

**Example: Automated Email Spam Classifier**

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Maximize correctly classified emails; minimize false positives (legitimate mail wrongly marked as spam). |
| **Environment** | The user's email inbox, mail server, ongoing stream of new mail. |
| **Actuators** | Mark email as spam, move to spam folder, notify user, delete email. |
| **Sensors** | Email header, sender address, body text, attachments, user feedback ("Mark as spam"). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — PEAS:** Students often write generic descriptions like *"actuators are motors."* The examiner expects **domain-specific** actuators and sensors. Always tailor PEAS to the *exact* problem statement — generic descriptions will be penalized 1–2 marks.

---

#### **(b)** Explain any four types of intelligent agents with neat diagrams. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer

> **Note:** Any four from the five canonical types may be chosen. Below all five are listed — examiners typically accept any four.

**1. Simple Reflex Agent (1.5 Marks)**

```
   PERCEPT ─► CONDITION-ACTION RULES ─► ACTION
```

- Reacts only to the **current percept**.
- Uses **if-then** rules: *If car-ahead-braking, then brake.*
- **Limitation:** Fails in partially observable environments (no memory).
- **Example:** Automatic door, simple thermostat.

**2. Model-Based Reflex Agent (1.5 Marks)**

```
   PERCEPT ─► STATE UPDATE ─► HOW WORLD EVOLVES ─► RULES ─► ACTION
                ▲
                │
         INTERNAL STATE (World Model)
```

- Maintains an **internal state** that depends on the percept history.
- Uses two models: *how the world evolves* and *how the agent's actions affect the world*.
- **Example:** Roomba vacuum remembering cleaned areas.

**3. Goal-Based Agent (1.5 Marks)**

```
   PERCEPT + GOAL ─► SEARCH/PLANNING ─► ACTION
                          ▲
                          │
                   WORLD MODEL
```

- Has explicit **goal information** describing desirable situations.
- Uses **search and planning** to find action sequences that achieve the goal.
- **Example:** GPS navigator, chess engine, route-planning robot.

**4. Utility-Based Agent (1.5 Marks)**

```
   PERCEPT + GOAL + UTILITY FUNCTION ─► MAX EXPECTED UTILITY ─► ACTION
```

- Uses a **utility function** that maps states to a measure of "happiness" or quality.
- Enables **trade-offs** between conflicting goals.
- **Example:** Self-driving car balancing speed, safety, and comfort.

**5. Learning Agent (1 Mark — Optional / Bonus)**

- Has four sub-components: **Performance Element, Learning Element, Critic, Problem Generator**.
- Improves its own performance over time through feedback.
- **Example:** AlphaGo, recommendation systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Agent Diagrams:** Students often draw the agent as a black box with no internal modules. The KTU valuation key specifically awards marks for **internal components** (state, knowledge base, rules, goal, utility). Always show the **inside** of the agent, not just the agent-environment boundary.

---

### **Question B.** *(14 Marks — Alternative)*

#### **(a)** Define an intelligent agent. Explain the various properties of the environment with examples. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer

**Agent Definition (1 Mark):**
> An intelligent agent is a system that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators, where each action is chosen to maximize a performance measure.

**Seven Properties of Environments (6 Marks — 1 mark per property, with example):**

| # | Property | Possible Values | Example: Self-Driving Car |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Observability** | Fully / Partially / Unobservable | **Partially observable** — blind spots, occluded pedestrians |
| 2 | **Determinism** | Deterministic / Stochastic | **Stochastic** — other drivers behave unpredictably |
| 3 | **Episodic** | Episodic / Sequential | **Sequential** — a wrong turn affects the entire trip |
| 4 | **Static/Dynamic** | Static / Dynamic / Semi-dynamic | **Dynamic** — traffic, pedestrians, weather change in real time |
| 5 | **Discrete/Continuous** | Discrete / Continuous | **Continuous** — steering angle, speed are continuous values |
| 6 | **Single/Multi-agent** | Single / Multi-agent | **Multi-agent** — other cars, pedestrians, traffic signals |
| 7 | **Known/Unknown** | Known / Unknown | **Known** — traffic rules are predefined (though outcomes are stochastic) |

**Summary (Bonus 1 Mark):**
> *Fully observable + deterministic + episodic + static + discrete + single-agent + known* is the **easiest** environment. Real-world applications almost always face the *opposite* combination, which is why AI is hard.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Environment Properties:** A common mistake is to confuse *known/unknown* with *observable/unobservable*. **Known/Unknown** refers to the agent's knowledge of the environment's *rules*, while **Observable** refers to whether *all relevant state variables* are perceived. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

#### **(b)** Discuss the history and evolution of AI from 1943 to the modern era, highlighting key milestones. *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Answer

**Phase 1 — Foundations (1943–1955) (1 Mark):**
- **1943:** McCulloch & Pitts propose the first mathematical model of an artificial neuron, proving that neural networks can compute any logical function.
- **1950:** Alan Turing publishes *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"* and introduces the **Turing Test** — a behavioral criterion for machine intelligence.
- **1955:** Allen Newell & Herbert Simon create the **Logic Theorist**, the first program to mimic human problem-solving.

**Phase 2 — Birth of AI (1956) (1 Mark):**
- The **Dartmouth Conference** (summer 1956), organized by John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon, and Nathaniel Rochester, is widely regarded as the **founding event of AI**. The term *Artificial Intelligence* is officially coined here.

**Phase 3 — Early Enthusiasm (1956–1974) (1 Mark):**
- **1958:** John McCarthy invents **LISP**, the dominant AI language.
- **1959:** Arthur Samuel coins **"Machine Learning"** with his checkers program.
- **1966:** Joseph Weizenbaum builds **ELIZA**, the first chatbot.
- **1969:** SRI's **Shakey** robot pioneers integrated perception-planning-action.

**Phase 4 — First AI Winter (1974–1980) (1 Mark):**
- The **Lighthill Report** (1973) criticizes AI's grand promises; funding is cut. The combinatorial explosion in symbolic search is a major technical failure.

**Phase 5 — Expert Systems Boom (1980–1987) (1 Mark):**
- **XCON**, **MYCIN**, and other knowledge-based systems prove commercial value. AI re-emerges as an industry.

**Phase 6 — Second AI Winter (1987–1993) (1 Mark):**
- Expert systems prove brittle and expensive. Market collapses. The field retreats to mathematical foundations.

**Phase 7 — Modern Era (1993–Present) (1 Mark):**
- **1997:** Deep Blue defeats Kasparov.
- **2006:** Hinton's **Deep Learning** revival.
- **2011:** **IBM Watson** wins Jeopardy!
- **2012:** **AlexNet** wins ImageNet, igniting the deep learning revolution.
- **2016:** **AlphaGo** defeats Lee Sedol — reinforcement learning triumphs.
- **2017:** **Transformer** architecture introduces attention mechanisms.
- **2020:** **GPT-3** shows few-shot language understanding.
- **2022:** **ChatGPT** brings generative AI to the mainstream.
- **2024:** Generative AI is embedded in industry, science, and education.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — History:** Students often list dates without **explaining significance**. Always state *why* the milestone mattered in 1–2 lines. Memorize the link between milestone and impact — dates alone earn only 30–40% of the marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing "Rational" with "Omniscient":** Rationality = best *expected* outcome given what is known. Omniscience = knowing the actual outcome. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this conflation.
> 2. **Forgetting the Agent-Environment loop:** Every question on agents expects a diagram showing the **sense–think–act** cycle. Skipping the diagram = loss of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Treating PEAS generically:** Saying "actuators are motors, sensors are cameras" without tailoring to the *specific* problem = -1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the AI Winter:** Any 7-mark history question must mention **both AI Winters** and their causes. Forgetting them is a common 2-mark loss.
> 5. **No mention of the Dartmouth Conference (1956):** This is *the* foundational event of AI as a field. Omitting it in any history question = -1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing "Known" with "Observable":** Known = rules are known. Observable = all relevant state is perceived. They are independent dimensions.
> 7. **Listing agent types without diagrams:** KTU 2024 marking scheme mandates internal architecture diagrams. A textual-only answer caps at 70% of the marks for that sub-part.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

### 🧠 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **AI Definition:** Two valid framings — *human-centered* (mimicking humans) and *rational-agent* (maximizing performance measure). The KTU 2024 syllabus uses the **rational-agent** framing.
- [ ] **Agent = Architecture + Program.** The agent function $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ maps percept history to action.
- [ ] **Rational Agent** = chooses action that maximizes **expected** performance, given percept history and prior knowledge.
- [ ] **Five Agent Types (in increasing complexity):** Simple Reflex → Model-Based Reflex → Goal-Based → Utility-Based → Learning Agent.
- [ ] **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. Always specify concretely.
- [ ] **Seven Environment Properties:** O-D-E-S-D-S-K → Observability, Determinism, Episodic, Static/Dynamic, Discrete/Continuous, Single/Multi-agent, Known/Unknown.
- [ ] **Weak AI** = narrow task (Siri, chess). **Strong AI / AGI** = hypothetical general intelligence.
- [ ] **Russell & Norvig's 4 Approaches:** Acting Humanly, Thinking Humanly, Thinking Rationally, Acting Rationally. The modern paradigm is *Acting Rationally*.
- [ ] **History Highlights (must know):**
  - **1943** — McCulloch & Pitts neuron model
  - **1950** — Turing Test
  - **1956** — Dartmouth Conference (AI coined)
  - **1958** — LISP (McCarthy)
  - **1959** — "Machine Learning" coined (Samuel)
  - **1969** — Shakey robot
  - **1973** — Lighthill Report → First AI Winter
  - **1980** — Expert Systems boom (MYCIN, XCON)
  - **1987** — Second AI Winter
  - **1997** — Deep Blue beats Kasparov
  - **2006** — Deep Learning revival (Hinton)
  - **2011** — IBM Watson wins Jeopardy!
  - **2012** — AlexNet (CNN + GPU)
  - **2016** — AlphaGo
  - **2017** — Transformer architecture
  - **2022** — ChatGPT released
- [ ] **Turing Test:** A test proposed by Alan Turing in 1950 to determine if a machine can exhibit human-like intelligent behavior. The interrogator communicates via text only.
- [ ] **AI Winter = funding cuts + dashed expectations.** Two official winters: 1974–80 and 1987–93.
- [ ] **Percept Sequence vs Percept:** *Percept* = single observation at one time step. *Percept sequence* = entire history.
- [ ] **Performance Measure:** Defines what "success" means. The agent does not optimize for omniscience — it optimizes for this *defined* measure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Module 1 is foundational — it carries roughly **15–20% of the total marks**. Master the *Agent-Environment paradigm* and the *history timeline* first, then everything else in AI becomes intuitive. Good luck! 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Concept of Rationality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Concept of Rationality # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OECMT615 – Artificial Intelligence**, *Rationality* is formally defined as the property of an **Intelligent Agent** that selects actions expected to **maximize a predefined Performance Measure**, based on its current percept sequence, built-in prior knowledge, and the range of actions it can perform. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):** > *"A rational agent is one that does the right thing — that is, for each possible percept sequence, the agent selects an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built-in knowledge the agent has."* > — Adapted from Russell & Norvig, *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (AIMA), as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. The **Ideal Rational Agent** is mathematically expressed as a function mapping percept histories to actions: $$ f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A} $$ Where: - $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ is the set of all possible finite percept sequences. - $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of all possible actions the agent can execute. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:** > Rationality $\neq$ Omniscience. An omniscient agent knows the *actual* outcome of its actions, while a *rational* agent only knows the *expected* outcome based on its knowledge and percept history. ## 1.2 Components of Rationality — The Four Pillars For an agent to be classified as rational, it must satisfy four mandatory criteria stipulated by the KTU 2024 module outcomes (CO1 — Foundations of AI): | Pillar # | Criterion | Operational Meaning | |:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Performance Measure** | A clear, externally defined yardstick of success. | | 2 | **Prior Knowledge** | Built-in understanding of the environment before any percept is received. | | 3 | **Possible Actions** | The complete set of actions $\mathcal{A}$ the agent can legally perform. | | 4 | **Percept Sequence** | The complete history of everything the agent has perceived up to the current time step $t$. | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Village Doctor" > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Rational Doctor:** > Imagine a village doctor with a limited medicine box, no internet, and only the symptoms the patient describes (percepts). The doctor is **rational** if, for every symptom description, she prescribes the medicine that has the **highest expected probability of cure** based on her medical training (prior knowledge). She is **not omniscient** — she cannot foresee an unexpected allergic reaction, but she makes the **best possible decision** given her current knowledge and available tools (actions). ### 1.4 Omniscience vs. Rationality vs. Bounded Rationality > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Confusion Zone:** > Students frequently interchange these three terms. The KTU examiner specifically tests this distinction for **3-mark Part-A questions**. - **Omniscient Agent** — Knows the *true* result of all actions; this is **impossible in practice**. - **Rational Agent** — Acts to *maximize the expected* performance outcome. - **Bounded Rationality (Herbert Simon)** — A rational agent that operates under **computational limits** (finite time, finite memory). - **Bounded Optimality** — A program that performs as well as *any* program bounded by the same computational resources. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Formal Specification of a Rational Agent For every percept sequence $p^{*}$, a rational agent selects an action $a$ that **maximizes the expected value** of the performance measure $V$ over the future percept history. $$ a^{*} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \;\; \mathbb{E}\bigl[\,V(\text{Environment History after action } a) \;\bigm|\; p^{*}, \text{agent knowledge}\,\bigr] $$ > [!NOTE] > The expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ captures the agent's uncertainty about the environment. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests whether students understand **why expectation** is used and not **maximization** of a single outcome. ## 2.2 Percept, Percept Sequence, and Agent Function A **percept** is the agent's perceptual input at one discrete time instant, drawn from a percept set $\mathcal{P}$. A **percept sequence** is the entire ordered list of percepts the agent has observed so far: $$ p^{*} = \langle p_{1},\, p_{2},\, \dots,\, p_{t} \rangle $$ The **agent function** $f$ maps the history into a specific action: $$ f(p^{*}) = a, \quad a \in \mathcal{A} $$ For a vacuum-cleaner world with two locations A and B and a dirt sensor, the percepts could be `[Location, Status]`, giving percept sequences such as `[A, Clean], [A, Dirty]`, etc. ## 2.3 The Vacuum-Cleaner World — KTU's Favorite Example > [!IMPORTANT] > The **vacuum-cleaner world** is the *most repeated* illustrative example in the KTU 2024 OECMT615 syllabus. Be prepared to draw its percept-action table. **Percepts:** `[Location, Status]` where $\text{Status} \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$ and $\text{Location} \in \{A, B\}$. **Actions:** $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Left}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp}\}$. **Performance Measure:** Award **+1** for each clean square at every time step; penalize **−1** for each movement. **Prior Knowledge:** The agent knows the geography (two adjacent squares A and B) and the effect of the Suck action. | Percept Sequence | Rational Action | Justification | |:---|:---:|:---| | `[A, Clean]` | `Right` | Other square may be dirty; explore. | | `[A, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Cleans the current square immediately. | | `[B, Clean]` | `Left` | Symmetric reasoning to the first row. | | `[B, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Maximizes immediate clean-square reward. | | `[A, Clean], [A, Clean]` | `Right` | Persistence of state — keep moving. | | `[A, Clean], [A, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Reacts to new dirt perception. | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Agent Function | $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ | $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ = set of all percept sequences; $\mathcal{A}$ = action set | | 2 | Rational Action Choice | $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, K]$ | $V$ = performance measure; $K$ = prior knowledge | | 3 | Expected Performance | $\mathbb{E}[V] = \sum_{s \in S} P(s \mid p^{*}, a) \cdot V(s)$ | $S$ = world states; $P(s)$ = transition probability | | 4 | Percept Sequence Length | $\vert p^{*} \vert = t$ | $t$ = current discrete time step | | 5 | Bounded Rationality Bound | $f^{*}_{\text{bounded}} \approx \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}[V \mid C \le C_{\max}]$ | $C$ = computational cost; $C_{\max}$ = resource cap | | 6 | Omniscience (Hypothetical) | $a_{\text{omni}} = \arg\max_{a} V_{\text{actual}}(s')$ | $V_{\text{actual}}$ = true (not expected) outcome | | 7 | Discounted Cumulative Reward (Rationality in MDP) | $G_{t} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0,1]$ = discount factor | > [!CAUTION] > **Markdown Rendering Alert:** In Row 1, `mathcal{P}^{*}` uses `\mathcal` to render properly. The absolute value `|p^{*}|` has been replaced with `\vert p^{*} \vert` in Row 4 to preserve table integrity. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Rationality 1. **Autonomous Vehicles (Self-Driving Cars)** — Self-driving systems (Waymo, Tesla FSD) are rational agents that maximize safety performance measures while bounded by real-time GPU compute limits. 2. **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)** — Industrial robots select actions maximizing throughput under physical constraints. 3. **Algorithmic Trading** — Trading bots act rationally by maximizing expected Sharpe ratio given the market percept sequence. 4. **Medical Diagnosis Systems (e.g., IBM Watson Health)** — Recommend treatments maximizing patient outcome expectancy. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Rational Action Selector **Goal:** Prove that the *ideal rational agent function* $f^{*}$ is defined as the one that maximizes expected performance. ### Step 1 — Define the State of the World The environment is in some state $s \in S$ at time $t$. The agent does **not** know $s$ with certainty; it can only infer a probability distribution. $$ P(s \mid p^{*}) = \text{Posterior belief over world state given percept history} $$ ### Step 2 — Define the Performance Measure Let $V : S \times \mathcal{A} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be the value function. After taking action $a$, the environment transitions from $s$ to $s'$ according to a transition model $T(s, a, s')$. $$ T(s, a, s') = P(s' \mid s, a) $$ ### Step 3 — Write the Expected Performance Using the **law of total expectation**, the expected value of performing action $a$ after observing $p^{*}$ is: $$ \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, a] = \sum_{s \in S} \sum_{s' \in S} P(s \mid p^{*}) \cdot T(s, a, s') \cdot V(s') $$ > **Logic of the step:** We sum over all *current* states weighted by how likely they are, and for each, sum over all *next* states weighted by the transition probability, weighted by the performance value. This is the standard **expectation under uncertainty**. ### Step 4 — Maximize Over Actions The rational agent picks the action with the highest expected performance: $$ a^{*} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \;\; \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, a] $$ > **Logic of the step:** The `argmax` operator returns the argument (the specific action $a$) that maximizes the bracketed expectation. This is the **operational definition of rationality**. ### Step 5 — Special Case: Deterministic Environments If the environment is deterministic, then $T(s, a, s') = 1$ for exactly one $s'$; the expectation collapses to a single value: $$ \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, a] = \sum_{s \in S} P(s \mid p^{*}) \cdot V(s, a) $$ > **Logic of the step:** With no stochasticity, the agent's only remaining uncertainty is its belief $P(s \mid p^{*})$ about which state it is currently in. ## 3.2 Derivation — Rationality vs. Bounded Rationality Trade-Off Let $C(a)$ denote the computational cost of computing the optimal action $a$. The *bounded rational* action is: $$ a^{*}_{\text{bounded}} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A},\; C(a) \le C_{\max}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \;\; \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, a] $$ $$ a^{*}_{\text{bounded}} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A},\; C(a) \le C_{\max}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \;\; \sum_{s \in S} \sum_{s' \in S} P(s \mid p^{*}) \cdot T(s, a, s') \cdot V(s') $$ > **Logic of the step:** The Lagrangian-style constraint $C(a) \le C_{\max}$ reflects *Herbert Simon's* principle of *satisficing* — a bounded rational agent picks the **best feasible** action, not necessarily the globally optimal one. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of Agent Types | Agent Type | Knowledge | Compute | Optimality | Realism | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | **Omniscient** | Perfect | Infinite | Theoretically global | Physically impossible | | **Ideal Rational** | Full within model | Infinite | Maximizes *expected* value | Theoretical benchmark | | **Bounded Rational (Simon)** | Partial | Limited | *Satisficing* — best feasible | Realistic for software agents | | **Bounded Optimal (Russell–Subramanian)** | Full within model | Limited within cap $C_{\max}$ | Best *achievable* under cap | Engineering target for AI design | | **Random / Reflex** | None | Minimal | None | Used as baseline only | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Vacuum Cleaner Rationality **Scenario:** Percept sequence is `[A, Clean], [A, Clean]` in a 2-square vacuum world. **Performance Measure:** $+1$ per clean square per time step, $-1$ per move. **Step 1 — Belief State:** The agent has observed two clean percepts at A. Belief $P(\text{Square B dirty}) = 0.5$ (prior, before B is checked). **Step 2 — Expected Value of `Right` action:** $$ \mathbb{E}[V \mid \text{Right}] = P(\text{B dirty}) \cdot V(\text{Suck next}) + P(\text{B clean}) \cdot V(\text{NoOp next}) $$ $$ \mathbb{E}[V \mid \text{Right}] = 0.5 \cdot (+1_{\text{clean A}} + 1_{\text{clean B}} - 1_{\text{move}}) + 0.5 \cdot (+1_{\text{clean A}} + 1_{\text{clean B}} - 1_{\text{move}}) $$ $$ \mathbb{E}[V \mid \text{Right}] = 0.5 \cdot (1 + 1 - 1) + 0.5 \cdot (1 + 1 - 1) = 1.0 $$ **Step 3 — Expected Value of `Suck` action:** Sucking a clean square is a no-op: $$ \mathbb{E}[V \mid \text{Suck}] = 1.0 \;\; (\text{no movement penalty, no dirt removed}) $$ **Step 4 — Rational Choice:** Both actions yield $1.0$. In a tie, KTU board answers typically prefer `Right` to *explore* the other square, demonstrating **information-seeking** rational behavior. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Rational Agent Decision Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR envA[Environment] -->|provides percepts| agentA[Rational Agent] agentA -->|executes action| envA agentA --> perfA[Performance Measure Evaluator] agentA -->|uses| knowA[Prior Knowledge Base] perfA -->|feedback| agentA knowA --> agentA classDef envNode fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px classDef agentNode fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px classDef evalNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px class envA envNode class agentA agentNode class perfA evalNode ``` **Interpretation:** The diagram shows the closed perception–action feedback loop. The agent receives percepts, queries its prior knowledge, and the performance evaluator steers action selection. The arrow returning from the **Performance Measure Evaluator** represents the optimization pressure that defines rationality. ## 4.2 Decomposition of the Rationality Function ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Rationality Criterion] --> B[Performance Measure V] A --> C[Prior Knowledge K] A --> D[Action Set A] A --> E[Percept History pStar] B --> F[Expected Value Maximization] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[Optimal Action aStar] classDef root fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px classDef inNode fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px classDef outNode fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px class A root class B,C,D,E inNode class F,G outNode ``` **Interpretation:** The four pillars of rationality (Performance Measure, Prior Knowledge, Action Set, Percept History) all feed into a single **Expected Value Maximization** engine, producing the optimal action. ## 4.3 Spectrum of Agent Rationality (Comparative Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[Omniscient Agent
Theoretical Only] --> N2[Ideal Rational
Benchmark Agent] N2 --> N3[Bounded Optimal
Resource Limited] N3 --> N4[Bounded Rational
Satisficing] N4 --> N5[Simple Reflex
Reactive] N5 --> N6[Random Agent
Baseline] classDef best fill:#86efac,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px classDef mid fill:#fef08a,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px classDef base fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px class N1,N2 best class N3,N4 mid class N5,N6 base ``` **Interpretation:** This spectrum places agents along a continuum of rationality. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify **which type of agent** is appropriate for a given engineering problem. ## 4.4 Mapping Percept Histories to Rational Actions (Vacuum World) ```mermaid flowchart TB S0[Start] --> P1[Percept: A Clean] P1 --> P2[Percept: A Clean] P2 --> P3[Percept: A Dirty] P3 --> P4[Percept: B Clean] P4 --> P5[Percept: B Dirty] S0 -->|init| A1[Action: Right - explore] P1 -->|rational| A1 P2 -->|rational| A1 P3 -->|rational| A2[Action: Suck - clean] P4 -->|rational| A3[Action: Left - explore] P5 -->|rational| A4[Action: Suck - clean] classDef state fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px classDef action fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px class S0,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 state class A1,A2,A3,A4 action ``` **Interpretation:** Each percept sequence uniquely determines a rational action. This mapping is precisely the *agent function* $f(p^{*}) = a$ introduced in Section 1. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **Define a rational agent. Explain the four key components that determine whether an agent is rational.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A *rational agent* is one that, for every possible percept sequence, selects an action that is **expected to maximize its performance measure**, given the evidence provided by the percepts and any prior knowledge it possesses **(1 mark)**. The four key components are: 1. **Performance Measure** — the externally defined criterion of success **(0.5)**. 2. **Prior Knowledge** — built-in knowledge of the environment **(0.5)**. 3. **Possible Actions** — the set $\mathcal{A}$ of actions the agent can perform **(0.5)**. 4. **Percept Sequence** — the complete history of percepts up to time $t$ **(0.5)**. ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between an omniscient agent and a rational agent. Give one example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **omniscient agent** knows the *actual outcome* of its actions and the *true state* of the world in advance **(1 mark)**, whereas a **rational agent** only knows the *expected outcome* of its actions based on its percept history and prior knowledge, and acts to maximize this expectation **(1 mark)**. Omniscience is impossible in practice due to uncertainty and partial observability, while rationality is the realistic engineering target. **Example (1 mark):** A self-driving car that *knows* (omniscient) that a hidden pedestrian will cross the road in 2 seconds vs. one that *expects* a pedestrian might cross based on a zebra-crossing percept (rational). --- ## 📝 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** *“Answer any one of the following: (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each.”* --- ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the concept of rationality in Artificial Intelligence. With the help of the vacuum-cleaner world example, illustrate how an ideal rational agent operates. Discuss why rationality does not imply omniscience.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. Definition of Rationality (2 marks):** A rational agent, as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, selects actions that **maximize the expected performance measure** given the percept sequence and built-in knowledge. Formally: $$ a^{*} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \;\; \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, K] $$ **2. Vacuum-Cleaner World Illustration (3 marks):** - **Percepts:** `[Location, Status]` where Status $\in \{$ Clean, Dirty $\}$ and Location $\in \{A, B\}$. - **Actions:** $\mathcal{A} = \{$ Left, Right, Suck, NoOp $\}$. - **Performance Measure:** $+1$ for every clean square per time step; $-1$ for every movement. - **Behavior Table:** | Percept | Rational Action | Reason | |:---|:---:|:---| | `[A, Clean]` | `Right` | Other square may be dirty — explore. | | `[A, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Clean current square immediately. | | `[B, Clean]` | `Left` | Symmetric exploration. | | `[B, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Maximize immediate reward. | - The agent function $f$ maps every possible percept sequence $p^{*} \in \mathcal{P}^{*}$ to a rational action. For example, $f([A, \text{Clean}], [A, \text{Dirty}]) = $ `Suck`. **3. Why Rationality $\neq$ Omniscience (2 marks):** - Omniscience requires *perfect* foreknowledge of the environment state. In a stochastic vacuum world, the agent cannot predict *with certainty* when new dirt will appear. - Rationality only requires choosing the action with the *highest expected* performance, which is achievable with finite information. - **Example:** The agent may *rationally* choose `Suck` at A, but if dirt simultaneously reappears at B (an unforeseeable event), the agent is *not* omniscient — yet its decision was still the best possible given what it knew. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Compare and contrast ideal rationality, bounded rationality, and bounded optimality. How do computational limitations affect the design of real-world AI systems?** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. Ideal Rationality (2 marks):** - An *ideal rational* agent always selects the action that maximizes the *expected* performance measure, with **unlimited computational resources**. - It serves as a **theoretical benchmark** (Russell & Norvig's agent function $f^{*}$). - **Limitation:** Real systems cannot afford infinite computation. **2. Bounded Rationality — Herbert Simon (2 marks):** - Introduced by **Herbert A. Simon (1957)**. Acknowledges that real agents have **finite time, memory, and processing power**. - A bounded rational agent *satisficing* — it selects a "good enough" action that meets a satisfactory threshold rather than the global optimum. - **Mathematical Formulation:** $$ a^{*}_{\text{bounded}} = \underset{a \in \mathcal{A},\; C(a) \le C_{\max}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \;\; \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, a] $$ **3. Bounded Optimality — Russell & Subramanian (2 marks):** - A *bounded optimal* program is one that performs **as well as possible** given its resource constraints. - Unlike bounded rationality (which is behavior-based), bounded optimality is a **design objective** — you want your agent's *code* to be the best program achievable within its resource bound. - This is the practical engineering target for real AI systems (autonomous vehicles, recommendation engines, trading bots). **4. Impact on Real-World AI Design (1 mark):** - Real systems use **heuristics, approximation algorithms, and anytime algorithms** to remain within the resource envelope. - Example: **Google DeepMind's AlphaGo** uses Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) — it does not explore the entire game tree (impossible), but explores the *most promising* branches first. --- ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Discuss the role of the performance measure in determining agent rationality. What are the difficulties in defining an appropriate performance measure? Illustrate with two real-world examples.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. Role of the Performance Measure (2 marks):** The performance measure $V$ is the **objective yardstick** that evaluates how well an agent is doing. A rational agent acts to **maximize $V$**. Without a clearly defined $V$, rationality itself becomes undefined. **2. Difficulties in Defining a Performance Measure (3 marks):** - **Proxy vs. True Objective:** Designers often specify an easily measurable proxy that may *not* align with the true goal. Example: measuring *click-through rate* for a news recommender may encourage clickbait, not user satisfaction. - **Short-term vs. Long-term Rewards:** Optimizing for immediate reward may sacrifice long-term value (e.g., a vacuum agent that just sits still to avoid movement penalties). - **Multi-objective Trade-offs:** Real systems must balance competing criteria (e.g., accuracy vs. latency vs. fairness in a medical AI). - **Unforeseen Side Effects:** Reward hacking — agents exploiting loopholes in $V$. **3. Real-World Examples (2 marks):** - **Example 1 — Self-Driving Cars:** A natural performance measure is *safety* (zero collisions). However, this must be balanced against *travel time* and *passenger comfort*. Designers use a weighted sum: $$ V = w_{1}(\text{Safety}) + w_{2}(\text{Efficiency}) + w_{3}(\text{Comfort}) $$ - **Example 2 — Conversational AI (Chatbots):** Performance could be measured by *user retention*, but this may lead to manipulative engagement. Better measures include *task completion rate* and *user satisfaction scores*. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the concepts of percept, percept sequence, and agent function. For a 2×1 vacuum-cleaner world with a dirt sensor, construct a partial table of the agent function and justify each entry.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **1. Definitions (2 marks):** - **Percept** — The agent's perceptual input at one time instant, drawn from a percept set $\mathcal{P}$. - **Percept Sequence** — The complete history $p^{*} = \langle p_{1}, p_{2}, \dots, p_{t} \rangle$. - **Agent Function** — The abstract mathematical specification $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ that maps every percept history to a chosen action. The **agent program** is the concrete implementation of $f$. **2. Constructing the Agent Function Table (3 marks):** | Percept Sequence $p^{*}$ | Action $f(p^{*})$ | Justification (Performance) | |:---|:---:|:---| | `[A, Clean]` | `Right` | Move to explore B; maximize clean-square count. | | `[A, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Cleaning yields $+1$ reward; immediate gain. | | `[B, Clean]` | `Left` | Symmetric exploration; check A. | | `[B, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Direct cleaning action; same reasoning as row 2. | | `[A, Clean], [A, Clean]` | `Right` | Persistence of cleanliness at A; explore B. | | `[A, Clean], [A, Dirty]` | `Suck` | Environment changed; respond to new dirt. | | `[A, Clean], [A, Clean], [A, Clean]` | `Right` | Strong belief A is permanently clean; explore B. | **3. Discussion (2 marks):** - The agent function must be defined for **all possible percept sequences** of any length. In practice, the table grows exponentially — that is why AI uses *internal representations* (states) to compactly summarize history. - The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the distinction between the **agent function** (abstract) and the **agent program** (concrete running software). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Rationality Questions:** > 1. **Confusing rationality with omniscience** (most common; loses 2–3 marks in Part A). > 2. **Writing `max` instead of `argmax`** in the action-selection equation — the KTU board specifically checks whether you return the *action*, not the *value*. > 3. **Failing to specify the performance measure** explicitly in vacuum-world questions; the agent's rationality is *undefined* without $V$. > 4. **Treating bounded rationality and bounded optimality as synonyms** — they differ in framing (behavioral vs. design). > 5. **Skipping the prior-knowledge clause** in the definition; the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates all four pillars (V, K, A, p*). --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Rational Agent** = selects the action that **maximizes expected performance** given percept history, prior knowledge, and available actions. - ✅ **The Four Pillars:** Performance Measure $V$, Prior Knowledge $K$, Action Set $\mathcal{A}$, Percept Sequence $p^{*}$. - ✅ **Agent Function** $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ — the *abstract* specification. - ✅ **Agent Program** — the *concrete* running implementation of $f$. - ✅ **Omniscient $\neq$ Rational** — omniscient knows true outcomes; rational knows only *expected* outcomes. - ✅ **Bounded Rationality (Herbert Simon, 1957)** — satisficing under computational limits. - ✅ **Bounded Optimality (Russell & Subramanian, 1993)** — best achievable program within the resource cap $C_{\max}$. - ✅ **Rationality maximizes *expected* value, not *actual* value** — use $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ in equations. - ✅ **Vacuum-Cleaner World:** Percept = `[Location, Status]`; Actions = `{Left, Right, Suck, NoOp}`; typical $V$ = $+1$ clean, $-1$ move. - ✅ **Performance measure must be defined *before* rationality can be evaluated** — never compute rational action without $V$. - ✅ **Equation to memorize:** $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[V \mid p^{*}, K]$. - ✅ **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level tested:** *Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze* — Part A tests "Remember/Understand"; Part B tests "Apply/Analyze". - ✅ **Real-world link:** Self-driving cars, AlphaGo, medical diagnosis AIs — all designed around the *ideal rational agent* paradigm.

topics.title: The Nature of Environments, Structure of Agents, Problem Solving Agents, Well-Defined Problems and Solutions, Formulating Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Nature of Environments, Structure of Agents, Problem Solving Agents, Well-Defined Problems and Solutions, Formulating Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Environment in AI?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An **Environment** in Artificial Intelligence is the external world with which an **Agent** interacts through its **Sensors** (to perceive) and **Actuators** (to act upon). Formally, an environment $\mathcal{E}$ is defined as the tuple $\langle S, A, T, R \rangle$ where $S$ is the set of percepts, $A$ is the set of possible actions, $T$ represents the transition dynamics, and $R$ denotes the reward/performance signal (in reinforcement learning contexts) or implicit performance measure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEAS Framework):**
> Every task environment in AI is characterized by the **PEAS** specification:
> **P**erformance, **E**nvironment, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors.
> This is the *first* thing KTU examiners look for in a 7-mark question on agents.

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of a **delivery robot** operating in a hotel lobby:
- **Sensors** = cameras, lidar, touch sensors (eyes & ears)
- **Actuators** = wheels, gripper arms (hands & legs)
- **Environment** = the lobby itself (rooms, furniture, people)
- **Performance Measure** = number of successful deliveries, time taken, battery conservation

Just like a human navigating a room, the AI agent must *perceive → decide → act* repeatedly. The **environment** is everything "out there" the agent cannot fully control but must adapt to.

## 1.2 Properties of Environments (The 7-Dimensional Taxonomy)

Environments are classified along **seven orthogonal axes**. Each axis is binary (or finite), and the combination defines the environment's nature. These properties are essential for KTU questions worth 3–7 marks.

| # | Property | Possible Values | Description |
|---|----------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Observability | Fully / Partially / Unobservable | Can the agent see the complete state? |
| 2 | Determinism | Deterministic / Stochastic | Is the next state fully determined by current state + action? |
| 3 | Episodicity | Episodic / Sequential | Are actions evaluated independently (episodic) or with future impact (sequential)? |
| 4 | Staticity | Static / Dynamic / Semidynamic | Does the environment change while the agent deliberates? |
| 5 | Discreteness | Discrete / Continuous | Is the state-action space finite/countable? |
| 6 | Agency | Single-agent / Multi-agent | How many interacting entities exist? |
| 7 | Knownness | Known / Unknown | Are the outcomes of actions known to the designer? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Card Game Example (KTU favorite):**
> - **Poker** = Partially observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Static, Discrete, Multi-agent (adversarial), Unknown (opponent strategy).
> - **Chess** = Fully observable, Deterministic, Sequential, Static (with clock: semidynamic), Discrete, Multi-agent, Known.

## 1.3 What is an Agent?

**Formal Definition:**
An **Agent** is any entity that can be viewed as *perceiving* its environment through sensors and *acting* upon that environment through actuators. Mathematically, an agent implements a function $f: \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$ that maps the entire history of percepts to an action.

$$\text{Agent Function: } f: P^* \rightarrow A$$

where $P^*$ is the set of all possible percept sequences and $A$ is the set of possible actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rationally ≠ Omniscience:** A *rational* agent always selects the action that **maximizes its expected performance measure** given its percept sequence and prior knowledge — it need not know the true outcome, only the expected one.

## 1.4 Problem-Solving Agents — The Core Abstraction

A **Problem-Solving Agent** is a specific subclass of goal-based agents that uses **atomic** representations (states as indivisible wholes) and operates in a four-phase loop:
1. **Goal Formulation** — What must be achieved?
2. **Problem Formulation** — What actions and states are relevant?
3. **Search** — Find the best action sequence.
4. **Execution** — Perform the actions.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Definition: "A problem-solving agent decides what to do by finding sequences of actions that lead to desirable states." — *Russell & Norvig, AIMA* (standard KTU reference).

## 1.5 Well-Defined Problems and Solutions

A **well-defined problem** is one where:
- The **initial state** is fully specified.
- The **goal state(s)** can be clearly recognized.
- The **actions** available in each state are known.
- The **cost** of each action (path cost) is known.

A **solution** is a sequence of actions $\langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$ that transforms the initial state into a goal state. An **optimal solution** is the one with the **minimum path cost**.

## 1.6 Formulating Problems

Problem formulation is the bridge between the *real world* and the *abstract problem space*. The formulator must:
1. Decide what to include in the state representation.
2. Decide what to omit (abstraction).
3. Ensure the abstraction is *useful* (a solution in the abstract model should be usable in the real world).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Confusing *problem formulation* with *problem solving*. Formulation is **modeling**; solving is **searching**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PEAS Specification (Detailed Breakdown)

The PEAS framework is the **single most-tested** topic in this module. Let us dissect each component.

### P — Performance Measure
The criterion that defines *success* of the agent.
- Must be **objective** (not subjective "intelligence").
- Must be **measurable** (numerical).
- **Example (Automated Taxi):** safety, speed, legality, comfort, profit.

### E — Environment
The external world the agent inhabits. Specified as a set of states plus rules of evolution.
- **Example (Taxi):** roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, signals.

### A — Actuators
The physical (or simulated) mechanisms through which the agent exerts influence.
- **Example (Taxi):** steering, accelerator, brake, display, speaker.

### S — Sensors
The perceptual channels providing information to the agent.
- **Example (Taxi):** cameras, sonar, GPS, speedometer, odometer, keyboard input.

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Environment Properties

### A. Observability

$$O(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if agent perceives complete state } s \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- **Fully Observable:** All relevant aspects of state are accessible via sensors. *Example:* Chess (full board visible).
- **Partially Observable:** Some state variables are hidden. *Example:* Poker (opponent's cards hidden).
- **Unobservable:** No sensors at all. *Example:* Vacuum agent in a totally dark room.

### B. Determinism

$$T(s, a, s') = P(s' \mid s, a) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{Deterministic} \\ [0, 1] & \text{Stochastic} \end{cases}$$

- **Deterministic:** The next state is uniquely determined by the current state and action.
- **Stochastic:** Next state is described by a probability distribution.

### C. Episodicity

- **Episodic:** Each action is independent; the next episode does not depend on the current. *Example:* Defect detection on an assembly line.
- **Sequential:** Current decision affects all future decisions. *Example:* Chess, driving.

### D. Staticity

- **Static:** Environment unchanged during deliberation.
- **Dynamic:** Environment changes continuously.
- **Semidynamic:** Environment itself static, but performance score changes with time. *Example:* Chess with clock.

### E. Discreteness

- **Discrete:** Finite/countable states, percepts, actions. *Example:* Chess.
- **Continuous:** Real-valued state space. *Example:* Autonomous driving.

### F. Agency

- **Single-Agent:** Agent is the only intelligent entity. *Example:* Solving a crossword puzzle.
- **Multi-Agent Cooperative:** Agents share the same utility. *Example:* RoboCup soccer (allied team).
- **Multi-Agent Competitive:** Agents have opposing utilities. *Example:* Chess (zero-sum).
- **Multi-Agent Mixed:** Both cooperation and competition. *Example:* Trading scenarios.

### G. Knownness

- **Known:** Agent knows the outcome distribution of its actions in advance.
- **Unknown:** Agent must learn outcomes through experience.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula & Concept Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Formula Sheet** — All essential equations, definitions, and decision rules in one place. Memorize before the exam.

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Example |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Agent Function | $f: P^* \rightarrow A$ | Maps percept history to action |
| PEAS | $\langle P, E, A, S \rangle$ | Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors |
| Transition | $T(s, a, s') = P(s' \vert s, a)$ | Probability of reaching $s'$ from $s$ via $a$ |
| Path Cost | $c(s_0, a_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} c(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1})$ | Total cost of a solution path |
| Step Cost | $c(s, a, s')$ | Cost of a single action |
| Rationality | $\arg\max_{a} E[\text{Performance} \vert \text{PerceptSequence}]$ | Choose action maximizing expected performance |
| State Space | $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ | Number of possible states |
| Branching Factor | $b$ | Max successors per state |
| Depth of Goal | $d$ | Depth of shallowest goal node |
| Goal Test | $\text{GoalTest}(s) \in \{0, 1\}$ | Predicate: is $s$ a goal? |

## 2.4 Structure of Agents (The Four-Level Hierarchy)

KTU 2024 emphasizes the **four agent architectures** in increasing sophistication:

### Level 1: Simple Reflex Agent
- Architecture: `Condition-Action Rule`
- Internals: None — directly maps percepts to actions.
- **Pros:** Simple, fast.
- **Cons:** Fails in partially observable environments (no memory).

### Level 2: Model-Based Reflex Agent
- Maintains an **internal state** $S_t$ tracking aspects of the world not currently observable.
- Uses a **transition model** $P(s' \vert s, a)$ and **sensor model** $P(e \vert s)$.
- Handles **partial observability**.

### Level 3: Goal-Based Agent
- Augments model with a **goal description**.
- Chooses actions that achieve the goal.
- Involves **planning** and **search** ahead.

### Level 4: Utility-Based Agent
- Augments goal with a **utility function** $U: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$.
- Handles **conflicting goals** and **uncertainty** by maximizing expected utility.
- **Most general** rational agent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
