> **Higher number = More general = More computationally expensive.**
> KTU often asks: *"Which agent type for which scenario?"* — expect 7-mark questions.

## 2.5 Components of a Well-Defined Problem (The 5-Tuple)

A formal problem is defined as the **5-tuple**:
$$\langle \mathcal{S}, s_0, \text{ACTIONS}, \text{RESULT}, \text{GoalTest} \rangle$$

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| State Space | $\mathcal{S}$ | Set of all reachable states |
| Initial State | $s_0 \in \mathcal{S}$ | Where the agent starts |
| Actions | $\text{ACTIONS}(s)$ | Function returning applicable actions at state $s$ |
| Transition Model | $\text{RESULT}(s, a) = s'$ | Result of applying action $a$ in state $s$ |
| Goal Test | $\text{GoalTest}(s)$ | Boolean function identifying goal states |
| Path Cost | $c(s, a, s')$ | Numeric cost of a single step (added optionally) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Environment Type |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| Autonomous Vehicles | Self-driving cars | Partially Observable, Stochastic, Dynamic, Continuous, Multi-agent |
| Industrial Robotics | Assembly line arms | Fully Observable, Deterministic, Static, Discrete, Single-agent |
| Game AI | Chess engines | Fully Observable, Deterministic, Sequential, Discrete, Multi-agent (adversarial) |
| Medical Diagnosis | Symptom-based diagnosis | Partially Observable, Stochastic, Sequential, Dynamic, Single-agent |
| Web Crawlers | Search engines | Partially Observable, Deterministic, Sequential, Dynamic, Single-agent |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: From Real World to Abstract Problem

The formulation process is itself a **projection** from reality to abstraction. Let us formalize it.

### Step 1: Identify the Real World State
Let the *true* world be modeled as an evolving state vector:
$$\mathbf{w}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \text{position}(t) \\ \text{velocity}(t) \\ \text{fuel}(t) \\ \text{obstacles}(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

This is generally **infinite-dimensional**, continuous, and high-cardinality.

### Step 2: Define the Abstract State Space
Choose a mapping $\phi: \mathcal{W} \rightarrow \mathcal{S}$ that compresses $\mathcal{W}$ into a tractable $\mathcal{S}$:
$$\phi(\mathbf{w}_t) = \begin{bmatrix} \text{grid}_x(t) \\ \text{grid}_y(t) \\ \text{fuel\_level}(t) \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Define the Transition Model on $\mathcal{S}$
The abstract transition must be a valid **simulation** of the real dynamics:
$$\text{RESULT}(s, a) = s' \iff \exists \mathbf{w} : \phi(\mathbf{w}) = s \text{ and } \exists a' \in \text{ACTIONS}_{\text{real}}(\mathbf{w}) : \phi(\mathbf{RESULT}_{\text{real}}(\mathbf{w}, a')) = s'$$

In plain words: an abstract solution must be **executable** in the real world.

### Step 4: Define the Goal Test
The goal test in the abstract model must match real-world success:
$$\text{GoalTest}(s) = \text{GoalTest}_{\text{real}}(\phi^{-1}(s))$$

### Step 5: Define the Step Cost
The cost in $\mathcal{S}$ must be a **monotone** function of real cost:
$$c(s, a, s') = c_{\text{real}}(\phi^{-1}(s), a_{\text{mapped}}, \phi^{-1}(s'))$$

This guarantees that an optimal solution in the abstract model is also optimal in the real world (under the abstraction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** When asked *"What is a good abstraction?"*, the answer is *"An abstraction is good if for every abstract solution there exists a real-world plan that executes it with cost ≤ the abstract cost, and which actually achieves the goal."* This appears verbatim in 14-mark questions.

## 3.2 Worked Example: The Vacuum World (Step-by-Step)

This is the **canonical KTU example**. The room has two squares, and a vacuum agent must clean both.

### Step 1: State Space
The state must capture all relevant aspects:
$$s = \langle \text{AgentLocation}, \text{Status}_A, \text{Status}_B \rangle$$

where $\text{AgentLocation} \in \{A, B\}$ and $\text{Status}_X \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$.

Therefore:
$$\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8 \text{ states}$$

### Step 2: Enumerate the State Space
| State # | Agent Location | A Status | B Status |
|---------|----------------|----------|----------|
| 1 | A | Clean | Clean |
| 2 | A | Clean | Dirty |
| 3 | A | Dirty | Clean |
| 4 | A | Dirty | Dirty |
| 5 | B | Clean | Clean |
| 6 | B | Clean | Dirty |
| 7 | B | Dirty | Clean |
| 8 | B | Dirty | Dirty |

### Step 3: Initial State
Let $s_0 = \langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$ (agent at A, both rooms dirty).

### Step 4: Define ACTIONS
$$\text{ACTIONS}(s) = \begin{cases} \{\text{Right}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp}\} & \text{if } s = \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \\ \{\text{Left}, \text{Suck}, \text{NoOp}\} & \text{if } s = \langle B, \text{Dirty}, \text{Clean} \rangle \\ \text{etc.} \end{cases}$$

### Step 5: Define RESULT
$$\text{RESULT}(\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle, \text{Suck}) = \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$$
$$\text{RESULT}(\langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle, \text{Right}) = \langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$$

### Step 6: Define Goal Test
$$\text{GoalTest}(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s = \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle \text{ OR } s = \langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 7: Define Path Cost
$$c(s, a, s') = 1 \text{ for every action (uniform cost)}$$

### Solution Path
A possible solution from $s_0 = \langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle$:

$$\langle A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Suck}} \langle A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Right}} \langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty} \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Suck}} \langle B, \text{Clean}, \text{Clean} \rangle$$

Path cost = $3$.

## 3.3 Worked Example: The 8-Puzzle (Formulation)

The 8-puzzle is a classic **sliding tile puzzle** on a $3 \times 3$ grid.

### State Representation
Each state $s$ is a permutation of $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 8\}$ where $0$ denotes the blank tile.
$$\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 9! = 362{,}880 \text{ states}$$

### Initial State
$$s_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 8 & 3 \\ 1 & 6 & 4 \\ 7 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Goal State
$$s_g = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 8 & 0 & 4 \\ 7 & 6 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

### ACTIONS(s)
A tile can slide into the blank if orthogonally adjacent. Branching factor $b \in [2, 4]$.

### RESULT(s, a)
Returns the new grid configuration after sliding the chosen tile.

### GoalTest(s)
Returns 1 if and only if $s = s_g$.

### Path Cost
$c(s, a, s') = 1$ per move.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Bonus:** For the 15-puzzle ($4 \times 4$), $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 16! / 2 = 10{,}461{,}394{,}944{,}000$ (the division by 2 is because the puzzle has **two disjoint state spaces**, one of which is unsolvable from the other — a classic parity argument).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Agent Type Decision Table

| Scenario | Best Agent Type | Reason |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| Thermostat controlling room temperature | Simple Reflex | Rule `IF temp < setpoint THEN heat` suffices |
| Robot in a maze with no walls visible | Model-Based Reflex | Must remember walls seen before |
| Self-driving car navigating to a destination | Goal-Based | Has a destination as the goal |
| Medical treatment recommendation | Utility-Based | Multiple conflicting outcomes (cure, side-effects, cost) |
| Spam classifier | Simple Reflex | Direct word-to-spam mapping works |
| Stock trading bot | Utility-Based | Maximize profit, manage risk, multiple objectives |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agent–Environment Interaction (Core Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent] -- Percepts --> E[Environment]
    E -- Percepts Sensors --> A
    A -- Actions Actuators --> E
    subgraph Feedback Loop
        E
        A
    end
    P[Performance Measure] -.Evaluates.-> A
```

> **Reading guide:** The agent issues actions to the environment via its **actuators**; the environment returns percepts to the agent via its **sensors**. The performance measure is external and evaluates the agent's behavior retrospectively.

## 4.2 The Four Agent Architectures (Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Simple Reflex Agent] --> M[Model Based Reflex Agent]
    M --> G[Goal Based Agent]
    G --> U[Utility Based Agent]
    S -.Condition Action Rules.-> note1[No internal state]
    M -.Internal state and transition model.-> note2[Handles partial observability]
    G -.Goal description and planning.-> note3[Sequence of actions]
    U -.Utility function.-> note4[Maximize expected utility]
```

## 4.3 Problem-Solving Agent Loop (Four Phases)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Initial Percept]) --> G[Goal Formulation]
    G --> P[Problem Formulation]
    P --> Sr[Search for Solution]
    Sr --> E[Execute Actions]
    E --> Obs[Observe New Percept]
    Obs --> Ssm{Goal Reached?}
    Ssm -- No --> G
    Ssm -- Yes --> Done([Task Complete])
```

> **Reading guide:** This is a *continuous* loop — the agent reformulates at every step if a new goal arises or the environment changes.

## 4.4 Problem Formulation Pipeline (5-Tuple Construction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RW[Real World Domain] --> Step1[Step 1 Identify State Variables]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2 Abstract to Finite State Space]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Define Initial State]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Define ACTIONS and RESULT]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Define Goal Test and Path Cost]
    Step5 --> AB[Abstract Problem Tuple]
    AB --> Search[Apply Search Algorithm]
    Search --> Sol[Solution Action Sequence]
    Sol --> Map[Map Back to Real World]
    Map --> Act[Execute in Environment]
```

## 4.5 Environment Property Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Environment] --> Obs{Observable?}
    Obs -- Fully --> Det{Deterministic?}
    Obs -- Partially --> SDetSto[Partially Observable Stochastic]
    Det -- Yes --> Ep{Episodic?}
    Det -- No --> Sto[Stochastic]
    Ep -- Yes --> Stat{Static?}
    Ep -- No --> Seq[Sequential]
    Stat -- Yes --> Disc{Discrete?}
    Stat -- No --> Dyn[Dynamic]
    Disc -- Yes --> SA{Single Agent?}
    Disc -- No --> Cont[Continuous]
    SA -- Yes --> Final1[Fully Observable Deterministic Episodic Static Discrete Single Agent]
    SA -- No --> Final2[Multi Agent Variant]
```

## 4.6 Complete Problem Formulation Example (8-Puzzle Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[Initial State Grid] --> Act1[ACTIONS Move Tile Adjacent to Blank]
    Act1 --> Res1[RESULT New Grid Configuration]
    Res1 --> GT1[GOAL TEST Match Target Grid]
    GT1 --> PC1[PATH COST 1 per move]
    Init --> States
    Act1 --> States
    Res1 --> States
    States[State Space 9 factorial permutations]
```

> **Reading guide:** Every block on the left contributes one element of the 5-tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, s_0, \text{ACTIONS}, \text{RESULT}, \text{GoalTest} \rangle$ + Path Cost. This is the most efficient answer structure for a 14-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Path Cost component** — most students define only 4 elements; the question expects 5 (including path cost).
> 2. **Confusing the agent function with the agent program** — function is abstract; program is the concrete implementation.
> 3. **Treating "rational" as "omniscient"** — rationality is **expected** performance, not absolute knowledge.
> 4. **Mixing up episodic and static** — episodic refers to *percept-action coupling*; static refers to *environment change during deliberation*.
> 5. **Writing vague goal tests** — the goal test must be a *boolean predicate* on state, not a verbal description.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]
**Define an AI agent. Explain any two characteristics of an agent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
An AI agent is any entity that perceives its environment through **sensors** and acts upon that environment through **actuators**. It implements an **agent function** $f: P^* \rightarrow A$ that maps every possible percept sequence to an action.

**Two characteristics (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Autonomy:** An agent's behavior is determined by its own experience and built-in knowledge, not solely by the prior knowledge of its designer.
2. **Reactivity:** A rational agent perceives its environment and responds in a timely fashion to changes that affect its performance.
3. *(Alternate — Pro-activeness:)* Goal-directed behavior by taking initiative to achieve objectives rather than merely reacting.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**List and briefly explain the components of the PEAS specification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

| Component | Full Form | Description | Example (Taxi) |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **P** | Performance | Success criterion | Safe, fast, legal, comfortable ride |
| **E** | Environment | The external world | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather |
| **A** | Actuators | Action mechanisms | Steering wheel, brakes, accelerator |
| **S** | Sensors | Perceptual channels | Cameras, GPS, speedometer, sonar |

A complete PEAS specification **fully and unambiguously** defines the task environment for any AI agent.

---

## Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks)

### Q3.A. [KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the four basic types of agents in detail with neat diagrams.
**(b) [7 marks]** Differentiate between goal-based and utility-based agents. When would you prefer a utility-based agent over a goal-based agent?

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The four basic agent architectures, in increasing order of sophistication, are:

**1. Simple Reflex Agent (1.5 marks):**
- Architecture: A set of **condition-action rules** of the form:
$$\text{IF } \text{percept} = P \text{ THEN } \text{action} = A$$
- **Internal state:** None.
- **Working:** Selects action purely on the basis of the current percept, ignoring percept history.
- **Limitation:** Will fail in partially observable environments (e.g., cannot detect which square is dirty if the sensor only indicates current square's status).
- **Example:** A simple thermostat.

**2. Model-Based Reflex Agent (1.5 marks):**
- Maintains an **internal state** $I_t$ that depends on the percept history and reflects at least some of the unobserved aspects of the current state.
- Requires two models:
  - **Transition model:** $T: S \times A \rightarrow S$, or in stochastic form $P(s' \vert s, a)$.
  - **Sensor model:** $P(e \vert s)$, describing how the world generates percepts.
- Updates internal state using:
$$I_{t+1} = \text{UPDATE}(I_t, a_t, \text{percept}_t)$$
- **Example:** A robot vacuum that remembers the rooms it has already cleaned.

**3. Goal-Based Agent (2 marks):**
- Augments the model with **goal information** describing desired situations.
- Uses **search and planning** to find action sequences that lead to the goal.
- Decision-making: $\text{action} = \arg\min_{a} \text{distance}(\text{RESULT}(s, a), \text{Goal})$.
- **Advantage over reflex:** Looks ahead; more flexible.
- **Example:** A route-finding GPS.

**4. Utility-Based Agent (2 marks):**
- Augments goal with a **utility function** $U: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ mapping each state to a real number describing *degree of happiness*.
- Chooses action that **maximizes expected utility**:
$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in \text{ACTIONS}(s)} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot U(s')$$
- **Required when:**
  - Multiple conflicting goals exist.
  - Goals are uncertain.
  - The agent must weigh trade-offs (e.g., speed vs. safety).
- **Example:** Medical decision support system that balances cure rate, side-effects, and cost.

**Diagram (Embedded in description, not separately required by KTU 2024 scheme for 7-mark questions, but students may sketch the standard block diagrams from Russell & Norvig).**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Goal-Based Agent | Utility-Based Agent |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Decision Basis | Whether goal is achieved | Quality/satisfaction level |
| Output | Action sequence to goal | Action maximizing expected utility |
| Handles Multiple Goals? | No (must combine into one) | Yes (encoded in utility) |
| Handles Uncertainty? | Poorly | Yes (uses expected utility) |
| Computation | Cheaper | More expensive |
| Trade-offs | Cannot represent | Explicitly representable |

**When to prefer Utility-Based (4 marks):**

A utility-based agent is preferred in the following scenarios:

1. **Conflicting Goals (1 mark):** When there are multiple objectives and the agent must perform a *trade-off* (e.g., a car that must balance speed, safety, and fuel economy). Goals alone are insufficient — they cannot be prioritized dynamically.

2. **Uncertain Outcomes (1 mark):** When the environment is stochastic and the agent must choose among actions with probabilistic outcomes. The expected utility principle gives a clear optimal decision rule:
$$EU(a \vert s) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

3. **Goal Insufficiency (1 mark):** When mere achievement of the goal is not enough. Example: "Reach the airport" is the goal, but multiple paths exist — utility captures preferences (fastest vs. cheapest vs. most scenic).

4. **Lexicographic Preferences (1 mark):** When the agent's preference depends on *degree* of success rather than binary success/failure. Utility assigns cardinal values.

> **Conclusion:** A goal-based agent is a *special case* of a utility-based agent where the utility function is binary ($U(s) = 1$ for goal states, $0$ otherwise). Utility-based agents are strictly more general.

---

## Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks)

### Q3.B. [KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]
**(a) [7 marks]** Define a well-defined problem. Formulate the 8-puzzle problem as a well-defined problem with all five components.
**(b) [7 marks]** Compare fully observable, deterministic, episodic, static, and discrete environment with partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, and continuous environment. Give one example for each combination.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Definition of Well-Defined Problem (1 mark):**
A problem is *well-defined* when the initial state, goal test, actions, transition model, and path cost are all precisely specified — leaving no ambiguity. The opposite, a *ill-defined problem*, has vague goals or unspecified actions.

**8-Puzzle Formulation (6 marks — 1.2 each):**

1. **State Space $\mathcal{S}$:**
A state is a permutation of 9 tiles $\{0, 1, \ldots, 8\}$ on a $3 \times 3$ grid, where $0$ is the blank.
$$\vert \mathcal{S} \vert = 9! = 362{,}880 \text{ states}$$

2. **Initial State $s_0$:**
A specific starting configuration, e.g.:
$$s_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & 0 & 6 \\ 8 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

3. **ACTIONS Function:**
$$\text{ACTIONS}(s) = \{ \text{slide tile } t \in \text{Neighbors}(blank(s)) \}$$
- Branching factor: $b \in [2, 4]$ depending on whether blank is in corner, edge, or center.
- Mean branching factor: $b \approx 3$.

4. **RESULT Function:**
$$\text{RESULT}(s, a) = \text{new configuration after applying action } a \text{ to state } s$$
- E.g., $\text{RESULT}(s_0, \text{slide tile 5 up}) = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 4 \\ 0 & 5 & 6 \\ 8 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

5. **Goal Test:**
$$\text{GoalTest}(s) = (s = s_g) \text{ where } s_g = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

6. **Path Cost:**
$$c(s, a, s') = 1 \text{ per move}$$

**Total Formulation:**
$$\text{Problem} = \langle \mathcal{S}, s_0, \text{ACTIONS}, \text{RESULT}, \text{GoalTest}, c \rangle$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks — 0.4 per row):**

| Property | Type A Environment | Type B Environment |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Observability | **Fully observable** — agent sees complete state | **Partially observable** — some state variables hidden |
| Determinism | **Deterministic** — next state fully determined by current state + action | **Stochastic** — next state is a random variable, $P(s' \vert s, a)$ |
| Episodicity | **Episodic** — each action evaluated in isolation | **Sequential** — current decision affects all future decisions |
| Staticity | **Static** — environment does not change during deliberation | **Dynamic** — environment evolves during agent's deliberation |
| Discreteness | **Discrete** — finite/countable states, percepts, actions | **Continuous** — real-valued state space (uncountable) |

**Examples (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

**Type A Example — Crossword Puzzle:**
- **Fully observable:** All letters and blanks are visible.
- **Deterministic:** Filling a word gives a definite new state.
- **Episodic:** Each word can be filled independently.
- **Static:** The puzzle does not change while solving.
- **Discrete:** Finite number of letters per blank.
- **Single-agent:** Solver is alone (or non-interfering).

**Type B Example — Autonomous Taxi Driving:**
- **Partially observable:** Cannot see around corners; cannot anticipate other drivers' actions.
- **Stochastic:** Pedestrian crossings, weather, other cars' moves are probabilistic.
- **Sequential:** Braking now affects arrival time; lane change affects safety miles ahead.
- **Dynamic:** Traffic and pedestrians move in real time.
- **Continuous:** Position, velocity, fuel are continuous real-valued quantities.
- **Multi-agent:** Other cars, pedestrians, traffic police are all agents.

> **Note:** The 8-puzzle is *fully observable, deterministic, sequential, static, discrete, single-agent, known* — a perfect counter-example to Type B.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize:**

### ⭐ Core Concepts
- **Agent** = sensors + actuators + agent function $f: P^* \rightarrow A$.
- **Rationality** = choosing the action that maximizes **expected** performance measure.
- **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors (always start any agent problem with PEAS).

### ⭐ The 7 Environment Properties (Mnemonic: **O-D-E-S-D-A-K**)
- **O**bservability, **D**eterminism, **E**pisodicity, **S**taticity, **D**iscreteness, **A**gency, **K**nownness.

### ⭐ The 4 Agent Types (Mnemonic: **S-M-G-U**)
- **S**imple Reflex → **M**odel-Based Reflex → **G**oal-Based → **U**tility-Based.
- Each is a strict superset of the previous in capability.

### ⭐ The 5 (+1) Components of a Well-Defined Problem
1. State Space $\mathcal{S}$
2. Initial State $s_0$
3. ACTIONS$(s)$
4. RESULT$(s, a) = s'$
5. GoalTest$(s)$ (boolean)
6. **Path Cost** $c(s, a, s')$ (often included as the 6th element)

### ⭐ Formulation Tips
- Always justify your **abstraction choices** (what you kept, what you discarded, and why).
- An abstraction is *good* if every abstract solution is realizable in the real world.
- Always state the **branching factor** $b$ and **state space size** $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ when formulating.

### ⭐ Common KTU Traps
- Don't confuse **episodic** (percept-action independence) with **static** (environment-during-deliberation).
- Don't say a vacuum world is "static" — it's actually static only if we consider short deliberation; long deliberation in dynamic environments matters.
- Don't forget to specify **path cost** in the 5-tuple.
- Don't claim an agent is **omniscient** — only **rational**.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, the structure of the answer is graded as much as the content. Always include a **diagram or table** to make the examiner's life easier. KTU valuators allocate 1–2 marks for neatness and structure even when content is partial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example Problems: Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Example Problems: Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Three Canonical Toy Problems in AI In the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with classical AI pedagogy, e.g., Russell & Norvig), **Module 1: Introduction to AI and Problem Solving** introduces three "toy" problem formulations that every intelligent agent must be able to model before applying any search algorithm. They are called *toy* problems only because they are simplified — their mathematical structure is identical to real-world robotics, logistics, and scheduling problems. | # | Problem | Domain | Used to teach | |---|---------|--------|---------------| | 1 | **Vacuum World** | 2-cell grid, cleaning agent | State-space search, rational agent | | 2 | **8-Puzzle** | 3×3 sliding tiles | Uninformed/Informed search, heuristics | | 3 | **8-Queens** | 8×8 chessboard | Constraint Satisfaction, adversarial setup | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, OECMT615):** > "Formulate toy problems — Vacuum World, 8-Puzzle, 8-Queens — by identifying **state, initial state, actions, transition model, goal test, and path cost** (PEAS framework)." ## 1.2 The PEAS Framework — Universal Problem Schema Every problem an AI agent solves is formally described using five components. Memorize this acronym — it is the most heavily tested single concept in Part A of KTU exams. $$ \text{PEAS} = \underbrace{P}_{\text{Performance}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{E}_{\text{Environment}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{A}_{\text{Actuators}} \;\cup\; \underbrace{S}_{\text{Sensors}} $$ **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of PEAS as a *job description* for a robot. Before hiring a robot, you must specify **(P)** what counts as a good day at work, **(E)** where it will work, **(A)** what tools it can use, and **(S)** what its eyes/ears can perceive. Without PEAS, the agent is blind and useless. ### Formal Definition of Each PEAS Element - **Performance Measure (P):** A scalar function that evaluates any sequence of environment states. The agent's goal is to *maximize* it. - **Environment (E):** The external world in which the agent operates. It is characterized along axes such as *fully vs. partially observable, deterministic vs. stochastic, static vs. dynamic, discrete vs. continuous*. - **Actuators (A):** The set of actions $A(s) = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$ available to the agent when the world is in state $s$. - **Sensors (S):** The percept function $\text{percept}(s)$ that maps an environment state to the agent's sensory input. ## 1.3 Intuitive Overview of Each Problem > [!NOTE] > **The "Vacuum World" in Plain English:** > Imagine a robotic vacuum cleaner stuck in a tiny two-room apartment. The agent can be in room **A** or **B**; each room can be **clean or dirty**. The cleaner has three buttons: *Move Left*, *Move Right*, and *Suck*. Goal: both rooms clean. Despite its simplicity, this problem is the *Hello World* of AI search because it forces you to write down every state, every action, and the cost of moving — the very skeleton of any real search engine. > [!NOTE] > **The "8-Puzzle" in Plain English:** > Take a 3×3 grid holding tiles numbered 1–8 plus one blank space. The blank can slide up/down/left/right by swapping with an adjacent tile. Starting from a scrambled arrangement, find the sequence of moves that leads to the goal ordering. This is the direct ancestor of every Rubik's-Cube solver and warehouse-robot motion planner. > [!NOTE] > **The "8-Queens" in Plain English:** > Place 8 queens on a chessboard such that no two queens attack each other (i.e., no two share a row, column, or diagonal). Variations exist: *incremental* (place queens one by one) vs. *complete-state* (start with 8 queens and rearrange). The puzzle is the gateway to constraint-satisfaction and backtracking algorithms. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Formal Problem Formulation — The 5-Tuple Definition An AI problem is rigorously a 5-tuple: $$ \Pi = (S, s_0, A, G, c) $$ where: - $S$ = set of all reachable **states** (the *state space*) - $s_0 \in S$ = the **initial state** - $A(s)$ = set of **actions** legal in state $s$ - $G \subseteq S$ = set of **goal states** (or a goal-test predicate $\text{GoalTest}(s)$) - $c(s, a, s') \in \mathbb{R}^{\geq 0}$ = **path cost** of moving from $s$ to $s'$ via action $a$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this important for KTU exams?** > Almost every 14-mark question in Module 1 ends with the directive *"Formulate the given problem as a state-space search problem."* The answer is *always* this 5-tuple. A student who writes it correctly earns 4–5 marks automatically. ## 2.2 Problem 1 — Vacuum World (Detailed PEAS & Formulation) ### PEAS for the Vacuum World | Component | Specification | |-----------|---------------| | **Performance (P)** | Award **+1** for each clean square per time step. Penalty **-1** per action. | | **Environment (E)** | Two squares A and B. Dirt distribution: $\{0, 1, 2\}$ dirt units. Agent position: $\{A, B\}$. Fully observable, deterministic, discrete, static, single-agent. | | **Actuators (A)** | $\{$ Left, Right, Suck $\}$ | | **Sensors (S)** | Position sensor + dirt sensor at current location. | ### The 5-Tuple for Vacuum World - **State space $S$:** There are **2** agent positions × **2** dirt states at A × **2** dirt states at B = **8 states**. - Notation: state = tuple $(L, D_A, D_B)$ where $L \in \{A, B\}$, $D_A, D_B \in \{\text{Clean}, \text{Dirty}\}$. - Example: $(A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Clean})$ means "agent in A, A is dirty, B is clean." - **Initial state $s_0$:** Any of the 8 states (problem is defined for arbitrary start). - **Actions $A(s)$:** Left, Right, Suck — sometimes with the restriction that Left is illegal in column A, Right illegal in column B. - **Goal test $G$:** Both squares clean, i.e., $D_A = D_B = \text{Clean}$ (the agent's position is irrelevant for the goal). - **Path cost $c$:** **1** per action (each step costs 1). ### Number of States (Generalized Vacuum World) If the world has $n$ squares, the agent has $n$ positions, and each square has 2 dirt states: $$ \mid S \mid = n \cdot 2^{n} $$ For $n = 2$, this gives $\mid S \mid = 2 \cdot 2^2 = 8$ (matches our enumeration). For $n = 3$, $\mid S \mid = 24$. ## 2.3 Problem 2 — 8-Puzzle (Detailed Formulation) ### State Space Size — A Famous Result The 8-puzzle is a $3 \times 3$ sliding puzzle with 9 positions and one blank. The total number of *reachable* states is: $$ \mid S \mid = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440 $$ The factor of 2 comes from the **parity invariant**: the blank cannot reach every permutation — exactly half of $9!$ are reachable from any given state because each move is a transposition, preserving/breaking parity in a fixed way. ### The 5-Tuple for 8-Puzzle - **State $s$:** A permutation of $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ where 0 denotes the blank. - **Initial state $s_0$:** Any reachable scrambled arrangement. - **Actions $A(s)$:** Blank can move to at most 4 neighbors. In a corner, only 2 moves; on an edge, 3 moves; in the center, 4 moves. - **Goal test $G$:** A specific ordered configuration (typically: $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$). - **Path cost $c$:** **1** per move. ### Heuristic Functions (for Module 2 continuity) KTU Module 1 introduces the heuristic concept briefly. Two famous ones: $$ h_1(n) = \text{number of misplaced tiles} $$ $$ h_2(n) = \text{Manhattan distance} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert x_i - x_i^{\text{goal}} \vert + \vert y_i - y_i^{\text{goal}} \vert $$ > [!NOTE] > We use `\vert` (LaTeX) here instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax. KTU 2024 valuation scripts use markdown parsers that can fail on raw `|` in math. ## 2.4 Problem 3 — 8-Queens (Detailed Formulation) The 8-Queens problem has **two distinct formulations**, and KTU examiners *love* to ask "compare incremental vs complete-state." Memorize the differences. ### Formulation A — Incremental - **State $s$:** Board with $0$ to $8$ queens placed, *at most one per column*. - **Initial state $s_0$:** Empty board. - **Actions $A(s)$:** Place a queen in the leftmost empty column, in any of the 8 rows (or in the *next* empty column, depending on variant). - **Goal test $G$:** All 8 queens placed, **no two attacking**. - **Path cost $c$:** Irrelevant (all solutions have the same depth = 8). ### Formulation B — Complete-State - **State $s$:** Board with **exactly 8 queens**, one per row **and** one per column (i.e., a permutation of $\{1, \dots, 8\}$). - **Initial state $s_0$:** A random permutation of rows. - **Actions $A(s)$:** Move a single queen within its column to any other row. - **Goal test $G$:** No two queens share a diagonal. - **Path cost $c$:** 1 per move. ### State-Space Comparison | Formulation | $\mid S \mid$ | Search difficulty | |------------|---------------|-------------------| | Incremental (only-column-constraint) | $8^8 = 16{,}777{,}216$ | Easiest — backtracking prunes fast | | Incremental (one-per-row & column) | $8! = 40{,}320$ | Medium | | Complete-state | $8! = 40{,}320$ | Hardest — search depth is large | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** > The **incremental formulation** is the *only* one that turns 8-Queens into a tractable search problem with depth-first search. The complete-state formulation is exponentially harder. This is a 7-mark question waiting to happen. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Unit | Typical Value | |---------------|---------|------|---------------| | $\mid S \mid$ | Cardinality of state space | states | Varies by problem | | $b$ | Branching factor | actions/state | 2–4 (8-Puzzle) | | $d$ | Depth of optimal solution | moves | $\leq 31$ (8-Puzzle) | | $m$ | Maximum depth of search tree | moves | $\infty$ in cyclic problems | | $h(n)$ | Heuristic estimate from node $n$ to goal | cost units | $\geq 0$, admissible iff $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A* evaluation function | cost units | Computed at each node | | $c(s, a, s')$ | Step cost | real number $\geq 0$ | Usually 1 | | $n \cdot 2^n$ | Vacuum-world state count | states | $n$ = # of squares | | $\frac{(N+1)!}{2}$ | N-puzzle state count | states | $9!/2 = 181{,}440$ for 8-puzzle | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why should a Kerala B.Tech student care about toy problems?** > - **Vacuum World → Robotics SLAM:** A real Roomba has the same PEAS structure, with $n$ = number of rooms mapped. > - **8-Puzzle → Warehouse Logistics:** Amazon's Kiva robots plan 8-puzzle-like paths in 2D grids to fetch shelves. > - **8-Queens → VLSI Chip Design:** Placing macro cells on a chip without overlapping routing channels is structurally identical to N-Queens. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vacuum World State Count We need to prove $\mid S \mid = n \cdot 2^n$ for an $n$-square vacuum world. **Step 1 — Count agent positions.** The agent can be in any of the $n$ squares. $$ \text{Positions} = n $$ **Step 2 — Count dirt distributions.** Each of the $n$ squares is independently Clean or Dirty. By the multiplication rule: $$ \text{Dirt configurations} = 2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2 \;(n \text{ times}) = 2^{n} $$ **Step 3 — Combine (independence).** The agent's position is independent of the dirt pattern. Total states: $$ \mid S \mid = (\text{agent positions}) \times (\text{dirt configs}) = n \cdot 2^{n} $$ **Step 4 — Specialize to $n=2$.** $$ \mid S \mid = 2 \cdot 2^{2} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ states} $$ This matches our explicit enumeration: $(A,\text{C},\text{C})$, $(A,\text{C},\text{D})$, $(A,\text{D},\text{C})$, $(A,\text{D},\text{D})$, $(B,\text{C},\text{C})$, $(B,\text{C},\text{D})$, $(B,\text{D},\text{C})$, $(B,\text{D},\text{D})$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 8-Puzzle Reachability (181,440 States) **Step 1 — Total permutations.** With 9 distinct positions, the number of arrangements is $9!$: $$ 9! = 362{,}880 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the parity argument.** Each move swaps the blank with an adjacent tile. A swap is a *transposition*, which changes the parity of the permutation. A move in a *corner* does not change parity (because the two swapped positions occupy the same *row parity* — both even or both odd row indices). A move on an *edge or center* does change parity. > [!NOTE] > This is the **standard proof** from Russell & Norvig. We restrict to "legal" 8-puzzle moves on a $3\times 3$ grid: a blank starting at an even sum of coordinates can only reach states whose blank is at an even-sum coordinate. The reachable set is exactly half of $9!$. **Step 3 — Final count.** $$ \mid S_{\text{reachable}} \mid = \frac{9!}{2} = \frac{362{,}880}{2} = 181{,}440 $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check on a smaller analogue.** The 15-puzzle (4×4) has $\frac{16!}{2} = 10{,}461{,}394{,}944{,}000$ reachable states — over 10 trillion. This explains why the 15-puzzle is the *de facto* stress-test for IDA* and pattern-database heuristics. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 8-Queens Incremental State Count **Step 1 — Place queens one column at a time.** For each of the 8 columns, we may place a queen in any of the 8 rows. We do not impose row or diagonal constraints at intermediate states — only at the goal. **Step 2 — Multiply.** $$ \mid S \mid = 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 = 8^{8} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate.** $$ 8^{8} = 16{,}777{,}216 $$ **Step 4 — Restrict to one-per-row-and-column.** If we additionally require that intermediate states have one queen per row, each column is restricted to a *unique* row. The number of arrangements becomes: $$ \mid S \mid = 8! = 40{,}320 $$ This is a $416 \times$ reduction — a critical observation for KTU's design question: *the choice of formulation changes the size of the search space by orders of magnitude.* ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Number of Distinct 8-Queens Solutions **Step 1 — Apply the column-permutation constraint.** There are $8!$ permutations of rows in 8 columns. **Step 2 — Count non-attacking solutions by brute force (a classic result).** The number of *distinct* solutions to 8-Queens is: $$ N_{\text{solutions}} = 92 $$ **Step 3 — Account for symmetry.** If we treat rotations and reflections as the same solution: $$ N_{\text{fundamental}} = 12 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This number (92) is highly testable.** KTU 2023 (model paper) asked: *"How many distinct solutions does the 8-Queens problem have? Justify with the symmetry argument."* ## 3.5 Worked Example — A Solution Path in the Vacuum World Let $s_0 = (A, \text{Dirty}, \text{Dirty})$. Compute the optimal action sequence. **Step 1 — State $s_0$:** Agent at A; both A and B dirty. Action chosen: **Suck** → cleans A. **Step 2 — State $s_1$:** Agent still at A. Now $(A, \text{Clean}, \text{Dirty})$. Action chosen: **Right** → agent moves to B. **Step 3 — State $s_2$:** Agent at B; A clean, B dirty. Action chosen: **Suck** → cleans B. **Step 4 — State $s_3$:** Agent at B; both clean. **Goal reached.** Action sequence: $[\text{Suck}, \text{Right}, \text{Suck}]$ with path cost $3$. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary | Property | Vacuum World | 8-Puzzle | 8-Queens (Incremental) | |----------|--------------|----------|------------------------| | $\mid S \mid$ | 8 | 181,440 | 16,777,216 (or 40,320 restricted) | | Branching factor $b$ | 3 | 2–4 | 8 | | Optimal depth $d$ | $\leq 3$ | $\leq 31$ | 8 | | Fully observable? | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Deterministic? | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Discrete? | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Static? | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Path cost = 1? | Yes | Yes | N/A (any solution has equal cost) | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vacuum World — State Transition Diagram The following Mermaid block renders the **complete 8-state transition graph** for the vacuum world. Each node is one of the 8 states; arrows are actions (Suck, Left, Right). Goal states are highlighted. ```mermaid graph LR node1["A_C_C"]:::goal node2["A_C_D"] node3["A_D_C"] node4["A_D_D"] node5["B_C_C"]:::goal node6["B_C_D"] node7["B_D_C"] node8["B_D_D"] node1 -- "Suck stays A_C_C" --> node1 node2 -- "Suck cleans A" --> node1 node3 -- "Suck cleans A" --> node2 node4 -- "Suck cleans A" --> node3 node5 -- "Suck stays B_C_C" --> node5 node6 -- "Suck cleans B" --> node5 node7 -- "Suck cleans B" --> node6 node8 -- "Suck cleans B" --> node7 node1 -- "Right" --> node5 node2 -- "Right" --> node6 node3 -- "Right" --> node7 node4 -- "Right" --> node8 node5 -- "Left" --> node1 node6 -- "Left" --> node2 node7 -- "Left" --> node3 node8 -- "Left" --> node4 classDef goal fill:#9be7ac,stroke:#0a6b1f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation:** The two solid-green nodes (`A_C_C` and `B_C_C`) are **goal states**. Every other state has a path of length $\leq 3$ to one of them. This is why the vacuum world is solvable by BFS in 3 steps worst case. ## 4.2 8-Puzzle — Functional Block Architecture of a Search Agent The following block diagram shows how an 8-Puzzle solver is structured into cooperating modules — the exact flow a B.Tech student should draw in their answer scripts. ```mermaid flowchart TB sensorA["Sensors: Read Board State"] stateSpace["State Space Generator
Permutations of 9 tiles"] goalCheck{"Goal Test?
Tiles 1-8 in order"} actionSel["Action Selector
Blank can slide L R U D"] transition["Transition Model
Swap blank with neighbor"] pathCost["Path Cost Accumulator
c = 1 per move"] searchAlgo["Search Algorithm
BFS, DFS, A-Star"] output["Output: Sequence of Moves"] sensorA --> stateSpace stateSpace --> goalCheck goalCheck -- "No" --> actionSel actionSel --> transition transition --> pathCost pathCost --> searchAlgo searchAlgo --> goalCheck goalCheck -- "Yes" --> output ``` **Interpretation:** The loop `goalCheck → actionSel → transition → pathCost → searchAlgo → goalCheck` is the *expand-a-node* cycle at the heart of any graph-search algorithm. ## 4.3 8-Queens — Recursive Backtracking Tree (Partial) ```mermaid graph TD q0["Column 1
8 choices"] q1["Column 2
prune if same row or diagonal"] q2["Column 3
prune same row or diagonal"] q3["Column 4
prune same row or diagonal"] goal["Goal: 8 non-attacking queens"] q0 -- "Row 1" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 2" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 3" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 4" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 5" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 6" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 7" --> q1 q0 -- "Row 8" --> q1 q1 -- "valid row" --> q2 q1 -- "ATTACK" --> dead1["X Pruned"] q2 -- "valid row" --> q3 q2 -- "ATTACK" --> dead2["X Pruned"] q3 -- "..." --> goal ``` **Interpretation:** At each column, the algorithm tries to place a queen. If the chosen row creates an attack (same row or diagonal as a previous queen), the branch is **pruned immediately** (the `X Pruned` nodes). This is the essence of *backtracking-based constraint satisfaction*, which KTU Module 1 introduces as a precursor to Module 2's search algorithms. ## 4.4 Combined PEAS Comparison Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph VW["Vacuum World"] vP["P: Clean squares"] vE["E: 2 squares"] vA["A: Move, Suck"] vS["S: Position, Dirt"] end subgraph P8["8-Puzzle"] pP["P: Solve in min moves"] pE["E: 3x3 grid"] pA["A: Slide blank"] pS["S: Read tile config"] end subgraph Q8["8-Queens"] qP["P: No attacks"] qE["E: 8x8 board"] qA["A: Place queen"] qS["S: See attacks"] end vP --- vE --- vA --- vS pP --- pE --- pA --- pS qP --- qE --- qA --- qS ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define the PEAS framework. Specify the PEAS description for the Vacuum World agent.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** PEAS stands for **Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — a four-tuple used to specify the design of a rational agent. For the Vacuum World: - **Performance (P):** Award +1 for each clean square per time step; impose a penalty of −1 for every action (energy cost). - **Environment (E):** Two discrete cells A and B; each can be Clean or Dirty. Agent position is in $\{A, B\}$. Fully observable, deterministic, static, single-agent. - **Actuators (A):** Three discrete actions: **Left, Right, Suck**. - **Sensors (S):** A location sensor (returns current square) and a local dirt sensor (returns Clean/Dirty of current square). > *Allocated marks: 1 for defining PEAS + 2 for correct Vacuum-World specification.* ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **List any three differences between the incremental and complete-state formulation of the 8-Queens problem.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **State definition:** In the incremental formulation, states have 0 to 8 queens placed one per column. In the complete-state formulation, states always have exactly 8 queens, one per row and one per column. 2. **Initial state:** Incremental starts from the empty board. Complete-state starts from a random permutation of rows in 8 columns. 3. **Search strategy:** Incremental suits *depth-first search with backtracking*. Complete-state requires a much deeper search and is exponentially harder. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Formulate the 8-Puzzle problem as a state-space search problem. Clearly specify state, initial state, actions, transition model, goal test, and path cost. Calculate the size of the state space. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of a heuristic function. Define the two most commonly used heuristics for the 8-Puzzle and state which one dominates the other. Justify your answer using the admissibility property. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer for (a):** 1. **State (2 marks):** A state is any permutation of the 9 tiles $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ where $0$ denotes the blank. The tile configuration is encoded as a tuple. 2. **Initial state (1 mark):** Any reachable scrambled arrangement specified in the problem instance. 3. **Actions (1 mark):** $\{$ Left, Right, Up, Down $\}$ — the blank slides into an adjacent cell. Illegal moves (off-grid) are discarded. 4. **Transition model (1 mark):** $\text{Result}(s, a)$ returns the new state after swapping the blank with the neighbor in direction $a$. 5. **Goal test (1 mark):** State matches the canonical goal: $(1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,0)$ row-major. 6. **Path cost (1 mark):** Each step costs **1**; total cost = number of moves. 7. **State space size (not part of the 7, but commonly asked):** $\mid S \mid = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440$ states. **Model Answer for (b):** A **heuristic** $h(n)$ is a domain-specific *estimate* of the cost of the cheapest path from node $n$ to a goal. It guides informed search algorithms (A*, Greedy Best-First). The two heuristics are: - $h_1(n)$ = number of misplaced tiles. - $h_2(n)$ = sum of Manhattan distances of every tile from its goal position. $$ h_2(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \vert x_i - x_i^{\star} \vert + \vert y_i - y_i^{\star} \vert $$ **Dominance relation (3 marks):** $h_2$ dominates $h_1$, i.e., $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$. Justification: every misplaced tile must be moved at least once, so $h_1 \leq h_2$. Both are **admissible** (never over-estimate) because moving a tile by Manhattan distance is the cheapest possible way to place it correctly. Because $h_2$ is the larger admissible heuristic, A* with $h_2$ expands fewer or equal nodes than A* with $h_1$ — this is the **dominance theorem**. ### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a neat state-transition diagram, explain the working of the Vacuum World agent. List all 8 states and 3 actions. Identify the goal states. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the 8-Queens problem: - (i) State the 5-tuple formulation in the **incremental** approach. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Prove that the number of states is $8^8$. Reduce it to $8!$ by imposing the one-per-row constraint. **(4 marks)** **Model Answer for (a):** The Vacuum World has 8 states, denoted as $(L, D_A, D_B)$ where $L$ is the agent's location and $D_A, D_B$ are dirt states of squares A and B respectively: | State | Meaning | |-------|---------| | $(A, C, C)$ | Agent at A, both clean **(goal)** | | $(A, C, D)$ | Agent at A, A clean, B dirty | | $(A, D, C)$ | Agent at A, A dirty, B clean | | $(A, D, D)$ | Agent at A, both dirty | | $(B, C, C)$ | Agent at B, both clean **(goal)** | | $(B, C, D)$ | Agent at B, A clean, B dirty | | $(B, D, C)$ | Agent at B, A dirty, B clean | | $(B, D, D)$ | Agent at B, both dirty | **Actions (1 mark):** Left, Right, Suck. *(Left illegal at A; Right illegal at B.)* **Goal states (1 mark):** $(A, C, C)$ and $(B, C, C)$ — both rooms clean. **State-transition diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid graph in SECTION 4.1. The expected answer should show all 8 nodes and 16 directed edges (Suck self-loops and cross-room moves). **Optimal solution (1 mark):** For any starting state, an optimal solution exists in $\leq 3$ steps. Example for $s_0 = (A, D, D)$: $[\text{Suck, Right, Suck}]$, cost = 3. **Model Answer for (b)(i):** 5-tuple for incremental 8-Queens: - **State $S$:** Board with $k$ queens placed ($0 \leq k \leq 8$), at most one per column. - **Initial state $s_0$:** Empty board (0 queens). - **Actions $A(s)$:** Place a queen in the leftmost empty column, in any of the 8 rows. - **Goal test $G$:** 8 queens on the board, no two attack each other. - **Path cost $c$:** Not relevant — all solutions have depth 8. **Model Answer for (b)(ii):** Without the row constraint, each of the 8 columns has 8 independent row choices: $$ \mid S \mid = 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 \times 8 = 8^{8} = 16{,}777{,}216 $$ Imposing "at most one queen per row" reduces the problem to counting permutations of 8 distinct rows in 8 distinct columns: $$ \mid S \mid = 8! = 40{,}320 $$ Reduction factor: $$ \frac{8^8}{8!} = \frac{16{,}777{,}216}{40{,}320} \approx 416 $$ So the row constraint prunes the search space by a factor of ~416 — a huge win. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Conflating state and node.** A *state* is a board configuration. A *node* is a data structure in the search tree (state + parent pointer + path cost + depth). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you write "node" when you mean "state." > 2. **Forgetting the path cost in PEAS.** Path cost is a separate component, not part of the performance measure. Many students merge them and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Writing "8-Puzzle has 9! states"** without the parity correction. The correct reachable count is $9!/2 = 181{,}440$. Examiners explicitly test parity awareness. > 4. **Confusing incremental and complete-state formulations of 8-Queens.** They are *not* the same. The action set, initial state, and search strategy all differ. > 5. **Not labeling goal states** in diagrams. KTU 2024 scheme gives explicit marks for marking goal states (e.g., double circles or shading). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - The **PEAS** framework is the universal schema for any AI problem; memorize it. - The **5-tuple** $(S, s_0, A, G, c)$ is the formal problem formulation. - **Vacuum World:** $n$ squares ⇒ $\mid S \mid = n \cdot 2^n$; for $n=2$ this gives 8 states. - **8-Puzzle:** Reachable state count is $\frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440$ due to parity. - **8-Queens (Incremental):** $8^8 = 16{,}777{,}216$ states; with row constraint it is $8! = 40{,}320$. - **8-Queens** has **92 distinct solutions** and **12 fundamental solutions** (modulo symmetry). - $h_2$ (Manhattan) **dominates** $h_1$ (misplaced tiles); both are admissible. - All three toy problems are: **fully observable, deterministic, discrete, static, known** — the easiest class of environments. - The choice of **problem formulation** can change the size of the state space by orders of magnitude — always choose the tightest formulation. - For KTU Module 1, focus on *formulation*; Module 2 builds on this with the actual search algorithms (BFS, DFS, A*, etc.). - A solution **path** has a *cost*; a solution **sequence** has a *length*; in unit-cost worlds, these are equal.

topics.title: Searching: Depth First Search, Breadth First Search, Iterative Deepening Search, Heuristic Search Strategies, Heuristic Functions, The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Searching: Depth First Search, Breadth First Search, Iterative Deepening Search, Heuristic Search Strategies, Heuristic Functions, The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Search Algorithms in AI

In Artificial Intelligence, **searching** is the foundational problem-solving technique where an agent systematically explores a state-space to find a goal state. The state-space is essentially a graph where nodes represent configurations (states) and edges represent actions (operators/transition functions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Searching refers to the process of navigating through a problem's state-space representation by applying operators to transform one state into another, with the dual objective of finding a goal state and/or an optimal path from the start state to the goal state.

---

## 1.2 Uninformed (Blind) Search Strategies

Uninformed searches have **no additional information** about states beyond what is defined in the problem formulation. The agent cannot tell whether one non-goal state is closer to the goal than another.

### A. Breadth-First Search (BFS)

**Formal Definition:** Breadth-First Search is a uniform-cost exploration strategy that expands all nodes at the present depth level before proceeding to nodes at the next depth level. It uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue** data structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Ripple in a Pond**
> Imagine dropping a stone into a still pond. The ripples expand outward in concentric circles. Each circle (depth level) is completed before the next one begins. BFS behaves exactly like this — it explores all neighbors of the start node first, then their neighbors, and so on. It's like searching for a lost key by checking every room on the first floor of a building before going upstairs.

### B. Depth-First Search (DFS)

**Formal Definition:** Depth-First Search expands the deepest node in the current frontier first. It uses a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) stack** data structure and backtracks only when a dead-end is reached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Maze Explorer**
> Picture a person lost in a maze holding only one long rope. They pick a corridor and keep walking deeper and deeper, leaving the rope behind them. When they hit a dead end, they retrace the rope (backtrack) to the last junction and try a different corridor. DFS works this way — it dives deep into one path, and only backtracks when forced to.

### C. Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)

**Formal Definition:** Iterative Deepening Search (also called Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search, IDDFS) repeatedly runs a depth-limited DFS with increasing depth limits (0, 1, 2, 3, ...) until the goal is found.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Layered Archaeology**
> An archaeologist wanting to find an ancient coin buried in a field doesn't dig straight down 100 feet on a guess. Instead, they dig 1 foot across the whole field, then 2 feet across the whole field, then 3 feet, layer by layer. This way, they find the coin at the *shallowest possible depth* first. IDS works on exactly this principle — it gives DFS the optimality of BFS while keeping memory usage minimal.

---

## 1.3 Informed (Heuristic) Search Strategies

Informed searches use **problem-specific knowledge** beyond the definition of the problem itself, typically in the form of a **heuristic function** $h(n)$, which estimates the cost from any state $n$ to the nearest goal state.

### A. Heuristic Search Strategies

**Formal Definition:** A heuristic search is any search strategy that employs a heuristic function $h(n)$ to prioritize the exploration of nodes believed to be closer to the goal, thereby pruning the search space and improving efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — GPS Navigation**
> A GPS doesn't blindly try every road (blind search). It uses a heuristic — straight-line distance to the destination — to *estimate* which direction is most promising. This is heuristic search. Just as a GPS always heads roughly "toward" the destination, a heuristic search always expands nodes that "look closer" to the goal first.

### B. Heuristic Functions

**Formal Definition:** A heuristic function $h(n)$ is a function that estimates the cost of the cheapest path from state $n$ to a goal state. The function must be **non-negative** ($h(n) \geq 0$) and for any goal node $g$, $h(g) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Heuristic Properties Required by KTU:**
> - **Admissibility:** $h(n)$ never overestimates the true cost $h^*(n)$.
> - **Consistency (Monotonicity):** $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for every node $n$ and successor $n'$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Crow vs. The Car**
> A bird looking at two cities can directly "see" the straight-line distance between them — that's a heuristic. A car driver can also estimate using a map. The straight-line distance is *admissible* (never longer than the actual road distance), making it the most commonly used heuristic in routing problems.

### C. The Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on Performance

**Formal Definition:** The accuracy (or quality) of a heuristic function is measured by its **effective branching factor $b^*$**, which represents the average number of successors expanded by a heuristic search on a uniform $b$-ary tree. A more accurate heuristic yields a smaller $b^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — A Doctor's Diagnostic Skill**
> A great doctor with excellent diagnostic intuition (an accurate heuristic) needs to run very few tests before identifying a disease. A novice doctor (poor heuristic) may run many tests, exploring numerous possibilities, taking longer and costing more. Similarly, an accurate $h(n)$ prunes the search tree aggressively, reducing both time and memory consumption.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Search Strategies — A Bird's-Eye View

| **Category** | **Strategy** | **Uses Heuristic?** | **Data Structure** | **Optimal?** |
|:-------------|:-------------|:--------------------|:--------------------|:-------------|
| Uninformed | BFS | No | FIFO Queue | Yes (unit costs) |
| Uninformed | DFS | No | LIFO Stack | No |
| Uninformed | IDS | No | Stack + Depth Bound | Yes (unit costs) |
| Informed | Greedy Best-First | Yes ($h$ only) | Priority Queue | No |
| Informed | A* Search | Yes ($f = g + h$) | Priority Queue | Yes (admissible $h$) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always explicitly mention the data structure used by each search strategy in your exam answers — this is a frequently tested 1-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS) — Operational Logic

BFS explores the search tree in **level-order traversal**. Below is the operational breakdown:

1. **Initialize:** Place the start node $S$ in a FIFO queue. Maintain a `visited` set.
2. **Loop Condition:** If the queue is empty, return **failure** (no solution).
3. **Dequeue:** Remove the leftmost node $n$ from the queue.
4. **Goal Test:** If $n$ is a goal node, return **success** and the path to $n$.
5. **Expansion:** Generate all successors of $n$. For each successor $m$ not yet visited:
   - Mark $m$ as visited.
   - Add $m$ to the right end of the queue.
6. **Repeat** from Step 2.

### Properties of BFS
- **Completeness:** Yes, if the branching factor $b$ is finite.
- **Optimality:** Yes, for uniform step costs.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ where $d$ is the depth of the shallowest goal.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ — *memory is the major bottleneck*.
- **Drawback:** Explores the entire level before finding the goal, even if the goal is on one side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** BFS's space complexity is exponential, which is why IDS is often preferred for problems with large branching factors.

---

## 2.2 Depth-First Search (DFS) — Operational Logic

DFS dives as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.

1. **Initialize:** Push the start node $S$ onto a LIFO stack.
2. **Loop Condition:** If the stack is empty, return **failure**.
3. **Pop:** Remove the top node $n$ from the stack.
4. **Goal Test:** If $n$ is a goal node, return **success**.
5. **Expansion:** Generate all successors of $n$ and push them onto the stack.
6. **Repeat** from Step 2.

### Properties of DFS
- **Completeness:** Yes, for finite trees. **No** for infinite trees or graphs with cycles (without visited check).
- **Optimality:** **No** — DFS may return a deeper solution even if a shallower one exists.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(b^m)$ where $m$ is the maximum depth.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(b \cdot m)$ — *linear* in depth! This is DFS's main advantage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse DFS's space complexity as $O(b^m)$. It is actually $O(bm)$ because only one path's worth of siblings is stored at a time.

---

## 2.3 Iterative Deepening Search (IDS) — Operational Logic

IDS combines the **optimality of BFS** with the **memory efficiency of DFS**.

1. Set depth limit $L = 0$.
2. Perform **Depth-Limited DFS (DLS)** with limit $L$.
3. If DLS finds the goal, return the path.
4. Else, increment $L$ by 1 and go to Step 2.

### Properties of IDS
- **Completeness:** Yes, for finite trees.
- **Optimality:** Yes, for uniform step costs (finds the shallowest goal first).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ — same as BFS, surprisingly.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(b \cdot d)$ — same as DFS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is IDS Time Not Worse Than BFS?** The nodes at depth $d$ are generated only once; nodes at depth $d-1$ are generated twice; nodes at depth $d-2$ are generated thrice, and so on. The total is dominated by the deepest level, making it asymptotically equivalent to BFS in time but vastly superior in memory.

---

## 2.4 Heuristic Functions — Mathematical Foundation

The quality of a heuristic is evaluated using these metrics:

### Effective Branching Factor $b^*$

If a heuristic search expands $N$ nodes to find a goal at depth $d$, then $b^*$ is the solution to:

$$N + 1 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \dots + (b^*)^d$$

This is a geometric series, and the sum is:

$$N + 1 = \frac{(b^*)^{d+1} - 1}{b^* - 1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for $b^*$:** A smaller $b^*$ means fewer redundant nodes are expanded, meaning the heuristic is more informative. An ideal heuristic has $b^* \approx 1$.

### Dominance of Heuristics

Heuristic $h_a$ **dominates** heuristic $h_b$ if for all nodes $n$:

$$h_a(n) \geq h_b(n)$$

A dominating heuristic expands fewer nodes and is computationally preferred.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| **Algorithm** | **Time Complexity** | **Space Complexity** | **Complete?** | **Optimal?** |
|:--------------|:--------------------|:---------------------|:--------------|:-------------|
| BFS | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | Yes | Yes (unit cost) |
| Uniform-Cost | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | Yes | Yes |
| DFS | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No (infinite) | No |
| Depth-Limited | $O(b^l)$ | $O(bl)$ | No (if $l < d$) | No |
| IDS | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ | Yes | Yes (unit cost) |
| Greedy Best-First | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^m)$ | No | No |
| A* | Exponential (worst case) | Keeps all nodes in memory | Yes | Yes (admissible) |

**Legend:**
- $b$ = branching factor
- $d$ = depth of shallowest goal
- $m$ = maximum depth of search tree
- $l$ = depth limit
- $C^*$ = cost of optimal solution
- $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BFS** is used in **shortest-path problems** on unweighted graphs (e.g., social network "degrees of separation", web crawlers discovering URLs level by level).
- **DFS** is used in **cycle detection in graphs**, **topological sorting**, **solving mazes/puzzles**, and **garbage collection** in programming language runtimes.
- **IDS** is the algorithm of choice in **chess engines** and **Go programs** for fixed-depth lookahead under memory constraints.
- **Heuristic Search** powers modern **GPS systems** (A* on road networks), **automated theorem provers**, and **protein-folding predictors** (AlphaFold uses heuristic-driven search).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "give a real-world application," always mention both the algorithm and *why* it's suitable for that application.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Iterative Deepening Search Time Complexity

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the time complexity of Iterative Deepening Search is $O(b^d)$.

**Setup:** Consider a uniform tree with branching factor $b$ and shallowest goal at depth $d$. IDS performs DLS at depths $0, 1, 2, \dots, d$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Count nodes generated at depth $k$ across all iterations.

Nodes at depth $k$ are generated in **all iterations with limit $\geq k$**, i.e., $(d - k + 1)$ times.

Number of nodes at depth $k$ in one iteration $= b^k$.

**Step 2:** Total nodes generated $N_{IDS}$ is:

$$N_{IDS} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k \cdot (d - k + 1)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute and split into two sums:

$$N_{IDS} = (d+1) \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k - \sum_{k=0}^{d} k \cdot b^k$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the geometric sum $\sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the standard result for $\sum_{k=0}^{d} k \cdot b^k$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{d} k \cdot b^k = \frac{b - (d+1)b^{d+1} + d \cdot b^{d+2}}{(1-b)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Combine Step 4 and Step 5 into Step 2. After algebraic simplification:

$$N_{IDS} = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1} + O(b^d) = O(b^d)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The dominant term is $b^d$** — the contribution from nodes at the goal depth. Lower levels, despite being regenerated, contribute only lower-order polynomial terms, so asymptotically IDS matches BFS in time complexity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Effective Branching Factor $b^*$

**Problem Statement:** Given a heuristic that expanded $N$ total nodes to reach a goal at depth $d$, derive the formula for $b^*$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Total nodes in a uniform $b$-ary tree of depth $d$:

$$N + 1 = 1 + b + b^2 + \dots + b^d$$

(The $+1$ accounts for the root being counted once.)

**Step 2:** Recognize this as a finite geometric series with first term $1$, ratio $b$, and $(d+1)$ terms:

$$N + 1 = \sum_{i=0}^{d} b^i = \frac{b^{d+1} - 1}{b - 1}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $b$:

$$(N + 1)(b - 1) = b^{d+1} - 1$$

$$(N + 1) \cdot b - (N + 1) = b^{d+1} - 1$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite in standard form:

$$b^{d+1} - (N + 1)b + N = 0$$

**Step 5:** This is a polynomial of degree $d+1$ in $b$. For any given $N$ and $d$, $b^*$ is the **unique real root** of this equation, obtained numerically (e.g., via Newton-Raphson method).

**Step 6:** Verification of edge cases:
- If $N = (b^{d+1} - 1)/(b-1) - 1$ (full tree), then $b^* = b$.
- If $N = d$ (perfect heuristic expanding only the path), then $b^* \approx 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the formula $N + 1 = (b^{d+1} - 1)/(b-1)$. Exam questions often phrase it as "Given $N$ nodes expanded and depth $d$, calculate $b^*$."

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison of BFS, DFS, and IDS

| **Metric** | **BFS** | **DFS** | **IDS** | **Winner** |
|:-----------|:--------|:--------|:--------|:-----------|
| Time (worst case) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ | BFS, IDS |
| Space (worst case) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(bd)$ | DFS, IDS |
| Optimal (unit cost) | Yes | No | Yes | BFS, IDS |
| Complete (finite) | Yes | No | Yes | BFS, IDS |
| Implementation ease | Easy | Easy | Moderate | BFS, DFS |
| Suitable for deep trees | Poor | Good | Best (IDS) | IDS |

**Inferences from the Matrix:**
- IDS is the **most balanced** algorithm: BFS's optimality with DFS's low memory.
- DFS should **never** be used when an optimal solution is required.
- BFS should **never** be used when $b^d$ is astronomically large (e.g., $b=10, d=15$ yields $10^{15}$ nodes).

---

## 3.4 Effect of Heuristic Accuracy on A*-like Search Performance

Consider A* search using heuristic $h(n)$, with total cost function $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

### Analytical Derivation

**Step 1:** Let $h^*(n)$ be the true cost to goal. A perfect heuristic satisfies $h(n) = h^*(n)$.

**Step 2:** The number of nodes expanded by A* depends on the **relative error** of the heuristic:

$$E = \frac{h(n) - h^*(n)}{h^*(n)}$$

**Step 3:** Empirical results (from Russell & Norvig-style analysis) show that as long as $|E|$ is bounded, the number of nodes expanded grows **polynomially** in the solution depth $d$. However, as the heuristic degrades (larger $|E|$), the exponent grows.

**Step 4:** Define the **quality penalty** $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{N_h}{N_{h^*}}$$

where $N_h$ is nodes expanded by heuristic $h$, and $N_{h^*}$ is nodes expanded by perfect heuristic.

- $Q = 1$: Perfect heuristic (no penalty).
- $Q \to \infty$: Heuristic becomes uninformative (essentially blind search).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A slightly inadmissible heuristic (overestimating by 10%) may drastically increase $N$ in deep searches. This is why domain experts spend significant effort designing tight-but-admissible heuristics, especially in real-time systems.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: IDS on a Binary Tree

**Problem:** Apply IDS to a binary tree of depth 3, with the goal at the rightmost leaf.

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Iteration 1 ($L=0$):** Visit root only. Goal not found.

**Iteration 2 ($L=1$):** Visit root, then both children. Goal not found.

**Iteration 3 ($L=2$):** Visit root, expand left child → visit its two children, expand right child → visit its two children. Goal not found.

**Iteration 4 ($L=3$):** Visit root, descend down to depth 3. The rightmost leaf is the goal. **Success!**

**Total Nodes Generated Across All Iterations:**

$$N_{IDS} = 1 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 3 + 4 \cdot 2 + 8 \cdot 1 = 4 + 6 + 8 + 8 = 26$$

**BFS Would Generate at Depth 3:** $1 + 2 + 4 + 8 = 15$ nodes. (More efficient in time.)

**Memory Used by IDS:** Only $O(bd) = O(2 \cdot 3) = 6$ nodes at any time. (Far more efficient in space.)

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** IDS trades a modest increase in time (26 vs 15 in this example) for a massive decrease in memory (6 vs 15). For large $d$, this trade-off is highly favorable.

```mermaid
    backtrack["Backtracking occurs at D and F"]:::back

```

> **Reading the Diagram:** DFS dives into the leftmost path (A → B → D) until a dead-end, backtracks, and tries the next alternative. The order of expansion here is **A, B, D, E, C, F, G, H**.

## 4.4 Heuristic Search Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    initState["Initial State n0"]:::input
    eval["Heuristic Function hn"]:::compute
    prioQueue["Priority Queue OPEN"]:::queue
    expand["Expand Best Node n"]:::process
    goalCheck{"Is n a Goal?"}:::decision
    goalOutput["GOAL FOUND Return Path"]:::output
    failure["OPEN Empty FAILURE"]:::output2
    addSucc["Generate Successors and Add to OPEN"]:::process2

    initState --> eval
    eval --> prioQueue
    prioQueue --> expand
    expand --> goalCheck
    goalCheck -->|Yes| goalOutput
    goalCheck -->|No| addSucc
    addSucc --> eval
    goalCheck -.->|OPEN empty| failure

    classDef input fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef queue fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef process2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the **canonical loop** for any heuristic (informed) search. The heuristic function $h(n)$ evaluates each candidate state, and the most promising one is expanded next. The loop terminates either on finding a goal or exhausting the search space.
