# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Breadth-First Search. Mention any two properties. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**BFS** is a uniform uninformed search strategy that expands all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next depth level. It uses a **FIFO queue** to maintain the frontier.

**Two Properties:**
1. **Complete:** BFS is complete — it will find a solution if one exists, for any finite graph.
2. **Optimal:** BFS is optimal for problems with **uniform step costs**, as it finds the shallowest goal first.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each property = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: What is a heuristic function? State the admissibility condition. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal state. It is a domain-specific estimate used by informed search strategies to guide exploration.

**Admissibility Condition:** A heuristic is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to reach the goal, i.e., for all nodes $n$:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n)$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the actual cost from $n$ to the nearest goal. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark admissibility, 1 mark stating the inequality = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions come with **internal choice**. Below, OR-Question A and OR-Question B are equivalent 14-mark options. Solve any **ONE**.

---

### **Question A: Heuristic Search and Its Performance**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the heuristic search strategies in detail with examples. How is a heuristic function evaluated? [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Motivation [1 Mark]:**
Heuristic search strategies are informed search techniques that use a problem-specific **heuristic function $h(n)$** to estimate the cost from the current node $n$ to the goal. This guides the search toward promising nodes, avoiding exhaustive exploration of the state-space.

**2. Types of Heuristic Searches [3 Marks]:**
- **Greedy Best-First Search:** Expands the node with the lowest $h(n)$ value, i.e., the node believed to be closest to the goal. It uses a priority queue sorted by $h(n)$. **Drawback:** Not optimal, can get stuck in dead-ends.
- **A* Search:** Expands the node with the lowest $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, where $g(n)$ is the cost from the start to $n$, and $h(n)$ is the estimated cost from $n$ to the goal. A* combines the benefits of UCS (completeness via $g$) and greedy (efficiency via $h$).
- **Iterative Deepening A\* (IDA\*):** Combines IDS's space efficiency with A\*'s optimality by using $f$-cost as the depth bound.

**3. Heuristic Function Evaluation [3 Marks]:**
- **Effective Branching Factor $b^*$:** Measures the average number of children expanded per node. Lower $b^*$ means a more accurate heuristic.
- **Formula:** $N + 1 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \dots + (b^*)^d$, where $N$ is total nodes expanded and $d$ is solution depth.
- **Dominance:** A heuristic $h_a$ dominates $h_b$ if $h_a(n) \geq h_b(n)$ for all $n$. The dominant heuristic expands fewer nodes.
- **Admissibility & Consistency:** Admissibility guarantees optimality; consistency guarantees optimality for graph search.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Discuss the effect of heuristic accuracy on the performance of informed search algorithms. Illustrate with an example. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Impact on Nodes Expanded [2 Marks]:**
The accuracy of a heuristic $h(n)$ directly affects the number of nodes expanded. A perfect heuristic $h(n) = h^*(n)$ expands only the nodes on the optimal path. A trivial heuristic $h(n) = 0$ for all $n$ makes the search behave like Uniform-Cost Search, expanding the entire reachable space.

**2. Role of $b^*$ [2 Marks]:**
The effective branching factor $b^*$ quantitatively measures the heuristic's impact. For a fixed $d$:
- A random heuristic gives $b^* \approx b$ (the actual branching factor).
- A well-designed admissible heuristic gives $b^* \approx 1$.

**Numerical Example [3 Marks]:**
Consider the 8-puzzle with $b \approx 3$ and $d = 22$ (the typical optimal solution length).
- Using $h_1(n) =$ number of misplaced tiles: $b^* \approx 2.79$, $N \approx 539$ nodes.
- Using $h_2(n) =$ sum of Manhattan distances: $b^* \approx 1.42$, $N \approx 113$ nodes.

**Conclusion:** $h_2$ is more accurate (dominates $h_1$), and consequently A* with $h_2$ expands fewer than half the nodes that A* with $h_1$ does — yet both are admissible and return optimal solutions.

---

### **Question B: Comparison of Uninformed Search Strategies**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compare Depth-First Search, Breadth-First Search, and Iterative Deepening Search in terms of completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

| **Criterion** | **BFS** | **DFS** | **IDS** |
|:--------------|:--------|:--------|:--------|
| **Strategy** | Level-by-level exploration | Deepest path first | DLS with increasing limits |
| **Data Structure** | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack | Stack with depth bound |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(bd)$ |
| **Complete?** | Yes | No (infinite trees) | Yes |
| **Optimal?** | Yes (unit cost) | No | Yes (unit cost) |
| **Best Use Case** | Shallow trees, shortest path | Deep trees with solutions | Deep trees, optimal + memory-constrained |

**Key Insights [3 Marks]:**
1. IDS combines the **optimality of BFS** (finds shallowest goal) with the **space efficiency of DFS** (only $O(bd)$ memory).
2. DFS's space is $O(bm)$ — linear in max depth $m$ — making it ideal for **deep trees with limited memory**.
3. BFS's space $O(b^d)$ makes it impractical for very deep problems.
4. IDS is asymptotically the same time as BFS but exponentially better in space.

**Practical Recommendation [1 Mark]:**
When the solution depth is unknown and memory is limited, **IDS is the preferred choice** — it is the default search in many chess engines.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Apply Iterative Deepening Search on the following graph and show all iterations. Find the path from node S to node G. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

```
        S
       / \
      A   B
     / \   \
    C   D   G
   / \
  E   F
```

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1: Depth Limit = 0**
- Visit **S**. Goal test fails (S is not G).
- Stop. Goal not found.

**Iteration 2: Depth Limit = 1**
- Visit **S**, expand children: A, B.
- Goal test on A: fail. Goal test on B: fail.
- Stop. Goal not found.

**Iteration 3: Depth Limit = 2**
- Visit **S** → expand A, B.
- From A, expand children: C, D. From B, expand child: G.
- **Goal test on G: SUCCESS!**
- Path returned: **S → B → G**

**Final Answer: Goal node G is found at depth 2 via path S → B → G.**

**Nodes Generated Across All Iterations:**
- Iter 1: 1 node (S).
- Iter 2: 3 nodes (S, A, B).
- Iter 3: 7 nodes (S, A, C, D, B, G).
- Total: $1 + 3 + 7 = 11$ nodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip iterations** — list every iteration up to the successful one. Skipping even one iteration costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Draw the search tree**, not just the original graph, in your solution. Many students lose 1 mark for failing to clarify which node is expanded at each step.
> 3. **Always state the goal path explicitly** (e.g., "Path: S → B → G"), not just "Goal found." Examiners look for this specific phrasing.
> 4. For heuristic questions, **always state the admissibility/consistency condition** explicitly with the inequality. Just naming the property is not enough.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Searching**

### 🔑 Uninformed Searches — Key Facts
- **BFS:** FIFO queue, complete, optimal for unit costs, time/space $O(b^d)$.
- **DFS:** LIFO stack, NOT complete on infinite trees, NOT optimal, time $O(b^m)$, space $O(bm)$.
- **Depth-Limited Search (DLS):** DFS with depth bound $l$; avoids infinite descent.
- **Iterative Deepening Search (IDS):** Repeated DLS with increasing limits; combines BFS's optimality with DFS's memory efficiency. Time $O(b^d)$, space $O(bd)$.
- **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS):** BFS variant for non-uniform step costs; expands the lowest-$\text{cost}$ node.

### 🔑 Heuristic Searches — Key Facts
- **Heuristic $h(n)$:** Estimates cost from $n$ to goal; must be $h(n) \geq 0$ and $h(\text{goal}) = 0$.
- **Admissible Heuristic:** $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates. **Consistent (Monotonic):** $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$.
- **Dominance:** $h_a$ dominates $h_b$ if $h_a(n) \geq h_b(n) \forall n$. Dominant heuristics expand fewer nodes.
- **Effective Branching Factor $b^*$:** The single-number summary of heuristic quality. Lower is better; ideal is $b^* = 1$.
- **Formula:** $N + 1 = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \dots + (b^*)^d = (b^{*d+1} - 1)/(b^* - 1)$.

### 🔑 Heuristic Accuracy → Performance
- A **perfect heuristic** ($h = h^*$) expands only the path nodes: $N = d$, $b^* \approx 1$.
- A **trivial heuristic** ($h = 0$) reduces to Uniform-Cost Search: $b^* \approx b$.
- Slight inaccuracy in $h$ (small $|E|$) keeps $N$ polynomial; gross inaccuracy can cause exponential blowup.
- **Real-world heuristics** must balance **informativeness** (low $b^*$) with **computational cost** of computing $h(n)$.

### 🔑 KTU Exam Pointers
- Always state the **data structure** used by each search.
- For complexity questions, **derive** $O(b^d)$ for IDS using the geometric series argument.
- For heuristic questions, **prove admissibility** with the inequality $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$.
- For IDS trace questions, **list every iteration** — this is the most common source of mark loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generate and Test, Greedy Best First Search, A* Algorithm, Constraint Satisfaction Problems, Adversarial Search, Games, Optimal Decision in Games, The Minimax Algorithm, Alpha-Beta Pruning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generate and Test, Greedy Best First Search, A* Algorithm, Constraint Satisfaction Problems, Adversarial Search, Games, Optimal Decision in Games, The Minimax Algorithm, Alpha-Beta Pruning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Generate and Test Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus):** *Generate and Test* is the simplest problem-solving paradigm where a generator enumerates candidate solutions, and a tester checks each candidate against the goal/constraints. The first candidate that passes all tests is returned as the solution.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you have lost your house key somewhere in the house. You walk room by room (the *generator* proposes locations: living room → kitchen → bedroom...) and at each room you visually scan (the *tester*) to check if the key is there. The first room where the test passes is your answer. You don't learn from previous failures — the generator simply moves on.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Generate-and-Test is the conceptual foundation for search algorithms. Pure generate-and-test is *blind* (no heuristic feedback), making it inefficient. Heuristic improvements lead to Hill Climbing and Best-First Search families.

**Algorithm Outline (Pseudocode):**

```
function GenerateAndTest():
    while True:
        candidate ← GENERATE()
        if TEST(candidate):
            return candidate
```

---

## 1.2 Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Greedy Best-First Search* expands the node that appears closest to the goal according to a heuristic function $h(n)$ alone, ignoring the cost already incurred. It is "greedy" because it commits to whatever looks best *right now*.

**Conceptual Analogy:** You are driving from KTU campus to a specific shop. Instead of checking the full route cost, you simply always turn toward the direction where the shop is visible/nearest. You may get stuck in a dead-end or take a longer path because you never considered how far you've already driven.

$$f(n) = h(n)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** GBFS is **not optimal** and **not complete** in general. It can get stuck in loops or dead-ends. Use only when a quick, "good enough" answer is acceptable.

---

## 1.3 A* Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Board-Examiner Favourite):** A* is a *best-first*, *informed* search that combines the actual cost from start $g(n)$ with the heuristic estimate to goal $h(n)$:
>
> $$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$
>
> It expands the node with the lowest $f(n)$ first, and is **optimal** if the heuristic is *admissible* (and *consistent* for graph search).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Reusing the driving analogy — A* is like Google Maps: it considers **how far you have already driven** ($g$) **plus** a *smart estimate* of the remaining distance ($h$). This balance prevents both short-sighted greedy mistakes and brute-force Dijkstra slowness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Critical Properties for KTU 2024:**
> 1. **Admissible heuristic:** $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ where $h^*$ is the true cost to goal — *never overestimates*.
> 2. **Consistent (monotonic) heuristic:** $h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$ for every successor $n'$ — guarantees $f$ is non-decreasing along a path.

---

## 1.4 Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Constraint Satisfaction Problem* is defined by three components — a set of **variables** $X = \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$, a set of **domains** $D = \{D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n\}$ (each $D_i$ is the set of allowed values for $X_i$), and a set of **constraints** $C$ specifying allowable combinations of values for subsets of variables.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A Sudoku puzzle. Cells are variables, the digits 1–9 are the domain, and the rules ("each row must contain 1–9 exactly once") are constraints. You assign digits step by step, *propagating* the impact of each choice (if I put 5 here, no other cell in this row can be 5).

**Standard CSP Solution Techniques (KTU Module 2):**
- Backtracking search
- Constraint propagation (Arc Consistency / AC-3)
- Forward Checking
- Variable / Value ordering heuristics (MRV, LCV)
- Local search for CSPs (min-conflicts)

---

## 1.5 Adversarial Search, Games & Optimal Decisions

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Adversarial Search* is search in environments where another agent (the *opponent*) is simultaneously planning against you. *Games* in AI are formal, deterministic, perfect-information, two-player zero-sum encounters. The *optimal decision* in such games is one that maximises the player's payoff *assuming the opponent also plays optimally*.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A chess match. You cannot simply "search to a winning state" because your opponent will actively try to stop you. Every move must be evaluated under the assumption that your opponent will pick the move **worst for you** and **best for themselves**.

**Key Game Formalism:**
- **Initial state**
- **Player(s)** function: whose turn is it?
- **Actions / Moves** function
- **Terminal test:** is the game over?
- **Utility / Payoff function:** $u(s, p)$ — value to player $p$ in terminal state $s$
- Zero-sum ⇒ $u(s, \text{MAX}) = -u(s, \text{MIN})$

---

## 1.6 Minimax Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Minimax* is a depth-first, recursive, backtracking algorithm that computes the optimal move for MAX assuming MIN plays perfectly. At MAX nodes, the value is the **maximum** of successors; at MIN nodes, the value is the **minimum** of successors. The chosen move is the one leading to the backed-up value.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A chess player mentally asking: *"If I move here, my opponent will respond with their best reply, then I will respond with my best, and so on…"* — Minimax formalises exactly this "look-ahead under opposition."

---

## 1.7 Alpha-Beta Pruning

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Alpha-Beta Pruning* is an optimisation of Minimax that eliminates branches that **cannot possibly influence the final decision**. It tracks two parameters: $\alpha$ = best (highest) value MAX can guarantee so far along the path; $\beta$ = best (lowest) value MIN can guarantee so far. When $\alpha \ge \beta$, the remaining siblings are pruned.

**Conceptual Analogy:** While pricing a car, the salesperson hears you say your *maximum* budget is ₹8 lakh. The moment a configuration exceeds that, the dealer stops listing options for that car. You (MAX) won't accept more than 8; the dealer (MIN) won't accept less than a hidden minimum. Once the dealer realises the gap can't be bridged, the negotiation for that branch collapses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generate and Test — Operational Decomposition

- **Generator:** Enumerates the *solution space* $\mathcal{S}$ — either completely (exhaustive), randomly, or by structured traversal.
- **Tester:** Applies the *goal predicate* $G(s)$ to each candidate $s \in \mathcal{S}$.
- **Termination:** Returns the first $s$ such that $G(s) = \text{True}$, or reports failure.

**Strengths:** Conceptual simplicity, applicable to any problem with a finite (or parameterised) search space.
**Weaknesses:** Worst-case **exponential** time; no learning from failure; inefficient for large spaces.

| Aspect | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Strategy class | Blind / Uninformed |
| Time complexity | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ in the worst case |
| Space complexity | $O(1)$ — only stores the current candidate |
| Optimality | Not guaranteed (returns *a* solution, not *the best*) |
| Completeness | Complete if $\mathcal{S}$ is finite and exhaustively generated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Forms the conceptual scaffold for *hill climbing*, *simulated annealing*, and *genetic algorithms* — all of which generate candidate solutions and test them, often with a feedback loop.

---

## 2.2 Greedy Best-First Search — Operational Logic

The priority queue orders nodes by $f(n) = h(n)$ only. At each step, the node with the **smallest $h$** (closest to goal by heuristic) is expanded.

**Bulleted logic chain:**
- Step 1: Insert start node $S$ with $f(S) = h(S)$ into OPEN list.
- Step 2: Pop the node $n$ with minimum $f(n)$.
- Step 3: **Goal test.** If $n$ is the goal, return it.
- Step 4: Generate all successors of $n$ and compute $h$ for each.
- Step 5: Insert successors into OPEN (deduplicating as needed).
- Step 6: Repeat until goal found or OPEN is empty.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** GBFS is **not optimal** because it ignores path cost $g$. A heuristic may favour a node *near the goal* in straight-line distance but requiring a long detour.

---

## 2.3 A* Algorithm — Operational Logic

**Bulleted "Why" and "How":**

- **Why include $g(n)$?** Because $g$ tracks *commitment* — it prevents the algorithm from re-considering shallow, near-goal states reached via a costly path.
- **Why include $h(n)$?** Because $h$ is *lookahead* — it steers the search toward the goal efficiently.
- **How does it work?** Maintains a priority queue (OPEN) keyed by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. At each step, expands the lowest-$f$ node.

**Termination:** When the goal node $G$ is popped from OPEN, the path traced back via parent pointers is the optimal solution (under admissibility).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Informed Search

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Evaluation function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A* |
| Greedy evaluation | $f(n) = h(n)$ | GBFS |
| Uniform-cost | $f(n) = g(n)$ | Dijkstra |
| Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ | Never overestimates true cost |
| Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n, n', a) + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality on $h$ |
| Optimality of A* | Guaranteed if $h$ admissible (tree) / consistent (graph) | Board favourite |
| Effective branching | $b^*$ with perfect $h$ | Measures heuristic quality |
| Time complexity | $O(b^d)$ worst case | $b$ = branching, $d$ = solution depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** A* is the workhorse of **route planning** (Google Maps, GPS), **robot motion planning** (ROS nav stack), **video-game pathfinding** (NPC behaviour), and **VLSI design rule checking**.

---

## 2.4 Constraint Satisfaction Problems — Deep Dive

**State:** An *assignment* of values to some (or all) variables.
**Goal test:** All variables assigned AND all constraints satisfied.
**Successor function:** Assign a value to one currently-unassigned variable.

**Refinements (KTU Module 2 syllabus):**

1. **Backtracking Search** — DFS with one variable assigned per step; backtracks on violation.
2. **Arc Consistency (AC-3)** — A variable $X_i$ is arc-consistent with $X_j$ if for every value $v_i \in D_i$ there exists a value $v_j \in D_j$ satisfying the binary constraint.
3. **Forward Checking** — When a variable $X_i$ is assigned, delete values from neighbours' domains that are inconsistent with the chosen $v_i$.
4. **MRV (Minimum Remaining Values) heuristic** — Pick the variable with the fewest legal values left — *fail-fast* principle.
5. **LCV (Least Constraining Value)** — Pick the value that rules out the fewest choices for neighbours.
6. **Min-Conflicts (Local Search for CSPs)** — Start with a complete (but possibly invalid) assignment; repeatedly move to the neighbour state with the fewest conflicts.

| Concept | Formula / Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| Variables | $X = \{X_1, \dots, X_n\}$ |
| Domains | $D_i$ = finite set of values for $X_i$ |
| Binary constraint | Relation $R_{ij} \subseteq D_i \times D_j$ |
| Arc Consistency | $\forall v_i \in D_i \, \exists v_j \in D_j : (v_i, v_j) \in R_{ij}$ |
| $k$-consistency | Any consistent assignment to $k-1$ variables can be extended to $k$ |
| Strong $k$-consistency | Strong $(k-1)$, $(k-2)$, \dots, $1$-consistency all hold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** CSPs model **scheduling** (timetable, airline crew rostering), **configuration** (car assembly, software product lines), **VLSI channel routing**, **cryptarithmetic puzzles**, and **resource allocation** in cloud systems.

---

## 2.5 Games & Adversarial Search — Deep Dive

A game is formalised as the **6-tuple**:

$$
\text{GAME} = \langle S, S_0, \text{Players}(s), \text{Actions}(s), \text{Result}(s, a), \text{IsTerminal}(s), \text{Utility}(s, p) \rangle
$$

**Standard assumptions for KTU 2024:**

- Two players: **MAX** and **MIN**.
- **Deterministic** — no chance nodes.
- **Perfect information** — both see the full state.
- **Zero-sum** — $u(s, \text{MAX}) = -u(s, \text{MIN})$.
- **Discrete**, finite, alternating turns.

**Game tree basics:**
- **Branching factor $b$** — number of legal moves at a typical state.
- **Depth $d$** — average number of moves to a terminal state.
- **Effective depth $m$** — number of plies the program can search in time.
- **Total leaves** of a fully expanded game tree $\approx b^m$ — so full Minimax is infeasible for chess ($b \approx 35$, $m \approx 100$).

| Concept | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| Zero-sum | MAX gain = MIN loss |
| Perfect information | Both see full state |
| Deterministic | No randomness |
| Terminal utility | $u(s) \in \mathbb{R}$ at leaves |
| Backed-up value | $\text{MINIMAX}(s)$ propagated up the tree |

---

## 2.6 Minimax Algorithm — Operational Logic

```
function MINIMAX(node, depth, maximisingPlayer):
    if depth == 0 or IsTerminal(node):
        return Utility(node)
    if maximisingPlayer:
        value ← -∞
        for each child in Successors(node):
            value ← max(value, MINIMAX(child, depth-1, False))
        return value
    else:
        value ← +∞
        for each child in Successors(node):
            value ← min(value, MINIMAX(child, depth-1, True))
        return value
```

**Bulleted logic chain:**
- Step 1: Recurse depth-first to terminal or depth-limit.
- Step 2: At **MAX node**, back up the **maximum** of child values.
- Step 3: At **MIN node**, back up the **minimum** of child values.
- Step 4: At the root, MAX picks the action leading to the max backed-up value.

**Properties (KTU High-Yield):**

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Complete | Yes, if tree is finite |
| Optimal | Yes, against optimal opponent |
| Time | $O(b^m)$ |
| Space | $O(bm)$ — depth-first |

---

## 2.7 Alpha-Beta Pruning — Operational Logic

**Key parameters:**
- $\alpha$ = value of the *best (highest-value)* choice found so far for MAX along the path to the root.
- $\beta$ = value of the *best (lowest-value)* choice found so far for MIN along the path to the root.

**Pruning rules (KTU Board Examiner Favourite):**

$$
\text{PRUNE} \iff \alpha \ge \beta
$$

- At a MAX node: $\alpha$ updates as $\alpha \leftarrow \max(\alpha, v)$. **Prune** if $v \ge \beta$.
- At a MIN node: $\beta$ updates as $\beta \leftarrow \min(\beta, v)$. **Prune** if $v \le \alpha$.

**Initial values:** $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

### Alpha-Beta Performance Cheat Sheet

| Move ordering | Effective branching $b^*$ | Leaves explored |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Perfect ordering | $\approx \sqrt{b}$ | $O(b^{m/2})$ |
| Random ordering | $\approx b^{3/4}$ | $O(b^{3m/4})$ |
| Worst case (adversarial) | $b$ | $O(b^m)$ (no improvement) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** Alpha-Beta powers the **Deep Blue** (1997) chess defeat of Kasparov, modern Stockfish, Leela Chess Zero, and most competitive game engines in adversarial domains (chess, checkers, Connect-N, Othello).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The worked examples below are the **highest-yield numerical/derivational content** for KTU 2024 Module 2 questions. Trace through them with pen and paper.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Greedy Best-First vs A* on the Same Graph

**Graph (straight-line distance heuristic to goal $G$ given in brackets):**

```
S --- 1 --- A --- 1 --- B --- 1 --- G
|                                   ^
+----- 3 ----- C ------ 2 ----------+
```

Heuristic $h$ values to $G$: $h(S) = 4$, $h(A) = 3$, $h(B) = 1$, $h(C) = 2$, $h(G) = 0$.
Edge costs are as labelled (1, 1, 1, 3, 2).

### Step 1 — Greedy Best-First ($f = h$ only)

- OPEN: $\{S\}$, $h(S) = 4$. Pop $S$.
- Expand $S$: $A$ with $h=3$, $C$ with $h=2$. OPEN = $\{C, A\}$ (lowest $h$ first).
- Pop $C$ ($h=2$). Goal test: not $G$. Expand $C$ → $G$ (cost 2, $h=0$).
- Pop $G$ from OPEN (it was added) → **GOAL REACHED**.

**Path Greedy found:** $S \to C \to G$, total cost = $3 + 2 = 5$.

### Step 2 — A* ($f = g + h$)

- $g(S) = 0$, $f(S) = 0 + 4 = 4$. OPEN = $\{S\}$.
- Pop $S$. Expand: $A$ ($g=1, h=3, f=4$), $C$ ($g=3, h=2, f=5$).
- OPEN = $\{A(f=4), C(f=5)\}$. Pop $A$.
- Expand $A$: $B$ ($g=2, h=1, f=3$).
- OPEN = $\{B(f=3), C(f=5)\}$. Pop $B$.
- Expand $B$: $G$ ($g=3, h=0, f=3$).
- OPEN = $\{G(f=3), C(f=5)\}$. Pop $G$ → **GOAL REACHED**.

**Path A* found:** $S \to A \to B \to G$, total cost = $1 + 1 + 1 = 3$.

### Result Comparison (Analytical Matrix)

| Algorithm | $f(n)$ | Path found | Total cost | Optimal? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Greedy Best-First | $h(n)$ | $S \to C \to G$ | $5$ | **No** |
| A* | $g(n) + h(n)$ | $S \to A \to B \to G$ | $3$ | **Yes** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** GBFS took the short-cut through $C$ because $C$ was "closer" in straight-line terms, but that path was *actually* more expensive. A* balanced already-paid cost with the remaining estimate — yielding the optimal route.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Admissibility and Consistency Proof Sketch

**Claim:** If $h$ is **consistent**, then $f$ is non-decreasing along any path.

**Proof by induction along a successor $n'$ of $n$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(n') &= g(n') + h(n') \\
      &= g(n) + c(n, a, n') + h(n') \\
      &\ge g(n) + c(n, a, n') + h(n) - c(n, a, n') \quad \text{(by consistency)} \\
      &= g(n) + h(n) \\
      &= f(n)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore $f(n') \ge f(n)$. **QED.** $\square$

**Corollary:** The first goal node popped from OPEN is optimal (no later goal can have a smaller $f$ since $f$ is non-decreasing along paths and $h(G)=0$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — CSP: Map Colouring Australia

**Problem:** Colour the regions of Australia (WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T) with 3 colours {Red, Green, Blue} such that no two adjacent regions share a colour.

**Variables:** $\{WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T\}$.
**Domains:** $D_i = \{R, G, B\}$ for all.
**Constraints (binary, "not equal"):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& WA \neq NT,\ WA \neq SA \\
& NT \neq SA,\ NT \neq Q \\
& SA \neq Q,\ SA \neq NSW,\ SA \neq V \\
& Q \neq NSW \\
& NSW \neq V
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — MRV Variable Selection
Initially all variables have 3 values. After constraint propagation, **SA** is the most constrained (adjacent to WA, NT, Q, NSW, V) — chosen first.

### Step 2 — Assign $SA = R$
Forward Checking removes $R$ from $D_{WA}, D_{NT}, D_{Q}, D_{NSW}, D_{V}$.
Remaining domains: all = {G, B}.

### Step 3 — Recursive backtrack
A valid complete assignment is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& SA = R, \quad WA = G, \quad NT = B, \quad Q = G, \quad NSW = B, \quad V = G, \quad T = R
\end{aligned}
$$

All pairwise inequality constraints satisfied ⇒ **Solution found.**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Minimax on a 3-Ply Game Tree

**Leaf values from left to right (MAX's perspective, depth 3):**

```
                  MAX
                 /   \
               MIN    MIN
              / \    / \
            MAX MAX MAX MAX
            /\  /\  /\  /\
           3  12 2  ?  ? 5 2 14
```

Leaf values: [3, 12, 2, 15, 1, 5, 2, 14] (in level order).

### Backed-up values — explicit step-by-step

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(\text{MAX}_{\text{left}})   &= \max(3, 12) = 12 \\
v(\text{MAX}_{\text{right-L}}) &= \max(2, 15) = 15 \\
v(\text{MAX}_{\text{mid}})    &= \max(1, 5)  = 5 \\
v(\text{MAX}_{\text{right-R}}) &= \max(2, 14) = 14 \\
v(\text{MIN}_{\text{left}})  &= \min(12, 15) = 12 \\
v(\text{MIN}_{\text{right}}) &= \min(5, 14)  = 5 \\
v(\text{root MAX}) &= \max(12, 5) = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision at root:** MAX picks the left child, leading to backed-up value **12**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Alpha-Beta Pruning Step-by-Step

Same tree as §3.4, traversed **left-to-right**.

### Initial Call: $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$ at root (MAX)

**Step 1:** Descend to left MIN with $\alpha=-\infty, \beta=+\infty$.

**Step 2:** Left MAX: evaluate child 3.
- $v=3$, update $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$. Test: $\alpha \ge \beta$? $3 \ge +\infty$? **No**, continue.

**Step 3:** Left MAX: evaluate child 12.
- $v=12$, $\alpha = \max(3, 12) = 12$. Test $12 \ge +\infty$? No.
- Return **12** to parent MIN.

**Step 4:** Left MIN updates $\beta = \min(+\infty, 12) = 12$. Test $\alpha \ge \beta$? $-\infty \ge 12$? No, continue.

**Step 5:** Right MAX of left MIN: evaluate child 2.
- $v=2$, $\alpha=-\infty$ → $\alpha = 2$. Test $2 \ge 12$? No.
- Evaluate child 15.
- $v=15$, $\alpha = \max(2, 15) = 15$. Test $15 \ge 12$? **YES — PRUNE remaining children.**
- Return 15 to MIN.

**Step 6:** Left MIN: $v = \min(12, 15) = 12$. Update root $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 12) = 12$.

**Step 7:** Descend to right MIN with $\alpha = 12, \beta = +\infty$.

**Step 8:** Left MAX of right MIN: child 1.
- $v=1$, $\alpha = -\infty \to 1$. Test $1 \ge +\infty$? No.
- Child 5.
- $v=5$, $\alpha = \max(1, 5) = 5$. Test $5 \ge +\infty$? No.
- Return 5 to MIN.

**Step 9:** Right MIN: $v = \min(+\infty, 5) = 5$. Test $\alpha \ge \beta$? $12 \ge 5$? **YES — PRUNE entire right subtree.**

### Alpha-Beta Analytical Matrix

| Node | Type | $\alpha$ in | $\beta$ in | Pruned? | Value returned |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Root | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | — | $12$ |
| Left MIN | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | — | $12$ |
| Left-Left MAX | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | No | $12$ |
| Left-Right MAX | MAX | $-\infty$ | $12$ | **Yes (after 15)** | $15$ |
| Right MIN | MIN | $12$ | $+\infty$ | **Yes (entire subtree)** | $5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Out of 8 leaves, only **5** were evaluated (3 and 12, 2 and 15, 1 and 5). The subtree of the right side was entirely pruned — a massive saving for deeper trees.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of All Module 2 Search Algorithms

| Algorithm | $f(n)$ | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space | Uses $g$? | Uses $h$? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Generate & Test | — | If finite $\mathcal{S}$ | No | $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ | $O(1)$ | No | No |
| Greedy BFS | $h(n)$ | No (loops) | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^m)$ | No | Yes |
| A* (tree) | $g+h$ | Yes (admissible) | Yes | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | Yes | Yes |
| A* (graph) | $g+h$ | Yes (consistent) | Yes | exponential worst | exponential | Yes | Yes |
| Minimax | — | Yes (finite) | Yes (zero-sum) | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | — | — |
| Alpha-Beta | — | Yes (finite) | Yes | $O(b^{m/2})$ best | $O(bm)$ | — | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

graph TD

## 4.4 A* Heuristic Design Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Problem Domain] --> H1[Possible Heuristics]
    H1 --> A[Admissible?]
    A -- Yes --> C[Consistent?]
    A -- No --> Reject[Discard Heuristic]
    C -- Yes --> Use[Use in A*]
    C -- No --> W[Weaker Guarantee<br/>Use with caution]
    W --> Use
    Reject --> H2[Try New Heuristic]

    style Use fill:#b6fcb6,stroke:#006400

```

## 4.5 Information-Flow Architecture for Informed Search

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["Start Node S<br/>Goal G<br/>Heuristic h(n)"] --> Init["Initialise OPEN = {S}<br/>CLOSED = {}"]
    Init --> Loop{"OPEN empty?"}
    Loop -- No --> Pop["Pop node n with<br/>lowest f(n)=g+h"]
    Pop --> Test{"n is goal?"}
    Test -- Yes --> Output["Return path<br/>via parent pointers"]
    Test -- No --> Gen["Generate successors<br/>of n"]
    Gen --> Visited{"n' in CLOSED?"}
    Visited -- No --> Add["Add n' to OPEN<br/>with parent=n"]
    Visited -- Yes --> Better{"g(n') < previous?"}
    Better -- Yes --> Update["Update g, parent"]
    Better -- No --> Skip["Skip"]
    Add --> Loop
    Update --> Loop
    Skip --> Loop
    Loop -- Yes --> Fail["Failure:<br/>No path exists"]

    style Output fill:#b6fcb6,stroke:#006400
    style Fail fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Greedy Best-First Search and A* Search. State one limitation of Greedy Best-First Search.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Greedy BFS | A* |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Evaluation | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| Uses path cost? | No | Yes |
| Optimal? | Not guaranteed | Yes (with admissible $h$) |
| Complete? | Not in general (loops) | Yes (tree search with admissible $h$) |

**Limitation:** Greedy BFS can get trapped in dead-ends or cycles and does not guarantee the optimal path because it ignores the actual cost already paid ($g(n)$). **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)? List its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A CSP is a problem defined by:
1. **Variables** $X = \{X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n\}$ — the unknowns to be assigned. **(1 mark)**
2. **Domains** $D_i$ for each variable — the set of allowed values. **(1 mark)**
3. **Constraints** $C$ — relations that specify allowable tuples of values for subsets of variables. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the A* algorithm with the formula $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. State and prove the condition under which A* is **optimal**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A* is a best-first search algorithm that uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from start to $n$.
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate from $n$ to goal.
- $f(n)$ = estimated total cost of a path through $n$.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
- Insert $S$ into OPEN with $f(S) = h(S)$.
- Repeat: pop node $n$ with lowest $f$. If goal, return path; else expand successors, compute $f$, insert into OPEN.
- Tie-break in favour of smaller $h$.

**Optimality Condition (1 mark):** A* is optimal in tree search if $h$ is **admissible**: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$ (where $h^*$ is the true minimum cost to goal).

**Proof Sketch (2 marks):** Suppose an optimal goal $G_2$ is in OPEN with $f(G_2) = g(G_2) > C^*$ (the true optimal cost), while a suboptimal goal $G_1$ is about to be popped with $f(G_1) = g(G_1) = C^{sub}$. Admissibility means $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ along any path; therefore, no node on the optimal path to $G_2$ can have $f > C^*$. So $G_2$ will be popped before $G_1$ — contradiction. Hence A* returns the optimal goal first.

---

**(b)** Given the graph below, with $h$ values in brackets (heuristic to goal $G$), find the path returned by A* and the total cost. **(7 marks)**

```
S --1-- A --4-- B --3-- G
|             ^
+--2-- C --1-+
|             |
+-----3------ D --2-- G
```

$h(S) = 7,\ h(A) = 6,\ h(B) = 3,\ h(C) = 5,\ h(D) = 2,\ h(G) = 0$.

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Pop | OPEN (with $f$) | $g$-values of generated |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S$ ($f=7$) | $A(f=7), C(f=7), D(f=10)$ | $g(A)=1, g(C)=2, g(D)=3$ |
| 2 | $A$ ($f=7$) | $C(f=7), D(f=10), B(f=8)$ | $g(B)=5$ |
| 3 | $C$ ($f=7$) | $B(f=8), D(f=10)$ | $g(D)=\min(3,2+1)=3$ |
| 4 | $B$ ($f=8$) | $D(f=10), G(f=8)$ | $g(G)=8$ |
| 5 | $G$ ($f=8$) | — | **GOAL** |

**Path returned:** $S \to A \to B \to G$, total cost = $1 + 4 + 3 = \mathbf{8}$.

**Verifying $h$ admissible:**
- $h(S) = 7 \le 8 = h^*(S)$ ✓
- $h(A) = 6 \le 7 = h^*(A)$ ✓
- $h(B) = 3 \le 3 = h^*(B)$ ✓
- $h(C) = 5 \le 5 = h^*(C)$ ✓
- $h(D) = 2 \le 2 = h^*(D)$ ✓

All admissible — so A* is guaranteed optimal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Minimax algorithm** for game playing. Apply it to the following game tree where it is MAX's turn at the root, and the leaves have values as shown. What is the best move for MAX? **(7 marks)**

```
                         MAX (root)
                        /         \
                     MIN           MIN
                    /    \        /    \
                 MAX    MAX    MAX    MAX
                 / \    / \    / \    / \
                3  12  2  15  1   5  2  14
```

**Model Solution:**

**Minimax Explanation (3 marks):** Minimax is a depth-first recursive algorithm for two-player zero-sum games. At MAX levels, it returns the maximum child value; at MIN levels, it returns the minimum child value; at leaves or depth limit, it returns the static evaluation. The move leading to the maximum backed-up value is chosen.

**Application (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(\text{MAX}_L) &= \max(3, 12) = 12 \\
v(\text{MAX}_R) &= \max(2, 15) = 15 \\
v(\text{MAX}_M) &= \max(1, 5) = 5 \\
v(\text{MAX}_R2) &= \max(2, 14) = 14 \\
v(\text{MIN}_L) &= \min(12, 15) = 12 \\
v(\text{MIN}_R) &= \min(5, 14) = 5 \\
v(\text{root}) &= \max(12, 5) = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best move for MAX:** Take the **left** branch, achieving backed-up value **12**.

---

**(b)** Apply **Alpha-Beta Pruning** to the same tree. List the order in which leaves are evaluated and the number of leaves pruned. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (with $\alpha, \beta$ tracking):**

Traversing **left-to-right**:

| Order | Node | Type | $\alpha$ in | $\beta$ in | Leaf evaluated | Pruning triggered? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Root | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | — | — |
| 2 | Left-MIN | MIN | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | — | — |
| 3 | Left-MAX | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | **3** | No |
| 4 | Left-MAX | MAX | $-\infty$ | $+\infty$ | **12** | No |
| 5 | Right-MAX-of-Left-MIN | MAX | $-\infty$ | $12$ | **2** | No |
| 6 | Right-MAX-of-Left-MIN | MAX | $-\infty$ | $12$ | **15** | **Yes — $15 \ge 12$ prunes remaining** |
| 7 | Right-MIN | MIN | $12$ | $+\infty$ | — | — |
| 8 | Left-MAX-of-Right-MIN | MAX | $12$ | $+\infty$ | **1** | No |
| 9 | Left-MAX-of-Right-MIN | MAX | $12$ | $+\infty$ | **5** | **Yes — $12 \ge 5$ prunes entire right subtree of right-MIN** |

**Leaves evaluated: 5 out of 8** (3, 12, 2, 15, 1, 5). The remaining **2 leaves (2, 14) are pruned** along with the second MAX of the right MIN.

**Total pruning: 3 leaves** saved (3, 12, 2, 15, 1, 5 evaluated = 6 leaves actually; 2, 14 pruned = 2 leaves saved — counted by the 1 + 1 cuts made at internal nodes).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students confuse $\alpha$ and $\beta$ — remember: $\alpha$ is the *floor* MAX is guaranteed (max of MAX-path), $\beta$ is the *ceiling* MIN is guaranteed (min of MIN-path).
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to **update** $\alpha$ or $\beta$ *before* checking the pruning condition at each level.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Reporting pruned leaves as "evaluated" — show clearly with `✂` or `(pruned)` in the solution.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Reporting the *root value* incorrectly when the pruned sub-tree would have changed the *displayed* value but not the *decision* (root value remains 12 in this example).
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Forgetting the "$\le$" or "$\ge$" in the pruning condition — write $\alpha \ge \beta$ explicitly.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Generate & Test**
> - Generator + Tester. Blind. $O(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ worst case. Foundation for heuristic search.

> **Greedy Best-First Search**
> - $f(n) = h(n)$ only. **Not optimal**, **not complete** in general. Fast but myopic.

> **A\* Algorithm**
> - $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. **Optimal** with admissible heuristic (tree search); **optimal + complete** with consistent heuristic (graph search).
> - Admissibility: $h(n) \le h^*(n)$. Consistency: $h(n) \le c(n, n', a) + h(n')$.
> - $f$ is non-decreasing along any path if $h$ is consistent.

> **Constraint Satisfaction Problems**
> - Components: Variables + Domains + Constraints.
> - Techniques: Backtracking, Arc Consistency (AC-3), Forward Checking, MRV, LCV, Min-Conflicts.

> **Adversarial Search / Games**
> - 6-tuple: $\langle S, S_0, \text{Players}, \text{Actions}, \text{Result}, \text{IsTerminal}, \text{Utility} \rangle$.
> - Two-player, deterministic, perfect-information, zero-sum, alternating turns.

> **Minimax**
> - MAX = max of children, MIN = min of children. Time $O(b^m)$, space $O(bm)$. Complete & optimal.

> **Alpha-Beta Pruning**
> - Track $\alpha$ (best for MAX) and $\beta$ (best for MIN). **Prune when $\alpha \ge \beta$.**
> - Best case: $O(b^{m/2})$ with perfect move ordering.
> - Initial: $\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$. Update at each node *before* testing.
> - Effective branching $b^* \approx \sqrt{b}$ with perfect ordering.

> **Key Equivalence to Memorise**
> - GBFS = A\* with $g(n) \equiv 0$ (no path-cost memory).
> - Uniform-cost = A\* with $h(n) \equiv 0$ (no lookahead).
> - BFS = A\* with $g = $ depth and $h = 0$ on uniform-cost graphs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge-Based Agents: The Wumpus World
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Knowledge-Based Agents: The Wumpus World ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Knowledge-Based Agent A **Knowledge-Based Agent (KBA)** is a rational, goal-directed agent that operates by maintaining an explicit, symbolic representation of a world model inside an internal data structure called the **Knowledge Base (KB)**. The agent's cycle consists of three primary operations: 1. **TELL** – Assert new sentences (percepts, derivations) into the KB. 2. **INFER (ASK)** – Query the KB using a reasoning engine to decide what to do. 3. **PERFORM** – Execute the chosen action on the environment. Mathematically, the agent implements the decision rule: $$Action_t = \text{ASK}\bigl(KB_t, \text{MAKE-ACTION-QUERY}\bigr)$$ $$KB_{t+1} = \text{TELL}\bigl(KB_t, \text{PERCEPT}_t \cup \text{Action}_t\bigr)$$ ### 1.2 What is the Wumpus World? > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Wumpus World** is the canonical toy environment introduced by *Michael Genesereth* and later popularized in the Russell & Norvig AI textbook, used to benchmark the behaviour of a logic-based knowledge-based agent. It is a **4 × 4 grid cave** containing hazards, treasure, and a single hostile creature, where the agent must navigate and reason deductively to survive. **Standard Metrics & Constants of the Environment:** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Grid Size | **4 × 4** cells | | Pits | Typically **1 to 3** randomly distributed | | Wumpus | Exactly **1** stationary creature | | Gold | Exactly **1** piece of treasure | | Arrows available | **1** | | Agent start | Cell **(1,1)** facing **East** | | Gold reward | **+1000** | | Death (Wumpus/Pit) | **-1000** | | Action cost | **-1** per move | | Arrow cost | **-10** | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blind Cave Explorer" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine you are exploring a pitch-dark, foggy cavern with only five faint sensations: a *stench* (smell of monster nearby), a *breeze* (draft from a pit), a *glitter* (gold reflection), a *bump* (you walked into a wall), and a *scream* (the monster died). You cannot *see* the cave. The only way to decide your next step safely is to *write down* every sensation, *apply logical rules* ("a stench means a Wumpus is in an adjacent cell"), and *deduce* where the safe squares are — exactly like a knowledge-based agent maintaining and querying its KB. ### 1.4 The Five Sensors (Percepts) The agent receives a 5-element percept vector at every time step: $$\text{Percept} = [\,\text{Stench},\ \text{Breeze},\ \text{Glitter},\ \text{Bump},\ \text{Screa m}\,]$$ Each element is a boolean (present / absent). ### 1.5 The Seven Actuators (Actions) 1. **Forward** – move one cell ahead (blocked by walls). 2. **TurnLeft** / **TurnRight** – rotate 90° in place. 3. **Grab** – pick up gold in current cell (instantly removes it). 4. **Shoot** – fire the single arrow in the agent's facing direction. 5. **Climb** – exit the cave (only valid in cell (1,1)). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The PEAS Specification of Wumpus World | Component | Description | |-----------|-------------| | **Performance** | +1000 gold, -1000 death, -1 per action, -10 for using arrow | | **Environment** | 4 × 4 grid, walls, 1 Wumpus, 1 gold, multiple pits | | **Actuators** | Forward, TurnLeft, TurnRight, Grab, Shoot, Climb | | **Sensors** | Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream | ### 2.2 Environment Properties (Board Exam Favourite) | Property | Classification | Justification | |----------|----------------|----------------| | Fully Observable? | **No** | The agent only senses the *current* cell, not the whole grid. | | Deterministic? | **Yes** | Each action has a single, well-defined outcome. | | Episodic? | **No** | The agent's decisions depend on the entire percept history. | | Static? | **Yes** | Pits and Wumpus do not move. | | Discrete? | **Yes** | Finite grid, finite percepts, finite actions. | | Single-Agent? | **Yes** | Wumpus does not strategize adversarially (it is stationary). | ### 2.3 Logical Vocabulary Used Inside the KB | Symbol | Meaning | |--------|---------| | $P_{x,y}$ | There is a **Pit** in cell $(x,y)$ | | $W_{x,y}$ | There is a **Wumpus** in cell $(x,y)$ | | $S_{x,y}$ | There is a **Stench** in cell $(x,y)$ | | $B_{x,y}$ | There is a **Breeze** in cell $(x,y)$ | | $G_{x,y}$ | There is **Gold** in cell $(x,y)$ | | $OK_{x,y}$ | Cell $(x,y)$ is **safe** to enter | | $L_{x,y}$ | The agent is currently in cell $(x,y)$ | ### 2.4 The Two Foundational Causal Rules (Most Important for KTU) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 1 — Breeze Equivalence:** > A cell has a breeze **iff** at least one of its four neighbours contains a pit. $$B_{x,y} \iff \bigl(P_{x,y+1} \lor P_{x,y-1} \lor P_{x+1,y} \lor P_{x-1,y}\bigr)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule 2 — Stench Equivalence:** > A cell has a stench **iff** at least one of its four neighbours contains the Wumpus. $$S_{x,y} \iff \bigl(W_{x,y+1} \lor W_{x,y-1} \lor W_{x+1,y} \lor W_{x-1,y}\bigr)$$ ### 2.5 Inference Mechanisms Used | Inference Type | Statement | Example in Wumpus World | |----------------|-----------|--------------------------| | **Modus Ponens** | From $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha$, infer $\beta$ | "If breeze, then pit adjacent" + "breeze is true" ⇒ "pit adjacent" | | **Resolution** | $(\alpha \lor \beta), (\neg\beta \lor \gamma) \models (\alpha \lor \gamma)$ | Used in resolution-refutation proofs | | **Model Checking** | Enumerate truth assignments | Used by inference engines for small KBs | | **Forward Chaining** | Apply rules whose premises are in the KB | Derive new facts from sensed cells | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Knowledge-Based Reasoning > [!NOTE] > Although Wumpus World is pedagogical, the same KB–Tell/Ask pattern is foundational to **expert systems** (MYCIN, DENDRAL), **semantic-web reasoners** (OWL, RDF), **automated theorem provers**, and **declarative programming languages** like **Prolog** and **Datalog**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: Deducing Safe Cells **Initial Knowledge Base (given):** The agent has visited cell (1,1) and recorded: - $R_1:\ \neg P_{1,1}$ - $R_2:\ \neg W_{1,1}$ - $R_3:\ \neg S_{1,1}$ - $R_4:\ \neg B_{1,1}$ The agent then moves to (2,1) and senses a **breeze but no stench**: - $R_5:\ B_{2,1}$ - $R_6:\ \neg S_{2,1}$ The agent then moves to (1,2) and senses a **stench but no breeze**: - $R_7:\ S_{1,2}$ - $R_8:\ \neg B_{1,2}$ **Step 1 — Apply Rule 1 to (1,1):** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{1,1} &\iff (P_{1,2} \lor P_{2,1}) \\ \neg B_{1,1} &\iff \neg(P_{1,2} \lor P_{2,1}) \\ \neg B_{1,1} &\iff (\neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the known fact $R_4$ into the equivalence:** $$ \begin{aligned} \neg B_{1,1} &\Rightarrow (\neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1}) \\ \text{True} &\Rightarrow (\neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1}) \end{aligned} $$ By **Modus Ponens**, the KB now contains: $$R_9:\ \neg P_{1,2} \qquad\qquad R_{10}:\ \neg P_{2,1}$$ > [!NOTE] > By symmetry of the Wumpus-world rules, the same reasoning for the 4-neighbourhood gives the general **square-of-safety** pattern: if a cell has *no breeze*, all its *orthogonal neighbours* are pit-free. **Step 3 — Apply Rule 1 to cell (2,1):** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{2,1} &\iff (P_{1,1} \lor P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) \\ \text{True} &\iff (P_{1,1} \lor P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) \\ \text{True} &\iff (\text{False} \lor P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) \quad [\text{since } R_1 : \neg P_{1,1}] \\ \text{True} &\iff (P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) \end{aligned} $$ Hence the agent deduces the **disjunction**: $$R_{11}:\ P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The KB does **not** know *which* cell has the pit — only that *one of them does*. This is called the **Horn-clause disjunction**, and it is the typical output of a forward-chaining reasoner. **Step 4 — Apply Rule 2 to cell (1,1):** $$ \begin{aligned} \neg S_{1,1} &\iff \neg(W_{1,2} \lor W_{2,1}) \\ \text{True} &\iff (\neg W_{1,2} \land \neg W_{2,1}) \end{aligned} $$ So the KB now also contains: $$R_{12}:\ \neg W_{1,2} \qquad\qquad R_{13}:\ \neg W_{2,1}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Rule 2 to cell (2,1):** $$ \begin{aligned} \neg S_{2,1} &\iff \neg(W_{1,1} \lor W_{2,2} \lor W_{3,1}) \\ \text{True} &\iff (\neg W_{1,1} \land \neg W_{2,2} \land \neg W_{3,1}) \end{aligned} $$ Combined with $R_2\ (\neg W_{1,1})$, this yields: $$R_{14}:\ \neg W_{2,2} \qquad\qquad R_{15}:\ \neg W_{3,1}$$ **Step 6 — Apply Rule 2 to cell (1,2):** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{1,2} &\iff (W_{1,1} \lor W_{1,3} \lor W_{2,2}) \\ \text{True} &\iff (\text{False} \lor W_{1,3} \lor \text{False}) \quad [R_2,\ R_{14}] \\ \text{True} &\iff W_{1,3} \end{aligned} $$ So the agent deduces — *with certainty* — that the Wumpus is in (1,3): $$R_{16}:\ W_{1,3}$$ **Step 7 — Combine everything into the OK predicate:** The agent defines $OK_{x,y} \iff \neg P_{x,y} \land \neg W_{x,y}$. The KB now contains the following safe-cell set: $$ \begin{aligned} OK_{1,1} &= \text{True} \\ OK_{1,2} &= \text{True} \quad [R_9,\ R_{12}] \\ OK_{2,1} &= \text{True} \quad [R_{10},\ R_{13}] \\ OK_{3,1} &= \text{True} \quad [R_{11}\ \text{plus single possibility eliminated later}] \\ OK_{2,2} &= \text{partially known} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8 — Decision rule from $OK$:** The agent's plan is now driven by the simple logical query: $$\text{Move to } (x^*,y^*)\ \text{where}\ (x^*,y^*) = \arg\max\ ASK\bigl(KB,\ OK_{x,y}\bigr)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final summary of deductions from the worked example:** The agent can prove (with certainty) that **(1,2), (2,1) and (3,1) are completely safe** to visit, that **the Wumpus is exactly at (1,3)**, and that **either (2,2) or (3,1) contains a pit** — but since (3,1) was already declared safe, the chain forces $P_{2,2}$ as the unique remaining possibility. The agent may now safely navigate to fetch the gold. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Percepts to KB Updates | Sensed Cell | Percept | New KB Sentence | Rule Applied | Derived Sentences | |-------------|---------|-----------------|--------------|-------------------| | (1,1) | None | $\neg P_{1,1}, \neg W_{1,1}$ | Domain axiom | — | | (1,1) | $\neg S_{1,1}$ | $\neg S_{1,1}$ | $S \iff$ adjacent W | $\neg W_{1,2}, \neg W_{2,1}$ | | (1,1) | $\neg B_{1,1}$ | $\neg B_{1,1}$ | $B \iff$ adjacent P | $\neg P_{1,2}, \neg P_{2,1}$ | | (2,1) | $B_{2,1}$ | $B_{2,1}$ | $B \iff$ adjacent P | $P_{1,1} \lor P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}$ ⇒ narrowed to $P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}$ | | (1,2) | $S_{1,2}$ | $S_{1,2}$ | $S \iff$ adjacent W | $W_{1,1} \lor W_{1,3} \lor W_{2,2}$ ⇒ $W_{1,3}$ (certain) | ### 3.3 Truth-Table Sketch for Cell (1,1) Breeze Equivalence | $P_{1,2}$ | $P_{2,1}$ | $P_{1,2} \lor P_{2,1}$ | $B_{1,1}$ | |:----------:|:----------:|:----------------------:|:---------:| | F | F | F | **F** | | F | T | T | **T** | | T | F | T | **T** | | T | T | T | **T** | This truth table compactly encodes **Rule 1**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Knowledge-Based Agent in the Wumpus World ```mermaid flowchart TB Env["Wumpus Cave Environment"] -->|percepts| Sens["Sensor Interface
Stench Breeze Glitter Bump Scream"] Sens -->|symbolic sentence| TELL["TELL Operation
Assert into KB"] TELL --> KB["Knowledge Base
Propositional Logic Sentences"] KB -->|ASK query| Infe["Inference Engine
Modus Ponens Resolution Forward Chaining"] Infe -->|new facts| KB Infe -->|best action| Decs["Decision Module
argmax OK x,y"] Decs -->|action command| Act["Actuator Interface
Forward Turn Grab Shoot Climb"] Act -->|action| Env Decs -->|justification| Log["Explanation Trace
for User or Debug"] ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Tell / Ask / Act Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Percept_Loop["Percept Loop"] P1["Observe World"] --> P2["Form Percept Sentence"] P2 --> P3["TELL to KB"] end subgraph Reasoning_Loop["Reasoning Loop"] R1["ASK KB about OK x,y"] --> R2["Apply Inference Rules"] R2 --> R3["Update KB with derived facts"] end subgraph Action_Loop["Action Loop"] A1["Choose best safe cell"] --> A2["Execute Actuator"] A2 --> A3["Sense outcome"] end P3 --> R1 R3 --> A1 A3 --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Logical Flow: From Stench/Breeze to OK ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Agent enters cell x,y"]) --> Sense{"Senses
present"} Sense -->|Stench| Srule["S x,y iff W in neighbours"] Sense -->|Breeze| Brule["B x,y iff P in neighbours"] Sense -->|None| Safe["No hazard detected"] Srule --> Inferred["Add not W neighbour to KB"] Brule --> Inferred2["Add not P neighbour to KB"] Inferred --> Ask["ASK KB: OK at candidate cell"] Inferred2 --> Ask Safe --> Ask Ask --> Move{"ASK returns
True"} Move -->|Yes| Go["Move to safe cell"] Move -->|No| Backtrack["Backtrack and try alternative"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Cave Layout — Worked Example State ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Cave["Wumpus World 4 by 4"] C11["cell 1,1
Agent Start
OK True"] -->|east| C21["cell 2,1
Breeze True
OK True"] C11 -->|north| C12["cell 1,2
Stench True
OK True"] C21 -->|north| C22["cell 2,2
Suspect Pit
OK False"] C12 -->|north| C13["cell 1,3
Wumpus Here"] C13 -->|north| C14["cell 1,4"] C22 -->|north| C23["cell 2,3"] C23 -->|north| C24["cell 2,4"] C21 -->|east| C31["cell 3,1
OK True"] C31 -->|east| C41["cell 4,1"] C31 -->|north| C32["cell 3,2"] C32 -->|north| C33["cell 3,3"] C33 -->|north| C34["cell 3,4"] C41 -->|north| C42["cell 4,2"] C42 -->|north| C43["cell 4,3"] C43 -->|north| C44["cell 4,4"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The cave layout above is a flowchart representation of the Wumpus-World grid (north is up). It marks the conclusions of the worked example in Section 3: cells (1,1), (1,2), (2,1) and (3,1) are certified safe; cell (1,3) is the Wumpus; cell (2,2) is a suspected pit. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** *Define a knowledge-based agent. With the help of a block diagram, explain how a generic KBA operates in the Wumpus World.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A knowledge-based agent (KBA) is a rational agent that maintains an internal symbolic model of the world inside a **Knowledge Base (KB)** and uses logical inference to decide actions. The agent's operation cycle is: **(1) Perceive** the world, **(2) TELL** the percept to the KB, **(3) ASK** the KB (via an inference engine) what action to perform, **(4) Execute** the chosen action, and **(5) Goto 1**. In the Wumpus World, the agent receives 5-tuples of percepts (Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream), encodes them as propositional-logic sentences, and uses inference rules such as Modus Ponens and Resolution to determine which adjacent cells are safe to enter. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *State the PEAS specification of the Wumpus World environment. Mention whether it is fully observable, deterministic, and static.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **P**erformance: +1000 for gold retrieval, -1000 for death, -1 per action, -10 for shooting the arrow. - **E**nvironment: 4 × 4 cave with 1 Wumpus, 1 gold pile, 1–3 pits, stationary hazards. - **A**ctuators: Forward, TurnLeft, TurnRight, Grab, Shoot, Climb. - **S**ensors: Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream. - **Properties:** *Not fully observable* (only the current cell is sensed), *deterministic* (each action has a definite outcome), *static* (Wumpus and pits do not move), *discrete*, *sequential*, and *single-agent*. ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 3 Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of a knowledge-based agent. How is the knowledge base updated and queried in the Wumpus World? Illustrate with a TELL/ASK block diagram. **(b) [7 marks]** Using the propositional logic rules for the Wumpus World, derive the conclusions an agent can make after visiting cells (1,1), (2,1) and (1,2) given the percepts: no stench and no breeze at (1,1); breeze at (2,1) but no stench; stench at (1,2) but no breeze. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** The knowledge-based agent (KBA) comprises **three sub-systems**: 1. **Knowledge Base (KB):** A set of sentences in a formal language (here, propositional logic) describing the world. 2. **Inference Engine:** A generic procedure (e.g., forward chaining, resolution) that derives new sentences entailed by the KB. 3. **TELL–ASK interface:** Allows the agent to insert percepts (TELL) and to query the KB (ASK). The operational cycle is shown below: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{KB}_0 &\leftarrow \text{domain axioms (e.g. } \neg P_{1,1},\ \neg W_{1,1}\text{)} \\ \text{For } t = 1, 2, \dots: & \\ \quad \alpha &\leftarrow \text{PERCEPT}(\text{World}_t) \\ \quad \text{KB}_t &\leftarrow \text{TELL}(\text{KB}_{t-1},\ \alpha) \\ \quad \text{action} &\leftarrow \text{ASK}(\text{KB}_t,\ \text{MAKE-ACTION-QUERY}) \\ \quad \text{KB}_{t+1} &\leftarrow \text{TELL}(\text{KB}_t,\ \text{action}) \\ \quad \text{Execute}(\text{action}) \end{aligned} $$ In the Wumpus World, percepts are translated into ground literals (e.g., "$B_{2,1}$ is true"), combined with the two causal rules $S \iff$ adjacent Wumpus and $B \iff$ adjacent Pit, and the inference engine (typically Modus Ponens plus resolution) is used to compute the set of safe cells $OK_{x,y}$. **(b) [7 marks]** The full step-by-step derivation has been worked out in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key results to be listed in the answer sheet are: - $\neg P_{1,2},\ \neg P_{2,1}$ (from $\neg B_{1,1}$ by Rule 1). - $\neg W_{1,2},\ \neg W_{2,1}$ (from $\neg S_{1,1}$ by Rule 2). - $P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}$ (from $B_{2,1}$ and $\neg P_{1,1}$ by Rule 1). - $\neg W_{2,2},\ \neg W_{3,1}$ (from $\neg S_{2,1}$ by Rule 2). - $W_{1,3}$ (from $S_{1,2}$ and the eliminations above by Rule 2). - $OK_{1,1}, OK_{1,2}, OK_{2,1}, OK_{3,1}$ are all `True`. - The agent is therefore free to move along the path (1,1) → (2,1) → (3,1) → (3,2) → (3,3) → … or to retreat via (1,2) as it searches for the gold. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the properties of the Wumpus World environment with respect to observability, determinism, episodicity, and staticity. Justify each classification with an example. **(b) [7 marks]** Encode the Wumpus World's breeze and stench rules as propositional-logic equivalences. Show, using Modus Ponens, that if the agent perceives no breeze at (1,1) and no breeze at (2,1), it can conclude that (3,1) is pit-free *only* if the disjunction $P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}$ is resolved using an additional percept. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** | Property | Classification | Justification (1.75 marks each) | |----------|----------------|---------------------------------| | Observability | **Partially observable** | The agent senses only its *current* cell. E.g., from (1,1) the agent cannot directly know whether a pit lies in (1,2) or (4,4). | | Determinism | **Deterministic** | Every action has exactly one outcome. Shooting the arrow in the Wumpus's direction *will* hit it; turning left *will* rotate 90°. | | Episodicity | **Sequential (non-episodic)** | The current action depends on the entire percept history. The decision to enter (2,1) depends on deductions made at (1,1). | | Staticity | **Static** | Pits and the Wumpus do not move, so the world does not change while the agent deliberates. | | Agents | **Single-agent** | The Wumpus is stationary and non-strategic; the player acts alone. | | Discreteness | **Discrete** | Finite cells, finite percepts, finite actions. | **(b) [7 marks]** The two foundational equivalences are: $$ \begin{aligned} B_{x,y} &\iff \bigl(P_{x+1,y} \lor P_{x-1,y} \lor P_{x,y+1} \lor P_{x,y-1}\bigr) \\ S_{x,y} &\iff \bigl(W_{x+1,y} \lor W_{x-1,y} \lor W_{x,y+1} \lor W_{x,y-1}\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ Apply Rule 1 to cells (1,1) and (2,1): $$ \begin{aligned} \neg B_{1,1} &\Rightarrow \neg(P_{1,2} \lor P_{2,1}) \equiv (\neg P_{1,2} \land \neg P_{2,1}) \\ B_{2,1} &\Rightarrow (P_{1,1} \lor P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) \end{aligned} $$ With the domain axiom $\neg P_{1,1}$: $$ (P_{1,1} \lor P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) \equiv (P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}) $$ This is a **Horn-clause disjunction** — the KB knows *one* of (2,2) or (3,1) is a pit, but **not which one**. To eliminate the ambiguity, the agent must gather an *additional percept*, e.g.: - Visit (2,2): if $B_{2,2}$ is false, then $P_{2,2}$ is false, so $P_{3,1}$ must be true. The agent concludes that (3,1) is **unsafe**. - Visit (3,1): if $B_{3,1}$ is false, then $P_{3,1}$ is false, so $P_{2,2}$ must be true. The agent concludes that (3,1) is **safe**. Hence the proposition *"the agent can conclude (3,1) is pit-free"* is true **only** after this extra percept triggers the application of **Modus Ponens** to one branch of the disjunction, *not* from the original two percepts alone. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. Students frequently forget that the cell coordinates are written as $(x,y)$ with $x$ = **column** (east-increasing) and $y$ = **row** (north-increasing). Mixing up the axes leads to wrong deductions — *lose 1 to 2 marks*. > 2. Many answers confuse the **causal direction**: $B_{x,y} \iff$ *adjacent* $P$, *not* $B_{x,y} \iff$ *self* $P$. The biconditional nature of the rule must be stated explicitly. > 3. Do **not** invent Wumpus-world rules (e.g., "stench implies gold nearby"). Only the two standard rules are admissible. > 4. A 14-mark answer must include a **diagram** (cave layout or TELL/ASK cycle). Skipping the diagram can cost 2 marks. > 5. When asked to "derive", you must show *all* inference steps using Modus Ponens or resolution — writing only the final conclusion earns partial credit at best. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Knowledge-Based Agents & Wumpus World** - **KBA cycle** = Perceive → TELL → ASK → Act → repeat. - **Wumpus World** is a 4 × 4 cave with 1 Wumpus, 1 gold, 1–3 pits, 1 arrow. - **Percepts** (5): Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream — each boolean. - **Actions** (7): Forward, TurnLeft, TurnRight, Grab, Shoot, Climb. - **Performance**: +1000, -1000, -1, -10. - **Properties**: *Not* fully observable, deterministic, sequential, static, discrete, single-agent. - **Two causal rules** (memorize verbatim): * $B_{x,y} \iff$ adjacent $P_{x,y}$ *(breeze ⇔ adjacent pit)* * $S_{x,y} \iff$ adjacent $W_{x,y}$ *(stench ⇔ adjacent Wumpus)* - **OK predicate**: $OK_{x,y} \iff \neg P_{x,y} \land \neg W_{x,y}$. - **Inference methods**: Modus Ponens, Resolution, Forward Chaining, Model Checking. - **Logical resolution** is the standard complete refutation procedure for propositional logic. - **Safe-cell algorithm**: TELL each percept, then iteratively ASK for the next OK cell. - **Disjunctions** like $P_{2,2} \lor P_{3,1}$ are *not* sufficient to plan a path; an *extra percept* is required to break the tie. - **Climb action is only valid at (1,1)** — agent must return to start to exit. - **Real-world analogues**: expert systems (MYCIN), semantic-web reasoners (OWL), Prolog programs, automated theorem provers. - **Coordinate convention**: $(x,y)$ with $x \in \{1..4\}$ = column (east), $y \in \{1..4\}$ = row (north); agent starts at $(1,1)$ facing east. - **KB does not store percepts as raw numbers** — they are encoded as **propositional-logic sentences** before being inserted.

topics.title: Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic in Artificial Intelligence** is the formal study of the principles of valid reasoning, inference, and demonstration. It provides an agent with a rigorous, syntactic framework to represent knowledge about the world (as **sentences** or **well-formed formulas (WFFs)**) and to derive new knowledge by applying sound **inference rules**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, logic is the *reasoning engine* of a Knowledge-Based Agent. The agent maintains a **Knowledge Base (KB)** — a set of sentences expressed in a formal language — and uses logical inference to answer queries, plan actions, and prove theorems.

The generic logical agent loop is:

$$\text{TELL} \xrightarrow{\text{new percepts}} \text{KB} \xrightarrow{\text{ASK}} \text{Action / Conclusion}$$

The two primary logical systems studied in KTU Module 3 are:

| System | Granularity | Expressive Power | Tractability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Propositional Logic (PL)** | Atomic propositions | Low | Decidable (efficient) |
| **First-Order Logic (FOL / FOPC)** | Objects, relations, functions | High | Semi-decidable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Propositional logic is the **mandatory** foundation. First-Order Logic and inference (Modus Ponens, Resolution, Forward/Backward Chaining) are the **high-weightage** topics. Expect a 14-mark Part B question on resolution proof or FOL conversion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Logic as Courtroom Argument"

Imagine you are a **judge in a courtroom**. A lawyer presents statements (the *Knowledge Base*). Each statement is a sentence in a language both sides understand. The judge applies **rules of argument** (inference rules) to decide whether the accused is *guilty beyond reasonable doubt*.

- A statement like *"The defendant was at the scene"* is analogous to a **proposition** $P$.
- A statement like *"If it rains, the ground is wet"* is a **conditional / implication** $P \Rightarrow Q$.
- A statement like *"Every student must submit a project"* is a **universally quantified** claim $\forall x \, \text{Student}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Submits}(x)$.
- The judge's deduction that *"the ground is wet"* from *"it rains"* + *"if it rains, ground is wet"* is exactly **Modus Ponens**.

Logic formalizes this courtroom procedure so a silicon-based agent can perform it without fatigue, bias, or forgetting.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever you are stuck on a logic problem, think: *What does the judge know? What rule can the judge apply? What is the judge trying to prove?*

## 1.3 The Knowledge-Based Agent — Architectural Snapshot

A logical agent operates in three cyclical phases:

1. **TELL** — Inject new percepts / facts into the KB.
2. **INFER** — Apply inference rules to derive new sentences.
3. **ASK** — Query the KB; the answer drives the next action.

This cycle is sometimes called the **TELL–ASK** loop. The KB must satisfy two fundamental properties:

- **Soundness:** Every derived sentence is entailed by the KB (no false conclusions).
- **Completeness:** Every sentence that is entailed by the KB can be derived (no missing conclusions).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, you may be asked to "justify whether an inference rule is sound." Always respond with: *"The rule is sound because it preserves truth — if premises are true, the conclusion must be true."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Propositional Logic (PL) — Building Blocks

A **proposition** is a declarative statement that is either **True (T)** or **False (F)** — never both, never neither. Propositional variables are typically denoted by uppercase letters $P, Q, R, S, \ldots$ and take truth values from the set $\{\text{T}, \text{F}\}$.

### 2.1.1 Logical Connectives (The Five "Vocab Words" of PL)

| Symbol | Name | Read As | Truth Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\neg P$ | Negation | "not P" | True iff $P$ is False |
| $P \land Q$ | Conjunction | "P AND Q" | True iff **both** $P$ and $Q$ are True |
| $P \lor Q$ | Disjunction | "P OR Q" | True iff **at least one** is True |
| $P \Rightarrow Q$ | Implication | "IF P THEN Q" | False **only** when $P$ is True and $Q$ is False |
| $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ | Biconditional | "P IFF Q" | True iff $P$ and $Q$ have the **same** truth value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap:** The implication $P \Rightarrow Q$ is **TRUE** when $P$ is False, regardless of $Q$. This is called a "vacuously true" implication and is the single most common source of marks lost in Part A.

### 2.1.2 Truth Table Reference (for the five connectives)

| $P$ | $Q$ | $\neg P$ | $P \land Q$ | $P \lor Q$ | $P \Rightarrow Q$ | $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F | F | T | F | F | T | T |
| F | T | T | F | T | T | F |
| T | F | F | F | T | F | F |
| T | T | F | T | T | T | T |

### 2.1.3 Logical Equivalences (The Algebra of Logic)

Two sentences $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are **logically equivalent** (written $\alpha \equiv \beta$) iff they have identical truth columns in every row. The following are the **KTU-mandated** equivalences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Commutativity} &: \quad (P \land Q) \equiv (Q \land P), \quad (P \lor Q) \equiv (Q \lor P) \\
\text{Associativity} &: \quad ((P \land Q) \land R) \equiv (P \land (Q \land R)) \\
\text{Distributivity} &: \quad P \land (Q \lor R) \equiv (P \land Q) \lor (P \land R) \\
\text{De Morgan's Laws} &: \quad \neg(P \land Q) \equiv \neg P \lor \neg Q, \quad \neg(P \lor Q) \equiv \neg P \land \neg Q \\
\text{Double Negation} &: \quad \neg(\neg P) \equiv P \\
\text{Implication Elimination} &: \quad P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q \\
\text{Contrapositive} &: \quad P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg Q \Rightarrow \neg P \\
\text{Biconditional} &: \quad P \Leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \Rightarrow Q) \land (Q \Rightarrow P)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Exam Heuristic:** Any sentence in PL can be converted to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** — a conjunction of disjunctions of literals. CNF is the input language of the **Resolution** algorithm (the crown jewel of KTU Module 3).

## 2.2 First-Order Logic (FOL) — Adding Objects & Quantifiers

Propositional logic cannot express statements like *"All humans are mortal"* without enumerating every human. **First-Order Logic (FOL)** solves this by introducing:

| FOL Element | Symbol | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant** | Lowercase $a, b, c$ | $james, 2, kerala$ |
| **Variable** | Lowercase $x, y, z$ | $x, y$ |
| **Predicate** | Uppercase $P, Q, R$ | $Human(x), Likes(x, y)$ |
| **Function** | Lowercase $f, g, h$ | $father(james)$ |
| **Universal Quantifier** | $\forall$ | $\forall x \, P(x)$ — "for all $x$, $P(x)$ holds" |
| **Existential Quantifier** | $\exists$ | $\exists x \, P(x)$ — "there exists an $x$ such that $P(x)$" |

### 2.2.1 Quantifier Duality (De Morgan for Quantifiers)

$$
\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)
$$

$$
\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** The negation of "Everyone passed" is **not** "Everyone failed" — it is "**Someone** failed." Examiners test this nuance every year.

### 2.2.2 Quantifier Distribution Rules

$$
\forall x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)) \equiv \forall x \, P(x) \land \forall x \, Q(x)
$$

$$
\exists x \, (P(x) \lor Q(x)) \equiv \exists x \, P(x) \lor \exists x \, Q(x)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> The analogous rules with swapped quantifiers **do NOT hold**:
> $\forall x \, (P(x) \lor Q(x)) \not\equiv \forall x \, P(x) \lor \forall x \, Q(x)$.
> Example: "Every number is even or odd" is TRUE, but "Every number is even OR every number is odd" is FALSE.

## 2.3 Inference Rules — The Deduction Engine

### 2.3.1 Propositional Inference Rules (KTU High-Yield)

| Rule | Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens (MP)** | From $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha$, infer $\beta$ | "The affirming mode" |
| **Modus Tollens (MT)** | From $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ and $\neg \beta$, infer $\neg \alpha$ | "Denying the consequent" |
| **And-Elimination** | From $\alpha \land \beta$, infer $\alpha$ (or $\beta$) | Drop conjuncts |
| **And-Introduction** | From $\alpha$ and $\beta$, infer $\alpha \land \beta$ | Join statements |
| **Or-Introduction** | From $\alpha$, infer $\alpha \lor \beta$ | Add false possibilities |
| **Resolution** | From $\alpha \lor \beta$ and $\neg \beta \lor \gamma$, infer $\alpha \lor \gamma$ | The **universal** rule |
| **Unit Resolution** | From $\alpha \lor \beta$ and $\neg \beta$, infer $\alpha$ | Special case of resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolution is the ONLY inference rule needed for a complete proof procedure in PL.** All other rules can be derived as special cases. This is why KTU Module 3 devotes the heaviest weightage to it.

### 2.3.2 FOL Inference Rules

| Rule | Form |
|---|---|
| **Universal Elimination (UE)** | From $\forall x \, P(x)$, infer $P(c)$ for any constant $c$ |
| **Existential Elimination (EE)** | From $\exists x \, P(x)$, infer $P(c)$ for a *new* Skolem constant $c$ |
| **Existential Introduction (EI)** | From $P(c)$, infer $\exists x \, P(x)$ |
| **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** | From $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n, (P_1 \land P_2 \land \cdots \land P_n) \Rightarrow Q$, infer $Q\theta$ where $\theta$ unifies premises with antecedent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unification** is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two FOL expressions syntactically identical. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution. KTU often asks: *"Find the MGU of $P(x, f(y))$ and $P(a, z)$."*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Implication → CNF | $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \lor Q$ | Converting to CNF |
| Biconditional → CNF | $P \Leftrightarrow Q \equiv (\neg P \lor Q) \land (\neg Q \lor P)$ | Converting to CNF |
| Negate quantifier | $\neg \forall \equiv \exists \neg$; $\neg \exists \equiv \forall \neg$ | Negating FOL statements |
| Skolemization of $\forall \exists$ | Replace existentials with Skolem **functions** of universals | Eliminating $\exists$ |
| Modus Ponens | $\frac{\alpha \Rightarrow \beta, \; \alpha}{\beta}$ | Forward deduction |
| Resolution | $\frac{\alpha \lor \beta, \; \neg \beta \lor \gamma}{\alpha \lor \gamma}$ | CNF refutation |
| **Syllogism (Transitivity)** | $\frac{P \Rightarrow Q, \; Q \Rightarrow R}{P \Rightarrow R}$ | Chain inference |
| **Contrapositive** | $P \Rightarrow Q \equiv \neg Q \Rightarrow \neg P$ | Converting to MT form |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Logic forms the backbone of **propositional satisfiability (SAT) solvers**, **automated theorem provers** (e.g., Coq, Isabelle), **circuit verification** in chip design, **database query optimization**, and **rule-based expert systems** (e.g., MYCIN medical diagnosis).
