# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*What is First Order Logic (FOL)? Mention its components.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
First-Order Logic (FOL) is a knowledge representation language that extends propositional logic by introducing **objects, variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers** (∀ and ∃), enabling the representation of generalized facts and relations.

**Components (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Objects** — constants/individuals (e.g., Ram, 5).
2. **Predicates** — relations over objects (e.g., `Loves(x, y)`).
3. **Functions** — mappings from objects to objects (e.g., `fatherOf(x)`).
4. **Variables** — placeholders (x, y, z).
5. **Quantifiers** — `∀` (universal) and `∃` (existential).
6. **Connectives** — ¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*State and explain the De Morgan rules for quantifiers with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rule 1 (1 Mark):** `¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x)`
*"Not all x satisfy P" is equivalent to "There exists some x that does not satisfy P."*

**Rule 2 (1 Mark):** `¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x)`
*"There does not exist an x satisfying P" is equivalent to "For every x, P is false."*

**Example (1 Mark):**
- `¬∀x (Student(x) → Passed(x))` ≡ `∃x (Student(x) ∧ ¬Passed(x))`
  → *"Not every student passed"* ≡ *"There exists a student who did not pass."*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Translate the following English statements into First Order Logic:
1. (2 Marks) Every student enrolled in a course has a unique course code.
2. (2 Marks) Some students are not present in the class.
3. (3 Marks) All the students who are present in the class are intelligent and study hard.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Apply the quantifier De Morgan rules to simplify:
`¬∀x ∃y (Loves(x, y) ∧ ¬Loves(y, x))` and explain each step in plain English.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution for (a):**

**(1)** Let:
- `Enrolled(x, y)` = student x is enrolled in course y
- `Student(x)` = x is a student
- `Code(y, c)` = course y has code c

$$
\forall x \, \forall y \, \forall c_1 \, \forall c_2 \; \big[ (\text{Enrolled}(x, y) \land \text{Code}(y, c_1) \land \text{Code}(y, c_2)) \rightarrow c_1 = c_2 \big]
$$

**(2)** Let `Present(x)` = x is present in class.

$$
\exists x \; (\text{Student}(x) \land \neg \text{Present}(x))
$$

**(3)** Let `Intelligent(x)` and `StudiesHard(x)`.

$$
\forall x \; \big( (\text{Student}(x) \land \text{Present}(x)) \rightarrow (\text{Intelligent}(x) \land \text{StudiesHard}(x)) \big)
$$

---

**Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Apply negation of universal `¬∀x …` → `∃x ¬(…)`

$$
\exists x \; \neg \big( \exists y \; (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \neg \text{Loves}(y, x)) \big)
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Apply negation of existential `¬∃y …` → `∀y ¬(…)`

$$
\exists x \; \forall y \; \neg (\text{Loves}(x, y) \land \neg \text{Loves}(y, x))
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Apply De Morgan's law to the inner conjunction: `¬(A ∧ B) ≡ ¬A ∨ ¬B`

$$
\exists x \; \forall y \; (\neg \text{Loves}(x, y) \lor \neg \neg \text{Loves}(y, x))
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Simplify double negation `¬¬P = P`

$$
\exists x \; \forall y \; (\neg \text{Loves}(x, y) \lor \text{Loves}(y, x))
$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Plain English interpretation:
*"There exists some person x such that for every person y, either x does not love y, or y loves x."*
In other words: *"There is someone who, for every other person, either doesn't love them or is loved by them."*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the syntax of First Order Logic with suitable examples. Differentiate between **free** and **bound** variables.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the Wumpus World. Represent the following rules in FOL:
1. (3 Marks) "A square is breezy if and only if there is a pit in an adjacent square."
2. (2 Marks) "There is exactly one pit in cell (1, 3)."
3. (2 Marks) "The wumpus is in exactly one square."

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Syntax (4 Marks):** The FOL syntax consists of the following BNF grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sentence} &::= \text{AtomicSentence} \;\vert\; \text{ComplexSentence} \\
\text{AtomicSentence} &::= \text{Predicate}(\text{Term}, \ldots) \;\vert\; \text{Term} = \text{Term} \\
\text{ComplexSentence} &::= (\text{Sentence}) \;\vert\; \neg \text{Sentence} \;\vert\; \text{Sentence} \land \text{Sentence} \\
&\;\;\vert\; \text{Sentence} \lor \text{Sentence} \;\vert\; \text{Sentence} \Rightarrow \text{Sentence} \;\vert\; \text{Sentence} \Leftrightarrow \text{Sentence} \\
&\;\;\vert\; \text{Quantifier} \; \text{variable}, \ldots \; \text{Sentence} \\
\text{Term} &::= \text{Function}(\text{Term}, \ldots) \;\vert\; \text{Constant} \;\vert\; \text{Variable} \\
\text{Quantifier} &::= \forall \;\vert\; \exists
\end{aligned}
$$

**Free vs Bound Variables (3 Marks):**
- A variable is **bound** if it lies within the scope of a quantifier referring to it. Example: In `∀x (P(x) → Q(x))`, both occurrences of `x` are bound.
- A variable is **free** if it is not within the scope of any quantifier. Example: In `P(x) ∧ Q(y)`, both `x` and `y` are free.
- A **well-formed sentence** must have **no free variables**.

---

**Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Let the vocabulary be:**
- `Pit(x, y)` = there is a pit at (x, y)
- `Breezy(x, y)` = the square (x, y) is breezy
- `Adjacent((x, y), (u, v))` = square (x, y) is adjacent to square (u, v)
- `Wumpus(x, y)` = the wumpus is at (x, y)

**(1)** Breeze ↔ Pit in adjacent square:

$$
\forall x \, \forall y \; \big[ \text{Breezy}(x, y) \Leftrightarrow \exists u \, \exists v \; (\text{Adjacent}((x,y), (u,v)) \land \text{Pit}(u, v)) \big]
$$

**(2)** Exactly one pit in cell (1, 3):
This is encoded as: *There is a pit at (1,3), AND for any other cell, there is no pit.*

$$
\text{Pit}(1, 3) \;\land\; \forall x \, \forall y \; \big[ \neg(x = 1 \land y = 3) \rightarrow \neg \text{Pit}(x, y) \big]
$$

**(3)** Exactly one wumpus:

$$
\exists x \, \exists y \; \text{Wumpus}(x, y) \;\land\; \forall x_1 \, \forall y_1 \, \forall x_2 \, \forall y_2 \; \big[ (\text{Wumpus}(x_1, y_1) \land \text{Wumpus}(x_2, y_2)) \rightarrow (x_1 = x_2 \land y_1 = y_2) \big]
$$

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong connectives inside quantifiers (–2 Marks):** Using `∧` instead of `→` for universal statements like *"All A are B"*. Rule: `∀x (A(x) ∧ B(x))` is WRONG; correct is `∀x (A(x) → B(x))`.
> 2. **Confusing `∀` with `∃` (–2 Marks):** Re-read the English — *"some"* means existential, *"all/every"* means universal.
> 3. **Free variables in final sentence (–1 Mark):** A proper FOL sentence must have all variables bound.
> 4. **Skipping the De Morgan intermediate steps (–1 Mark):** Always show the step-by-step transformation; do not jump directly to the final answer.
> 5. **Forgetting parentheses in nested quantifiers (–1 Mark):** Ambiguity in scope loses marks.
> 6. **Mixing up `fatherOf` (function) vs `Father` (predicate):** If it returns a *value* (a unique object), it is a **function**; if it asserts a *property*, it is a **predicate**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **FOL = Propositional Logic + Quantifiers + Predicates + Functions + Variables**.
- **Universal `∀`** is used with `→` (implication) for *"All A are B"* patterns.
- **Existential `∃`** is used with `∧` (conjunction) for *"Some A is B"* patterns.
- **De Morgan Duality:** `¬∀x P ≡ ∃x ¬P` and `¬∃x P ≡ ∀x ¬P`. These are tested **every year** in KTU.
- **`∀x∀y ≢ ∀y∀x`? Actually yes ≡** — same-type quantifiers **can** be swapped. Only **mixed** `∀∃` swaps are non-equivalent.
- **Bound variables** = inside the scope of a quantifier; **Free variables** = not inside any quantifier's scope.
- **Atomic sentences** = predicate applied to terms (no connectives). **Complex sentences** = built with connectives.
- **Equality `=`** is itself a predicate in FOL; `Term₁ = Term₂` asserts that they refer to the same object.
- **Knowledge Engineering Pipeline:** Identify task → Assemble knowledge → Encode in FOL → Query with inference.
- **Three Model Components:** Objects, Relations, Functions. Always declare your **vocabulary (signature)** before translating.
- **Wumpus World** is the standard KTU example for FOL encoding of rules and situations.
- A FOL sentence must be a **closed formula** (no free variables) to be evaluated as true or false.
- **Kinship domain** (Mother, Father, Sibling) is the most commonly used pedagogical domain in KTU questions.
- Always specify the **domain of discourse** (e.g., all students, all integers) when stating a quantified sentence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inference in First Order Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inference in First Order Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Inference in First-Order Logic (FOL)** is the systematic process of deriving new logical statements (conclusions) from a given set of premises (knowledge base) using formally sound rules of deduction. It extends propositional inference by incorporating **quantifiers** ($\forall$, $\exists$), **predicates**, **variables**, and **functions**, enabling reasoning about entire classes of objects rather than just boolean facts.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, inference in FOL is positioned as the **deductive reasoning engine** of an intelligent agent — the mechanism that allows an agent to conclude actions, beliefs, or new facts that were not explicitly programmed into the knowledge base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from Propositional Logic**
> Propositional inference operates on atomic boolean symbols. FOL inference operates on quantified sentences, requiring additional mechanisms like **Unification** (variable substitution) and **Skolemization** (elimination of existential quantifiers) before standard rules like Modus Ponens or Resolution can be applied.

The general inference pattern in FOL is:

$$ KB \vdash \alpha $$

This denotes that sentence $\alpha$ is **entailed** (or derivable) from the knowledge base $KB$ via a sequence of valid inference steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soundness vs. Completeness**
> - A inference procedure is **sound** if it derives only entailed sentences (no false conclusions).
> - A inference procedure is **complete** if it can derive every sentence that is entailed.
> Resolution is both **sound and refutation-complete** for FOL, which is why it is the cornerstone of automated theorem proving.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective's Deduction

Imagine a detective at a crime scene (the **Knowledge Base**):

- **Premises (Evidence):** "All fingerprints at the scene belong to the suspect" ($\forall x\ Fingerprint(x, scene) \implies Suspect(x)$).
- **Observation:** "There is a fingerprint at the scene" ($\exists x\ Fingerprint(x, scene)$).
- **Inference:** The detective **deduces** that the suspect was at the scene.

The detective does not store a precomputed fact for every possible crime. Instead, they apply **general rules** to **specific observations**. This is precisely what FOL inference does — it uses universal rules to instantiate specific facts and apply them via logical rules.

**Real-world engineering analogy:** A medical diagnosis system (e.g., MYCIN) uses FOL rules like $\forall x\ (Fever(x) \land Rash(x) \implies Measles(x))$ combined with patient data to infer diseases. The inference engine is the "reasoning doctor" that walks through the rules.

## 1.3 Components Required for FOL Inference

| Component | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Base (KB)** | Stores axioms (ground facts + rules) | $\forall x\ King(x) \implies Person(x)$ |
| **Unification Algorithm** | Finds variable substitutions making expressions match | $\{x/Skyler\}$ |
| **Inference Engine** | Applies rules to derive new sentences | Modus Ponens, Resolution |
| **Query** | The sentence whose entailment is being tested | $Person(Skyler)$ |
| **Substitution $\theta$** | A mapping from variables to terms | $\{x/Skyler, y/Crownguard\}$ |

## 1.4 The Need for Inference — Why FOL Cannot Use Brute Force

Propositional logic with $n$ symbols has $2^n$ possible models. FOL has **infinitely many models** (because of function symbols and variables). Therefore, truth-table enumeration is impossible. FOL inference must rely on **syntactic rule application** rather than model checking.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reduction to Propositional Inference

The fundamental idea of FOL inference is to **reduce it to propositional inference** by instantiating universally quantified sentences with every possible ground term in the domain. This is the foundation of **Generalized Modus Ponens**.

## 2.2 Universal Instantiation (UI)

**Rule Statement:** From a universally quantified sentence, we can infer any ground instance obtained by substituting a ground term for the variable.

$$\frac{\forall v\ \phi}{\phi\{v/g\}} \quad \text{(UI)}$$

Where $g$ is a **ground term** (a term with no variables).

**Example:**
$$ \forall x\ King(x) \implies Person(x) \;\vdash\; King(John) \implies Person(John) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** UI is the mathematical basis of how database query systems operate — a query like "List all kings" implicitly applies UI to the universal rule over every record.

## 2.3 Existential Instantiation (EI)

**Rule Statement:** From an existentially quantified sentence, we can infer a ground instance with a **Skolem constant** — a new constant symbol not used elsewhere.

$$\frac{\exists v\ \phi}{\phi\{v/C\}} \quad \text{(EI)}$$

Where $C$ is a **Skolem constant** representing "some unnamed object."

**Example:**
$$ \exists x\ Crown(x) \land Royal(x) \;\vdash\; Crown(C_1) \land Royal(C_1) $$

> [!NOTE]
> EI is **only valid once** — the Skolem constant cannot be re-used to make a second inference about the same existential, or we may incorrectly infer that the same object satisfies two different existentials.

## 2.4 Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

GMP combines UI with standard Modus Ponens by allowing substitutions during the rule application.

$$\frac{p_1',\; p_2',\; \dots,\; p_n',\; (p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \implies q)}{q\theta} \quad \text{(GMP)}$$

Where $\theta$ is a **unifier** such that $p_i' \theta = p_i \theta$ for all $i$.

## 2.5 Unification

**Unification** is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest (most general) such substitution.

### 2.5.1 Unification Algorithm (Robinson, 1965)

The recursive procedure:

1. Apply substitutions sequentially to both expressions.
2. Recursively unify the arguments of matched function/predicate symbols.
3. If a variable is unified with a term containing that variable (**occurs check failure**), report failure.

**Occurs Check Rule:** A variable $x$ cannot be unified with a term $t$ if $x$ occurs in $t$ (otherwise it creates an infinite structure $x = f(x)$).

### 2.5.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Inference Rules

| Rule | Schema | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens (MP)** | $\frac{\alpha \implies \beta,\; \alpha}{\beta}$ | Basic deduction |
| **Universal Instantiation (UI)** | $\frac{\forall v\ \phi}{\phi\{v/g\}}$ | Instantiate universal rules |
| **Existential Instantiation (EI)** | $\frac{\exists v\ \phi}{\phi\{v/C\}}$ | Replace existential with Skolem |
| **Generalized MP (GMP)** | Applies MP with unifier $\theta$ | Forward/Backward chaining |
| **Resolution (FOL)** | $\frac{\ell_1 \lor \dots \lor \ell_k,\; m_1 \lor \dots \lor m_n}{(\ell_1 \lor \dots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \dots \lor \ell_k \lor m_1 \lor \dots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \dots \lor m_n)\theta}$ | Theorem proving |

### 2.5.3 Standard Unification Examples

| Expression 1 | Expression 2 | MGU $\theta$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Knows(John, x)$ | $Knows(John, Jane)$ | $\{x/Jane\}$ | Simple substitution |
| $Knows(John, x)$ | $Knows(y, Mary)$ | $\{x/Mary,\; y/John\}$ | Two substitutions |
| $Knows(John, x)$ | $Knows(y, Mother(y))$ | $\{y/John,\; x/Mother(John)\}$ | Functional term |
| $Knows(John, x)$ | $Knows(x, Mary)$ | **FAIL** | $x$ cannot be both John and Mary |
| $P(x, x)$ | $P(A, B)$ | **FAIL** | $x$ must unify to same term |
| $P(x, f(x))$ | $P(A, y)$ | $\{x/A,\; y/f(A)\}$ | Sequential substitution |

## 2.6 Forward Chaining

**Forward chaining** is a data-driven reasoning strategy that starts from known facts and repeatedly applies rules whose premises are satisfied until the query is proved (or no new facts can be derived).

**Algorithm Outline:**
1. Start with the initial KB.
2. For each rule whose premises are satisfied (unifiable with known facts), apply GMP to derive the conclusion.
3. Add the new fact to the KB.
4. Repeat until the query is derived or no progress is made.

**Properties:**
- **Sound:** Yes (uses sound inference rules).
- **Complete:** Yes, for **definite clause KBs** (Horn clauses with exactly one positive literal).
- **Complexity:** Polynomial in the size of the KB.

## 2.7 Backward Chaining

**Backward chaining** is a goal-driven strategy that starts from the query and works backward, finding rules whose conclusions unify with the goal, and then recursively proving the premises.

**Algorithm Outline:**
1. Given a goal $q$.
2. Find all rules whose head (conclusion) unifies with $q$ via MGU $\theta$.
3. For each such rule, recursively try to prove all premises (with substitution $\theta$ applied).
4. If all premises are proved, return success with composed substitutions.

**Properties:**
- **Sound:** Yes.
- **Complete:** Yes, for definite clause KBs.
- **Complexity:** Linear in the size of the KB.
- Used in logic programming languages like **Prolog**.

## 2.8 Resolution in FOL

**Resolution** is a complete inference rule for FOL used primarily in **refutation systems**. The strategy is:

1. Convert all sentences to **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**.
2. Negate the query (to prove entailment by contradiction).
3. Repeatedly apply the resolution rule to pairs of clauses.
4. Derive the **empty clause** $\square$ — this signals a contradiction, meaning the original KB entails the query.

### 2.8.1 Steps to Convert FOL to CNF

1. **Eliminate implications:** $A \implies B \equiv \neg A \lor B$.
2. **Move negations inward** using De Morgan's laws and quantifier duality.
3. **Standardize variables** apart (each quantifier uses a unique variable).
4. **Skolemize** (eliminate existential quantifiers with Skolem functions).
5. **Drop universal quantifiers** (implicit in CNF).
6. **Convert to CNF** using distributive laws.

## 2.9 Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | FOL Inference Role |
|---|---|
| **Automated Theorem Proving** | Resolution-based provers (Vampire, E, SPASS) |
| **Semantic Web / Ontologies** | OWL reasoners (HermiT, Pellet) use description logic FOL |
| **Software Verification** | Hoare logic uses FOL inference for program correctness |
| **AI Planning** | Situation calculus with STRIPS uses FOL inference |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Compositional semantics translated to FOL |
| **Database Query Optimization** | Datalog engines use forward chaining over FOL rules |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Unification Algorithm

**Problem:** Unify $P(f(x, g(y)), z)$ with $P(f(g(a), h(b)), w)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We apply Robinson's unification algorithm with the occurs check.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial: } & \text{Unify}\big(P(f(x, g(y)), z),\; P(f(g(a), h(b)), w)\big) \\
& \text{Symbols match (both P), descend into arguments:}\\
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Unify}\big(f(x, g(y)),\; f(g(a), h(b))\big) \\
& \text{Symbols match (both f), descend into args:}\\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Unify}(x,\; g(a)) \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_1 = \{x/g(a)\} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Apply } \theta_1 \text{ to remaining: } \text{Unify}\big(g(y),\; h(b)\big) \\
& \text{Symbols differ (g vs h), FAIL — cannot unify.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Unification **fails**. There is no substitution that makes these two expressions identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Learning Point:** The recursive descent into function arguments is the heart of unification. If the top-level function symbols differ, the entire expression cannot be unified regardless of the arguments.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Generalized Modus Ponens with Substitutions

**Knowledge Base:**
- $\forall x\ (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \implies Evil(x))$
- $King(John)$
- $Greedy(John)$

**Query:** $Evil(John)$?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule: } & \forall x\ (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \implies Evil(x)) \\
\text{Goal: } & \text{Unify } Evil(John) \text{ with the rule's head } Evil(x) \\
\text{MGU: } & \theta = \{x/John\} \\
\text{Apply GMP: } & \frac{King(John) \land Greedy(John) \implies Evil(John),\; King(John),\; Greedy(John)}{Evil(John)} \\
\therefore\; & KB \vdash Evil(John)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Forward Chaining — Criminal Identification

**Knowledge Base (in FOL):**
1. $\forall x\ (American(x) \land Weapon(y) \implies Sells(x, y, z))$
2. $\forall x\ \forall y\ \forall z\ (Missile(y) \implies Weapon(y))$
3. $American(West)$
4. $Hostile(West)$
5. $\forall x\ (\forall y\ Missile(y) \implies Sells(Robert, y, x))$
6. $\forall x\ (Hostile(x) \implies Criminal(x))$
7. $\forall x\ (Missile(x) \implies Owns(Nono, x))$
8. $Missile(M_1)$
9. $\forall x\ (Enemy(x, America) \implies Hostile(x))$
10. $Enemy(Nono, America)$

**Query:** Is West a criminal? (i.e., $Criminal(West)$?)

### Forward Chaining Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 0: } & \text{Facts: } \{American(West),\; Hostile(West),\; Missile(M_1),\; Enemy(Nono, America)\} \\
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Apply (2) with } \{y/M_1\}: Missile(M_1) \implies Weapon(M_1) \\
& \therefore Weapon(M_1) \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Apply (7) with } \{x/M_1\}: Missile(M_1) \implies Owns(Nono, M_1) \\
& \therefore Owns(Nono, M_1) \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Apply (5) with } \{x/M_1,\; y/M_1\}: \\
& Missile(M_1) \implies Sells(Robert, M_1, Nono) \\
& \therefore Sells(Robert, M_1, Nono) \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Apply (1) with } \{x/West,\; y/M_1,\; z/Nono\}: \\
& American(West) \land Weapon(M_1) \implies Sells(West, M_1, Nono) \\
& \therefore Sells(West, M_1, Nono) \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Apply (9) with } \{x/Nono\}: Enemy(Nono, America) \implies Hostile(Nono) \\
& \therefore Hostile(Nono) \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{Apply (6) with } \{x/West\}: Hostile(West) \implies Criminal(West) \\
& \therefore \boxed{Criminal(West)} \quad \text{— QUERY PROVED}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observations:**
> - Forward chaining derives **12 new facts** before finding the answer.
> - Each step uses **GMP with a MGU**.
> - The algorithm is **monotonic** — once a fact is added, it is never removed.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Backward Chaining — Prolog-Style

**Same KB. Query: $Criminal(West)$?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal: } & Criminal(West) \\
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Find rules with head matching } Criminal(West). \\
& \text{Only rule (6): } Hostile(x) \implies Criminal(x) \\
& \text{MGU: } \theta_1 = \{x/West\} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{New goal: } Hostile(West) \\
& \text{Match: Fact (4) } Hostile(West) \text{ is directly in KB.} \\
& \therefore \text{Sub-goal proven.} \\
\text{Result: } & \theta_1 = \{x/West\} \text{ gives } Criminal(West). \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Contrast:** Backward chaining proved the goal in **2 steps** vs. forward chaining's **6 steps** for this particular query. This is because backward chaining is **goal-directed** and avoids irrelevant derivations.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Resolution — Refutation Proof

**KB (CNF form):**
1. $\neg American(x) \lor \neg Weapon(y) \lor Sells(x, y, z)$
2. $\neg Missile(y) \lor Weapon(y)$
3. $American(West)$
4. $Hostile(West)$
5. $\neg Missile(y) \lor Sells(Robert, y, x)$
6. $\neg Hostile(x) \lor Criminal(x)$
7. $\neg Missile(x) \lor Owns(Nono, x)$
8. $Missile(M_1)$
9. $\neg Enemy(x, America) \lor Hostile(x)$
10. $Enemy(Nono, America)$

**Negated Query:** $\neg Criminal(West)$

### Resolution Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R11: } & \text{Resolve (4) and (6) with } \{x/West\}: \\
& \neg Hostile(West) \lor Criminal(West)\; \text{vs}\; Hostile(West) \\
& \Rightarrow Criminal(West) \\
\text{R12: } & \text{Resolve R11 with } \neg Criminal(West): \\
& \Rightarrow \square \;\; \text{(EMPTY CLAUSE — CONTRADICTION)} \\
& \therefore KB \vdash Criminal(West) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The empty clause $\square$ represents **False**. Its derivation means "the assumption that the KB does not entail the query leads to a contradiction," hence the query is entailed.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Forward vs. Backward Chaining vs. Resolution

| Property | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven | Goal-driven | Refutation |
| **Strategy** | Antecedent $\to$ Consequent | Consequent $\to$ Antecedent | Negate goal, derive $\square$ |
| **Search Space** | All derivable facts | Only relevant to query | All clause combinations |
| **Best For** | Monitoring, planning | Query answering, Prolog | Theorem proving |
| **Completeness** | Yes (definite clauses) | Yes (definite clauses) | Yes (refutation-complete) |
| **Soundness** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Complexity** | Polynomial | Linear (in KB size) | Exponential (worst case) |
| **Handles Negation** | No (Horn only) | No (Horn only) | Yes (full FOL) |
| **Implementations** | Production systems, Datalog | Prolog, Datalog | Vampire, E, SPASS |
| **Mark Recommendation** | High-yield | High-yield | Most-asked topic in KTU |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inference Mechanism Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Knowledge Base in FOL] --> B[Parsing and Tokenization]
    B --> C[Convert to Clause Form CNF]
    C --> D{Choose Inference Strategy}

    D -->|Data Driven| E[Forward Chaining Engine]
    D -->|Goal Driven| F[Backward Chaining Engine]
    D -->|Refutation| G[Resolution Engine]

    E --> H[Apply Generalized Modus Ponens]
    F --> H
    G --> I[Apply Resolution Rule with MGU]

    H --> J[Unification Algorithm]
    I --> J

    J --> K{New Facts Derived?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Add to KB]
    L --> J
    K -->|No| M{Query Proven?}

    M -->|Yes| N[Return Success with Substitutions]
    M -->|No| O[Return Failure]
```

## 4.2 Forward Chaining Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Facts: F1, F2, F3] --> B{Any Rule Premises Satisfied?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Select Rule R]
    C --> D[Compute MGU theta]
    D --> E[Apply GMP to Derive New Fact]
    E --> F[Add New Fact to KB]
    F --> G{Query Derived?}
    G -->|No| B
    G -->|Yes| H[Return Proof]
    B -->|No, no progress| I[Return Failure]
```

## 4.3 Backward Chaining Goal Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Query: Criminal West] --> R1[Rule 6: Hostile x implies Criminal x]
    R1 --> M1[MGU theta equals x:West]
    M1 --> G1[Sub-goal: Hostile West]
    G1 --> F1[Fact: Hostile West in KB]
    F1 --> S1[SUCCESS - Proved]
```

## 4.4 Resolution Refutation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: KB and Query alpha] --> B[Convert KB to CNF]
    B --> C[Negate Query: add not alpha]
    C --> D[Initialize Clause Set]
    D --> E[Select Two Resolvable Clauses]
    E --> F[Compute MGU theta]
    F --> G[Apply Resolution Rule]
    G --> H{Empty Clause Derived?}
    H -->|No| I[Add Resolvent to Clause Set]
    I --> J{No More Resolutions Possible?}
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[Return: Query NOT Entailed]
    H -->|Yes| L[Return: Query IS Entailed]
```

## 4.5 Unification Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Expression E1 and E2] --> B{Both Atomic Symbols?}
    B -->|Yes, same symbol| C[Return Empty Substitution]
    B -->|Yes, different| D[Return FAIL]
    B -->|No| E{E1 is Variable?}
    E -->|Yes| F{Occurs Check: E1 in E2?}
    F -->|Yes| D
    F -->|No| G[Return Substitution E1: E2]
    E -->|No, E2 is Variable| H[Symmetric Check on E2]
    H --> D
    H --> I[Return Substitution E2: E1]
    G --> J[Recursively Unify Function Arguments]
    I --> J
    C --> K[Apply Substitution to Remaining Arguments]
    D --> L[Unification FAILED]
    J --> M{All Arguments Unified?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Return Composed MGU]
    M -->|No| L
```

## 4.6 CNF Conversion Pipeline (For Resolution)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FOL Sentence] --> B[Step 1: Eliminate Implications]
    B --> C[Step 2: Move Negation Inward De Morgan]
    C --> D[Step 3: Standardize Variables Apart]
    D --> E[Step 4: Skolemize Existential Quantifiers]
    E --> F[Step 5: Drop Universal Quantifiers]
    F --> G[Step 6: Convert to CNF via Distributive Laws]
    G --> H[Step 7: Standardize Apart Again]
    H --> I[Set of Clauses Ready for Resolution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Unification in First-Order Logic with a Suitable Example. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions identical by binding their variables to terms. The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such substitution that does not introduce unnecessary bindings.

**Example:** Unify $Knows(John, x)$ with $Knows(y, Mary)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unify: } & Knows(John, x) = Knows(y, Mary) \\
\text{MGU } \theta: & \{x/Mary,\; y/John\}
\end{aligned}
$$

After applying $\theta$: $Knows(John, Mary)$ — both expressions become identical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MGU is **unique up to variable renaming**, which guarantees deterministic unification.

---

### Question 2: What is Skolemization? Why is it Important in FOL Inference? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Skolemization** is the process of eliminating existential quantifiers ($\exists$) from a FOL sentence by replacing existentially quantified variables with **Skolem functions** of the surrounding universally quantified variables.

**Example:**
$$ \forall x\ \exists y\ Loves(x, y) \;\longrightarrow\; \forall x\ Loves(x, f(x)) $$
where $f$ is a Skolem function representing "the person that $x$ loves."

**Importance:**
1. Enables conversion to **CNF** (which contains only universal quantifiers).
2. Allows application of **resolution** and other refutation methods.
3. **Preserves satisfiability** — a sentence is satisfiable iff its Skolemized form is satisfiable.
4. Reduces the inference problem to propositional-style clause manipulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** inference rule with a suitable example. How does it differ from standard Modus Ponens? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Unification Algorithm** step-by-step to find the MGU of $P(a, x, f(g(y)))$ and $P(z, f(a), f(w))$. Show all substitutions. If unification fails, state the reason. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP):**

GMP is an extension of Modus Ponens that incorporates **unification** to handle quantified premises. It allows rules and facts to be matched via variable substitution before applying the rule.

**Schema:**
$$
\frac{p_1',\; p_2',\; \dots,\; p_n',\; (p_1 \land p_2 \land \dots \land p_n \implies q)}{q\theta} \quad \text{(GMP)}
$$

where $\theta$ is a substitution that unifies $p_i$ with $p_i'$ for all $i$.

**Differences from Standard Modus Ponens:**

| Property | Modus Ponens | Generalized MP |
|---|---|---|
| **Variables** | No variables | Variables allowed |
| **Matching** | Exact symbol match | Unification with MGU |
| **Expressiveness** | Limited | Captures universal rules |
| **Application** | Direct | FOL forward/backward chaining |

**Example:**
- Rule: $\forall x\ (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \implies Evil(x))$
- Facts: $King(John)$, $Greedy(John)$
- Unifier: $\theta = \{x/John\}$
- Conclude: $Evil(John)$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** Unify $P(a, x, f(g(y)))$ with $P(z, f(a), f(w))$.

**Step-by-step Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Unify } P(a, x, f(g(y)))\; \text{with}\; P(z, f(a), f(w)) \\
& \text{Predicate symbol matches (P = P). Recurse on arguments.} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Unify } a \text{ with } z \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_1 = \{z/a\} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Apply } \theta_1. \text{ Unify } x \text{ with } f(a) \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_2 = \{x/f(a)\} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Apply } \theta_2. \text{ Unify } f(g(y)) \text{ with } f(w) \\
& \text{Function symbols match (f = f). Recurse on arguments.} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Unify } g(y) \text{ with } w \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_3 = \{w/g(y)\} \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{Compose all substitutions:} \\
\theta_{\text{MGU}} = & \{z/a,\; x/f(a),\; w/g(y)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The MGU is $\{z/a,\; x/f(a),\; w/g(y)\}$. After applying it to both expressions, they both become $P(a, f(a), f(g(y)))$.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** What is **Resolution** in First-Order Logic? Explain the steps to convert a FOL sentence into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following KB:
- $\forall x\ (Student(x) \implies \exists y\ Friend(x, y) \land Studies(x))$
- $Student(Anu)$
- $\forall x\ \forall y\ (Friend(x, y) \implies Helps(x, y))$

Prove that $\exists y\ Helps(Anu, y)$ using **Resolution Refutation**. Show all resolution steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Resolution in FOL:**

Resolution is a sound and refutation-complete inference rule used to prove entailment by contradiction. It works on clauses (disjunctions of literals) and derives the empty clause ($\square$) when the KB entails the negated query.

**Steps to Convert FOL to CNF:**

1. **Eliminate biconditionals and implications** using:
   - $A \iff B \equiv (A \implies B) \land (B \implies A)$
   - $A \implies B \equiv \neg A \lor B$

2. **Move negations inward** using:
   - $\neg \neg A \equiv A$
   - $\neg (A \land B) \equiv \neg A \lor \neg B$
   - $\neg (A \lor B) \equiv \neg A \land \neg B$
   - $\neg \forall x\ P \equiv \exists x\ \neg P$
   - $\neg \exists x\ P \equiv \forall x\ \neg P$

3. **Standardize variables apart** — rename bound variables to be unique.

4. **Skolemize** — replace each existentially quantified variable with a Skolem function of the enclosing universally quantified variables.

5. **Drop universal quantifiers** (implicit in CNF).

6. **Distribute $\lor$ over $\land$** to get CNF.

**Example:** Convert $\forall x\ (P(x) \implies \exists y\ (Q(x, y) \land \neg R(y)))$ to CNF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original: } & \forall x\ (P(x) \implies \exists y\ (Q(x, y) \land \neg R(y))) \\
\text{Step 1: } & \forall x\ (\neg P(x) \lor \exists y\ (Q(x, y) \land \neg R(y))) \\
\text{Step 4: } & \forall x\ (\neg P(x) \lor (Q(x, f(x)) \land \neg R(f(x)))) \\
\text{Step 6: } & \big(\neg P(x) \lor Q(x, f(x))\big) \land \big(\neg P(x) \lor \neg R(f(x))\big) \\
\text{CNF Clauses: } & C_1: \neg P(x) \lor Q(x, f(x)) \\
& C_2: \neg P(x) \lor \neg R(f(x))
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**KB in CNF:**
- $C_1: \neg Student(x) \lor Friend(x, f(x))$
- $C_2: \neg Student(x) \lor Studies(x)$
- $C_3: Student(Anu)$
- $C_4: \neg Friend(x, y) \lor Helps(x, y)$

**Negated Query:** $C_5: \neg Helps(Anu, y)$

**Resolution Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R6: } & \text{Resolve } C_1 \text{ and } C_3 \text{ with } \{x/Anu\}: \\
& \neg Student(Anu) \lor Friend(Anu, f(Anu)) \text{ vs } Student(Anu) \\
& \Rightarrow Friend(Anu, f(Anu)) \\
\text{R7: } & \text{Resolve R6 and } C_4 \text{ with } \{x/Anu,\; y/f(Anu)\}: \\
& Friend(Anu, f(Anu)) \text{ vs } \neg Friend(Anu, f(Anu)) \lor Helps(Anu, f(Anu)) \\
& \Rightarrow Helps(Anu, f(Anu)) \\
\text{R8: } & \text{Resolve R7 and } C_5 \text{ with } \{y/f(Anu)\}: \\
& Helps(Anu, f(Anu)) \text{ vs } \neg Helps(Anu, f(Anu)) \\
& \Rightarrow \square \;\; \text{(EMPTY CLAUSE)} \\
\therefore & KB \vdash \exists y\ Helps(Anu, y) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the Negation Step:** Students often forget to **negate the query** before applying resolution. The query must be in negated form to derive a contradiction. *Marks lost: 2–3.*
> 2. **Skolemization Errors:** Replacing $\exists y$ with a constant (instead of a Skolem function of the surrounding universals) is a common mistake. *Marks lost: 1–2.*
> 3. **Skipping CNF Conversion Steps:** Examiners expect **all six steps** to be shown. Skipping them loses method marks. *Marks lost: 2.*
> 4. **Wrong Unifier Notation:** Using inconsistent substitution notation (e.g., $\theta = x/John$ vs $\theta(x) = John$). Standardize on $\theta = \{x/John\}$. *Marks lost: 1.*
> 5. **Confusing MGU with any unifier:** Always present the **most general unifier** (MGU), not just any unifier. *Marks lost: 1.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Unification:** Finding substitution $\theta$ that makes two FOL expressions identical.
- ☐ **MGU:** Most General Unifier — unique up to variable renaming.
- ☐ **Skolemization:** Replacing $\exists$-quantified variables with Skolem functions.
- ☐ **GMP:** Modus Ponens extended with unification.
- ☐ **Resolution:** Refutation-complete inference rule on CNF clauses.
- ☐ **Forward Chaining:** Data-driven reasoning (antecedent $\to$ consequent).
- ☐ **Backward Chaining:** Goal-driven reasoning (consequent $\to$ antecedent).

### Critical Rules
- ☐ **UI:** From $\forall v\ \phi$, infer $\phi\{v/g\}$ for any ground term $g$.
- ☐ **EI:** From $\exists v\ \phi$, infer $\phi\{v/C\}$ where $C$ is a fresh Skolem constant.
- ☐ **Occurs Check:** $x$ cannot unify with a term containing $x$ (prevents infinite structures).
- ☐ **Resolution:** Complementary literals resolved via MGU yield a resolvent clause.

### High-Yield Comparisons
- ☐ Forward vs. Backward Chaining (data vs. goal-driven).
- ☐ GMP vs. Standard Modus Ponens (with vs. without unification).
- ☐ Resolution vs. GMP (refutation-based vs. direct derivation).
- ☐ Soundness vs. Completeness (no false conclusions vs. finds all true conclusions).

### CNF Conversion Order (Mnemonic: **E-N-S-S-D-D-S**)
1. ☐ **E**liminate implications
2. ☐ **N**egation inward
3. ☐ **S**tandardize variables
4. ☐ **S**kolemize
5. ☐ **D**rop universals
6. ☐ **D**istribute $\lor$ over $\land$
7. ☐ **S**tandardize apart again

### Exam Strategy Tips
- ☐ Always **show the MGU** explicitly in resolution steps.
- ☐ **Number clauses** ($C_1, C_2, \dots$) and resolvents ($R_1, R_2, \dots$) for clarity.
- ☐ For forward chaining questions, present results in a **numbered table** with each rule, its premises, and derived fact.
- ☐ For backward chaining, draw a **search tree** to demonstrate goal decomposition.
- ☐ Memorize the **six-step CNF conversion** — it is a guaranteed sub-question in KTU exams.
- ☐ Remember that **Resolution is the only rule that handles full FOL** (including negation and disjunction) while forward/backward chaining require Horn clauses.

### Key Constants/Metrics to Memorize
- ☐ Refutation completeness of Resolution (established by Robinson, 1965).
- ☐ Unification algorithm complexity: **O(n)** where $n$ is the size of the expressions.
- ☐ Forward chaining complexity for Horn clauses: **polynomial**.
- ☐ Backward chaining complexity: **linear** in the size of the KB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propositional vs. First Order Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Propositional vs. First Order Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inference** is the process of deriving new logical conclusions (or sentential consequences) from a knowledge base of explicitly stated premises using formally valid rules of reasoning.

### 1.1.1 Propositional Logic (PL) Inference
**Propositional Logic** (also called Sentential Logic / Boolean Logic) is a formal language in which the atomic units of meaning are **whole declarative statements (propositions)** that are either **True (T)** or **False (F)**. Inference in PL manipulates these propositions through a fixed set of **logical connectives** — negation (¬), conjunction (∧), disjunction (∨), implication (→), and biconditional (↔) — to determine whether a query sentence *q* logically follows from a knowledge base *KB* (written **KB ⊨ q**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of PL:** It is **propositional** because every symbol represents a *complete fact* (e.g., `R`, `P`, `Q`), with no internal structure, no variables, and no quantifiers.

### 1.1.2 First-Order Logic (FOL) Inference
**First-Order Predicate Logic (FOL / FOPC)** extends propositional logic by introducing:
- **Variables** ($x, y, z$) ranging over a domain of discourse
- **Predicates** $P(x, Q(y))$ relating objects
- **Functions** $f(x)$ mapping objects to objects
- **Quantifiers** — the **Universal Quantifier (∀)** and the **Existential Quantifier (∃)**

An FOL knowledge base allows statements like "For every person, if that person is a student, then that person studies" — an expressivity that PL simply cannot capture compactly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** FOL inference is the process of applying sound rules (Universal Elimination, Existential Elimination, Generalized Modus Ponens, Resolution) to a set of quantified sentences to derive new quantified conclusions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Library Catalog" Analogy**

Imagine you are a librarian trying to answer a researcher's question.

- **Propositional Logic** is like a card catalog where **every card describes an entire book** as a single labeled fact: `Book_About_Aerodynamics = True`, `Book_On_Shelf_42 = True`. You can only ask: *"Is there a book that satisfies fact X AND fact Y?"* You cannot ask *"What kind of book?"* or *"Which author?"* — the structure inside the book is invisible.

- **First-Order Logic** is like a **relational database** where books are broken into structured records: `Author(Book42) = "Newton"`, `Subject(Book42) = Physics`, `Year(Book42) = 1687`. Now you can ask: *"Find every book whose subject is Physics AND whose author is Newton."* The library has moved from *flat fact-tagging* to *structured relational reasoning*.

| Library Concept | Propositional Logic | First-Order Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic unit | `Card_42` (a label) | `Book(x)` (a relation) |
| Question types | Truth of whole facts | Truth about objects & relations |
| Quantification | None (all/each must be enumerated) | ∀ and ∃ built-in |
| Scalability | **Exponential blow-up** | **Compact, structured** |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a KTU student:** The transition from PL to FOL is essentially the transition from *boolean variables* to *objects, properties, and relationships* — the same conceptual leap that takes you from flat files to relational databases.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Expressivity Hierarchy:** Propositional Logic $\subset$ First-Order Logic $\subset$ Higher-Order Logic $\subset$ Type Theory.
- **Decidability:** PL is **decidable** (can be solved by truth tables, though SAT is **NP-Complete**). FOL validity is **semi-decidable** — if a sentence is valid, a proof will eventually be found; if invalid, the procedure may never halt.
- **Soundness:** A rule is *sound* if it never derives false conclusions from true premises.
- **Completeness:** A rule is *complete* if it can derive every conclusion that logically follows.
- **Refutation Completeness:** Resolution-based FOL is **refutation-complete** (proves inconsistency iff the KB is inconsistent).

---

## 1.4 Mark-Relevant Highlight Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Asked 3-Mark Question):**
> *PL* manipulates *unanalysed propositions*; *FOL* adds *variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers* to reason about **objects** and their **relations**. PL is decidable; FOL is only semi-decidable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of Propositional Inference

Propositional inference operates on **well-formed formulas (wff)** built from a finite set of atomic propositions $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ using the connectives $\{ \neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow \}$.

### 2.1.1 The Two Main Approaches
1. **Model Checking (Semantic):** Enumerate all $2^n$ truth assignments and check whether the KB entails the query. *Sound and complete* but **exponential in $n$**.
2. **Theorem Proving (Syntactic):** Apply inference rules to derive the query from the KB without enumerating models. *Linear in steps* but the search for the right sequence can be expensive.

### 2.1.2 Standard Inference Rules (PL)

| Rule Name | Form | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Modus Ponens (MP)** | $\frac{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \alpha}{\beta}$ | If "if A then B" and "A", then "B" |
| **Modus Tollens (MT)** | $\frac{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \neg \beta}{\neg \alpha}$ | If "if A then B" and "not B", then "not A" |
| **And-Elimination (∧E)** | $\frac{\alpha \wedge \beta}{\alpha}$ | From a conjunction, take any conjunct |
| **And-Introduction (∧I)** | $\frac{\alpha, \quad \beta}{\alpha \wedge \beta}$ | Combine two facts into a conjunction |
| **Or-Introduction (∨I)** | $\frac{\alpha}{\alpha \vee \beta}$ | A fact implies itself disjoined with anything |
| **Resolution** | $\frac{\alpha \vee \beta, \quad \neg \beta \vee \gamma}{\alpha \vee \gamma}$ | Cancel complementary literals |
| **Double Negation (DN)** | $\frac{\neg \neg \alpha}{\alpha}$ | Two negations cancel out |
| **Unit Resolution** | $\frac{\alpha \vee \beta, \quad \neg \beta}{\alpha}$ | Special case of resolution with one unit |

### 2.1.3 Resolution Procedure (PL)
Resolution is the **canonical refutation-complete rule** for PL. The procedure is:
1. Convert every sentence in the KB and the negated query $\neg Q$ into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**.
2. Repeatedly apply the **Resolution Inference Rule** to pairs of clauses containing complementary literals.
3. If a derivation of the **empty clause (⊥)** is reached, the KB entails $Q$. Otherwise, resolution terminates without proof.

---

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of First-Order Inference

FOL inference adds two powerful engines on top of PL: **Skolemization** (to eliminate existential quantifiers) and **Unification** (to match predicates with possibly different arguments).

### 2.2.1 FOL Inference Rules

| Rule | Form (with substitution $\theta$) | Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Universal Elimination (UE)** | $\frac{\forall x \; P(x)}{P(\text{Ground Term})}$ | Replace $x$ with any ground term |
| **Existential Elimination (∃E)** | $\frac{\exists x \; P(x)}{P(c)}$ where $c$ is a *Skolem constant* | Replace the variable with a new constant |
| **Universal Introduction (UI)** | $\frac{P(g) \text{ where } g \text{ is arbitrary}}{\forall x \; P(x)}$ | If it holds for an arbitrary term, it holds for all |
| **Existential Introduction (∃I)** | $\frac{P(c)}{\exists x \; P(x)}$ | A specific fact implies its existence |
| **Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)** | $\frac{p_1', p_2', \ldots, p_n', \quad (p_1 \wedge p_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge p_n \rightarrow q)}{\text{subst}(\theta, q)}$ | Lifted MP using unifier $\theta$ |
| **FOL Resolution** | $\frac{C_1, C_2}{\text{Res}(\theta, C_1, C_2)}$ | Resolve complementary unifiable literals |

### 2.2.2 Unification
**Unification** finds a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions syntactically identical. It is the heart of FOL inference.

- A substitution $\theta = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \ldots\}$ replaces each $x_i$ with term $t_i$.
- A substitution $\theta$ *unifies* expressions $E_1$ and $E_2$ iff $E_1\theta = E_2\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unification Algorithm (Robinson, 1965):** Recursively process the two expressions in lockstep. If the heads disagree, fail. If a variable is encountered, apply the *Occurs Check* — a variable cannot be bound to a term that contains itself (otherwise infinite loops). Result is the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**.

### 2.2.3 Conversion to CNF in FOL (Critical for Resolution)

The reduction of an arbitrary FOL sentence to CNF involves these steps:
1. **Eliminate $\rightarrow$ and $\leftrightarrow$** using $A \rightarrow B \equiv \neg A \vee B$ and $A \leftrightarrow B \equiv (A \rightarrow B) \wedge (B \rightarrow A)$.
2. **Move $\neg$ inwards** using De Morgan's laws and $\neg \forall x P \equiv \exists x \neg P$, $\neg \exists x P \equiv \forall x \neg P$.
3. **Skolemize:** Replace each existentially quantified variable with a **Skolem function** of the enclosing universally quantified variables. If there are no enclosing universals, use a **Skolem constant**.
4. **Drop universal quantifiers** (implicit in CNF).
5. **Standardize variables** apart.
6. **Move quantifiers / Distribute** $\vee$ over $\wedge$ to obtain CNF.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Inference Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important compilation** for answering KTU questions on this topic. Memorize it.

| Property | Propositional Logic (PL) | First-Order Logic (FOL) |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic Unit | Proposition $P$ (whole fact) | Predicate $P(x, y, z)$ (relation) |
| Variables | **None** | $x, y, z, \ldots$ (range over domain) |
| Quantifiers | **None** | $\forall$ (universal), $\exists$ (existential) |
| Functions | **None** | $f(x), g(x,y), \ldots$ (map objects → objects) |
| Expressivity | Limited (no "for all" or "there exists") | High (can express relations & quantified facts) |
| Decidability | **Decidable** (truth table = $O(2^n)$; SAT = NP-Complete) | **Semi-decidable** (FOL validity) |
| Inference Methods | Modus Ponens, Resolution, Truth Table | GMP, Forward/Backward Chaining, Resolution, Unification |
| Key Procedure | Resolution on propositional clauses | Resolution + **Skolemization + Unification** |
| Completeness of Resolution | **Refutation complete** | **Refutation complete** (Gödel, 1930) |
| Scalability | Poor (flat facts) | Good (structured relations) |
| Example Sentence | $R \vee \neg P$ | $\forall x \; (Student(x) \rightarrow \exists y \; Studies(x, y))$ |
| Real-World Use | Digital circuits, Boolean SAT solving | Database querying, knowledge graphs, theorem provers |

### 2.3.1 Critical Boundary Definitions

- **Ground Term:** A term containing **no variables** (e.g., `Father(John)`, `5`, `plus(2,3)`).
- **Ground Instance:** A sentence with all variables replaced by ground terms.
- **Herbrand Universe:** The countable set of **all ground terms** that can be constructed from the function and constant symbols of a KB — the foundation of Herbrand's theorem, which reduces FOL inference to (infinite) propositional inference.
- **Lifting Lemma:** A *lifted* inference rule (one that operates on quantified sentences) is sound iff its *ground* (propositional) counterpart is sound.

### 2.3.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Logic Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **VLSI / Digital Circuit Design** | PL | Boolean gates = propositions; SAT solvers optimize gate layouts |
| **SQL Query Optimization** | FOL | Relational algebra is a fragment of FOL; quantifiers in `∀`/`∃` correspond to SQL semantics |
| **Automated Theorem Provers (Isabelle, Coq)** | Higher-Order FOL | Formal verification of hardware, crypto, compilers |
| **Knowledge Graphs (Google, Wikidata)** | FOL | "Every capital is a city" = $\forall x (Capital(x) \rightarrow City(x))$ |
| **SAT Solvers (z3, MiniSAT)** | PL | Hardware verification, planning, cryptography |
| **Medical Diagnosis Systems** | FOL | "If patient has symptom X and test Y is positive, then disease Z" — needs quantifiers over patients |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through **two exhaustive worked examples** — one for PL resolution and one for FOL resolution with Skolemization and Unification. Each algebraic and logical step is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Propositional Resolution (Refutation Proof)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
Given the propositional knowledge base:
- $P_1$: $\neg P \vee Q$  (i.e., $P \rightarrow Q$)
- $P_2$: $\neg R \vee S$  (i.e., $R \rightarrow S$)
- $P_3$: $P \vee R$  (i.e., either P is true or R is true)
- $P_4$: $\neg S$  (i.e., S is false)

**Query:** Prove that $Q$ is entailed — i.e., $KB \models Q$.

### 3.1.2 Strategy: Refutation by Contradiction
We assume $\neg Q$ and show that the KB together with $\neg Q$ is **unsatisfiable**. If a contradiction (empty clause) is derived, the original KB must entail $Q$.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 0 — Negate the query** and add it to the clause set.

$$
\text{Clause Set } = \{ C_1: \neg P \vee Q,\; C_2: \neg R \vee S,\; C_3: P \vee R,\; C_4: \neg S,\; C_5: \neg Q \}
$$

Here, we use the fact that every sentence is already in CNF (a disjunction of literals), so no further conversion is needed for PL.

**Step 1 — Resolve $C_1$ and $C_5$.**
We look for complementary literals. $C_1$ contains $Q$ (positive) and $C_5$ contains $\neg Q$ (negative). They cancel. The resolution rule yields:

$$
\text{Resolve}(C_1, C_5) = \frac{(\neg P \vee Q),\; (\neg Q)}{(\neg P)} = C_6
$$

> **Logic:** $Q$ and $\neg Q$ are the *complementary pair*. All other literals survive. The surviving literal is $\neg P$.

**Step 2 — Resolve $C_2$ and $C_4$.**
$C_2$ contains $S$ (positive) and $C_4$ contains $\neg S$ (negative). They cancel.

$$
\text{Resolve}(C_2, C_4) = \frac{(\neg R \vee S),\; (\neg S)}{(\neg R)} = C_7
$$

> **Logic:** $S$ and $\neg S$ cancel, leaving $\neg R$.

**Step 3 — Resolve $C_3$ and $C_7$.**
$C_3$ contains $R$ (positive) and $C_7$ contains $\neg R$ (negative). They cancel.

$$
\text{Resolve}(C_3, C_7) = \frac{(P \vee R),\; (\neg R)}{(P)} = C_8
$$

> **Logic:** $R$ and $\neg R$ cancel, leaving $P$.

**Step 4 — Resolve $C_6$ and $C_8$.**
$C_6$ contains $\neg P$ and $C_8$ contains $P$. They cancel.

$$
\text{Resolve}(C_6, C_8) = \frac{(\neg P),\; (P)}{()} = \square \; (\text{empty clause})
$$

> **Logic:** The empty clause $\square$ is **False** by definition. A refutation is achieved.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
The empty clause is derived, so the clause set $\{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4, \neg Q\}$ is **unsatisfiable**. By the principle of refutation, $KB \models Q$ is **proved**.

$$
\therefore KB \models Q \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — First-Order Resolution (Skolemization + Unification + Refutation)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement (The "Animal" Puzzle)
Given the FOL knowledge base:
- $F_1$: $\forall x \; (Cat(x) \rightarrow Mammal(x))$
- $F_2$: $\forall x \; (Mammal(x) \rightarrow Animal(x))$
- $F_3$: $Cat(Tom)$

**Query:** Prove that $Animal(Tom)$ is entailed — i.e., $KB \models Animal(Tom)$.

### 3.2.2 Strategy: Refutation by Contradiction in FOL

We assume $\neg Animal(Tom)$ and, after converting the entire FOL clause set to CNF, apply FOL resolution (which internally uses **unification** to find substitutions $\theta$).

### 3.2.3 Step 1 — Convert Each Sentence to Prenex Normal Form
The sentences are already in prenex form (quantifiers at the front).

### 3.2.4 Step 2 — Eliminate Implications

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Cat(x) \vee Mammal(x)) \\
F_2: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Mammal(x) \vee Animal(x)) \\
F_3: \quad Cat(Tom) \\
\neg Q: \quad \neg Animal(Tom)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $A \rightarrow B$ becomes $\neg A \vee B$ by material implication.

### 3.2.5 Step 3 — Skolemize (no existentials to remove)
The only quantifier is $\forall x$ (universal). When we drop universal quantifiers (they become implicit), no Skolem functions are needed. The sentences are already variable-free in their universal parts, except for $F_1$ and $F_2$ which still carry the variable $x$.

### 3.2.6 Step 4 — Standardize Variables Apart
Rename $x$ in $F_2$ to $y$ so the two clauses do not share variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1: \quad \neg Cat(x) \vee Mammal(x) \\
F_2: \quad \neg Mammal(y) \vee Animal(y) \\
F_3: \quad Cat(Tom) \\
\neg Q: \quad \neg Animal(Tom)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.7 Step 5 — Drop Universal Quantifiers (Implicit in CNF)
The four CNF clauses are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1: \quad \neg Cat(x) \vee Mammal(x) \\
C_2: \quad \neg Mammal(y) \vee Animal(y) \\
C_3: \quad Cat(Tom) \\
C_4: \quad \neg Animal(Tom)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.8 Step 6 — Perform FOL Resolution

**Resolution Step 1: Unify $C_2$ with $C_4$.**
We seek a substitution $\theta_1$ such that the positive literal in $C_2$ (namely $Animal(y)$) and the negative literal in $C_4$ (namely $\neg Animal(Tom)$) become complementary.

Apply the substitution $\theta_1 = \{ y / Tom \}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2\theta_1 &= \neg Mammal(Tom) \vee Animal(Tom) \\
C_4\theta_1 &= \neg Animal(Tom)
\end{aligned}
$$

The literals $Animal(Tom)$ and $\neg Animal(Tom)$ are complementary. Apply resolution:

$$
C_5 = \text{Resolve}(C_2\theta_1, C_4) = \neg Mammal(Tom)
$$

> **Logic:** $Animal(Tom)$ cancels between the two clauses, leaving $\neg Mammal(Tom)$.

**Resolution Step 2: Unify $C_1$ with $C_3$.**
We seek $\theta_2$ such that $Cat(x)$ from $C_1$ and $Cat(Tom)$ from $C_3$ become identical.

Apply the substitution $\theta_2 = \{ x / Tom \}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1\theta_2 &= \neg Cat(Tom) \vee Mammal(Tom) \\
C_3\theta_2 &= Cat(Tom)
\end{aligned}
$$

Resolve complementary literals $Cat(Tom)$ and $\neg Cat(Tom)$:

$$
C_6 = \text{Resolve}(C_1\theta_2, C_3) = Mammal(Tom)
$$

> **Logic:** $Cat(Tom)$ cancels, leaving $Mammal(Tom)$.

**Resolution Step 3: Unify $C_5$ with $C_6$.**
We seek $\theta_3$ such that $\neg Mammal(Tom)$ (from $C_5$) and $Mammal(Tom)$ (from $C_6$) cancel.

Apply the substitution $\theta_3 = \{\}$ (the empty substitution, since the ground terms already match):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_5\theta_3 &= \neg Mammal(Tom) \\
C_6\theta_3 &= Mammal(Tom)
\end{aligned}
$$

Resolve:

$$
C_7 = \text{Resolve}(C_5, C_6) = \square \quad (\text{empty clause})
$$

> **Logic:** $Mammal(Tom)$ and $\neg Mammal(Tom)$ cancel, leaving the empty clause.

### 3.2.9 Step 7 — Conclusion
The empty clause is derived. Hence, $KB \cup \{\neg Animal(Tom)\}$ is **unsatisfiable**, and therefore:

$$
\therefore KB \models Animal(Tom) \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Unification Example (Supplementary)

### 3.3.1 Problem
Find the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** $\theta$ for the two atomic expressions:

$$
E_1 = P(a, x, F(G(y))) \quad \text{and} \quad E_2 = P(z, F(b), F(w))
$$

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Unification

| Step | Comparison (Predicate then arguments) | Decision | Substitution $\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P$ vs $P$ | Predicate symbol matches, continue | $\theta_0 = \{\}$ |
| 2 | $a$ vs $z$ | $z$ is a variable, bind $z/a$ (Occurs Check passes: $a$ has no $z$) | $\theta_1 = \{z/a\}$ |
| 3 | $x$ vs $F(b)$ | $x$ is a variable, bind $x/F(b)$ (Occurs Check passes) | $\theta_2 = \{z/a,\; x/F(b)\}$ |
| 4 | $F(G(y))$ vs $F(w)$ | Function symbol $F$ matches, recurse on $G(y)$ vs $w$ | (unchanged) |
| 5 | $G(y)$ vs $w$ | $w$ is a variable, bind $w/G(y)$ | $\theta_3 = \{z/a,\; x/F(b),\; w/G(y)\}$ |

**Final MGU:**

$$
\boxed{\theta = \{ z/a,\; x/F(b),\; w/G(y) \}}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
E_1\theta = P(a, F(b), F(G(y))) = E_2\theta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both expressions reduce to the identical ground form. Unification succeeded.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Aspect | Propositional Resolution | First-Order Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Set of propositional clauses (literals are atomic) | Set of FOL clauses after Skolemization (literals are atomic predicate applications) |
| Mechanism | Direct cancellation of complementary literals | **Unification first**, then cancellation on unifiable complementary literals |
| Search Space | Bounded by $2^n$ possible truth assignments | Can be infinite (Herbrand universe is often infinite) |
| Termination | Guaranteed for finite KB | **Not guaranteed** in general (semi-decidable) |
| Implementation | Truth table, DPLL, SAT solvers | First-order theorem provers (Vampire, E, Prover9, Lean) |
| Key Additional Step | None | **Skolemization + Unification** |
| Refutation Complete? | Yes | Yes (Robinson, 1965) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams visualize the **inference pipeline**, the **resolution refutation cycle**, and the **architectural comparison** between PL and FOL reasoning systems. All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed to avoid Mermaid reserved-keyword conflicts. Node labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generalized Inference Pipeline (PL and FOL)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START - INPUT KB AND QUERY Q]) --> B0[PARSE KB INTO SYMBOLIC SENTENCES]
    B0 --> C0{IS LOGIC FIRST ORDER}
    C0 -- NO --> D0[PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC BRANCH]
    C0 -- YES --> E0[FOL BRANCH]
    D0 --> D1[CONVERT EACH SENTENCE TO CNF]
    D1 --> D2[APPLY PL RESOLUTION ON LITERALS]
    E0 --> E1[ELIMINATE IMPLICATIONS AND BICONDITIONALS]
    E1 --> E2[MOVE NEGATION INWARDS]
    E2 --> E3[SKOLEMIZE EXISTENTIAL QUANTIFIERS]
    E3 --> E4[STANDARDIZE VARIABLES APART]
    E4 --> E5[DROP UNIVERSAL QUANTIFIERS]
    E5 --> E6[CONVERT TO CNF]
    E6 --> E7[APPLY UNIFICATION]
    D2 --> F0[COMBINE WITH NEGATED QUERY]
    E7 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[PERFORM RESOLUTION ON CLAUSE PAIRS]
    G0 --> H0{EMPTY CLAUSE DERIVED}
    H0 -- YES --> I0[KB ENTAILS Q - PROVED]
    H0 -- NO --> J0{ANY NEW CLAUSES FORMED}
    J0 -- YES --> G0
    J0 -- NO --> K0[CANNOT PROVE - RETURN FAILURE]
    I0 --> L0([END])
    K0 --> L0
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Note how the **FOL branch** introduces two extra preprocessing stages (Skolemization and Unification) before resolution can begin. This is the *only structural difference* in the algorithm — everything else is shared.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Resolution Refutation Cycle (Detailed Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[KB CLAUSES] --> S1[CANDIDATE PAIR SELECTION HEURISTIC]
    Q1[NEGATED QUERY IN CNF] --> S1
    S1 --> S2[UNIFY TWO CLAUSES]
    S2 --> S3{UNIFICATION SUCCESSFUL}
    S3 -- NO --> S4[DISCARD PAIR - TRY NEXT]
    S3 -- YES --> S5[IDENTIFY COMPLEMENTARY LITERALS]
    S5 --> S6[REMOVE COMPLEMENTARY LITERALS]
    S6 --> S7[COMBINE SURVIVING LITERALS INTO RESOLVENT]
    S7 --> R1{IS RESOLVENT EMPTY}
    R1 -- YES --> R2[REFUTATION SUCCESS - Q IS PROVEN]
    R1 -- NO --> R3[ADD RESOLVENT TO CLAUSE SET]
    R3 --> R4{MAX ITERATIONS REACHED}
    R4 -- NO --> S1
    R4 -- YES --> R5[TERMINATE - NO PROOF FOUND]
    S4 --> S1
    R2 --> END1([PROVED])
    R5 --> END2([UNPROVEN])
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — PL vs FOL Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROP[PROPOSITIONAL LOGIC ENGINE]
        P1A[KB OF ATOMIC PROPOSITIONS]
        P2A[CNF CONVERSION MODULE]
        P3A[PL RESOLUTION UNIT]
        P4A[OUTPUT - TRUE OR FALSE]
        P1A --> P2A
        P2A --> P3A
        P3A --> P4A
    end
    subgraph FOLL[FIRST ORDER LOGIC ENGINE]
        F1A[KB OF PREDICATES QUANTIFIERS AND TERMS]
        F2A[PREPROCESSOR - IMPLICATION AND NEGATION REMOVAL]
        F3A[SKOLEMIZATION MODULE]
        F4A[VARIABLE STANDARDIZATION]
        F5A[CNF CONVERSION MODULE]
        F6A[UNIFICATION ENGINE]
        F7A[FOL RESOLUTION UNIT]
        F8A[OUTPUT - GROUND OR QUANTIFIED CONCLUSION]
        F1A --> F2A
        F2A --> F3A
        F3A --> F4A
        F4A --> F5A
        F5A --> F6A
        F6A --> F7A
        F7A --> F8A
    end
    PROP -.UPGRADE BY ADDING.-> FOLL
```

> **Key takeaway from the comparison:** The FOL engine contains **all** the components of the PL engine, plus four extra preprocessing stages. This is *precisely* why FOL is more expressive but computationally more demanding.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — FOL to CNF Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[FOL SENTENCE INPUT] --> S1[STEP 1 - ELIMINATE IMPLICATIONS AND BICONDITIONALS]
    S1 --> S2[STEP 2 - MOVE NEGATION INWARDS USING DE MORGAN LAWS]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3 - SKOLEMIZE EXISTENTIAL VARIABLES]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4 - STANDARDIZE VARIABLES APART]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5 - DROP UNIVERSAL QUANTIFIERS]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6 - DISTRIBUTE OR OVER AND]
    S6 --> S7[CNF CLAUSE SET OUTPUT]
```

> This pipeline is **mandatory** before applying FOL resolution. Forgetting **Skolemization** is the single most common error KTU students make in CNF conversion.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** patterns. Part A questions carry 3 marks each. Part B questions are 14 marks each, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, and follow the KTU *Module Internal Choice* format (i.e., two alternative full-questions from which the student chooses one).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
> Differentiate between **Propositional Logic** and **First-Order Logic** with suitable examples. State one key limitation of propositional logic that first-order logic overcomes.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Propositional Logic | First-Order Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic Unit | Whole proposition (no internal structure) | Predicate applied to terms |
| Variables & Quantifiers | Absent | Present ($\forall$ and $\exists$) |
| Expressivity | Cannot represent general rules with variables | Can represent quantified general rules |
| Example | "Tom is a cat" → $C$ | "Every cat is a mammal" → $\forall x (Cat(x) \rightarrow Mammal(x))$ |

**Key limitation of PL overcome by FOL:** Propositional logic cannot compactly represent statements involving "for all" or "there exists". For example, to express "Every student in a class of 60 students reads a book" in PL, we would need 60 separate propositions ($S_1 \rightarrow R_1, S_2 \rightarrow R_2, \ldots, S_{60} \rightarrow R_{60}$). The same statement in FOL is a single quantified sentence: $\forall x (Student(x) \rightarrow \exists y \; Reads(x, y))$.

*(1 mark for each correct row of the table, 1 mark for the limitation example.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> What is **unification** in First-Order Logic? Explain with an example and state the **Occur Check** condition.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a substitution $\theta$ that makes two logical expressions (typically predicate applications) syntactically identical. The output of a successful unification is the **Most General Unifier (MGU)** — the simplest substitution that achieves the match.

**Example:** Unifying $P(x, f(y), b)$ with $P(a, f(z), b)$ yields the MGU $\theta = \{x/a,\; y/z\}$.

**Occur Check:** During unification, a variable $v$ must **never** be bound to a term that already contains $v$. For example, attempting to unify $P(x)$ with $P(f(x))$ must fail because binding $x/f(x)$ would create a circular term $f(f(f(\ldots)))$. This check prevents infinite recursion in Prolog-style systems. *(Modern systems sometimes omit the occur check for performance, but logically it is mandatory.)*

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark example, 1 mark occur check.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** The student must answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice within sub-parts is uncommon at KTU; full sub-parts must be answered.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 Choice A]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain **Forward Chaining** and **Backward Chaining** algorithms in First-Order Logic inference. Compare their data-flow direction, application domains, and termination behavior with examples.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Convert the following FOL sentences into **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)** step by step, and then apply **FOL resolution** to prove the query:
> - $F_1: \forall x \; (Doctor(x) \rightarrow \exists y \; (Patient(y) \wedge Treats(x, y)))$
> - $F_2: \forall x \forall y \; (Treats(x, y) \rightarrow Helps(x, y))$
> - $F_3: Doctor(DrAnu)$
> - **Query $Q$:** $\exists x \; Helps(DrAnu, x)$

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(a)

**Forward Chaining** is a *data-driven* inference algorithm that starts from the **known facts** in the knowledge base and repeatedly applies inference rules (typically Generalized Modus Ponens) to derive new facts until either the query is found or no new facts can be derived.

- **Data-flow direction:** Bottom-up (from atomic facts to conclusions).
- **Typical application:** Production systems, real-time monitoring, Rete-based expert systems (CLIPS, Jess).
- **Termination:** Guaranteed for Datalog (function-free, finite-domain FOL); may not terminate in general FOL.
- **Example:** Given $King(John)$, $\forall x (King(x) \rightarrow Person(x))$, forward chaining derives $Person(John)$.

**Backward Chaining** is a *goal-driven* inference algorithm that starts from the **query** and works backwards, attempting to find rules whose conclusions unify with the query, and recursively trying to prove their premises.

- **Data-flow direction:** Top-down (from query back to known facts).
- **Typical application:** Logic programming languages (Prolog), diagnostic systems, theorem proving with structured queries.
- **Termination:** Not guaranteed in full FOL; Prolog uses depth-bound search.
- **Example:** To prove $Person(John)$, backward chaining looks for rules with $Person(...)$ in the head, finds $King(x) \rightarrow Person(x)$, and then tries to prove $King(John)$.

**Comparison table (for the 7-mark answer):**

| Aspect | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Data-driven (bottom-up) | Goal-driven (top-down) |
| Starts from | Known facts | Query |
| Best for | Monitoring, planning | Query answering, diagnosis |
| Inference rule | Generalized Modus Ponens | Backward Generalized Modus Ponens |
| Inefficient when | Many irrelevant facts | Many irrelevant goals |
| Language example | CLIPS, production systems | Prolog |
| Completeness (Datalog) | Yes | Yes |
| Termination (general FOL) | Not guaranteed | Not guaranteed |

*(3 marks for forward chaining explanation with example, 3 marks for backward chaining explanation with example, 1 mark for the comparison table or summary.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B-A(b)

**Step 1 — Eliminate Implications.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Doctor(x) \vee \exists y \; (Patient(y) \wedge Treats(x, y))) \\
F_2: \quad \forall x \forall y \; (\neg Treats(x, y) \vee Helps(x, y)) \\
F_3: \quad Doctor(DrAnu) \\
\neg Q: \quad \neg \exists x \; Helps(DrAnu, x)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $A \rightarrow B \equiv \neg A \vee B$ and the negated query $\neg \exists x \ldots \equiv \forall x \neg \ldots$

**Step 2 — Move Negation Inwards (only on $\neg Q$).**

$$
\neg Q: \quad \forall x \; \neg Helps(DrAnu, x)
$$

**Step 3 — Skolemize $F_1$.**
The existential variable $y$ is inside the scope of the universal $x$. Replace $y$ with a **Skolem function** $f(x)$ that depends on $x$:

$$
F_1: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Doctor(x) \vee (Patient(f(x)) \wedge Treats(x, f(x))))
$$

> **Logic:** Skolemization preserves satisfiability; the function $f(x)$ represents *some* patient that $x$ treats, depending on $x$.

**Step 4 — Standardize Variables and Distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$.**
In $F_1$, distribute to separate the conjunction:

$$
F_1: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Doctor(x) \vee Patient(f(x))) \;\; \wedge \;\; \forall x \; (\neg Doctor(x) \vee Treats(x, f(x)))
$$

**Step 5 — Drop Universal Quantifiers and Form CNF Clauses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1: \quad \neg Doctor(x) \vee Patient(f(x)) \\
C_2: \quad \neg Doctor(x) \vee Treats(x, f(x)) \\
C_3: \quad \neg Treats(u, v) \vee Helps(u, v) \\
C_4: \quad Doctor(DrAnu) \\
C_5: \quad \neg Helps(DrAnu, w)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Variables $x, u, v, w$ are standardized apart.)*

**Step 6 — Perform FOL Resolution.**

*Resolution 1: Unify $C_2$ and $C_4$ with $\theta = \{x/DrAnu\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2\theta &= \neg Doctor(DrAnu) \vee Treats(DrAnu, f(DrAnu)) \\
C_4 &= Doctor(DrAnu) \\
\hline
C_6 &= Treats(DrAnu, f(DrAnu))
\end{aligned}
$$

*Resolution 2: Unify $C_3$ with $C_6$ using $\theta = \{u/DrAnu,\; v/f(DrAnu)\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3\theta &= \neg Treats(DrAnu, f(DrAnu)) \vee Helps(DrAnu, f(DrAnu)) \\
C_6 &= Treats(DrAnu, f(DrAnu)) \\
\hline
C_7 &= Helps(DrAnu, f(DrAnu))
\end{aligned}
$$

*Resolution 3: Unify $C_5$ with $C_7$ using $\theta = \{w/f(DrAnu)\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_5\theta &= \neg Helps(DrAnu, f(DrAnu)) \\
C_7 &= Helps(DrAnu, f(DrAnu)) \\
\hline
C_8 &= \square \quad (\text{empty clause})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The empty clause is derived, so the KB is unsatisfiable together with $\neg Q$. Therefore:

$$
\therefore KB \models \exists x \; Helps(DrAnu, x) \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Sub-part b):**
> 1. **Skolemization error:** Replacing $\exists y$ inside $\forall x$ with a Skolem *constant* instead of a Skolem *function* $f(x)$ loses the dependence on $x$. This is the #1 error and costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to negate the query:** Resolution is a *refutation* procedure. Students who try to derive the *positive* query directly (without $\neg Q$) will fail the proof and lose all 7 marks in part (b).
> 3. **Distribution omission:** Forgetting to distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$ in $F_1$ leaves a non-CNF clause, and the resolution will not work. Cost: 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not standardizing variables apart:** Reusing the same variable name in $C_1$ and $C_3$ causes incorrect unification. Cost: 1 mark.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3 Choice B]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **limitations of Propositional Logic** that motivate the use of First-Order Logic. Give two concrete examples to illustrate how FOL overcomes each limitation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Given the knowledge base:
> - $F_1: \forall x \; (Loves(x, Science) \rightarrow \exists y \; (Subject(y) \wedge Studies(x, y)))$
> - $F_2: \forall x \; (Student(x) \rightarrow Loves(x, Science))$
> - $F_3: Student(Riya)$
> - **Query $Q$:** $\exists z \; (Subject(z) \wedge Studies(Riya, z))$
>
> Perform **Skolemization**, **Unification**, and **Resolution** step by step to prove the query.

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(a)

Propositional logic, despite its simplicity and decidability, suffers from **four fundamental limitations** that restrict its usefulness in real-world AI:

**Limitation 1 — No Object-Level Representation (No Variables):**
Propositional logic cannot express relationships between objects or talk about *classes* of objects compactly. Every individual fact must be enumerated as a separate proposition. For example, "All humans are mortal" in PL requires $n$ separate implications for $n$ humans. **FOL solution:** A single quantified sentence $\forall x (Human(x) \rightarrow Mortal(x))$ captures the general rule.

**Limitation 2 — No Quantifiers:**
Statements involving "for all" or "there exists" cannot be written in PL. To express "Every student reads some book", PL needs a separate proposition for every student-book pair. **FOL solution:** $\forall x (Student(x) \rightarrow \exists y \; (Book(y) \wedge Reads(x, y)))$ — one sentence covers the entire domain.

**Limitation 3 — No Internal Structure of Propositions:**
A PL atom is opaque — `P` and `Q` are just labels. We cannot reason about *what* the proposition is about. **FOL solution:** Predicates like $Loves(John, Mary)$ expose the arguments, enabling relational reasoning.

**Limitation 4 — Limited Expressivity (Locality of Reasoning):**
In PL, inference is limited to the propositions explicitly in the KB; there is no mechanism to infer new objects or new relationships. **FOL solution:** The introduction of **function symbols** and **Skolem terms** allows the inference engine to *construct* new objects on demand (e.g., the father of $x$, the sum of $x$ and $y$).

**Summary Table:**

| Limitation of PL | FOL's Solution |
|---|---|
| No variables | Variables + Universal Quantifier |
| No quantifiers | $\forall$ and $\exists$ operators |
| Atomic, unanalysed propositions | Predicates with argument structure |
| No object construction | Function symbols and Skolem terms |
| No relation between facts | Predicate application enables relational reasoning |

*(2 marks each for the first two limitations with examples, 1 mark for the summary, 2 marks for the remaining two limitations with brief examples.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B-B(b)

**Step 1 — Eliminate Implications.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Loves(x, Science) \vee \exists y \; (Subject(y) \wedge Studies(x, y))) \\
F_2: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Student(x) \vee Loves(x, Science)) \\
F_3: \quad Student(Riya) \\
\neg Q: \quad \forall z \; (\neg Subject(z) \vee \neg Studies(Riya, z))
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $A \rightarrow B \equiv \neg A \vee B$; $\neg \exists z \ldots \equiv \forall z \neg \ldots$

**Step 2 — Skolemize $F_1$.**
The existential $y$ lies within the scope of the universal $x$. Replace with a Skolem function $g(x)$:

$$
F_1: \quad \forall x \; (\neg Loves(x, Science) \vee (Subject(g(x)) \wedge Studies(x, g(x))))
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$ to Form CNF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1: \quad \neg Loves(x, Science) \vee Subject(g(x)) \\
C_2: \quad \neg Loves(x, Science) \vee Studies(x, g(x)) \\
C_3: \quad \neg Student(u) \vee Loves(u, Science) \\
C_4: \quad Student(Riya) \\
C_5: \quad \neg Subject(v) \vee \neg Studies(Riya, v)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Variables standardized apart: $x, u, v$ are all distinct.)*

**Step 4 — FOL Resolution.**

*Resolution 1: Resolve $C_3$ and $C_4$ with $\theta = \{u/Riya\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3\theta &= \neg Student(Riya) \vee Loves(Riya, Science) \\
C_4 &= Student(Riya) \\
\hline
C_6 &= Loves(Riya, Science)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Resolution 2: Resolve $C_2$ and $C_6$ with $\theta = \{x/Riya\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2\theta &= \neg Loves(Riya, Science) \vee Studies(Riya, g(Riya)) \\
C_6 &= Loves(Riya, Science) \\
\hline
C_7 &= Studies(Riya, g(Riya))
\end{aligned}
$$

*Resolution 3: Resolve $C_1$ and $C_6$ with $\theta = \{x/Riya\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
