C_1\theta &= \neg Loves(Riya, Science) \vee Subject(g(Riya)) \\
C_6 &= Loves(Riya, Science) \\
\hline
C_8 &= Subject(g(Riya))
\end{aligned}
$$

*Resolution 4: Resolve $C_5$ and $C_7$ with $\theta = \{v/g(Riya)\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_5\theta &= \neg Subject(g(Riya)) \vee \neg Studies(Riya, g(Riya)) \\
C_7 &= Studies(Riya, g(Riya)) \\
\hline
C_9 &= \neg Subject(g(Riya))
\end{aligned}
$$

*Resolution 5: Resolve $C_8$ and $C_9$ with $\theta = \{\}$.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_8 &= Subject(g(Riya)) \\
C_9 &= \neg Subject(g(Riya)) \\
\hline
C_{10} &= \square \quad (\text{empty clause})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Refutation achieved. Hence:

$$
\therefore KB \models \exists z \; (Subject(z) \wedge Studies(Riya, z)) \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Sub-part b):**
> 1. **Wrong Skolemization scope:** Students often Skolemize $y$ as a constant (not $g(x)$) because they ignore the enclosing $\forall x$. Always check the *scope* of the existential.
> 2. **Incomplete CNF distribution:** If $F_1$ is left as one big clause, the resolution will stall. Distribute and split into separate clauses *before* resolution.
> 3. **Confusing the negated query:** $\neg \exists z (Subject(z) \wedge Studies(Riya, z))$ must become $\forall z (\neg Subject(z) \vee \neg Studies(Riya, z))$ using **De Morgan's Law on quantifiers**, not by negating only one part.
> 4. **Missing unification substitutions:** Every resolution step in FOL must show the unifying substitution $\theta$ explicitly. Steps without $\theta$ lose 1 mark each.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Warning for This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Frequent Marks-Loss Zones Across the Whole Module:**
> 1. **Mixing up Modus Ponens with Resolution:** Modus Ponens needs both the conditional and the antecedent explicitly. Resolution works on disjunctions. Students who try to apply MP directly to CNF clauses are penalized.
> 2. **Confusing Herbrand Universe with the Domain of Discourse:** The Herbrand universe is *all syntactically constructible ground terms*, not the actual semantic domain. Conflating the two leads to incorrect complexity estimates.
> 3. **Forgetting that Resolution requires CNF:** Resolution *only* operates on clauses in CNF. Any application of resolution to an implication-form sentence is invalid.
> 4. **Believing FOL is decidable:** It is **semi-decidable**. Writing "FOL is decidable" in an exam costs 1–2 marks immediately.
> 5. **Omitting the Negation of the Query:** In refutation proofs, the query *must* be negated and added to the KB. A "proof" without this step is structurally invalid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before the Exam):**

- **Propositional Logic** = whole propositions + connectives; no variables, no quantifiers, no functions.
- **First-Order Logic** = PL + predicates, functions, variables, $\forall$ and $\exists$ quantifiers.
- **Decidability:** PL is **decidable**; FOL is **semi-decidable**.
- **Expressivity:** FOL is strictly more expressive than PL.
- **Inference in PL:** Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Resolution (refutation complete), truth-table enumeration.
- **Inference in FOL:** Universal/Existential Elimination, Generalized Modus Ponens, **Unification** + **Resolution**.
- **Unification** finds the MGU (Most General Unifier) that makes two expressions identical; always apply the **Occur Check**.
- **CNF Conversion in FOL (6 steps):** Eliminate $\rightarrow$/$\leftrightarrow$ → Move $\neg$ inwards → **Skolemize** → Standardize variables → Drop $\forall$ → Distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$.
- **Skolemization Rule:** Replace $\exists y$ with a Skolem constant $c$ if there are no enclosing universals; otherwise with a Skolem function $f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ of the enclosing universal variables.
- **Refutation Procedure:** Negate the query, add to KB, convert everything to CNF, repeatedly apply resolution, hope for the **empty clause** $\square$.
- **Lifting Lemma:** A lifted (quantified) inference rule is sound iff its ground (propositional) instance is sound.
- **Forward Chaining** = data-driven, bottom-up; **Backward Chaining** = goal-driven, top-down; both use GMP in FOL.
- **Real-world FOL systems:** Prolog, Vampire, E-Prover, Isabelle, Lean, Coq.
- **Real-world PL systems:** SAT solvers (z3, MiniSAT, CryptoMiniSAT), digital circuit design, BDD libraries.
- **KTU Board Buzzwords to use:** *Refutation complete*, *sound*, *semi-decidable*, *Skolem function*, *Most General Unifier*, *Herbrand universe*, *Lifting lemma*, *Generalized Modus Ponens*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note:** Whenever a KTU question asks you to *"prove that $P$ follows from the KB"*, the answer is **always a refutation proof**: add $\neg P$ to the KB, convert to CNF, and resolve until you get the empty clause. This single pattern appears in roughly **70% of FOL inference questions** in KTU papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unification and Lifts Forward Chaining
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Unification and Lifts Forward Chaining

## 1. Unification

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unification** is the algorithmic process of finding a **substitution** $\sigma$ that makes two first-order logical expressions (terms, literals, or clauses) **syntactically identical** when applied simultaneously to both expressions.

In the context of First-Order Logic (FOL), unification is the backbone of:
- **Resolution-based theorem proving**
- **Logic programming languages** (Prolog, Datalog)
- **Production rule systems** using first-order rules
- **Semantic parsing** and natural language understanding

A **substitution** $\sigma$ is a finite mapping from variables to terms:

$$\sigma = \{x_1/t_1, x_2/t_2, \dots, x_n/t_n\}$$

where each $x_i$ is a variable and $t_i$ is a term (which may itself contain variables).

The application of $\sigma$ to an expression $\phi$ is denoted $\phi\sigma$ (postfix notation). The expression $p$ **unifies** with $q$ if there exists a substitution $\sigma$ such that $p\sigma = q\sigma$.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Substitution ($\sigma$)** | Mapping from variables to terms | $\{x/\text{Cat}, y/\text{Mammal}\}$ |
| **Unifier** | Any substitution that unifies two expressions | $\sigma = \{x/A, y/B\}$ |
| **Most General Unifier (MGU)** | The simplest, least restrictive unifier | $\sigma = \{x/A\}$ instead of $\{x/A, y/A, z/A\}$ |
| **Composition ($\sigma_1 \circ \sigma_2$)** | Apply $\sigma_1$ then $\sigma_2$ | See Section 2.3 |
| **Occur Check** | Verifies no variable binds to a term containing itself | $x$ cannot map to $f(x)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently ask for the **Unification Algorithm (Robinson, 1965)** and the **MGU** definition. The MGU is not unique but is unique up to variable renaming (i.e., variants).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Jigsaw Puzzle Solver"

Imagine two hand-written address forms:
- **Form A:** *"Mr. _____ lives at House No. _____, Street _____"*
- **Form B:** *"Mr. Sharma lives at House No. 14, Street MG Road"*

**Unification** is the process of discovering that:
- `_____` (first blank) in A = "Sharma" → so $x = \text{Sharma}$
- `_____` (second blank) in A = 14 → so $y = 14$
- `_____` (third blank) in A = "MG Road" → so $z = \text{MG Road}$

After applying substitution $\sigma = \{x/\text{Sharma}, y/14, z/\text{MG Road}\}$, both forms look identical. The **MGU** is the **shortest, most reusable** mapping — you don't overspecify (e.g., binding more variables than needed).

### 1.4 Why Unification Matters in AI

- It enables **pattern matching** between rules and known facts
- It allows **variable binding** during inference
- It is the core mechanism behind Prolog's execution
- It powers **Horn-clause resolution** and forward-chaining in FOL

---

## 2. Lifts Forward Chaining

### 2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lifts Forward Chaining** is the first-order logic generalization of the propositional forward-chaining algorithm. It derives new atomic sentences by triggering first-order definite clauses whenever there is a substitution that makes the rule's premises match known facts in the knowledge base.

A **definite clause** in FOL is a Horn clause of the form:

$$\forall \bar{x}: P_1(\bar{x}) \land P_2(\bar{x}) \land \dots \land P_n(\bar{x}) \Rightarrow Q(\bar{x})$$

The "lifts" terminology refers to the fact that we are **lifting** a propositional algorithm to the first-order level, by introducing variables and unification.

### 2.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Medical Diagnosis Chain"

Imagine a junior doctor in a hospital:
- **Known facts (KB):** "Patient X has fever", "Patient X has cough", "Patient X is 65 years old"
- **Rule 1:** If a patient has fever AND cough AND is elderly, then they have pneumonia risk.
- **Rule 2:** If a patient has pneumonia risk, then order a chest X-ray.

The doctor (forward chaining engine) **chains forward**:
1. Match Rule 1's premises with KB facts using **unification** (binding "patient" to "X")
2. Conclude: "Patient X has pneumonia risk"
3. Match Rule 2's premise with this new fact
4. Conclude: "Order chest X-ray for Patient X"

The **lift** is the realization that the rules and facts are *parameterized* by variables (patient, age, etc.) — the system works for **any** patient, not just Patient X.

---

## 3. Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Arity** of a predicate: the number of arguments it takes (e.g., $Arity(\text{Likes}) = 2$).
- **Substitution size**: number of variable-term pairs $|\sigma|$.
- **Clause length**: number of literals in the body of a definite clause.
- **Time complexity of Robinson's Unification**: $O(n \cdot g)$ where $n$ = term size, $g$ = number of distinct variables.
- **Occur check cost**: $O(n)$ per unification call.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard implementations (e.g., SWI-Prolog, XSB) use **rational tree unification** with **union-find** to achieve near-linear amortized complexity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 1. The Unification Algorithm — Robinson (1965)

The classical unification algorithm operates on two expressions (terms) $p$ and $q$ and returns either their **MGU** or a **FAIL** signal.

### 1.1 Operational Steps

The algorithm maintains:
- **Input:** A set of equation pairs $E = \{p_1 \doteq q_1, p_2 \doteq q_2, \dots\}$
- **Output:** Either substitution $\sigma$ (the MGU) or FAIL
- **Termination:** When $E$ is empty (success) or when the transformation rule fails

The algorithm applies the following **transformation rules** in any order until no rule applies:

| Rule | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Delete** | $t \doteq t$ | Remove the equation |
| **Swap** | $f(s_1,\dots,s_n) \doteq g(t_1,\dots,t_m)$ where $f \neq g$ or $n \neq m$ | **FAIL** |
| **Decompose** | $f(s_1,\dots,s_n) \doteq f(t_1,\dots,t_n)$ | Replace with $\{s_1 \doteq t_1, \dots, s_n \doteq t_n\}$ |
| **Orient (Check)** | $x \doteq f(\dots)$ where $x$ is a variable, not occurring in $f(\dots)$ | Replace with $f(\dots) \doteq x$ and add $\{x/f(\dots)\}$ to $\sigma$ |
| **Occurs Check** | $x \doteq f(\dots)$ where $x$ occurs in $f(\dots)$ | **FAIL** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Occurs Check** prevents infinite loops: a variable cannot be bound to a term that contains that same variable. For example, $x \doteq f(x)$ is rejected because binding $x$ to $f(x)$ would lead to $x = f(x) = f(f(x)) = \dots$, a circular definition.

### 1.2 Worked Algorithm Walkthrough

**Problem:** Unify $p(x, f(y), z)$ with $p(a, f(b), g(w))$.

| Step | Equation Set $E$ | Substitution $\sigma$ | Rule Applied |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | $\{p(x,f(y),z) \doteq p(a,f(b),g(w))\}$ | $\emptyset$ | Start |
| 1 | $\{x \doteq a, \; f(y) \doteq f(b), \; z \doteq g(w)\}$ | $\emptyset$ | Decompose |
| 2 | $\{f(y) \doteq f(b), \; z \doteq g(w)\}$ | $\{x/a\}$ | Orient (x/a) |
| 3 | $\{y \doteq b, \; z \doteq g(w)\}$ | $\{x/a\}$ | Decompose |
| 4 | $\{z \doteq g(w)\}$ | $\{x/a, y/b\}$ | Orient (y/b) |
| 5 | $\emptyset$ | $\{x/a, y/b, z/g(w)\}$ | Orient (z/g(w)) |
| Done | $\emptyset$ | $\{x/a, y/b, z/g(w)\}$ | Delete |

**Result:** The MGU is $\sigma = \{x/a, y/b, z/g(w)\}$.

---

## 2. Substitution Composition

When applying substitutions sequentially, we use **composition**:

$$(\sigma_1 \circ \sigma_2)(x) = \sigma_1(\sigma_2(x))$$

This is read as: *"apply $\sigma_2$ first, then $\sigma_1$."*

**Example:**

$$\sigma_1 = \{x/f(y), y/z\}, \quad \sigma_2 = \{y/a\}$$

$$\sigma_1 \circ \sigma_2 = \{x/f(a), y/z, y/a\} \text{ (after combining)}$$

---

## 3. KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Complexity / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Substitution application | $\phi\sigma$ | Apply $\sigma$ to every variable in $\phi$ |
| Composition | $(\sigma_1 \circ \sigma_2)(v) = \sigma_1(\sigma_2(v))$ | Apply $\sigma_2$ first, then $\sigma_1$ |
| Unification existence | $p$ and $q$ unify iff $\exists \sigma: p\sigma = q\sigma$ | Decidable |
| MGU uniqueness | MGU is unique up to variable renaming | Variants are equivalent |
| Occurs check condition | $x \in \text{Vars}(t)$ for binding $x \mapsto t$ | Required for correctness |
| Robinson's Unification | $O(n)$ per pair (with occurs check) | $n$ = term size |
| Most General Unifier (MGU) | $\sigma$ such that any other unifier $\theta$ satisfies $\theta = \sigma \circ \tau$ for some $\tau$ | Canonical form |
| Forward Chaining Rule | $\bigwedge_i P_i(\bar{x}) \Rightarrow Q(\bar{x})$ | FOL definite clause |
| Lifts FC correctness | $KB \vdash \phi \iff KB \models \phi$ | For Datalog (function-free) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not use $\vert$ (vertical bar) in KTU formula tables — it breaks the markdown pipe structure. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ instead.

---

## 4. Lifts Forward Chaining — Algorithm

### 4.1 Why "Lifts"?

The propositional forward-chaining algorithm deals with ground (variable-free) atomic sentences. To extend it to first-order logic, we:

1. **Lift** each propositional rule to a first-order rule (by adding variables)
2. **Use unification** to match rule premises with known facts
3. **Apply the unifier** to derive the new fact

### 4.2 The Lifted Forward Chaining Algorithm

```
Input:  KB = { first-order definite clauses }
        Query (optional, for entailment test)
Output: New facts derived
```

**Procedure:**

1. **Initialize** `count[c] = number of premises in clause c`
2. **Initialize** `inferred[s] = FALSE` for all symbols $s$
3. **Initialize** `Agenda = queue of known ground facts`
4. **Repeat** until Agenda is empty:
   - $p \leftarrow$ POP(Agenda)
   - If $p$ matches the query, return TRUE
   - If $inferred[p]$, continue
   - Mark $inferred[p] \leftarrow$ TRUE
   - For each rule $c$ in KB where $p$ unifies with a premise of $c$:
     - Decrement $count[c]$
     - If $count[c] = 0$ (all premises satisfied):
       - $p' \leftarrow$ HEAD($c$) with the unifying substitution applied
       - PUSH($p'$, Agenda)
5. **Return** FALSE (query cannot be inferred)

### 4.3 The Three Conceptual Lifts

| Step | Propositional Version | First-Order ("Lifted") Version |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern matching | Exact match of atomic symbol | **Unification** of predicate expressions |
| Rule firing | Boolean conjunction of ground atoms | Conjunction under substitution $\sigma$ |
| Fact generation | New ground atom | New atom with $\sigma$ applied |

### 4.4 Properties of Lifts Forward Chaining

| Property | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Soundness** | ✓ Yes | Every derived fact is entailed by KB |
| **Completeness (Datalog)** | ✓ Yes | For function-free, finite-domain FOL |
| **Completeness (full FOL)** | ✗ No | With function symbols, may not halt |
| **Termination (Datalog)** | ✓ Yes | Finite number of ground facts |
| **Termination (full FOL)** | ✗ No | May generate infinitely many facts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Datalog** is a restricted subset of FOL where rules are function-free definite clauses, guaranteeing termination. Lifts FC is **complete for Datalog** but only **semi-decidable** for full FOL.

### 4.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deductive databases** (LogicBlox, Datomic) use lifted FC
- **Production rule engines** (Drools, CLIPS) apply this principle
- **Cyc** knowledge base uses lifted reasoning
- **OWL 2 RL** reasoning (a W3C standard) is essentially lifted FC
- **ETL pipelines** in data engineering: rule-based data transformation

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations and Worked Examples

## 1. Exhaustive Unification Walkthrough

### Example 1: Multiple Variables, Nested Functions

**Given:** Unify $E_1 = P(x, g(y), f(g(z)))$ and $E_2 = P(a, g(h(b)), f(w))$.

We will apply Robinson's algorithm **step by step** with full detail.

**Initial State:**

$$E = \{P(x, g(y), f(g(z))) \doteq P(a, g(h(b)), f(w))\}$$

$$\sigma = \emptyset$$

**Step 1 — Decompose (same predicate symbol $P$ with arity 3):**

Replace the equation with one equation per argument:

$$E = \{x \doteq a, \; g(y) \doteq g(h(b)), \; f(g(z)) \doteq f(w)\}$$

**Step 2 — Orient (variable on left, term on right):**

$x$ is a variable, $a$ is a ground term. Add $\{x/a\}$ to $\sigma$:

$$E = \{g(y) \doteq g(h(b)), \; f(g(z)) \doteq f(w)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{x/a\}$$

**Step 3 — Decompose (same function symbol $g$, arity 1):**

$$E = \{y \doteq h(b), \; f(g(z)) \doteq f(w)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{x/a\}$$

**Step 4 — Orient (variable $y$ on left):**

$$E = \{f(g(z)) \doteq f(w)\}$$
$$\sigma = \{x/a, \; y/h(b)\}$$

**Step 5 — Decompose (same function $f$, arity 1):**

$$E = \{g(z) \doteq w\}$$
$$\sigma = \{x/a, \; y/h(b)\}$$

**Step 6 — Apply substitution propagation:**

Before orienting, we must check whether $g(z)$ or $w$ is a variable. Here $w$ **is a variable**, so we orient:

$$E = \emptyset$$
$$\sigma = \{x/a, \; y/h(b), \; w/g(z)\}$$

**Final MGU:**

$$\sigma = \{x \mapsto a, \; y \mapsto h(b), \; w \mapsto g(z)\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that $z$ is **not** bound — it remains a free variable. The MGU is the most general because it preserves as much variability as possible.

---

### Example 2: Failure Cases (Unification Impossible)

**Case A — Different predicate symbols:**

Unify $E_1 = P(x, y)$ with $E_2 = Q(x, y)$.

After decomposition, $P \neq Q$ → **FAIL**.

**Case B — Different arities:**

Unify $E_1 = P(x, y, z)$ with $E_2 = P(a, b)$.

Arity mismatch ($3 \neq 2$) → **FAIL**.

**Case C — Occurs check violation:**

Unify $E_1 = P(x)$ with $E_2 = P(f(x))$.

$$E = \{P(x) \doteq P(f(x))\}$$

Decompose:

$$E = \{x \doteq f(x)\}$$

Apply occurs check: variable $x$ occurs in term $f(x)$ → **FAIL**.

---

## 2. Lifts Forward Chaining — Complete Derivation

### Problem Statement

Given the knowledge base:

| # | Clause (FOL Definite Clause) |
|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{King}(x) \land \text{Greedy}(x) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(x)$ |
| 2 | $\text{King}(\text{John})$ |
| 3 | $\text{Greedy}(y)$ |
| 4 | $\text{Evil}(z) \Rightarrow \text{Punish}(z)$ |

**Query:** $\text{Punish}(\text{John})$ — is John to be punished?

### Step-by-Step Lifts Forward Chaining

**Initial Knowledge Base (ground facts):**

$$\text{Facts} = \{\text{King}(\text{John}), \; \text{Greedy}(y)\}$$

> Note: $\text{Greedy}(y)$ here is treated as a universally quantified ground fact. In strict FOL, we would have $\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$, $\text{Greedy}(\text{Mary})$, etc.

**Agenda (initial):**

$$\text{Agenda} = [\text{King}(\text{John}), \; \text{Greedy}(\text{John})]$$

> We assume $\text{Greedy}(\text{John})$ is known (instantiation of $y$).

---

**Iteration 1: POP** $p = \text{King}(\text{John})$.

- Try to unify $p$ with any rule premise.
- Match $p$ with $\text{King}(x)$ in Rule 1.
- **Unification:** $\text{King}(\text{John}) \doteq \text{King}(x)$ → $\sigma_1 = \{x/\text{John}\}$.
- Apply $\sigma_1$ to Rule 1:
  $$\text{King}(\text{John}) \land \text{Greedy}(\text{John}) \Rightarrow \text{Evil}(\text{John})$$
- One premise of Rule 1 satisfied: decrement $count[\text{Rule 1}]$ from $2$ to $1$.

---

**Iteration 2: POP** $p = \text{Greedy}(\text{John})$.

- Match $p$ with $\text{Greedy}(x)$ in Rule 1 (with $\sigma_1$).
- Unification: $\text{Greedy}(\text{John}) \doteq \text{Greedy}(x)$ → $\sigma_1' = \{x/\text{John}\}$ (consistent).
- All premises of Rule 1 satisfied: $count[\text{Rule 1}] = 0$.
- **Fire Rule 1:** Derive $\text{Evil}(\text{John})$ with $\sigma = \{x/\text{John}\}$.
- PUSH $\text{Evil}(\text{John})$ onto Agenda.

---

**Iteration 3: POP** $p = \text{Evil}(\text{John})$.

- Match $p$ with $\text{Evil}(z)$ in Rule 4.
- **Unification:** $\text{Evil}(\text{John}) \doteq \text{Evil}(z)$ → $\sigma_2 = \{z/\text{John}\}$.
- All premises of Rule 4 satisfied (only one premise).
- **Fire Rule 4:** Derive $\text{Punish}(\text{John})$.
- PUSH $\text{Punish}(\text{John})$ onto Agenda.

---

**Iteration 4: POP** $p = \text{Punish}(\text{John})$.

- $p$ matches the **query** $\text{Punish}(\text{John})$.
- **Return TRUE** — the query is entailed.

### Final Derivation Tree

```
King(John)              Greedy(John)
      \                       /
       \   σ₁ = {x/John}    /
        ──────[RULE 1]──────
              |
              v
           Evil(John)
              |
              | σ₂ = {z/John}
              |
        ──────[RULE 4]──────
              |
              v
         Punish(John)   ← Query Answered
```

### Mathematical Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{KB} &\models \text{Punish}(\text{John}) \\
&\text{via} \; \sigma_1 = \{x/\text{John}\}, \; \sigma_2 = \{z/\text{John}\} \\
&\text{using definite clauses R1, R4}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3. Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Propositional FC | Lifts FC (First-Order) |
|---|---|---|
| Match mechanism | Boolean equality | **Unification** + substitution |
| Variables | None | Universally quantified |
| Inference rule | Modus ponens (ground) | Modus ponens + substitution |
| Complexity | Polynomial | **Semi-decidable** (undecidable in general) |
| Termination | Always | Only for function-free (Datalog) |
| Expressiveness | Limited | **Turing-complete** (with function symbols) |
| Soundness | ✓ | ✓ |
| Completeness | ✓ (finite KB) | ✓ for Datalog, ✗ for full FOL |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 1. Unification Algorithm Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two terms p and q] --> B[Initialize equation set E and sigma empty]
    B --> C{Is E empty?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Return sigma as MGU]
    C -- No --> E[Pick first equation from E]
    E --> F{Equation form check}
    F -- t equals t --> G[Delete: Remove equation] --> C
    F -- f of s equals g of t with f not equals g or arity mismatch --> H[Return FAIL]
    F -- f of s1..sn equals f of t1..tn --> I[Decompose: Split into n equations] --> C
    F -- x equals t where x is variable and t is not --> J{Occurs check: x in vars of t?}
    F -- t equals x where x is variable --> K[Orient and add to sigma] --> C
    J -- Yes --> H
    J -- No --> L[Add binding x maps to t to sigma] --> C
    F -- x equals f with x in f --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart implements the transformation rules of Robinson's unification algorithm. The **occurs check** branch is the most common source of failure in FOL unification.

---

## 2. Lifts Forward Chaining Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KB[(Knowledge Base)]
    subgraph "Lifts FC Engine"
        A[Agenda Queue]
        B[Rule Index]
        C[Unification Module]
        D[Substitution Applier]
        E[Count Tracker]
        F[Inferred Set]
    end
    Q[Query]
    Out[Answer]

    KB --> B
    KB --> A
    A --> P[Pop Fact p]
    P --> C
    B --> C
    C --> S1{Unification success?}
    S1 -- No --> A
    S1 -- Yes --> D
    D --> E
    E --> C2{All premises satisfied?}
    C2 -- No --> A
    C2 -- Yes --> G[Generate new fact with substitution]
    G --> M{p equals Query?}
    M -- Yes --> Out[Return TRUE]
    M -- No --> F
    F --> A
    A --> End{Agenda empty?}
    End -- Yes --> Out2[Return FALSE]
    End -- No --> P
```

---

## 3. Knowledge Base State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initial: Load KB facts
    Initial --> Processing: Pop fact from agenda
    Processing --> Unifying: Try to unify with rule premises
    Unifying --> Failed: No unifier found
    Unifying --> Matched: Unifier found
    Matched --> Counting: Decrement count for that rule
    Counting --> Waiting: Count greater than zero
    Waiting --> Processing: Pop next fact
    Counting --> Firing: Count equals zero
    Firing --> Derived: New fact with substitution
    Derived --> CheckQuery: Compare with query
    CheckQuery --> Answered: Match found
    CheckQuery --> Enqueued: No match, push to agenda
    Answered --> [*]
    Enqueued --> Processing
    Failed --> Processing
    Processing --> Terminated: Agenda empty
    Terminated --> [*]
```

---

## 4. Derivation Tree for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fact: King John] --> R1
    A2[Fact: Greedy John] --> R1
    R1[Rule 1: King x and Greedy x implies Evil x] --> A3[Derived: Evil John]
    A3 --> R4
    R4[Rule 4: Evil z implies Punish z] --> A4[Derived: Punish John]
    A4 --> Q[Query Matched]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Define unification. What is the most general unifier (MGU)? Apply the unification algorithm to find the MGU of:**
- $P(x, f(y), z)$
- $P(a, f(g(b)), h(w))$

**Model Answer:**

**Unification:** Unification is the process of finding a substitution $\sigma$ that, when applied to two logical expressions, makes them syntactically identical.

**MGU:** The Most General Unifier is the simplest (least restrictive) substitution that unifies two expressions. It is unique up to variable renaming.

**Working:**

| Step | Equation Set | Substitution |
|---|---|---|
| Initial | $\{P(x,f(y),z) \doteq P(a,f(g(b)),h(w))\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| Decompose | $\{x \doteq a, f(y) \doteq f(g(b)), z \doteq h(w)\}$ | $\emptyset$ |
| Orient $x$ | $\{f(y) \doteq f(g(b)), z \doteq h(w)\}$ | $\{x/a\}$ |
| Decompose $f$ | $\{y \doteq g(b), z \doteq h(w)\}$ | $\{x/a\}$ |
| Orient $y$ | $\{z \doteq h(w)\}$ | $\{x/a, y/g(b)\}$ |
| Orient $z$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{x/a, y/g(b), z/h(w)\}$ |

**MGU:** $\sigma = \{x/a, \; y/g(b), \; z/h(w)\}$ — **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for MGU definition + 1 for the working)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**What is the "occurs check" in unification? Why is it necessary? Illustrate with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Occurs Check:** A variable $x$ cannot be unified with a term $t$ if $x$ occurs anywhere inside $t$.

**Necessity:** Without the occurs check, we could create circular substitutions like $x = f(x) = f(f(x)) = \dots$, leading to an infinite regress and incorrect unification results. It guarantees the substitution forms a **well-founded tree**.

**Example:** Unifying $x$ with $f(x)$:

$$\{x \doteq f(x)\} \rightarrow \text{occurs}(x, f(x)) = \text{TRUE} \rightarrow \text{FAIL}$$

Hence $x$ and $f(x)$ do **not unify**.

Without occurs check, the algorithm would yield $\sigma = \{x/f(x)\}$, which is **inconsistent** because there is no finite value of $x$ satisfying the equation.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark necessity + 1 mark example.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Unification Mastery

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Robinson's Unification Algorithm** in detail. List and explain all the transformation rules used. Apply the algorithm step-by-step to unify:

$$Q(a, g(x, a), f(y)) \quad \text{with} \quad Q(z, g(a, b), f(g(w)))$$

**(b)** [7 marks] What is the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**? Prove or explain why the MGU is unique up to variable renaming. Explain the significance of the **occurs check** with two counterexamples showing what goes wrong without it.

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Robinson's Unification Algorithm [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Algorithm Description (2 marks)**

Robinson's algorithm (1965) unifies two first-order terms $p$ and $q$ by transforming a set of equations $E$ using deterministic rules. It returns either the MGU $\sigma$ or **FAIL**.

**Step 2: Transformation Rules (2 marks)**

The rules are:
1. **Delete** $t \doteq t$ (trivial)
2. **Decompose** $f(s_1,\dots,s_n) \doteq f(t_1,\dots,t_n)$ → $\{s_1 \doteq t_1, \dots\}$
3. **Conflict** $f(\dots) \doteq g(\dots)$ with $f \neq g$ or arity mismatch → FAIL
4. **Orient** $x \doteq t$ where $x$ is variable, $t$ is not, and $x \notin \text{Vars}(t)$ → add to $\sigma$
5. **Check** $x \doteq t$ where $x \in \text{Vars}(t)$ → FAIL (occurs check)

**Step 3: Worked Example (3 marks)**

Unify $Q(a, g(x, a), f(y))$ with $Q(z, g(a, b), f(g(w)))$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_0 &= \{Q(a, g(x, a), f(y)) \doteq Q(z, g(a, b), f(g(w)))\} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Decompose}} \\
E_1 &= \{a \doteq z, \; g(x, a) \doteq g(a, b), \; f(y) \doteq f(g(w))\}, \quad \sigma = \emptyset \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Orient } a \doteq z} \\
E_2 &= \{g(x, a) \doteq g(a, b), \; f(y) \doteq f(g(w))\}, \quad \sigma = \{z/a\} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Decompose } g} \\
E_3 &= \{x \doteq a, \; a \doteq b, \; f(y) \doteq f(g(w))\}, \quad \sigma = \{z/a\} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Orient } x \doteq a} \\
E_4 &= \{a \doteq b, \; f(y) \doteq f(g(w))\}, \quad \sigma = \{z/a, x/a\} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Conflict: } a \neq b} \\
&\text{FAIL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The two terms **do not unify** because the second arguments yield $a = b$ which is unsatisfiable.

**(b) MGU Uniqueness & Occurs Check [7 marks]**

**MGU Definition (2 marks):** A unifier $\sigma$ is the MGU if for every other unifier $\theta$, there exists a substitution $\tau$ such that $\theta = \sigma \circ \tau$.

**Uniqueness up to renaming (2 marks):** The MGU is not strictly unique. If $\sigma$ and $\sigma'$ are both MGUs of $p, q$, then there exists a variable renaming $\rho$ such that $\sigma' = \rho \circ \sigma$. This is proven by induction on the structure of $p$ and $q$, using the fact that the only degree of freedom in the MGU is the choice of fresh variable names.

**Occurs Check Significance (3 marks):**

- **Counterexample 1:** $x \doteq f(x)$. Without occurs check, $\sigma = \{x/f(x)\}$ would be returned, but it creates a circular reference. Applying it yields $x = f(x) = f(f(x)) = \dots$, which is unsolvable.
- **Counterexample 2:** $P(x, x) \doteq P(f(y), f(g(y)))$. Without occurs check, we would return $\sigma = \{x/f(y), y/g(y)\}$, leading to $y = g(y) = g(g(y)) = \dots$, which is inconsistent.

The occurs check is **necessary for soundness** of the unification algorithm.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Lifts Forward Chaining

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **Lifts Forward Chaining** algorithm for First-Order Logic (FOL). Compare it with propositional forward chaining, listing at least three differences. Use a diagram to illustrate the architecture.

**(b)** [7 marks] Apply the Lifts Forward Chaining algorithm on the following knowledge base and prove whether $Q(\text{John})$ can be inferred:

| Rule | Clause |
|---|---|
| R1 | $P(x) \land R(x) \Rightarrow S(x)$ |
| R2 | $Q(x) \land S(x) \Rightarrow T(x)$ |
| R3 | $R(x) \Rightarrow Q(x)$ |
| F1 | $P(\text{John})$ |
| F2 | $R(\text{John})$ |

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Lifts Forward Chaining Explanation [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** Lifts Forward Chaining is a **data-driven** inference procedure for FOL. It starts with known ground facts, and at each step unifies the head of a rule with a known fact, then checks if all premises are satisfied. When satisfied, the rule is "fired" with the unifier applied, producing a new ground fact.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Propositional FC | Lifts FC |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern matching | Symbol identity | Unification |
| Rule generality | Specific propositions | Parameterized first-order rules |
| Output | New ground atom | New atom with substitution applied |

**Architecture (2 marks):** See Section 4.2 for the Mermaid block diagram.

**(b) Worked Forward Chaining Example [7 marks]**

**Initial State:**

- Agenda: $[\text{P}(\text{John}), \text{R}(\text{John})]$
- Inferred: $\emptyset$
- Counts: $\text{R1}=2, \text{R2}=2, \text{R3}=1$

**Step 1: Pop** $P(\text{John})$.

- Unify with $P(x)$ in R1: $\sigma = \{x/\text{John}\}$.
- $count[\text{R1}] \leftarrow 1$.

**Step 2: Pop** $R(\text{John})$.

- Unify with $R(x)$ in R1 (same $\sigma$): $count[\text{R1}] \leftarrow 0$.
- Fire R1 → push $S(\text{John})$.
- Unify with $R(x)$ in R3: $\sigma = \{x/\text{John}\}$.
- $count[\text{R3}] \leftarrow 0$.
- Fire R3 → push $Q(\text{John})$.

**Step 3: Pop** $S(\text{John})$.

- Unify with $S(x)$ in R2: $\sigma = \{x/\text{John}\}$.
- $count[\text{R2}] = 1$ (waiting for $Q(\text{John})$).

**Step 4: Pop** $Q(\text{John})$.

- **Matches the query $Q(\text{John})$** → **Return TRUE**.

**Final derivation chain:**

$$
P(\text{John}) \land R(\text{John}) \xrightarrow{\text{R1}, \sigma=\{x/\text{John}\}} S(\text{John}) \xrightarrow{\text{with } Q(\text{John})} \text{R2 fires} \rightarrow T(\text{John})
$$

$$
R(\text{John}) \xrightarrow{\text{R3}, \sigma=\{x/\text{John}\}} Q(\text{John}) \quad \checkmark \text{(Query confirmed)}
$$

**Conclusion:** $Q(\text{John})$ **CAN** be inferred from the KB.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unification direction:** Always orient variable $\doteq$ term (variable on left), never term $\doteq$ variable in formal write-ups. KTU valuators deduct 0.5–1 mark for swapped order.
> 2. **Occur check omission:** In exam answers, ALWAYS show the occurs check step explicitly, even if obvious — it's a high-value skill marker.
> 3. **Forward chaining queue vs. set:** Students often confuse the *Agenda* (queue, can have duplicates) with the *Inferred* set (boolean tracking). Be precise.
> 4. **MGU in tables:** When showing MGU, write $\sigma = \{x/A, y/B\}$, not $\sigma(x) = A, \sigma(y) = B$ — the set notation is the KTU-expected format.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- **Unification** is the process of finding a substitution that makes two FOL expressions syntactically identical.
- A **substitution** $\sigma$ is a finite set of variable-to-term bindings: $\sigma = \{x_1/t_1, \dots\}$.
- The **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest unifier; it is unique **up to variable renaming**.
- **Robinson's Unification Algorithm (1965)** uses transformation rules: *Delete, Decompose, Conflict, Orient, Occurs Check*.
- The **Occurs Check** is mandatory: a variable cannot be bound to a term containing itself (e.g., $x \doteq f(x)$ fails).
- **Lifts Forward Chaining** is the FOL extension of propositional FC; it uses unification to instantiate variables.
- It is **sound** and **complete for Datalog** (function-free FOL), but only **semi-decidable** for full FOL.
- The three key components of Lifts FC: **Agenda queue, Count tracker, Inferred set**.
- Substitution composition: $(\sigma_1 \circ \sigma_2)(v) = \sigma_1(\sigma_2(v))$ — apply $\sigma_2$ first.
- **Soundness of Unification:** $p\sigma = q\sigma$ implies $p$ and $q$ are unifiable with MGU $\sigma$.
- **Real-world applications:** Prolog, Datalog, OWL 2 RL, deductive databases, production rule engines.
- **KTU high-yield keywords:** *Unification, MGU, occurs check, substitution, definite clause, Agenda, lifting, Datalog, soundness, completeness.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backward Chaining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backward Chaining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Backward Chaining** is a *goal-driven* (or *query-driven*) inference strategy used in rule-based expert systems and logical reasoning engines. It begins with a hypothesis (goal) the system wants to prove and works **backwards** through the rule base, recursively decomposing the goal into sub-goals, until it either reaches facts in the working memory (proving the goal) or exhausts all possibilities (failing the goal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Backward chaining is an inference technique that starts from a conclusion (goal) and attempts to find supporting evidence (premises/facts) by recursively chaining backward through "IF-THEN" rules. It is the computational backbone of logic programming languages such as **Prolog**.

### Mathematical Notation

In propositional and first-order logic, a rule has the general form:

$$P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \rightarrow Q$$

Where:
- $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ = premises (antecedents / IF conditions)
- $Q$ = conclusion (consequent / THEN action)
- $\rightarrow$ denotes logical implication

**Backward chaining** seeks to prove $Q$ by attempting to prove each $P_i$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Detective Working Backwards"**

Imagine a **detective** trying to prove: *"Did Mr. X commit the crime?"* (`Goal: Crime(X)`)

Instead of starting from every piece of evidence in the city and seeing where it leads (forward chaining), the detective starts from the conclusion and asks:

1. *"For Mr. X to be the criminal, what must be true?"* → Rule: `Criminal(X) ← Motive(X) ∧ Weapon(X) ∧ Opportunity(X)`
2. *"Does Mr. X have a motive?"* → Search for a fact or another sub-rule.
3. *"Does Mr. X have access to the weapon?"* → Search again...
4. Continue until either all sub-goals resolve to **known facts** (Goal PROVED) or one sub-goal cannot be proven (Goal FAILS, backtrack).

This is **Backward Chaining** — *hypothesis → sub-hypotheses → facts*.

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics Summary

| Property | Forward Chaining | **Backward Chaining** |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Direction | Data-driven | **Goal-driven** |
| Starting Point | Known facts | **Hypothesis / Query** |
| Strategy | Antecedent → Consequent | **Consequent → Antecedent** |
| Use Case | Monitoring, control | **Diagnosis, query answering** |
| Example System | CLIPS, production systems | **Prolog, MYCIN, expert system shells** |
| Search Type | Breadth-first (typically) | **Depth-first (with backtracking)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask for a comparison table between Forward and Backward Chaining. Memorize the direction ("Forward = Data → Goal", "Backward = Goal → Data").

---

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** (e.g., MYCIN — diagnosis of bacterial infections)
- **Automated Theorem Provers**
- **Logic Programming Languages** (Prolog, Datalog)
- **Legal Reasoning Systems**
- **Configuration & Troubleshooting Expert Systems** (network fault diagnosis)
- **Natural Language Question Answering**
- **Robotics — Plan Recognition**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Backward Chaining Works

The algorithm operates in **5 systematic steps**:

### Step 1: Goal Acquisition
The system is supplied with a **goal** (a proposition the user wishes to prove, e.g., `Cancer(Patient)`).

### Step 2: Rule Matching (Unification)
The inference engine scans the rule base (knowledge base) for all rules whose **conclusion (head)** unifies with the current goal. This is the *modus ponens* matching in reverse — finding rules of the form:

$$R_i : P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \rightarrow G$$

Where $G$ matches the current goal after substitution $\theta$.

### Step 3: Sub-Goal Generation
For each matched rule, the premises $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ become **new sub-goals** added to the *goal stack* (a LIFO structure).

### Step 4: Recursive Resolution
The engine recursively applies Steps 2–3 to each sub-goal, descending deeper into the rule graph. A sub-goal is **satisfied** if:
- It matches a **fact** in the working memory, **OR**
- It matches the **conclusion** of another rule whose premises can all be proven.

### Step 5: Backtracking on Failure
If at any point a sub-goal **cannot** be proven (no matching rule, no matching fact), the engine **backtracks** to the previous choice point and tries an alternative rule. If all rules for a goal are exhausted, the goal is declared **FAIL**.

---

## 2.2 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Logic Engine Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> The pseudocode below is provided for **conceptual understanding** of the inference loop, not as a programming exercise. KTU 2024 may include a flow-based question on this.

```
PROCEDURE BackwardChain(Goal G, KnowledgeBase KB):
    IF G ∈ WorkingMemory THEN
        RETURN SUCCESS
    END IF

    ApplicableRules ← {R ∈ KB | Unify(Head(R), G) ≠ FAIL}

    IF ApplicableRules = ∅ THEN
        RETURN FAILURE        // No rule supports G
    END IF

    FOR EACH Rule R in ApplicableRules DO
        Substitution θ ← Unify(Head(R), G)
        SubGoals ← Apply(θ, Body(R))    // Premises of R
        AllProven ← TRUE

        FOR EACH SubGoal S in SubGoals DO
            IF BackwardChain(S, KB) = FAILURE THEN
                AllProven ← FALSE
                BREAK                  // Try next rule
            END IF
        END FOR

        IF AllProven = TRUE THEN
            Add G (with θ) to WorkingMemory
            RETURN SUCCESS
        END IF
    END FOR

    RETURN FAILURE
END PROCEDURE
```

---

## 2.3 The AND/OR Graph View

A rule base in backward chaining is naturally represented as an **AND-OR graph**:

| Node Type | Meaning | Visual Convention |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| **OR Node** | A goal — *true if ANY supporting rule succeeds* | Circular node |
| **AND Node** | A rule body — *true only if ALL premises succeed* | Rectangular node |
| **Leaf Node** | A fact in working memory | Shaded node |

> **OR Node:** Goal $G$ succeeds if at least one of $\{R_1, R_2, R_3\}$ succeeds.
> **AND Node:** A rule body succeeds only if ALL of its premises $\{P_1, P_2, P_3\}$ succeed.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formal Expression / Property | Notes |
|---------|----------------------------|-------|
| General Rule | $P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n \rightarrow Q$ | $P_i$ = premises, $Q$ = conclusion |
| Modus Ponens (forward) | From $P$ and $P \rightarrow Q$, infer $Q$ | Forward Chaining |
| Modus Tollens (variant) | From $\neg Q$ and $P \rightarrow Q$, infer $\neg P$ | Used in proof |
| Unification | $\text{Unify}(A, B) = \theta$ such that $A\theta = B\theta$ | Substitution $\theta$ |
| Goal Stack Depth | Depth = longest chain of nested sub-goals | Determines recursion depth |
| Time Complexity (worst case) | $O(b^d)$ | $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth |
| Space Complexity | $O(d)$ | Stack-based, depth-first |
| Soundness | Backward Chaining is **sound** | Only derives true conclusions from true premises |
| Completeness | Backward Chaining is **NOT complete** for FOL in general | May fail to find a proof that exists (infinite search) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Backward Chaining is **sound** but **not complete** for first-order logic. Many students incorrectly claim it is complete. The reason: depth-first search with iterative deepening is required for completeness.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection using rule-based backward chaining to verify if observed events indicate an attack pattern.
- **Healthcare AI:** Symptom → Disease backward reasoning in clinical decision support.
- **Fault Diagnosis:** Aircraft and industrial systems use backward chaining to identify root causes of failures.
- **Automated Planning:** Goal regression in classical AI planning.
- **Legal Tech:** Verdict prediction engines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — End-to-End Backward Chaining Derivation

This is the **canonical KTU-style problem**. Master this format completely.

### Knowledge Base (Rules + Facts)

| Rule ID | Rule (IF-THEN) |
|---------|----------------|
| R1 | `Fever(x) ∧ Cough(x) ∧ BodyPain(x) → Flu(x)` |
| R2 | `Flu(x) ∧ WeakImmunity(x) → Pneumonia(x)` |
| R3 | `HighTemp(x) → Fever(x)` |
| R4 | `DryCough(x) → Cough(x)` |
| R5 | `MusclePain(x) → BodyPain(x)` |
| R6 | `Virus(x) ∧ Aged(x) → WeakImmunity(x)` |

| Fact ID | Fact (Ground Truth) |
|---------|--------------------|
| F1 | `HighTemp(John)` |
| F2 | `DryCough(John)` |
| F3 | `MusclePain(John)` |
| F4 | `Virus(John)` |
| F5 | `Aged(John)` |

### Goal to Prove

$$\text{GOAL} : \text{Pneumonia(John)}$$

### Step-by-Step Backward Chaining Trace

**Level 0 — Goal Stack:** `[ Pneumonia(John) ]`

**Step 1:** Search rules where `Pneumonia(x)` is the head.
- Only **R2** matches: `Flu(x) ∧ WeakImmunity(x) → Pneumonia(x)`
- Unify: $\{x / \text{John}\}$
- Sub-goals generated: `Flu(John)`, `WeakImmunity(John)`

**Goal Stack after Step 1:** `[ Flu(John), WeakImmunity(John) ]`

**Step 2 (resolve `Flu(John)` first — top of stack):**
- Search rules where `Flu(x)` is the head.
- Only **R1** matches: `Fever(x) ∧ Cough(x) ∧ BodyPain(x) → Flu(x)`
- Unify: $\{x / \text{John}\}$
- Sub-goals generated: `Fever(John)`, `Cough(John)`, `BodyPain(John)`

**Goal Stack after Step 2:** `[ Fever(John), Cough(John), BodyPain(John), WeakImmunity(John) ]`

**Step 3 (resolve `Fever(John)`):**
- Rule **R3** matches: `HighTemp(x) → Fever(x)`
- Unify: $\{x / \text{John}\}$
- Sub-goal: `HighTemp(John)`
- Search working memory: **F1** = `HighTemp(John)` ✓ **PROVED**

**Step 4 (resolve `Cough(John)`):**
- Rule **R4** matches: `DryCough(x) → Cough(x)`
- Sub-goal: `DryCough(John)`
- Search working memory: **F2** = `DryCough(John)` ✓ **PROVED**

**Step 5 (resolve `BodyPain(John)`):**
- Rule **R5** matches: `MusclePain(x) → BodyPain(x)`
- Sub-goal: `MusclePain(John)`
- Search working memory: **F3** = `MusclePain(John)` ✓ **PROVED**

**Conclusion for `Flu(John)`:** All sub-goals succeeded → `Flu(John)` is **PROVED**.

**Step 6 (resolve `WeakImmunity(John)`):**
- Rule **R6** matches: `Virus(x) ∧ Aged(x) → WeakImmunity(x)`
- Sub-goals: `Virus(John)`, `Aged(John)`
- Search working memory: **F4** = `Virus(John)` ✓, **F5** = `Aged(John)` ✓
- `WeakImmunity(John)` is **PROVED**.

**Step 7 — Final Conclusion:**
- Both premises of R2 are proved: `Flu(John)` ✓ and `WeakImmunity(John)` ✓
- Therefore: **`Pneumonia(John)` is PROVED** ✓

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Backward Chaining Resolution Tree

The trace above is represented as a **proof tree**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Pneumonia(John)} \\
&\quad \leftarrow R2 : \Big[ \text{Flu(John)} \;\;\bigwedge\;\; \text{WeakImmunity(John)} \Big] \\
&\text{Flu(John)} \\
&\quad \leftarrow R1 : \Big[ \text{Fever(John)} \;\;\bigwedge\;\; \text{Cough(John)} \;\;\bigwedge\;\; \text{BodyPain(John)} \Big] \\
&\text{Fever(John)} \;\; \leftarrow R3 : \text{HighTemp(John)} \;\; \leftarrow \textbf{Fact F1} \\
&\text{Cough(John)} \;\; \leftarrow R4 : \text{DryCough(John)} \;\; \leftarrow \textbf{Fact F2} \\
&\text{BodyPain(John)} \;\; \leftarrow R5 : \text{MusclePain(John)} \;\; \leftarrow \textbf{Fact F3} \\
&\text{WeakImmunity(John)} \;\; \leftarrow R6 : \Big[ \text{Virus(John)} \;\;\bigwedge\;\; \text{Aged(John)} \Big] \\
&\text{Virus(John)} \;\; \leftarrow \textbf{Fact F4} \\
&\text{Aged(John)} \;\; \leftarrow \textbf{Fact F5}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marker Notation:** Each $\leftarrow$ denotes a rule application. **Leaves** of the tree are facts. A leaf is *shaded* (or bolded) in a KTU diagram to indicate a working-memory fact.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Table

| Step | Goal | Rule Applied | Sub-Goal Generated | Result |
|------|------|--------------|-------------------|--------|
| 1 | `Pneumonia(John)` | R2 | `Flu(John)`, `WeakImmunity(John)` | Sub-goals pushed |
| 2 | `Flu(John)` | R1 | `Fever(John)`, `Cough(John)`, `BodyPain(John)` | Sub-goals pushed |
| 3 | `Fever(John)` | R3 | `HighTemp(John)` | PROVED (F1) |
| 4 | `Cough(John)` | R4 | `DryCough(John)` | PROVED (F2) |
| 5 | `BodyPain(John)` | R5 | `MusclePain(John)` | PROVED (F3) |
| 6 | `WeakImmunity(John)` | R6 | `Virus(John)`, `Aged(John)` | Both PROVED (F4, F5) |
| 7 | `Pneumonia(John)` | — | All premises proved | **GOAL ACHIEVED** |

---

## 3.4 Failure Case — Backtracking Demonstration

> [!NOTE]
> **When does Backward Chaining fail?** When the goal has no supporting chain. Example: If Fact F4 (`Virus(John)`) is removed, then `WeakImmunity(John)` cannot be proved → R2 fails → No more rules support `Pneumonia(John)` → Goal **FAILS** (after exhausting all matching rules).

In a system with **multiple rules** supporting the same goal, failure of one rule triggers **backtracking** to try the next rule — this is the essence of Prolog's search strategy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backward Chaining — Top-Down Search Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START: Goal G]) --> B{Goal G in Working Memory?}
    B -- Yes --> C([SUCCESS: G Proved])
    B -- No --> D[Search KB for rules with Head matching G]
    D --> E{Matching rules found?}
    E -- No --> F([FAILURE: Cannot Prove G])
    E -- Yes --> G[Pick next rule R from matched set]
    G --> H[Push premises of R as new Sub-Goals]
    H --> I[Recursively BackwardChain on each Sub-Goal]
    I --> J{All Sub-Goals Proved?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Add G to Working Memory]
    K --> C
    J -- No --> L[Backtrack to previous rule choice]
    L --> G
```

## 4.2 AND/OR Proof Tree for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Goal([Pneumonia John]) --> OR1{OR Node R2}
    OR1 --> AND1[AND: Flu John AND WeakImmunity John]

    AND1 --> OR2{OR Node R1}
    AND1 --> OR3{OR Node R6}

    OR2 --> AND2[AND: Fever John AND Cough John AND BodyPain John]

    AND2 --> F1((F1: HighTemp John))
    AND2 --> F2((F2: DryCough John))
    AND2 --> F3((F3: MusclePain John))

    F1 --> R3[R3: HighTemp x -> Fever x]
    F2 --> R4[R4: DryCough x -> Cough x]
    F3 --> R5[R5: MusclePain x -> BodyPain x]

    OR3 --> AND3[AND: Virus John AND Aged John]
    AND3 --> F4((F4: Virus John))
    AND3 --> F5((F5: Aged John))

    R3 --> F1Prov([PROVED])
    R4 --> F2Prov([PROVED])
    R5 --> F3Prov([PROVED])
    F4 --> F4Prov([PROVED])
    F5 --> F5Prov([PROVED])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Round nodes = OR (goal) nodes. Rectangular AND nodes = rule bodies (all premises must be true). Double-circle leaves = facts in the working memory (terminal proof points). Each upward edge shows a sub-goal dependency.

## 4.3 Backward vs Forward Chaining — Structural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Forward["FORWARD CHAINING - Data to Goal"]
        F1F[Fact: HighTemp John] --> R3F[Apply R3]
        F2F[Fact: DryCough John] --> R4F[Apply R4]
        F3F[Fact: MusclePain John] --> R5F[Apply R5]
        R3F --> InferF1[Infer: Fever John]
        R4F --> InferF2[Infer: Cough John]
        R5F --> InferF3[Infer: BodyPain John]
        InferF1 --> R1F[Apply R1]
        InferF2 --> R1F
        InferF3 --> R1F
        R1F --> R1Out[Infer: Flu John]
    end

    subgraph Backward["BACKWARD CHAINING - Goal to Data"]
        GoalB([Pneumonia John]) --> R2B[Apply R2 backward]
        R2B --> SGB1[Sub-Goal: Flu John]
        R2B --> SGB2[Sub-Goal: WeakImmunity John]
        SGB1 --> R1B[Apply R1 backward]
        R1B --> SGB3[Sub-Goal: Fever John]
        R1B --> SGB4[Sub-Goal: Cough John]
        R1B --> SGB5[Sub-Goal: BodyPain John]
        SGB3 --> Fact1B((HighTemp John))
        SGB4 --> Fact2B((DryCough John))
        SGB5 --> Fact3B((MusclePain John))
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Forward chaining fires all rules in sequence from facts — many inferences may be generated that are never needed. Backward chaining only explores paths **relevant to the goal**, making it more efficient for query-driven systems with large rule bases.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Forward Chaining and Backward Chaining. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Direction | Data-driven (facts → conclusions) | Goal-driven (goal → supporting facts) |
| Strategy | Antecedent reasoning | Consequent reasoning |
| Search | Breadth-first typical | Depth-first with backtracking |
| Application | Process control, monitoring systems | Medical diagnosis, query-answering systems |

**Example Applications:**
- Forward: Real-time chemical plant monitoring, intrusion detection alert systems.
- Backward: MYCIN (bacterial infection diagnosis), Prolog-based expert systems.

*(1 mark for clear distinction table, 1 mark for direction, 1 mark for application.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of unification in backward chaining? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Unification is the pattern-matching process that determines whether a rule's conclusion (head) can be made identical to the current goal (or sub-goal) through a **substitution** $\theta$.

Given two logical expressions $A$ and $B$, unification finds the most general unifier (MGU) $\theta$ such that:

$$A\theta = B\theta$$

In backward chaining, unification is invoked at **every recursive step** to:
1. Match the current goal with the head of candidate rules.
2. Bind variables to specific constants (e.g., $x \rightarrow \text{John}$).
3. Propagate these bindings down to the sub-goals for consistent reasoning.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark MGU concept, 1 mark role in sub-goal propagation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, OR an internal choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Consider the following knowledge base of rules and facts for a **"Plant Disease Diagnosis System"**. Use **Backward Chaining** to prove the goal: `Disease(PlantA)`.

**Rules:**
- R1: `BrownSpots(x) ∧ YellowLeaves(x) → Diseased(x)`
- R2: `Overwatered(x) → BrownSpots(x)`
- R3: `NutrientDeficiency(x) → YellowLeaves(x)`
- R4: `DrySoil(x) ∧ AcidicSoil(x) → NutrientDeficiency(x)`

**Facts:**
- F1: `Overwatered(PlantA)`
- F2: `DrySoil(PlantA)`
- F3: `AcidicSoil(PlantA)`

**Show the complete goal stack evolution, rule applications, and the final proof tree.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial Goal Stack:** `[ Disease(PlantA) ]`

**Step 2 — Match R1:** `BrownSpots(PlantA) ∧ YellowLeaves(PlantA) → Diseased(PlantA)`
- Sub-goals generated: `BrownSpots(PlantA)`, `YellowLeaves(PlantA)`
- Stack: `[ BrownSpots(PlantA), YellowLeaves(PlantA) ]`

**Step 3 — Resolve `BrownSpots(PlantA)` via R2:**
- Sub-goal: `Overwatered(PlantA)`
- **F1** matches → PROVED ✓

**Step 4 — Resolve `YellowLeaves(PlantA)` via R3:**
- Sub-goal: `NutrientDeficiency(PlantA)`
- Rule R4 matches: `DrySoil(x) ∧ AcidicSoil(x) → NutrientDeficiency(x)`
- Sub-goals: `DrySoil(PlantA)`, `AcidicSoil(PlantA)`
- **F2** and **F3** match → PROVED ✓

**Step 5 — Both sub-goals of R1 satisfied → `Disease(PlantA)` is PROVED** ✓

**Proof Tree:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Disease(PlantA)} \\
&\quad \leftarrow R1 : \big[ \text{BrownSpots(PlantA)} \;\wedge\; \text{YellowLeaves(PlantA)} \big] \\
&\text{BrownSpots(PlantA)} \;\; \leftarrow R2 : \text{Overwatered(PlantA)} \;\; \leftarrow F1 \\
&\text{YellowLeaves(PlantA)} \;\; \leftarrow R3 : \text{NutrientDeficiency(PlantA)} \\
&\text{NutrientDeficiency(PlantA)} \;\; \leftarrow R4 : \big[ \text{DrySoil(PlantA)} \;\wedge\; \text{AcidicSoil(PlantA)} \big] \\
&\text{DrySoil(PlantA)} \;\; \leftarrow F2 \qquad\qquad \text{AcidicSoil(PlantA)} \;\; \leftarrow F3
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for goal stack trace, 2 marks for rule matching, 2 marks for proof tree, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of Backward Chaining** in rule-based expert systems. How does it differ in efficiency from Forward Chaining when the number of rules is large? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages (3 marks):**

1. **Efficiency for query-driven systems:** Backward chaining only explores rules relevant to the current goal, avoiding the firing of irrelevant rules — a major advantage when the rule base is large (e.g., 10,000+ rules).
2. **Explanations are natural:** Because the proof tree mirrors the goal's logical structure, the system can easily explain *why* a conclusion was reached (useful in MYCIN-style medical systems).
3. **Reduces computational overhead:** No need to maintain a constantly updated *agenda* of all derivable facts (as in forward chaining); only the goal stack is tracked.

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Not complete for FOL:** Depth-first search may descend into infinite sub-goal chains and miss valid proofs. Iterative deepening is required for completeness.
2. **Inefficient when many goals must be proven:** Each query requires a fresh search; in forward chaining, all facts are pre-computed once.

**Efficiency Comparison (2 marks):**

| Scenario | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Few goals, many facts | Inefficient (wastes work) | **Efficient** |
| Many goals, few facts | **Efficient** (one pass) | Redundant re-search |
| Very large rule base | May saturate memory | **Goal-focused exploration** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **AND-OR graph representation** of a rule base used in backward chaining. Construct the AND-OR graph for the following rules and show how a query `Loves(John, Mary)` is resolved. (7 marks)

**Rules:**
- R1: `Likes(x, y) ∧ Honest(x) → Loves(x, y)`
- R2: `Friend(x, y) → Likes(x, y)`
- R3: `Kind(x) → Honest(x)`
- R4: `GoodPerson(x) → Kind(x)`

**Facts:**
- `Friend(John, Mary)`, `GoodPerson(John)`

**Model Solution:**

The AND-OR graph consists of:
- **OR Nodes (circles):** Goals — true if any of the supporting sub-graphs succeeds.
- **AND Nodes (rectangles):** Rule bodies — true only if all branches succeed.

**AND-OR Graph Construction:**

```
OR-Node: Loves(John, Mary)
   |
   AND-Node R1: [ Likes(John, Mary) , Honest(John) ]
       |
       |---- OR: Likes(John, Mary)
       |        |
       |        AND R2: [ Friend(John, Mary) ]
       |              |
       |              FACT: Friend(John, Mary) ✓
       |
       |---- OR: Honest(John)
                |
                AND R3: [ Kind(John) ]
                        |
                        OR: Kind(John)
                            |
                            AND R4: [ GoodPerson(John) ]
                                    |
                                    FACT: GoodPerson(John) ✓
```

**Resolution Trace:**

1. Goal `Loves(John, Mary)` → match R1 → sub-goals: `Likes(John, Mary)`, `Honest(John)`
2. `Likes(John, Mary)` → match R2 → sub-goal: `Friend(John, Mary)` → **Fact** ✓
3. `Honest(John)` → match R3 → sub-goal: `Kind(John)` → match R4 → sub-goal: `GoodPerson(John)` → **Fact** ✓
4. Both branches succeed → `Loves(John, Mary)` **PROVED** ✓

*(7 marks: 2 marks AND-OR concept, 3 marks graph construction, 2 marks resolution trace.)*

---

**(b)** Why is backward chaining **sound but not complete** in first-order logic? Illustrate with a small example where backward chaining fails to find a proof that logically exists. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Soundness (3 marks):**

Backward chaining uses the **Modus Ponens** inference rule in reverse, combined with **unification**. Both operations are sound:
- Modus Ponens: From true $P$ and $P \rightarrow Q$, derive true $Q$.
- Unification preserves logical equivalence under substitution.

Therefore, every conclusion derived by backward chaining is **logically entailed** by the knowledge base — guaranteeing soundness.

**Incompleteness (4 marks):**

Backward chaining uses **depth-first search** to traverse the rule space. In first-order logic, the search tree may be **infinite** (recursive rules, unbounded domains). The algorithm may:
- Follow an infinite branch and never return.
- Exhaust computational resources before finding a finite proof.

**Illustrative Example:**

- Rule R1: `Human(x) → Mortal(x)` (premise `Human(x)`)
- Rule R2: `Greek(x) → Human(x)` (premise `Greek(x)`)
- Rule R3: `Philosopher(x) → Greek(x)` (premise `Philosopher(x)`)
- Rule R4: `Socrates(x) → Philosopher(x)` (premise `Socrates(x)`)
- Fact: `Socrates(Socrates)` exists, but facts for `Greek`, `Human` are missing.

Goal: `Mortal(Socrates)`

- Backward chain from `Mortal(x)` → R1 → need `Human(Socrates)`.
- Backward chain from `Human(x)` → R2 → need `Greek(Socrates)`.
- Backward chain from `Greek(x)` → R3 → need `Philosopher(Socrates)`.
- Backward chain from `Philosopher(x)` → R4 → need `Socrates(Socrates)` → ✓

This succeeds, but if the rule base had a **recursive rule** like `Ancestor(x,y) → Ancestor(x, Parent(y))` with no base case, backward chaining could loop forever searching the `Ancestor` chain — **failing to terminate** even though a finite proof may exist elsewhere.

**Solution:** Use **iterative deepening** or **breadth-first backward chaining** to achieve completeness at the cost of efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "direction" of chaining:** Many students write "Backward Chaining starts from data" — this is **Forward Chaining**. Loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always show the goal stack evolution** in working problems; examiners award 2–3 marks for this trace alone.
> 3. **Mention both soundness AND incompleteness** for FOL — claiming completeness is a frequent error (–1 mark).
> 4. **In AND-OR graphs, label OR nodes as circular and AND nodes as rectangular** in the diagram. Unlabeled diagrams lose up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Use proper variable substitution notation** ($\{x/\text{John}\}$) when discussing unification — sloppy notation is penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Backward Chaining**

- ✅ **Definition:** Goal-driven inference from consequent to antecedent.
- ✅ **Starting Point:** Goal (hypothesis), not facts.
- ✅ **Core Mechanism:** Recursive sub-goal generation, unification, backtracking.
- ✅ **Search Strategy:** Depth-first with backtracking (stack-based).
- ✅ **AND-OR Graph:** OR node = goal, AND node = rule body, leaves = facts.
- ✅ **Soundness:** YES — every derived conclusion is logically valid.
- ✅ **Completeness:** NO (in FOL) — depth-first may fail to find existing proofs.
- ✅ **Key Systems:** Prolog, MYCIN, Datalog.
- ✅ **Best Use Case:** Diagnostic, query-answering, troubleshooting expert systems.
- ✅ **Worst Use Case:** When many goals must be proven simultaneously (use Forward Chaining).
- ✅ **Unification:** Pattern-matching with substitution $\theta$; central to rule selection.
- ✅ **Modus Ponens:** Underlying logical inference rule.
- ✅ **Time Complexity:** $O(b^d)$ worst case; Space: $O(d)$ stack depth.
- ✅ **Comparison Mnemonic:** *Forward = Facts → Fire rules → Derive new facts*; *Backward = Goal → Find supporting rule → Prove premises*.
- ✅ **Mandatory Steps in Exam Solutions:** (1) State the goal, (2) List matching rules, (3) Generate sub-goals, (4) Recursively resolve, (5) Backtrack on failure, (6) Draw the proof tree.

> **End of Notes — Backward Chaining (Module 3, OECMT615, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning: Learning from Rewards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning: Learning from Rewards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a machine learning paradigm in which an autonomous **agent** learns an optimal **policy** (a mapping from states to actions) by interacting with a **dynamic environment** through a trial-and-error process. The agent's objective is to maximize a cumulative scalar signal called the **reward signal** $R_t$ received from the environment over time. Unlike supervised learning, the agent is never told the "correct" action; instead, it must discover which actions yield the highest long-term reward by exploring the consequences of its own actions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Reinforcement Learning is learning what to do — how to map situations to actions — so as to maximize a numerical reward signal. The learner is not told which actions to take, but instead must discover which actions yield the most reward by trying them.

### 1.2 The Four Core Constituents (KTU Mandatory)

| Symbol | Constituent | Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Policy** $\pi$ | Agent's behavior function | Maps perceived states of the environment to actions |
| **Reward Signal** $R_t$ | Primary basis for learning | Defines the goal of the RL problem (immediate scalar feedback) |
| **Value Function** $V_\pi(s)$ | Long-term desirability | Total reward an agent can expect to accumulate starting from state $s$ |
| **Model** (Optional) | Environment mimicry | Allows inferences about how the environment will behave |

> [!NOTE]
> A **policy** is a stochastic rule $\pi(a \mid s)$ — the probability of taking action $a$ in state $s$. A deterministic policy is a special case: $\pi(s) = a$.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Toddler & The Hot Stove"

Imagine teaching a toddler to navigate a kitchen:
- **Agent** → The toddler
- **Environment** → The kitchen (stove, fridge, toys)
- **State** $S_t$ → The toddler's current location and surroundings
- **Action** $A_t$ → Move, touch, grab, walk away
- **Reward** $R_t$ → 🍪 A biscuit (positive, $+1$) for opening the fridge, 🔥 a burn (negative, $-10$) for touching the stove
- **Policy** $\pi$ → The toddler's evolving "mental rule" about which actions to repeat

The toddler has **no teacher** saying "don't touch the stove." Instead, the burn itself is the feedback. After many such experiences, the toddler's policy converges to one that avoids the stove and heads for the biscuit jar. **This is precisely the essence of Reinforcement Learning.**

### 1.4 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

The fundamental RL interaction, illustrated below, is a discrete-time cyclical process:

1. At time $t$, the agent observes the current state $S_t$.
2. The agent executes action $A_t$ according to its policy $\pi$.
3. The environment transitions to a new state $S_{t+1}$.
4. The environment emits a reward $R_{t+1}$.
5. The agent updates its knowledge and repeats.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever asked to "draw the RL framework," always include the feedback arrows (reward + next state) returning from the environment to the agent. The board examiner deducts marks for missing the closed-loop structure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reinforcement Learning Framework — Formalized

The environment is mathematically formalized as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**, which is a 5-tuple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma \rangle$:

- $\mathcal{S}$ — Set of all possible states (state space)
- $\mathcal{A}$ — Set of all possible actions (action space)
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — Transition probability (probability of reaching state $s'$ from $s$ via action $a$)
- $R(s, a, s')$ — Expected immediate reward
- $\gamma \in [0,1]$ — Discount factor controlling the present value of future rewards

### 2.2 The Markov Property — Foundation of RL Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A state $S_t$ is **Markov** if and only if:
>
> $$\mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a, S_{t-1}, A_{t-1}, \dots) = \mathbb{P}(S_{t+1} = s' \mid S_t = s, A_t = a)$$
>
> The future is conditionally independent of the past, given the present.

### 2.3 Return, Policy, and Value Functions — Why They Matter

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

- **Why a discounted return?** Rewards far in the future are less certain; mathematically, the geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k$ only converges if $\gamma < 1$. This guarantees the return $G_t$ is finite and well-defined.
- **Why a value function?** It compresses the infinite future trajectory into a single scalar per state, enabling comparison between states and the construction of optimal policies.
- **Why distinguish $V$ and $Q$?** $V_\pi(s)$ evaluates being in a state, while $Q_\pi(s,a)$ evaluates taking a specific action. The action-value $Q$ is more actionable because the agent must pick an action, not a state.

### 2.4 Exploration vs. Exploitation Dilemma

The agent faces a fundamental trade-off:

- **Exploitation:** Choose the best-known action (maximize current reward).
- **Exploration:** Try uncertain actions to discover potentially better strategies.

A canonical solution is the **$\epsilon$-greedy policy**: with probability $\epsilon$ act randomly, otherwise act greedily with respect to current value estimates. $\epsilon$ is typically decayed (e.g., $\epsilon = 0.1 \to 0.01$) over training to favor exploitation as the agent gains confidence.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Policy** | $\pi(a \mid s) = \mathbb{P}(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ | Probability of action $a$ in state $s$ |
| 2 | **Discounted Return** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative future reward from time $t$ |
| 3 | **State-Value Function** | $V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$ | Expected return starting in $s$, following $\pi$ |
| 4 | **Action-Value Function** | $Q_\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$ | Expected return after taking $a$ in $s$ |
| 5 | **Bellman Equation for $V$** | $V_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]$ | Recursive decomposition of value |
| 6 | **Bellman Optimality for $V^*$** | $V^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]$ | Value under the optimal policy |
| 7 | **Bellman Optimality for $Q^*$** | $Q^*(s,a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$ | Optimal action-value |
| 8 | **Q-Learning Update** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | Off-policy TD control |
| 9 | **SARSA Update** | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | On-policy TD control |
| 10 | **TD Error** | $\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma V(S_{t+1}) - V(S_t)$ | Bootstrapping error term |
| 11 | **$\epsilon$-greedy probability** | $\pi(a \mid s) = 1 - \epsilon + \dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}$ (greedy), $\dfrac{\epsilon}{\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert}$ (others) | Action selection under $\epsilon$-greedy |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, the $\vert \mathcal{A}(s) \vert$ notation denotes the cardinality (number of elements) of the action set. It is **NOT** a markdown table separator — it is standard set-theory notation for the size of a set.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reinforcement Learning drives engineering systems that require **sequential decision-making under uncertainty**:

- **Robotics:** Teaching robot arms to grasp novel objects (OpenAI's Dactyl, Boston Dynamics locomotion).
- **Autonomous Driving:** Lane-changing, merging, and intersection negotiation.
- **Industrial Control:** HVAC optimization in smart buildings (DeepMind's Google data-center cooling — 40% energy reduction).
- **Finance:** Portfolio rebalancing and algorithmic trading.
- **Game AI:** AlphaGo, AlphaZero, and StarCraft II agents that surpassed human world champions.
- **Networking:** Dynamic packet routing and adaptive congestion control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman Equation for $V_\pi(s)$

**Starting Point:** The state-value function defined as the expected return:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the return $G_t$ using its recursive definition:**

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots
$$

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \dots \right)
$$

$$
G_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}
$$

> **Logic:** The return from time $t$ is the immediate reward plus the discounted return from the next step onward. This is the *bootstrap* identity at the heart of dynamic programming.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the expectation:**

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]
$$

By the linearity of expectation:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Condition on the next action $A_t$ and next state $S_{t+1}$:**

Using the law of total expectation, sum over all possible actions $a \in \mathcal{A}$ and next states $s' \in \mathcal{S}$:

$$
V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the inner expectation as $V_\pi(s')$:**

By the definition of the state-value function, $\mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] = V_\pi(s')$. Substituting yields the celebrated **Bellman Equation for $V_\pi$**:

$$
\boxed{\,V_\pi(s) \;=\; \sum_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, V_\pi(s') \right]\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The value of state $s$ equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state, averaged over the policy's action distribution and the environment's transition dynamics.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bellman Optimality Equation for $Q^*(s,a)$

**Step 1 — Begin with the optimal action-value definition:**

$$
Q^*(s,a) \;=\; \mathbb{E} \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Marginalize over the next state $s'$:**

$$
Q^*(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]
$$

> **Logic:** Because the optimal policy is greedy with respect to $Q^*$, at state $s'$ the optimal action is $\arg\max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$, and its value is $\max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$.

$$
\boxed{\,Q^*(s,a) \;=\; \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Q-Learning Update Rule

**Step 1 — Define the TD target.** For Q-learning, the target is constructed assuming the agent acts optimally from the next state onward:

$$
\text{Target}_t \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')
$$

**Step 2 — Define the prediction error (TD error):**

$$
\delta_t \;=\; \text{Target}_t - Q(S_t, A_t) \;=\; R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a stochastic gradient-descent step on the squared TD error** $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \delta_t^2$:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) - \alpha \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(S_t, A_t)}
$$

Since $\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial Q(S_t, A_t)} = -\delta_t$ (with respect to the old estimate), the update becomes:

$$
Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the TD error explicitly:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q(S_t, A_t) \;\leftarrow\; Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]\,}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** The learning rate $\alpha \in (0,1]$ controls how aggressively new samples override old estimates. A common schedule is $\alpha_t = \alpha_0 / (1 + t/\tau)$ for some decay constant $\tau$, ensuring convergence conditions (Robbins-Monro) are met.

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Q-Learning vs SARSA

| Dimension | Q-Learning | SARSA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Off-policy | On-policy |
| **Update Target** | $R + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')$ | $R + \gamma Q(s', a')$ |
| **Action $a'$ used** | Greedy (max) | Actually taken by current policy |
| **Convergence** | Converges to $Q^*$ under standard conditions | Converges to $Q^\pi$ for the *behavior* policy |
| **Exploration impact** | Robust to exploration choice | Learns the value of the exploratory policy |
| **Risk profile** | Can be risky (learns optimal path ignoring exploration) | Safer (accounts for action actually executed) |
| **Use case** | Game-playing agents, deterministic envs | Robotics, safety-critical navigation |
| **Tuple name origin** | State, Action, Reward, Next State, Action (max) | **S**tate, **A**ction, **R**eward, next **S**tate, next **A**ction |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — SARSA:** "**S**-**A**-**R**-**S**-**A**" — the quintuple of (current state, current action, reward, next state, next action). The *next* action $a'$ is sampled from the current policy $\pi$ (often $\epsilon$-greedy with respect to $Q$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Agent–Environment Interaction Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AGENT] -->|"Action A_t"| B[ENVIRONMENT]
    B -->|"Reward R_t+1"| A
    B -->|"Next State S_t+1"| A
    A -->|"Policy pi"| A
    B -->|"Transition Model P"| B
    B -->|"Reward Model R"| B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The agent emits an action at time $t$. The environment replies with the next state and a reward at time $t+1$. The policy and environment models are internal components that get refined through experience.

### 4.2 RL Algorithm Taxonomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REINFORCEMENT LEARNING] --> B[Model-Free]
    A --> C[Model-Based]
    B --> D[Policy Optimization]
    B --> E[Value-Based]
    B --> F[Actor-Critic]
    E --> G[Q-Learning]
    E --> H[SARSA]
    E --> I[Deep Q-Network DQN]
    D --> J[REINFORCE]
    D --> K[Trust Region Policy Optimization]
    C --> L[AlphaZero]
    C --> M[Model Predictive Control]
    F --> N[Advantage Actor Critic A2C]
    F --> O[Soft Actor Critic SAC]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** RL splits first by whether the agent learns a model of the environment. Model-free methods, which dominate KTU examinations, subdivide into value-based (focus on $V$ or $Q$), policy-based (focus on $\pi$), and actor-critic hybrids.

### 4.3 Q-Learning Update Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe state s_t] --> B[Select action a_t via epsilon-greedy]
    B --> C[Execute action in environment]
    C --> D[Receive reward r and next state s_t+1]
    D --> E[Compute target: r + gamma times max Q s_t+1 comma a prime]
    E --> F[Compute TD error: target minus Q s_t comma a_t]
    F --> G[Update Q s_t comma a_t by adding alpha times TD error]
    G --> H[Set s_t equals s_t+1]
    H --> A
```
