## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of RL with Function Approximation (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[Environment E] -->|state s_t| featA[Feature Extractor x_s_t]
    featA -->|feature vector| approxA[Function Approximator w]
    approxA -->|value V_hat_s| policyA[Policy pi]
    policyA -->|action a_t| envA
    approxA -->|prediction| tderrA[TD Error delta_t]
    rewardA[Reward r_t1] --> tderrA
    nextA[Next state s_t1] -->|via approximator| tderrA
    tderrA -->|gradient signal| updA[Parameter Update w_t1]
    updA -->|new parameters| approxA
    classDef ext fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#b9770e,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    class envA,rewardA,nextA ext
    class featA,approxA,policyA,tderrA,updA int
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The *Environment* (yellow) is outside the agent; the *internal learning machinery* (blue) is the agent. The TD error $\delta_t$ couples the reward, the next-state value, and the current value to produce a gradient signal that updates the shared parameter $\mathbf{w}$.

### 4.2 The Deadly Triad — Cause-Effect Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    fa[Function Approximation] --> instability[Value Function Divergence]
    boot[Bootstrapping] --> instability
    offpol[Off-Policy Learning] --> instability
    fa -.->|shared parameters| corr[State-Correlation Error]
    boot -.->|targets depend on w| targetmove[Moving Target Error]
    offpol -.->|distribution mismatch| dist[Distributional Shift Error]
    instability --> fixes[Stabilization Techniques]
    corr --> replay[Experience Replay]
    targetmove --> tnet[Target Networks]
    dist --> impweight[Importance Sampling / TRPO]
    fixes --> stable[Stable Generalized RL]
    classDef danger fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef cure fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    class fa,boot,offpol,instability,corr,targetmove,dist danger
    class replay,tnet,impweight,fixes,stable cure
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Each of the three red components contributes a distinct error type (correlation, moving target, distributional shift) that drives divergence. Green blocks show the corresponding stabilization remedy. KTU examiners expect a verbal mapping of this kind in 14-mark questions.

### 4.3 Generalized Policy Iteration with Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0[Policy pi_0] -->|Approx. Policy Eval| v0[Approx. Value V_w_0]
    v0 -->|Approx. Policy Improvement| p1[Policy pi_1]
    p1 -->|Approx. Policy Eval| v1[Approx. Value V_w_1]
    v1 -->|Approx. Policy Improvement| p2[Policy pi_2]
    p2 -->|... iterate ...| pN[Policy pi_ast]
    classDef step fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#566573,color:#000
    class p0,v0,p1,v1,p2,pN step
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Unlike tabular GPI, each step is an *approximation*. The value function is never exact, and the policy improvement step may use $\epsilon$-greedy or softmax, leading to oscillation around a near-optimal region.

### 4.4 Schematic: From Raw State to Generalized Value

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawS[Raw State s: pixel grid, joint angles, etc.] --> pipeS[Preprocessing]
    pipeS --> featS[Feature Vector x_s in R_d]
    featS --> mulS[Linear Layer: w_T x_s]
    mulS --> outS[Scalar Value V_hat_s]
    altrawS[Raw State s] --> nnS[Deep Network f_w]
    nnS --> altoutS[Value V_hat_s]
    outS -.->|linear approx| consS[Convergent Theory]
    altoutS -.->|deep approx| expS[Empirically Stable]
    classDef raw fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#0e6251,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1a5276,color:#000
    class rawS,pipeS,altrawS raw
    class featS,mulS,nnS mid
    class outS,altoutS,consS,expS out
```

> **Reading the diagram**: Two parallel paths — linear (yellow → green → blue) and deep (yellow → blue). The linear path has a mature convergence theory; the deep path relies on empirical regularizers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Define generalization in reinforcement learning. Why is it essential for large-scale problems? *(Module 4 — RL Adaptation)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Generalization in RL is the ability of an agent to produce accurate value estimates for states it has not encountered, by using a parameterized function approximator (such as a linear model or neural network) that captures the underlying structure of the value landscape. It is essential because in real-world problems the state space is either too large (e.g., Atari: $10^{1680}$ frames) or continuous (e.g., robotic joint angles), making tabular storage infeasible. Function approximation reduces the memory from $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$ to $\mathcal{O}(d)$ where $d$ is the number of parameters, and a single update improves the value estimate for *all* similar states — achieving sample-efficient learning. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for large-scale justification, 1 for memory/sample-efficiency point.)*

---

**Q2.** What is meant by the "deadly triad" in reinforcement learning? Name its three components. *(Module 4 — Function Approximation Stability)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The deadly triad refers to the combination of three elements in RL that, when present together, can cause the value function to diverge during learning. The three components are:
1. **Function approximation** — using a parameterized, compact representation that is only an approximation of the true value.
2. **Bootstrapping** — using the current value estimate as part of the target, e.g., $r + \gamma \hat{V}(s', \mathbf{w})$.
3. **Off-policy training** — learning about a target policy from data generated by a different behaviour policy.

This combination is dangerous because approximation errors can be amplified by the bootstrapped target, and the off-policy distribution mismatch compounds the error, leading to instability. *(3 marks: 1 for the term, 1 for all three components, 1 for the consequence.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. **Internal choice is mandatory** — the following two questions (A and B) are independent alternatives. Answer the one your examiner sets.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the value-function approximation approach in reinforcement learning. Derive the semi-gradient TD(0) update rule and discuss its convergence properties under linear function approximation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation and Setup (2 marks):**
In large state spaces, the tabular representation $\hat{V}(s)$ is infeasible. We instead use a parameterized function $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$ where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the learnable weight vector and $d \ll \vert \mathcal{S} \vert$. The quality of approximation is measured by the Mean Squared Value Error:

$$\text{MSVE}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_{s \in \mathcal{S}} \mu(s) \left[ V^{\pi}(s) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \right]^2$$

where $\mu(s)$ is the stationary distribution of the on-policy state visitation.

**Step 2 — Gradient of MSVE (2 marks):**
Differentiating with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \text{MSVE}(\mathbf{w}) = -2 \sum_{s} \mu(s) \left[ V^{\pi}(s) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \right] \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$$

A sample at $s_t$ gives the stochastic estimate, and using the one-step TD target $U_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(s_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t)$ as a substitute for $V^{\pi}(s_t)$:

**Step 3 — Semi-Gradient Update (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{V}(s_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{V}(s_t, \mathbf{w}_t) \right] \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{V}(s_t, \mathbf{w}_t)}$$

The bracketed term is the **TD error** $\delta_t$. The method is *semi*-gradient because $\hat{V}(s_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t)$ depends on $\mathbf{w}$ but is treated as a constant when differentiating.

**Step 4 — Convergence Properties (1 mark):**
For linear function approximation $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$, the TD(0) update converges (in expectation) to the unique **TD fixed point** $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$, where $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{D} (\mathbf{I} - \gamma \mathbf{P}) \mathbf{X}$ and $\mathbf{b} = \mathbb{E}[r \mathbf{x}(s)]$. The residual MSVE is bounded by $\frac{1 - \gamma \lambda}{1 - \gamma} \cdot \text{MSVE}_{\min}$, which is strictly greater than the minimum possible error — the price of bootstrapping.

---

**(b)** What is the deadly triad in RL? Explain how Deep Q-Networks (DQN) overcome the instability caused by the deadly triad in Q-learning. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recap of the Deadly Triad (2 marks):**
The deadly triad consists of:
1. **Function Approximation** — introduces approximation error at every state; small parameter changes can have large effects.
2. **Bootstrapping** — target $r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a', \mathbf{w})$ depends on the very parameters being learned, creating a moving-target problem.
3. **Off-policy Learning** — the target uses the *greedy* action (different from the $\epsilon$-greedy behaviour policy), introducing a distribution mismatch.

In Q-learning with a neural network, all three are present, and the value function can diverge or oscillate.

**Step 2 — DQN's Two Stabilization Mechanisms (4 marks):**

**(i) Experience Replay**: The agent stores transitions $(s, a, r, s')$ in a replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$. Each update samples a minibatch uniformly at random. This:
- Breaks temporal correlation between consecutive samples (mitigates the correlation error introduced by function approximation).
- Allows each transition to be used in multiple updates, improving sample efficiency.

**(ii) Target Network**: A separate network with parameters $\mathbf{w}^{-}$ is held fixed for $C$ update steps and then copied from the online $\mathbf{w}$. The DQN loss becomes:

$$L(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a', \mathbf{w}^{-}) - \hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}) \right)^2 \right]$$

This decouples the target from the rapidly changing online parameters, **stabilizing the moving target** caused by bootstrapping.

**Step 3 — Remaining Issue and Resolution (1 mark):**
The off-policy aspect (max over actions under a different distribution) is not fully resolved by replay and target nets, but their combination empirically yields stable learning on Atari. Later variants — Double DQN, Prioritized Replay, Rainbow — further tighten the off-policy bias.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss the difference between on-policy and off-policy learning. Show how the semi-gradient Sarsa and Q-learning update rules differ in the context of function approximation, and explain the convergence implications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (1 mark):**
- *On-policy*: the agent learns about the policy it is currently executing. Example: Sarsa.
- *Off-policy*: the agent learns about a *target* policy (e.g., greedy) while following a *behaviour* policy (e.g., $\epsilon$-greedy). Example: Q-learning.

**Step 2 — Sarsa Semi-Gradient Update (2 marks):**
For action-value approximation $\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w})$, Sarsa uses the actual next action $a_{t+1}$ taken under the behaviour policy:

$$\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \hat{Q}(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}, \mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{Q}(s_t, a_t, \mathbf{w}_t) \right] \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{Q}(s_t, a_t, \mathbf{w}_t)$$

**Step 3 — Q-Learning Semi-Gradient Update (2 marks):**
Q-learning replaces the next action with the **greedy** maximizer:

$$\mathbf{w}_{t+1} = \mathbf{w}_t + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s_{t+1}, a', \mathbf{w}_t) - \hat{Q}(s_t, a_t, \mathbf{w}_t) \right] \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{Q}(s_t, a_t, \mathbf{w}_t)$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Implications (2 marks):**
- Sarsa with linear approximation **converges** to a near-optimal value function (under standard i.i.d. sampling and step-size conditions).
- Q-learning with linear approximation **is not guaranteed to converge** in the general off-policy setting — it forms the full deadly triad. The tabular convergence proof for Q-learning relies on visiting every $(s,a)$ pair infinitely often, a guarantee lost under function approximation with a fixed parameter budget.

---

**(b)** Describe feature construction methods in reinforcement learning. Compare tile coding and Fourier basis as two approaches to designing the feature vector $\mathbf{x}(s)$ for linear function approximation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why Feature Construction Matters (1 mark):**
The quality of generalization in linear approximation depends entirely on the feature representation. A poor choice of $\mathbf{x}(s)$ limits expressiveness; a rich choice enables accurate value estimation with few parameters.

**Step 2 — Tile Coding (3 marks):**
- **Mechanism**: Partition the state space into multiple overlapping tilings, each consisting of disjoint tiles. The feature $x_i(s)$ is 1 if state $s$ falls in tile $i$, else 0.
- **Generalization**: A state generalizes to all other states in the same tile. With $T$ tilings, the *resolution* is the tile width, and generalization extent is the union of one tile from each tiling.
- **Advantages**: Binary, sparse features → fast computation; robust to noise; works well in continuous low-dim spaces.
- **Disadvantages**: Scales poorly to high dimensions (curse of dimensionality — number of tiles grows exponentially).

**Step 3 — Fourier Basis (2 marks):**
- **Mechanism**: The feature vector components are sine/cosine functions of state coordinates: $x_i(s) = \cos(\pi \, \mathbf{c}_i^{\top} s)$ for integer coefficient vectors $\mathbf{c}_i \in \{0, 1, \dots, N\}^d$.
- **Generalization**: A state generalizes smoothly to neighbouring states — the basis is smooth and differentiable.
- **Advantages**: Naturally suited to smooth value functions; a clean way to control the number of basis functions; theoretical approximation guarantees.
- **Disadvantages**: Requires bounded state ranges; not sparse; sensitive to choice of coefficient range $N$.

**Step 4 — Comparison Summary (1 mark):**
| Property | Tile Coding | Fourier Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Sparsity | High (binary) | Low (continuous) |
| Smoothness | Discontinuous tiles | Smooth sinusoidal |
| Hyperparameter sensitivity | Number of tilings, offsets | Coefficient bound $N$ |
| Best for | Discrete, noise-tolerant control | Smooth analytic value functions |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (14-Mark Questions)**:
> 1. **Losing 2 marks**: Forgetting to define $\mu(s)$ or the stationary distribution in the MSVE derivation.
> 2. **Losing 1 mark**: Writing the TD update as a *full* gradient (treating $\hat{V}(s_{t+1}, \mathbf{w})$ as constant but forgetting to take its gradient). The term *semi-gradient* must appear explicitly.
> 3. **Losing 2 marks**: In the DQN question, omitting the role of the **target network** or the **replay buffer** — both are essential for full marks.
> 4. **Losing 1 mark**: For off-policy questions, not mentioning that tabular convergence proofs for Q-learning do not extend to function approximation.
> 5. **Losing 1 mark**: Conflating "policy evaluation" with "control" — these are distinct algorithmic stages in GPI.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Generalization in RL**

- [x] **Definition**: Generalization = ability to estimate values in unseen states via a *parameterized* function $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w})$ or $\hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w})$.
- [x] **Why needed**: Tabular methods scale as $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{S} \vert)$; function approximation scales as $\mathcal{O}(d)$.
- [x] **Linear approximation** is the most theoretically tractable: $\hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}(s)$.
- [x] **MSVE** is the standard loss: $\text{MSVE}(\mathbf{w}) = \sum_s \mu(s) \left[ V^{\pi}(s) - \hat{V}(s, \mathbf{w}) \right]^2$.
- [x] **Semi-gradient TD(0) update** is the workhorse: $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} + \alpha \, \delta_t \, \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{V}(s_t, \mathbf{w})$.
- [x] **The Deadly Triad** = *Approximation + Bootstrapping + Off-policy*. Memorize this and its consequences.
- [x] **TD fixed point**: $\mathbf{w}_{TD} = \mathbf{A}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ for linear approximation; **not** equal to the MSVE minimum.
- [x] **DQN stabilizers**: experience replay + target network + $\epsilon$-greedy exploration. Loss: $L = \mathbb{E}[(r + \gamma \max_{a'} \hat{Q}(s', a', \mathbf{w}^{-}) - \hat{Q}(s, a, \mathbf{w}))^2]$.
- [x] **On-policy Sarsa** converges; **off-policy Q-learning** with function approximation may diverge.
- [x] **Feature construction methods**: tile coding (sparse, binary), Fourier basis (smooth), radial basis functions (Gaussian), neural embeddings.
- [x] **Eligibility traces** $(\lambda)$ extend semi-gradient TD to $n$-step credit assignment: $\mathbf{z}_{t+1} = \gamma \lambda \mathbf{z}_t + \nabla \hat{V}(s_t, \mathbf{w}_t)$.
- [x] **Convergence residual bound**: $\text{MSVE} \leq \frac{1 - \gamma \lambda}{1 - \gamma} \cdot \text{MSVE}_{\min} + \mathcal{O}(\alpha)$ — know the $\gamma, \lambda$ dependence.
- [x] **Engineering rule of thumb**: choose features that *match the smoothness of the true value function* — smoothness in $\mathbf{x}(s)$ implies smoothness in $\hat{V}$.
- [x] **Mnemonic — ABO** (Approximation, Bootstrapping, Off-policy) → divergence.
- [x] **Mnemonic — DQN cures triad with**: **R**eplay + **T**arget + ($\epsilon$-)greedy — "**R**ep**T**ile" in your memory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Policy Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Policy Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Policy Search** is a class of **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** algorithms that directly optimize a **parameterized policy** $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ by searching the policy parameter space $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^d$ to maximize an **expected return objective function** $J(\theta)$. Unlike value-based methods, policy search does not require the explicit construction of a value function as an intermediate step.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, policy search is studied as a **direct policy optimization** paradigm in which the agent learns a mapping from states to a probability distribution over actions, parameterized by a vector $\theta$. The learning algorithm updates $\theta$ along the **gradient of expected cumulative reward**.

Mathematically, the policy is represented as:

$$\pi_\theta(a \mid s) = P(a \mid s, \theta)$$

The objective is to find:

$$\theta^{*} = \arg\max_{\theta} J(\theta) = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[R(\tau)\right]$$

where $\tau = (s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, \ldots)$ is a trajectory sampled by executing the policy, and $R(\tau) = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t r_{t+1}$ is the **discounted return** with discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Apprentice Chef:**
> Imagine an apprentice chef who is learning to cook without a recipe book. Instead of memorizing which dish gives a 5-star rating (value function), the chef directly experiments with different ingredient combinations (policy parameters). After each dish, the customer gives feedback (reward). The chef reinforces combinations that received praise and adjusts those that received criticism. Over time, the chef's "cooking policy" is directly optimized through experience — this is **Policy Search**.

The key idea is **trial-and-error learning of a strategy**, rather than learning to predict how good each strategy would be (which is what value-based methods do).

## 1.3 Why Policy Search?

> [!TIP]
> **Three core motivations for using Policy Search:**
> 1. **Continuous Action Spaces**: Naturally handles high-dimensional or continuous action spaces (e.g., robotic joint torques), where value-based methods like Q-learning fail.
> 2. **Stochastic Policies**: Can learn **stochastic optimal policies**, which are required in partially observable or adversarial environments.
> 3. **Smooth Optimization**: The policy parameter space can be searched using **gradient-based methods**, ensuring stable convergence.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| Policy $\pi_\theta$ | Parameterized mapping from states to action probabilities |
| Parameter vector $\theta$ | Learnable weights of the policy (often neural network) |
| Episode / Trajectory $\tau$ | A complete sequence of states, actions, rewards |
| Return $R(\tau)$ | Cumulative discounted reward along a trajectory |
| Objective $J(\theta)$ | Expected return under the current policy |
| Step-size $\alpha$ | Learning rate used in gradient ascent updates |
| Baseline $b(s)$ | A reference value used to reduce gradient variance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Policy Search Pipeline

The policy search procedure follows a structured four-stage loop:

1. **Policy Parameterization**: Choose a differentiable function approximator (e.g., linear softmax, neural network) to represent $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$.
2. **Trajectory Sampling**: Roll out the current policy in the environment (or a simulator) to collect a batch of episodes $\mathcal{D} = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_N\}$.
3. **Return Estimation**: For each trajectory, compute the discounted return $R(\tau_i) = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t r_{t+1}$.
4. **Parameter Update**: Adjust $\theta$ in the direction of higher expected return using the **policy gradient**:
   
   $$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \, \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$$

This loop is repeated until the policy converges to a (locally) optimal $\theta^{*}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Policy Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why optimize the policy directly?**
> Value-based methods (Q-learning, SARSA) suffer from the **maximization bias** and struggle with **continuous actions**. Policy search bypasses the value function entirely, treating the policy as a black box that we improve using gradient signals from sampled experience.

## 2.3 The "How" — Policy Gradient Theorem

The cornerstone of policy search is the **Policy Gradient Theorem**, which provides a closed-form expression for $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ that does not require differentiation through the environment dynamics.

**Statement of the Theorem:**

For a differentiable policy $\pi_\theta$ and a Markov Decision Process (MDP), the gradient of the objective is:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, \, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right]$$

where:
- $d^\pi(s)$ is the **stationary state distribution** under policy $\pi_\theta$
- $Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)$ is the **action-value function** of the policy
- $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ is the **score function**

## 2.4 Variance Reduction — The Baseline Trick

The raw policy gradient estimator has **high variance** because it uses stochastic Monte-Carlo returns. The **baseline trick** subtracts a state-dependent function $b(s)$ from $Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)$ without introducing bias:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot \left(Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a) - b(s)\right)\right]$$

A common choice is $b(s) = V^{\pi_\theta}(s)$, which gives the **advantage function**:

$$A^{\pi}(s, a) = Q^{\pi}(s, a) - V^{\pi}(s)$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Use in Policy Search |
|------------------|---------|----------------------|
| $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ | Parameterized policy | Defines the agent's behaviour |
| $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\tau[R(\tau)]$ | Objective function | Quantity to maximize |
| $\nabla_\theta J(\theta)$ | Policy gradient | Direction of parameter update |
| $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ | Score function | Likelihood ratio weight |
| $R(\tau) = \sum \gamma^t r_t$ | Discounted return | Monte-Carlo target |
| $Q^{\pi}(s, a)$ | Action-value | Quality of taking action $a$ in state $s$ |
| $A^{\pi}(s, a) = Q - V$ | Advantage function | Variance-reduced gradient weight |
| $b(s) = V^{\pi}(s)$ | Baseline (often value function) | Reduces estimator variance |
| $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_\theta J$ | Gradient ascent update | Rule for learning $\theta$ |
| $\gamma \in [0,1)$ | Discount factor | Trades off immediate vs future reward |
| $\alpha$ | Learning rate | Step size for gradient ascent |
| $\mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi}$ | Expectation over state distribution | Averages across visited states |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

Policy search is the **backbone of modern AI systems** in:
- **Robotics** (continuous torque control, manipulation)
- **Autonomous Driving** (lane-keeping, obstacle avoidance)
- **Game Playing** (AlphaGo, AlphaStar, OpenAI Five)
- **Large Language Model Fine-Tuning** (RLHF — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)
- **Recommendation Systems** (long-term user engagement)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Policy Gradient Theorem

We derive the policy gradient from first principles. Let the MDP have horizon $T$, discount factor $\gamma$, and reward $r_{t+1}$ received after action $a_t$ in state $s_t$.

### Step 1 — Define the Objective

The expected return under a stochastic policy is:

$$J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[R(\tau)\right] = \sum_{\tau} P(\tau \mid \theta) \, R(\tau)$$

where $P(\tau \mid \theta)$ is the probability of trajectory $\tau$ under policy $\pi_\theta$.

### Step 2 — Expand the Trajectory Probability

The probability of a trajectory factorizes as:

$$P(\tau \mid \theta) = p(s_0) \prod_{t=0}^{T-1} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \, p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

Here, $p(s_0)$ is the initial state distribution and $p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ is the environment dynamics. The only $\theta$-dependent term is the policy.

### Step 3 — Differentiate the Objective

Differentiating $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_{\tau} \nabla_\theta P(\tau \mid \theta) \, R(\tau)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Log-Derivative Trick

Using the identity $\nabla P = P \cdot \nabla \log P$ (since $\nabla \log P = \frac{\nabla P}{P}$):

$$\nabla_\theta P(\tau \mid \theta) = P(\tau \mid \theta) \cdot \nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta)$$

Substituting:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_{\tau} P(\tau \mid \theta) \, \nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) \, R(\tau)$$

### Step 5 — Recognize the Expectation

This is precisely the definition of an expectation under the trajectory distribution:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) \, R(\tau)\right]$$

### Step 6 — Use the Factorization to Simplify

Since $p(s_0)$ and $p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$ do not depend on $\theta$:

$$\log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \log p(s_0) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \left[\log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) + \log p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)\right]$$

Differentiating and dropping the non-$\theta$ terms:

$$\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

### Step 7 — Substitute Back

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[\left(\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\right) R(\tau)\right]$$

### Step 8 — Causality (Reward-to-Go)

We can restrict the sum to rewards **after** time $t$ without loss of generality (causality). Define the **reward-to-go**:

$$\hat{R}_t = \sum_{t'=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{t'-t} r_{t'+1}$$

This yields the **REINFORCE gradient**:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot \hat{R}_t\right]$$

### Step 9 — Final Form (Policy Gradient Theorem)

Rewriting in state-action form using the state-visitation distribution $d^\pi$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, \, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right]$$

This completes the derivation of the **Policy Gradient Theorem**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the REINFORCE Update Rule

### Step 1 — Sample-Based Approximation

In practice, we cannot compute the expectation analytically. We approximate it using a **single trajectory** $\tau$ sampled from $\pi_\theta$:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \approx \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot \hat{R}_t$$

### Step 2 — Stochastic Gradient Ascent

Apply the update:

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \alpha_k \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot \hat{R}_t$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: This is **REINFORCE** (Monte-Carlo Policy Gradient). The factor $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ pushes the policy to make action $a_t$ **more likely** if $\hat{R}_t$ is positive, and **less likely** if $\hat{R}_t$ is negative.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Baseline Correction

### Step 1 — Show Adding a Baseline Does Not Change the Expectation

Consider subtracting a state-only baseline $b(s_t)$ from $\hat{R}_t$:

$$E_b = \mathbb{E}_{a_t \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot b(s_t)\right]$$

Compute the inner expectation:

$$E_b = b(s_t) \sum_{a_t} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \, \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

Using the identity $\pi \cdot \nabla \log \pi = \nabla \pi$:

$$E_b = b(s_t) \sum_{a_t} \nabla_\theta \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = b(s_t) \, \nabla_\theta \sum_{a_t} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

### Step 2 — Probability Sums to One

Since $\sum_{a_t} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) = 1$ and $\nabla_\theta 1 = 0$:

$$E_b = b(s_t) \cdot 0 = 0$$

### Step 3 — Unbiased Variance Reduction

Therefore:

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot \left(\hat{R}_t - b(s_t)\right)\right]$$

The expectation is unchanged, but the **variance is reduced** — the celebrated **baseline trick**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Trade-offs

| Method | Uses Value Function? | Variance | Bias | On/Off-Policy | Continuous Actions |
|--------|----------------------|----------|------|---------------|---------------------|
| REINFORCE | No | High | None | On-policy | Yes |
| REINFORCE + Baseline | Optional (for $b$) | Medium | None | On-policy | Yes |
| Actor-Critic | Yes (Critic) | Low | Low | Both | Yes |
| A2C / A3C | Yes (shared) | Low | Low | On-policy | Yes |
| PPO | Yes (clipped) | Low | Low | On-policy | Yes |
| DDPG | Yes (Q-net) | Low | Low | Off-policy | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Policy Search Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Policy Parameters theta] --> B[Roll Out Current Policy in Environment]
    B --> C[Collect Trajectory tau and Reward Sequence]
    C --> D[Compute Discounted Return R of tau]
    D --> E[Compute Score Function grad log pi of a given s theta]
    E --> F[Multiply by Return to Estimate Policy Gradient]
    F --> G[Update Parameters via Gradient Ascent]
    G --> H{Convergence Achieved?}
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[Output Optimal Policy pi star]
```

## 4.2 Actor-Critic Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Environment] -->|State s_t| ACTOR[Actor Network pi theta]
    ACTOR -->|Action a_t| ENV
    ENV -->|Reward r_t+1| CRITIC[Critic Network V phi]
    CRITIC -->|Value Estimate V of s_t| ADV[Advantage Estimator]
    ACTOR -->|Log Prob log pi| ADV
    ADV -->|Advantage Signal| PG[Policy Gradient Computation]
    PG -->|Update theta| ACTOR
    CRITIC -->|Update phi| CRITIC
    ACTOR -->|Update theta| ACTOR
```

## 4.3 Policy Search Algorithm Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PS[Policy Search Methods] --> GD[Gradient Based]
    PS --> GFL[Gradient Free]
    GD --> RF[REINFORCE]
    GD --> AC[Actor Critic]
    RF --> RFB[REINFORCE with Baseline]
    AC --> A2C[A2C]
    AC --> PPO[PPO]
    AC --> TRPO[TRPO]
    AC --> DDPG[DDPG]
    AC --> SAC[SAC]
    GFL --> ES[Evolution Strategies]
    GFL --> HC[Hill Climbing]
    GFL --> GA[Genetic Algorithms]
    GFL --> CMA[CMA-ES]
```

## 4.4 Information Flow in a Policy Gradient Update

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[State s_t at time t] --> P1[Policy Network pi theta]
    P1 --> A1[Action a_t sampled from pi]
    A1 --> S2[Next State s_t+1 and Reward r_t+1]
    S2 --> S1
    S2 --> R[Return Calculator R tau]
    R --> G[Gradient Estimator using Score Function]
    P1 --> G
    G --> U[Parameter Update theta new]
    U --> P1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern**: Part A contains short-answer questions testing **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. Each carries **3 marks** and expects a crisp, definition-style answer.

### Question 1: Define Policy Search in Reinforcement Learning. List any two advantages over value-based methods. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Policy Search is a reinforcement learning approach in which the agent directly optimizes a parameterized policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ by searching the parameter space $\theta$ to maximize the expected return $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[R(\tau)]$, without explicitly learning a value function.

**Advantages (2 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Handles Continuous Action Spaces**: Policy search naturally parametrizes policies over continuous action sets, whereas Q-learning requires maximizing over actions, which is intractable in continuous domains.

2. **Learns Stochastic Policies**: Policy search can learn stochastic optimal policies, which are essential in partially observable or non-Markovian environments (e.g., rock-paper-scissors where a deterministic policy is exploitable).

*(Optional third point for completeness):* **Smoother Optimization Landscape**: The objective $J(\theta)$ is a smooth, differentiable function of $\theta$, enabling gradient-based optimization with well-behaved convergence guarantees.

---

### Question 2: Write the REINFORCE update rule and explain the role of the score function in it. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Update Rule (1.5 Marks):**

$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \alpha_k \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot \hat{R}_t$$

where $\hat{R}_t = \sum_{t'=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{t'-t} r_{t'+1}$ is the **reward-to-go** from time $t$ onwards.

**Role of the Score Function (1.5 Marks):**
The score function $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$ is the **gradient of the log-probability** of taking action $a_t$ in state $s_t$. It indicates the direction in which $\theta$ must be changed to **increase the probability** of selecting $a_t$. When multiplied by the return $\hat{R}_t$:
- If $\hat{R}_t > 0$, the action is reinforced (made more likely).
- If $\hat{R}_t < 0$, the action is suppressed (made less likely).

This is the **fundamental learning signal** in policy gradient methods.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern**: Part B contains analytical/descriptive questions targeting **Apply**, **Analyze**, and **Evaluate** levels. Each carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) State and prove the Policy Gradient Theorem. Show how the log-derivative trick simplifies the derivation. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Statement (2 Marks):**

> *For a differentiable policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ in an MDP with discount factor $\gamma$, the gradient of the expected return objective $J(\theta)$ is given by:*
> $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, \, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right]$$

**Proof (4 Marks):**

1. **Define the objective**:
   $$J(\theta) = \sum_{\tau} P(\tau \mid \theta) R(\tau)$$

2. **Differentiate using the log-derivative trick**:
   $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \sum_{\tau} \nabla_\theta P(\tau \mid \theta) R(\tau) = \sum_{\tau} P(\tau \mid \theta) \nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) R(\tau)$$

3. **Factorize trajectory probability**:
   $$P(\tau \mid \theta) = p(s_0) \prod_{t=0}^{T-1} \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$$

4. **Differentiate the log-probability** (only the policy depends on $\theta$):
   $$\nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta) = \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)$$

5. **Substitute and convert to expectation**:
   $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) R(\tau)\right]$$

6. **Apply causality and convert to state-action form**:
   $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{s \sim d^\pi, \, a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)\right]$$

**Significance of the Log-Derivative Trick (1 Mark):**
The trick $\nabla P = P \cdot \nabla \log P$ converts the intractable gradient of an expectation into a tractable expectation of a gradient, **avoiding differentiation through the unknown environment dynamics** $p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$.

#### (b) Explain the Actor-Critic architecture. How does it reduce variance compared to vanilla REINFORCE? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (3 Marks):**

The Actor-Critic method consists of two components:
- **Actor**: A policy network $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ that selects actions.
- **Critic**: A value network $V_\phi(s)$ (or $Q_\phi(s, a)$) that estimates the expected return.

The critic provides a **low-variance baseline** for the actor's gradient updates.

**Variance Reduction (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | REINFORCE | Actor-Critic |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Return estimate | Monte-Carlo $R(\tau)$ — high variance | Bootstrapped $V_\phi(s_t)$ — low variance |
| Update timing | End of episode (offline) | Every step (online) |
| Bias | Unbiased | Slight bias (compensated by variance drop) |
| Sample efficiency | Low (needs full episodes) | High (reuses value estimates) |

The critic's value estimate $V_\phi(s_t)$ serves as a **baseline**, yielding the **advantage function**:

$$A(s_t, a_t) = r_{t+1} + \gamma V_\phi(s_{t+1}) - V_\phi(s_t)$$

The actor's update becomes:

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \cdot A(s_t, a_t)$$

Since $A$ has **lower variance** than raw returns, gradient estimates become more stable, leading to **faster convergence**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Compare and contrast policy search and value-based reinforcement learning. Discuss when each is preferred. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Criterion | Value-Based RL | Policy Search |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Learns | Value function $V(s)$ or $Q(s, a)$ | Policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ directly |
| Action selection | $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$ | Sample from $\pi_\theta$ |
| Continuous actions | Difficult (requires inner maximization) | Natural (policy outputs action directly) |
| Stochastic policies | Hard (greedy by default) | Natural |
| Exploration | $\epsilon$-greedy, UCB | Inherent via stochastic policy |
| Convergence | Strong guarantees in tabular case | Local optima possible |
| Sample efficiency | Often higher with replay buffer | Often lower (on-policy by default) |
| Function approximation | Stable with linear, unstable with NN | Stable with policy gradient theorem |

**When Each is Preferred (3 Marks):**

- **Prefer Value-Based** when the action space is **discrete and small**, the environment is fully observable, and **sample efficiency** is critical (e.g., Atari games, grid worlds).
- **Prefer Policy Search** when the action space is **continuous or high-dimensional** (e.g., robotics), **stochastic optimal policies** are required, or the policy must be **smoothly differentiable**.

#### (b) Explain the REINFORCE algorithm with a baseline. Derive the gradient update and show that the baseline does not introduce bias. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Algorithm (2 Marks):**

> **REINFORCE with Baseline:**
> 1. Initialize $\theta$ and baseline parameters $\phi$ (e.g., $\hat{V}_\phi$).
> 2. **For each episode**:
>    - Sample trajectory $\tau$ using $\pi_\theta$.
>    - For each time step $t$:
>      - Compute $\hat{R}_t = \sum_{t'=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{t'-t} r_{t'+1}$.
>      - Compute advantage $\delta_t = \hat{R}_t - \hat{V}_\phi(s_t)$.
>      - Update policy: $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) \delta_t$.
>      - Update baseline: $\phi \leftarrow \phi + \beta (\hat{R}_t - \hat{V}_\phi(s_t)) \nabla_\phi \hat{V}_\phi(s_t)$.

**Gradient Derivation (2 Marks):**

$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) \approx \sum_{t} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t) (\hat{R}_t - b(s_t))$$

**Bias Proof (3 Marks):**

We need to show:

$$\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot b(s)\right] = 0$$

Expand the expectation:

$$= b(s) \sum_a \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$$

Using $\pi \nabla \log \pi = \nabla \pi$:

$$= b(s) \sum_a \nabla_\theta \pi_\theta(a \mid s) = b(s) \cdot \nabla_\theta \left[\sum_a \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\right]$$

Since the policy is a valid probability distribution:

$$\sum_a \pi_\theta(a \mid s) = 1 \implies \nabla_\theta 1 = 0$$

Therefore, the baseline contributes **zero** to the expected gradient — it is **unbiased**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting causality**: In REINFORCE, students often use $R(\tau)$ for all timesteps instead of the reward-to-go $\hat{R}_t$. This biases the gradient. Always use $\hat{R}_t = \sum_{t'=t}^{T-1} \gamma^{t'-t} r_{t'+1}$. (Lose 1-2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing $\nabla \pi$ with $\nabla \log \pi$**: These are not equal. The log-derivative trick $\nabla P = P \cdot \nabla \log P$ is the key step — writing $\nabla \log \pi$ where $\nabla \pi$ is needed (or vice versa) loses significant marks. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Dropping the discount factor $\gamma$**: The expectation in the policy gradient theorem may include $\gamma^t$ weighting; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing Actor and Critic roles**: In Actor-Critic questions, students often describe only the actor or only the critic. Both must be explained, along with the TD-error as the advantage estimate. (Lose 2-3 marks)
> 5. **Confusing on-policy vs off-policy**: REINFORCE is strictly on-policy; sampling data from a different policy invalidates the gradient estimator. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Policy Search:**

- **Definition**: Policy Search directly optimizes a parameterized policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ to maximize expected return $J(\theta)$.
- **Objective**: $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[R(\tau)]$, where $R(\tau) = \sum_t \gamma^t r_{t+1}$.
- **Policy Gradient Theorem**: $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s) \cdot Q^{\pi_\theta}(s, a)]$.
- **Log-Derivative Trick**: $\nabla_\theta P(\tau \mid \theta) = P(\tau \mid \theta) \cdot \nabla_\theta \log P(\tau \mid \theta)$ — avoids differentiating environment dynamics.
- **REINFORCE**: Monte-Carlo policy gradient using sampled returns; high variance, unbiased, on-policy.
- **Score Function**: $\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ — points in the direction of higher action probability.
- **Baseline Trick**: Subtracting $b(s)$ from returns does not bias the gradient but reduces variance.
- **Advantage Function**: $A(s, a) = Q(s, a) - V(s)$ — ideal low-variance gradient weight.
- **Actor-Critic**: Two networks — Actor (policy) and Critic (value) — work together to balance bias and variance.
- **Causality**: Use reward-to-go $\hat{R}_t$ in REINFORCE, not the full trajectory return.
- **Applications**: Continuous control, robotics, RLHF, game AI, recommendation systems.
- **Comparison**: Policy search handles continuous actions and stochastic policies better; value-based methods are more sample-efficient in discrete domains.
- **Algorithm Family**: REINFORCE → REINFORCE w/ Baseline → Actor-Critic → A2C → PPO → TRPO → DDPG → SAC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apprenticeship and Inverse Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Apprenticeship and Inverse Reinforcement Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Apprenticeship Learning (AL)** is a paradigm in Artificial Intelligence where an agent learns a policy by observing the behavior of an expert demonstrator, **without** being explicitly told the reward function that the expert is optimizing. The goal is to recover a policy whose performance is at least as good as the expert's, on the *true* underlying reward structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL)** is the foundational sub-problem of Apprenticeship Learning. Given measurements of an expert's behavior (i.e., a trajectory or state-action distribution), IRL aims to **recover the reward function** that the expert is presumed to be maximizing. Formally, it inverts the standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) mapping from *reward → policy* into a mapping from *policy → reward*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **RL**: Given $R(s,a)$, find optimal $\pi^*$.
> - **IRL**: Given expert $\pi_E$, find $R(s,a)$.
> - **Apprenticeship Learning**: Given expert $\pi_E$, find $\pi$ that performs as well as $\pi_E$ on the *true* $R$, **even if we never recover $R$ explicitly**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a child learning to ride a bicycle by **watching a parent cycle** (the expert). The child is **not told the exact physics equations** (the "reward function") the parent is implicitly optimizing — i.e., balance, speed, avoiding falls, reaching a destination. Instead, the child tries to **mimic the trajectory** (the demonstrations). Over time, the child infers *what was important* (don't fall, move forward) and eventually rides **even better** than the parent in some unseen terrain. This is exactly Apprenticeship Learning.

> [!TIP]
> **The Driving Motivation:** In many real-world domains (autonomous driving, robotic manipulation, helicopter aerobatics), **designing a reward function is harder than learning the task itself**. The expert *knows* the task implicitly through demonstrations. IRL/AL lets us extract that knowledge without hand-engineering $R$.

### 1.3 Why These Methods Matter (Engineering Relevance)

- **Autonomous Driving**: Reward for "good driving" is multi-dimensional, hard to specify. We learn it from human demonstrations.
- **Robotic Manipulation**: Easier to demonstrate a grasp than to write a reward for "stable, collision-free, force-compliant grasping."
- **Healthcare / Game AI**: Capture *expert intent* rather than brittle hand-crafted heuristics.

| Concept | Input | Output | Goal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reinforcement Learning | Reward $R$ | Policy $\pi$ | Maximize cumulative reward |
| Inverse RL | Expert policy $\pi_E$ | Reward $R$ | Explain expert behavior |
| Apprenticeship Learning | Expert $\pi_E$ | Policy $\pi$ | Match/beat expert performance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Markov Decision Process (MDP) Backbone

All three settings operate on an MDP, defined as the tuple:

$$
\langle S, A, P, \gamma, R \rangle
$$

Where:
- $S$ : Set of states
- $A$ : Set of actions
- $P(s' \mid s,a)$ : Transition probability
- $\gamma \in [0,1)$ : Discount factor
- $R: S \times A \to \mathbb{R}$ : Reward function

> [!NOTE]
> In Apprenticeship Learning and IRL, **the reward function $R$ is unknown**. We are given either a *trajectory* $\tau = (s_0, a_0, s_1, a_1, \dots)$ or a *stationary policy* $\pi_E$ demonstrated by an expert.

### 2.2 Core Logic of Apprenticeship Learning (Step-by-Step)

1. **Assume** the unknown reward is a **linear combination of known features**:
$$
R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)
$$
   where $\phi(s) \in \mathbb{R}^k$ is a hand-crafted feature vector and $\mathbf{w}$ is the unknown weight vector.

2. **Define Feature Expectation (FE):** The expected cumulative discounted feature count under policy $\pi$:
$$
\mu(\pi) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t) \mid \pi \right]
$$

3. **Expert Feature Expectation:** Compute (or estimate) $\mu_E = \mu(\pi_E)$ from demonstrations.

4. **Goal:** Find a learner policy $\pi$ such that:
$$
\vert \mathbf{w}^T \mu(\pi) - \mathbf{w}^T \mu_E \vert \le \epsilon
$$
   for *all* possible reward weights $\mathbf{w}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \le 1$. This is equivalent to:
$$
\Vert \mu(\pi) - \mu_E \Vert_2 \le \epsilon
$$

5. **Output:** A policy $\pi$ whose **feature expectations are provably close to the expert's**, guaranteeing near-expert performance under any linear reward.

### 2.3 Core Logic of Inverse Reinforcement Learning

1. **Input:** Expert policy $\pi_E$ (or trajectories from it).
2. **Assumption:** $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$ for unknown $\mathbf{w}$.
3. **Constraint:** Expert's policy must be optimal under recovered $R$.
4. **Output:** $\mathbf{w}$ (reward weights) that justifies the expert's behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ill-Posed Nature of IRL:** There are infinitely many reward functions that make the expert's policy optimal. This is why **Maximum Margin IRL (Abbeel & Ng, 2004)**, **Bayesian IRL (Ramachandran & Amir, 2007)**, and **Maximum Entropy IRL (Ziebart et al., 2008)** add different priors to disambiguate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $\mu(\pi) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t) \right]$ | Feature expectation of policy $\pi$ | AL / IRL |
| $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$ | Linear reward assumption | Both |
| $\Vert \mu(\pi) - \mu_E \Vert_2 \le \epsilon$ | Apprenticeship matching objective | AL |
| $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_t \gamma^t R(s_t)\right]$ | Value function under $\pi$ | RL/IRL foundation |
| $Q^{\pi}(s,a) = R(s) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^{\pi}(s')$ | Q-value recursion | IRL optimality constraint |
| $\mu_E = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\gamma^t \phi(s_t^{(i)})$ | Empirical estimate from $m$ trajectories | IRL / AL |
| $\max_{\mathbf{w}} \min_j \; \mathbf{w}^T(\mu_E - \mu_j)$ | Max-margin IRL objective | IRL |
| $P(\tau) \propto \exp\left(\mathbf{w}^T \phi(\tau)\right)$ | MaxEnt IRL trajectory distribution | MaxEnt IRL |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Feature Expectation as Policy Linear Operator

We start with the standard value function under a linear reward $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$:

$$
V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R(s_t) \right] = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s_t) \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Linearity of expectation:** Swap the sum and the dot product:

$$
V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \cdot \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the inner term as the feature expectation:**

$$
\mu(\pi) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t) \right] \in \mathbb{R}^k
$$

**Step 3 — Final form:**

$$
\boxed{V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \mu(\pi)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Because $V^{\pi}(s_0)$ is a dot product of $\mathbf{w}$ with $\mu(\pi)$, **matching $\mu(\pi)$ to $\mu_E$ guarantees matching performance for *any* linear reward $\mathbf{w}$**. This is the algebraic heart of Apprenticeship Learning.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Apprenticeship Learning Algorithm (Abbeel & Ng, 2004)

The algorithm iteratively builds a mixed policy $\bar{\pi}$ that is provably near-expert.

**Step 1 — Initialize:** Set $i = 0$, initialize policy set $\{\pi_j\}_{j=0}^{i-1} = \emptyset$, and pick an arbitrary initial policy $\pi_0$.

**Step 2 — Feature expectation of current policy:** Solve for $\mu(\pi_i)$ by running the policy to collect trajectories and computing:

$$
\mu(\pi_i) = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{k=1}^{m} \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t^{(k)})
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Max-Margin optimization:**

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}, \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert \le 1} \; t \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mu_E \ge \mathbf{w}^T \mu_j + t, \quad \forall j \in \{0, 1, \dots, i\}
$$

This is a quadratic program (QP) in $\mathbf{w}$. The margin $t$ measures how much better the expert is than all previously tried policies under the inferred reward.

**Step 4 — Add a new candidate policy** $\pi_{i+1}$ by solving the standard RL problem with reward $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$ using the $\mathbf{w}$ from Step 3.

**Step 5 — Termination check:** Stop when

$$
\Vert \mu(\pi_i) - \mu_E \Vert_2 \le \epsilon
$$

Otherwise, increment $i$ and return to Step 2.

**Step 6 — Final policy:** Output a stochastic mixture over $\{ \pi_0, \pi_1, \dots, \pi_i \}$ weighted by the Lagrange multipliers from the QP — its feature expectation equals $\mu_E$ exactly.

### 3.3 Derivation: Ng & Russell's Linearly-Solvable IRL (2000)

**Step 1 — Optimality condition.** Expert $\pi_E$ is optimal under recovered $R$, so for all $(s,a)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \vert s,a)} \left[ V^*(s') \right] - V^*(s) \le 0 \quad \text{when } a \neq \pi_E(s)
$$

Equivalently, the **advantage** of the expert's action is non-negative:

$$
A^*(s,a) = Q^*(s,a) - V^*(s) \le 0, \quad \forall a \in A
$$

with equality at $a = \pi_E(s)$.

**Step 2 — Expand using linear reward.** Substitute $Q^* = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^*(s')$:

$$
\mathbf{w}^T \phi(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V^*(s') - V^*(s) \le 0
$$

**Step 3 — Formulate as a linear program (LP).** Choose an objective that maximizes the margin, e.g., maximize the sum of slack on the expert's chosen action:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}, V} \sum_{s,a} \mathbb{1}[a = \pi_E(s)] \cdot \left( V(s) - \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) V(s') \right)
$$

subject to the optimality constraints above and $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert \le 1$.

**Step 4 — Solve the LP.** The recovered $\mathbf{w}$ is a candidate reward. If multiple solutions exist, additional priors (max margin, max entropy) select one.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Major IRL Methods

| Method | Core Idea | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ng & Russell (2000) LP-IRL | Expert is Boltzmann-rational | Simple LP, first principled approach | Many reward solutions; brittle on stochastic experts |
| Abbeel & Ng (2004) Max-Margin | Max margin over candidate $\mathbf{w}$ | Provable performance bound | Requires repeated RL solves (expensive) |
| Ramachandran & Amir (2007) Bayesian IRL | Posterior over $\mathbf{w}$ | Principled uncertainty | Computationally heavy |
| Ziebart et al. (2008) MaxEnt IRL | Trajectory probability $\propto \exp(R)$ | Uniquely defines stochastic policy | Single mode, no multi-modal behavior |
| GAIL (2016) | Adversarial imitation, no $R$ recovery | Scales to high-dim states (e.g., images) | No interpretable reward |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apprenticeship Learning Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Expert Demonstrator] --> B[Collect Demonstration Trajectories]
    B --> C[Compute Expert Feature Expectation muE]
    D[Hand-Crafted Feature Map phi s] --> C
    C --> E[Max-Margin / QP Solver]
    F[Solve RL with Recovered Reward] --> G[New Candidate Policy pi i]
    G --> H[Compute mu pi i]
    H --> E
    E --> I{mu pi i close to muE?}
    I -- No --> F
    I -- Yes --> J[Output Mixed Policy bar pi]
    D --> F
```

### 4.2 Inverse Reinforcement Learning vs. Standard RL (Information Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StandardRL[Standard RL]
        R1[Reward Function R] --> P1[Policy pi star]
    end
    subgraph InverseRL[Inverse RL]
        P2[Expert Policy piE] --> R2[Recovered Reward R hat]
    end
    subgraph Apprentice[Apprenticeship Learning]
        P3[Expert Demonstrations] --> A1[Iterative AL Loop]
        A1 --> P4[Learner Policy pi]
        P4 -.feedback.-> A1
    end
```

### 4.3 The Max-Margin IRL Optimization Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Set of all reward vectors w with norm 1] --> G[Hyperplane Separating]
    G --> E[Expert Feature Expectation muE]
    G --> L[Learner Feature Expectations mu 0 mu 1 ...]
    E --- D1[Maximize Margin t]
    L --- D1
    D1 --> S[Solve QP for optimal w]
    S --> R[Run RL with w to get pi new]
    R --> L
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read the flow as follows:** The algorithm searches a *unit sphere* of possible reward weight vectors $\mathbf{w}$. The chosen $\mathbf{w}$ defines a hyperplane (via the value function) that maximally separates the expert's feature expectation $\mu_E$ from the learner policies seen so far. The new policy produced by RL with that $\mathbf{w}$ is added to the learner set, and the process repeats until no policy can out-perform the expert by more than $\epsilon$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
*Distinguish between Reinforcement Learning, Inverse Reinforcement Learning, and Apprenticeship Learning. Mention the input-output of each.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Input = reward function $R(s,a)$; Output = optimal policy $\pi^*$. The agent learns by trial-and-error interaction.
- **Inverse RL (IRL):** Input = expert policy $\pi_E$ (or its trajectories); Output = reward function $R(s)$ that explains $\pi_E$. Solves the inverse problem.
- **Apprenticeship Learning (AL):** Input = expert policy $\pi_E$; Output = learner policy $\pi$ whose performance matches/beats $\pi_E$ on the *true* (unknown) reward. Often solves IRL as an intermediate step.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse AL and IRL as synonyms. **Remember:** IRL *recovers the reward*; AL only requires a *good policy* and may never explicitly produce $R$. In GAIL-style methods, $R$ is never recovered at all.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`**
*Define the feature expectation $\mu(\pi)$ used in apprenticeship learning. Why is it central to the algorithm?*

**Model Answer:**
The **feature expectation** of a policy $\pi$ in an MDP with discount $\gamma$ is defined as:

$$
\mu(\pi) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t) \right] \in \mathbb{R}^k
$$

where $\phi(s)$ is a hand-crafted feature vector and the expectation is over trajectories induced by $\pi$ starting from the initial state distribution.

**Why central:** Under the linear reward assumption $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$, the value of a policy is $V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \mu(\pi)$. Therefore, **matching the feature expectation of the learner policy to that of the expert** guarantees equivalent performance for **every** valid reward weight $\mathbf{w}$ — i.e., without ever knowing the true reward.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Inverse Reinforcement Learning problem formally. Discuss the ill-posed nature of the problem and how Maximum Margin IRL addresses it.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe Abbeel & Ng's Apprenticeship Learning algorithm. Highlight the role of feature expectations and the termination condition.

**Model Solution (a) — Inverse RL, Ill-Posedness, and Max-Margin:**

**Formal Statement.** Given an MDP $\langle S, A, P, \gamma \rangle$ **without** reward, and a set of expert demonstrations $\{ \tau_1, \dots, \tau_m \}$ generated by policy $\pi_E$, find a reward function $R$ such that $\pi_E$ is optimal (or near-optimal) under $R$.

Under a linear reward $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$, the problem reduces to recovering $\mathbf{w}$.

**Ill-Posedness.** The optimality condition $V^{\pi_E}(s) \ge V^{\pi}(s)$ for all $s, \pi$ defines a polyhedral cone of feasible $\mathbf{w}$. The cone is often **wide** — many reward functions all rank $\pi_E$ as optimal. Examples:
- $R(s) = c$ (constant) makes every policy optimal — vacuous.
- $R(s) = -1$ everywhere — same as above.

**How Max-Margin IRL Solves It.** Abbeel & Ng choose the $\mathbf{w}$ with the **largest margin** between the expert's value and any other policy's value:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{w}} \min_{\pi \neq \pi_E} \left( V^{\pi_E}(s_0) - V^{\pi}(s_0) \right) = \max_{\mathbf{w}} \min_{\pi} \mathbf{w}^T (\mu_E - \mu(\pi))
$$

subject to $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \le 1$. This prefers the reward for which the expert **unambiguously** out-performs all other policies by the greatest margin, eliminating trivial degenerate solutions.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Abbeel & Ng's Apprenticeship Learning Algorithm:**

**Algorithm Steps (Project AL):**

1. **Input:** Expert feature expectation $\mu_E$, MDP (without $R$), feature map $\phi$, tolerance $\epsilon$.
2. **Initialize:** Set $\pi_0$ to an arbitrary base policy; compute $\mu_0 = \mu(\pi_0)$. Set $i = 0$.
3. **Repeat:**
   a. Solve the QP:
   $$
   \max_{\mathbf{w}, t} \; t \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mu_E \ge \mathbf{w}^T \mu_j + t, \; \forall j \in \{0, \dots, i\}, \quad \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \le 1
   $$
   b. Use the resulting $\mathbf{w}$ to define a reward $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$.
   c. Run a standard RL algorithm (e.g., policy iteration) with this $R$ to obtain a new policy $\pi_{i+1}$.
   d. Compute $\mu_{i+1} = \mu(\pi_{i+1})$ via trajectory rollouts.
   e. **Termination:** Stop if $\Vert \mu_{i+1} - \mu_E \Vert_2 \le \epsilon$.
   f. Else $i \leftarrow i+1$, go to (a).
4. **Output:** A stochastic mixture policy $\bar{\pi}$ with weights equal to the QP's Lagrange multipliers on the binding constraints; this $\bar{\pi}$ satisfies $\mu(\bar{\pi}) = \mu_E$ **exactly**.

**Role of Feature Expectation:** The full algorithm never *uses* the recovered $\mathbf{w}$ at deployment time. Its sole purpose is to **guide RL toward policies whose $\mu$ approaches $\mu_E$**. The final output is a *mixture* whose feature expectation matches the expert's, ensuring performance parity under any linear reward.

**Termination Condition:** When $\Vert \mu(\pi_i) - \mu_E \Vert_2 \le \epsilon$, the learner's performance gap on any unit-norm reward is bounded by $\epsilon$ (by Cauchy-Schwarz). This gives a **distribution-free guarantee** of near-expert behavior.

---

**Q3(B). `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4 Alternative]`**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the value function expression $V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \mu(\pi)$ under a linear reward assumption. Show why this result makes Apprenticeship Learning robust to unknown rewards.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Maximum Entropy IRL formulation. Compare it with the Max-Margin approach in terms of handling multi-modal expert behavior.

**Model Solution (a) — Value Function Derivation:**

Starting from the standard Bellman-style definition:

$$
V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R(s_t) \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the linear reward:**

$$
V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s_t) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Move the constant $\mathbf{w}$ out of the expectation (linearity):**

$$
V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \cdot \mathbb{E}_{\tau}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t \phi(s_t) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Define the feature expectation:**

$$
\boxed{V^{\pi}(s_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \mu(\pi)}
$$

**Why it makes AL robust:** Since the value is *bilinear* in $(\mathbf{w}, \mu)$ via a dot product, the learner does not need to estimate $\mathbf{w}$. Instead, it only needs to make $\mu(\pi)$ close to $\mu_E$ in Euclidean distance. The bound is:

$$
\vert V^{\pi}(s_0) - V^{\pi_E}(s_0) \vert = \vert \mathbf{w}^T (\mu(\pi) - \mu_E) \vert \le \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \mu(\pi) - \mu_E \Vert_2
$$

So if $\Vert \mu(\pi) - \mu_E \Vert_2 \le \epsilon$ and $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \le 1$, the learner's performance is at most $\epsilon$ worse than the expert's, **regardless of which linear reward is the true one**.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Maximum Entropy IRL and Comparison:**

**Maximum Entropy Formulation (Ziebart et al., 2008):** MaxEnt IRL resolves IRL ambiguity by picking the reward for which the **trajectory distribution has maximum entropy** subject to matching the expert's expected feature counts.

**Principled Statement:** The probability of a trajectory is modeled as:

$$
P(\tau \mid \mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathbf{w})} \exp\left( \mathbf{w}^T \phi(\tau) \right), \quad \text{where} \quad \phi(\tau) = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \phi(s_t)
$$

and $Z(\mathbf{w})$ is the partition function. The expert's empirical feature expectation is matched as a constraint:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim P(\cdot \mid \mathbf{w})}[\phi(\tau)] = \tilde{\mu}_E
$$

This gives a convex dual problem solved by gradient ascent on $\mathbf{w}$.

**Comparison with Max-Margin IRL:**

| Aspect | Max-Margin IRL | MaxEnt IRL |
|---|---|---|
| **Ambiguity Resolution** | Choose $\mathbf{w}$ with largest performance gap | Choose $\mathbf{w}$ giving max-entropy distribution |
| **Multi-modal behavior** | Cannot represent — assumes a single best $\mathbf{w}$ | Captures stochastic/multi-modal expert policies via the exponential family |
| **Distribution over trajectories** | Deterministic optimal only | Boltzmann-like stochastic |
| **Optimization** | QP (in original feature space) | Gradient descent on log-likelihood |
| **Resulting policy** | Unique deterministic $\pi$ for given $R$ | Stochastic $\pi$ that mimics expert's distribution |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse $\mu(\pi)$ with a probability distribution.** It is an expected cumulative *feature count* — a vector in $\mathbb{R}^k$, not a scalar.
> 2. **Linear reward is an assumption, not a theorem.** If $\phi$ is too small or badly chosen, AL can fail. State this caveat explicitly in answers for full marks.
> 3. **For the derivation question:** Do NOT skip the linearity-of-expectation step. Examiners award the "swap" step marks separately.
> 4. **For Max-Margin QP:** Be sure to mention the $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \le 1$ normalization. Forgetting it loses a mark.
> 5. **For the mixture policy output:** Many students write that the output is a single $\pi_i$. In Abbeel & Ng, the **final output is a stochastic mixture** over all generated $\pi_j$ — explicitly say so.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RL → IRL → AL** is a strict hierarchy: AL can be solved *via* IRL but does not require it (e.g., GAIL).
- The **linear reward assumption** $R(s) = \mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$ is the cornerstone — without it, $\mu(\pi)$ cannot replace $R$ in performance comparisons.
- The **feature expectation** $\mu(\pi)$ is the *only* sufficient statistic for matching an unknown linear reward.
- **Apprenticeship Learning algorithm** (Abbeel & Ng, 2004) is iterative: it solves an LP/QP at every step, then runs RL with the inferred reward, until $\mu(\pi)$ is within $\epsilon$ of $\mu_E$.
- The **final output** of Abbeel & Ng's AL is a **mixture policy**, not a single $\pi_i$ — mixture weights are the QP's Lagrange multipliers.
- **Ill-posedness** of IRL: many rewards explain the same expert behavior. Max-margin, Bayesian, and Max-entropy methods are the three main disambiguation strategies.
- **MaxEnt IRL** assigns a Boltzmann-like distribution over trajectories $P(\tau) \propto \exp(\mathbf{w}^T \phi(\tau))$ — making it the right tool for stochastic/multi-modal experts.
- **Performance bound:** $\vert V^{\pi}(s_0) - V^{\pi_E}(s_0) \vert \le \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \mu(\pi) - \mu_E \Vert_2$ — this is the headline guarantee to cite in exams.
- **Engineering applications** to recall: autonomous driving, robotic manipulation, helicopter aerobatics, recommender systems.
- **Caveat:** The quality of $\phi(s)$ is the **bottleneck** — bad features ⇒ bad reward recovery ⇒ bad policy. Always mention this in design-oriented questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "W-M-Phi-Mu-Eps":**
> **W**eights (w) → linear Reward $\mathbf{w}^T \phi(s)$ → **M**atch Feature Expectation ($\mu$) → **Eps**ilon bound on $\Vert \mu - \mu_E \Vert$. This is the entire AL/IRL pipeline in five characters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Reinforcement Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECMT615 - ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications of Reinforcement Learning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which an autonomous **agent** learns an optimal **policy** by interacting with a **dynamic environment** through a cycle of *state observation*, *action selection*, and *reward feedback*, with the explicit objective of maximizing the long-term cumulative reward signal. In the context of **Applications of Reinforcement Learning**, we move beyond theoretical algorithms (Q-learning, SARSA, DQN) and study how the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** framework — defined by the tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ — is mapped onto real-world engineering and business problems to achieve autonomous decision-making under uncertainty. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Training a Pet Dog** 🐕 > Imagine teaching a puppy to fetch a ball. Every time the dog moves *towards* the ball, you say "Good boy!" and offer a treat (**positive reward**). Every time the dog chews the sofa, you say "No!" and give no treat (**negative reward / no reward**). The dog does not understand English — it just learns a *pattern of behavior* that maximizes the total number of treats over an entire afternoon. After thousands of repetitions, the dog has effectively built an internal "policy" mapping *situation → best action*. > > **Reinforcement Learning works exactly the same way:** > - The **dog** = the RL Agent (the algorithm) > - The **sofa & ball** = the Environment > - The **treat** = the Reward Signal ($R_t$) > - The **dog's brain** = the Policy / Value Function ($\pi(a \vert s)$) > - The **afternoon** = the Episode ## 1.3 The Universal RL Application Pipeline Every real-world RL application, regardless of domain, follows the **Perception → Decision → Actuation → Feedback** loop: | Stage | Component | Real-World Example (Self-Driving Car) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1. **Perception** | State Observation ($s_t$) | Camera & LiDAR capture road, vehicles, pedestrians | | 2. **Decision** | Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$ | Neural network outputs steering angle | | 3. **Actuation** | Action Execution ($a_t$) | Steering wheel rotates by 5° left | | 4. **Feedback** | Reward + Next State ($r_t, s_{t+1}$) | +10 for safe lane change, new sensor frame | ## 1.4 Classification of RL Applications RL applications are broadly classified into **six engineering domains** that recur in the KTU 2024 syllabus: 1. **Game Playing & Board Games** — deterministic, fully observable environments 2. **Robotics & Control Systems** — continuous action spaces, physical embodiment 3. **Autonomous Vehicles & Transportation** — high-stakes, safety-critical 4. **Healthcare & Bioinformatics** — delayed rewards, ethical constraints 5. **Finance & Trading** — stochastic, non-stationary environments 6. **Natural Language Processing & Dialogue Systems** — discrete, high-dimensional state # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Canonical RL Formulation (Revisited) For any application, the environment is modeled as a **Markov Decision Process (MDP)**: $$\text{MDP} = (S, A, P, R, \gamma)$$ Where: - $S$ = finite/infinite set of **states** - $A$ = set of **actions** - $P(s' \mid s, a)$ = **transition probability** function - $R(s, a)$ = **reward function** - $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ = **discount factor** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Markov Property**: The future depends *only* on the present state, not the history. This is the cornerstone assumption that allows RL to scale to massive applications like AlphaGo and autonomous navigation. ## 2.2 The Two Foundational Equations of RL Applications Regardless of the application, the *quality* of any learned policy is measured by two recursive equations: ### Equation 1: The Bellman Optimality Equation (Value Function) $$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \, V^*(s') \right]$$ ### Equation 2: The Action-Value (Q) Function $$Q^*(s, a) = R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$ These two equations are the **mathematical heart** of every RL application — from a chess engine to a robotic arm. ## 2.3 Mapping Applications to RL Problem Types | RL Algorithm Family | Best-Suited Application | Key Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Q-Learning / DQN** | Atari games, Simple grid worlds | Discrete actions, fully observable | | **Policy Gradient (REINFORCE)** | Robotics, Continuous control | Continuous action spaces (motor torque) | | **Actor-Critic (A2C, A3C, PPO)** | Autonomous driving, Drone navigation | Need stable, sample-efficient learning | | **DDPG / TD3 / SAC** | Robotic manipulation, Self-driving | High-dimensional continuous control | | **AlphaZero / MCTS+NN** | Chess, Go, Shogi | Combinatorially large, deterministic | | **Inverse RL** | Healthcare, Imitation tasks | Expert demonstrations available | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **⭐ Memorize this table for the KTU End Semester Exam — it covers ~80% of RL application derivations.** | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Typical Use-Case | | :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Cumulative Discounted Reward | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$ | Total future reward the agent seeks to maximize | Universal — all RL apps | | 2 | Bellman Expectation | $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) Q^\pi(s, a)$ | Value of state under policy $\pi$ | Policy evaluation | | 3 | Bellman Optimality | $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s, a)$ | Best achievable value from state $s$ | Optimal decision-making | | 4 | Q-Learning Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | Off-policy TD learning rule | Atari, grid world | | 5 | SARSA Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | On-policy TD learning rule | Robot navigation | | 6 | Policy Gradient Theorem | $\nabla J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \vert s) \, Q^\pi(s, a) \right]$ | Direct policy optimization | Robotics, continuous control | | 7 | Exploration vs. Exploitation | $\varepsilon\text{-greedy: } a = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q(s,a) & \text{w.p. } 1-\varepsilon \\ \text{random} & \text{w.p. } \varepsilon \end{cases}$ | Balances trying new actions vs. using known good ones | All RL applications | | 8 | Discount Factor Constraint | $0 \le \gamma < 1$ | Ensures infinite sums converge | Convergence proofs | | 9 | Experience Replay (DQN) | Sample $(s, a, r, s')$ uniformly from buffer $D$ | Breaks correlation in training data | Deep RL applications | | 10 | Target Network Update | $\theta^- \leftarrow \tau \theta + (1 - \tau) \theta^-$ | Stabilizes DQN training | Deep Q-Networks | > **Note on units / parameters:** $\alpha$ = learning rate (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$), $\gamma$ = discount factor (typically $0.9$ to $0.99$), $\varepsilon$ = exploration rate (decayed from $1.0$ to $0.01$). ## 2.5 The "Why" Behind RL Application Success RL succeeds in applications because of three engineering properties: 1. **No labeled dataset required** — the agent generates its own training data by interacting with the environment (simulator or real world). This is why RL is a *first-principles* approach. 2. **Optimizes long-term objectives** — unlike supervised learning which minimizes immediate loss, RL directly maximizes cumulative future reward. Critical for game playing and resource scheduling. 3. **Handles sequential decision-making** — explicitly designed for problems where today's action affects tomorrow's outcome (e.g., a chess move now affects the position 20 moves later). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Reward Sequence to Optimal Policy The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks students to *derive* the Bellman equation from first principles. Here is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation**. --- ### Derivation 1: The Bellman Equation for $V^\pi(s)$ **Step 1 — Start with the cumulative discounted reward definition:** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$$ **Step 2 — Factor out the first reward term to expose a recursive structure:** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \dots \right)$$ **Step 3 — Recognize the parenthesized expression as $G_{t+1}$:** $$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \, G_{t+1}$$ This is the **fundamental recursive identity** of RL — every discounted return equals its immediate reward plus a discounted future return. **Step 4 — Define the state-value function as the expected return starting from state $s$ under policy $\pi$:** $$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the recursive identity from Step 3 into Step 4:** $$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s \right]$$ **Step 6 — Apply linearity of expectation and the law of iterated expectations (condition on next state $s'$):** $$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]$$ **Step 7 — Identify the inner expectation as $V^\pi(s')$, yielding the Bellman Expectation Equation:** $$V^\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \, V^\pi(s') \right]$$ > **Interpretation:** The value of being in state $s$ equals the expected immediate reward plus the discounted expected value of the next state.
