This is the *operational identity* used by every RL application from chess to autonomous cars. --- ### Derivation 2: Bellman Optimality Equation (Engine Behind AlphaGo, Atari) **Step 1 — Start from the Q-function definition:** $$Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]$$ **Step 2 — Take the maximum over actions to obtain the optimal Q-value:** $$Q^*(s, a) = \max_\pi Q^\pi(s, a)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the recursive identity and take the expectation over the next state distribution:** $$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E} \left[ \max_{a'} Q^*(S_{t+1}, a') \right] \right]$$ **Step 4 — Since the next state $S_{t+1}$ is sampled, take the maximum over the next action $a'$:** $$Q^*(s, a) = \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$ This is the **Bellman Optimality Equation for Q**, which is the update target for the Q-Learning algorithm. --- ### Derivation 3: The Q-Learning Update Rule (Workhorse of Modern RL Apps) **Step 1 — Define the temporal-difference (TD) target:** $$\text{TD Target} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$$ **Step 2 — Define the TD error (the gap between current estimate and target):** $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the stochastic gradient ascent on the squared TD error loss:** $$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \left( R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a'; \theta^-) - Q(S_t, A_t; \theta) \right)^2 \right]$$ **Step 4 — Take the gradient with respect to $\theta$ and perform gradient descent:** $$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a'; \theta^-) - Q(S_t, A_t; \theta) \right] \nabla_\theta Q(S_t, A_t; \theta)$$ **Step 5 — For tabular Q-learning (no neural network), this simplifies to the canonical update:** $$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$ This is the update rule used in nearly every discrete-action RL application (Atari, grid-world pathfinding, simple game AIs). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: A 3×3 Grid World (KTU-Style Numerical Problem) > **Problem:** A robot starts at cell $(1,1)$ in a $3 \times 3$ grid and must reach the goal cell $(3,3)$ which gives reward $+10$. All other transitions give reward $-1$. The discount factor is $\gamma = 0.9$. Using Q-Learning with learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$, compute the updated Q-value $Q((1,1), \text{right})$ after the robot takes action "right" and lands in $(1,2)$ with reward $-1$. **Step 1 — Identify given values:** $$s = (1,1), \quad a = \text{right}, \quad r = -1, \quad s' = (1,2), \quad \alpha = 0.5, \quad \gamma = 0.9$$ **Step 2 — Assume current Q-table is initialized to zero:** $$Q((1,1), \text{right}) = 0, \quad Q((1,2), \text{right}) = 0, \quad Q((1,2), \text{left}) = 0, \quad Q((1,2), \text{up}) = 0, \quad Q((1,2), \text{down}) = 0$$ **Step 3 — Compute the TD target:** $$\text{TD Target} = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') = -1 + 0.9 \times \max(0, 0, 0, 0) = -1 + 0 = -1$$ **Step 4 — Compute the TD error:** $$\delta = \text{TD Target} - Q(s, a) = -1 - 0 = -1$$ **Step 5 — Apply the Q-Learning update:** $$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \cdot \delta = 0 + 0.5 \times (-1) = -0.5$$ **Final Answer:** $Q((1,1), \text{right}) = -0.5$ after one update. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Applications to RL Components | Application Domain | State $s$ | Action $a$ | Reward $R$ | Algorithm | Real-World Deployment | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Chess / Go** | Board position (piece locations) | Move a piece | +1 win, -1 loss, 0 draw | AlphaZero (MCTS + DNN) | AlphaGo, Stockfish, Leela Chess Zero | | **Atari 2600 Games** | Raw pixel frame (84×84×4) | Joystick command | Game score delta | DQN, Rainbow | DeepMind Arcade Learning Env | | **Robotic Locomotion** | Joint angles, velocities | Motor torques | Forward velocity − energy | PPO, SAC | Boston Dynamics Atlas, ANYmal | | **Robotic Manipulation** | Object pose, gripper state | End-effector delta | Grasp success / task completion | Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) | OpenAI Dactyl, warehouse pickers | | **Autonomous Driving** | LiDAR point cloud + GPS | Steer / accelerate / brake | Safety + comfort + progress | DDPG, PPO | Waymo, Tesla FSD, Mobileye | | **Drone Navigation** | IMU + camera + altitude | 4 motor speeds | Distance to goal − crash penalty | PPO + domain randomization | Skydio, DJI autonomous flight | | **Stock Trading** | Price history, indicators | Buy / Hold / Sell | Portfolio $\Delta$PnL | PPO with custom reward | Quantitative hedge funds | | **Dialogue Systems** | Conversation history tokens | Next utterance | User engagement / task success | RLHF (PPO on LLM) | ChatGPT, Bard, Llama-Chat | | **Recommendation** | User history, context | Recommended item | Click / watch time / conversion | Contextual Bandits | YouTube, Netflix, Spotify | | **Smart Grid / HVAC** | Temperature, demand, tariff | Setpoint adjustment | Energy cost − comfort penalty | DQN | Google DeepMind HVAC (40% savings) | | **Traffic Light Control** | Queue lengths at intersection | Phase selection | Negative cumulative waiting time | DQN, MARL | Pittsburgh, Hangzhou deployment | | **Healthcare Dosing** | Patient vitals, lab values | Drug dosage | Survival proxy / biomarker | Off-policy Batch RL | Sepsis treatment, insulin dosing | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Universal RL Agent–Environment Loop The following Mermaid diagram captures the **agent–environment interaction cycle** that underlies *every* RL application. It is the single most important diagram to memorize for the KTU exam. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Agent
Policy pi and Value V] -->|Action a_t| B[Environment
Dynamics P and Reward R] B -->|Reward r_t| A B -->|Next State s_t+1| A A -->|State s_t| B C[State s_t arrives] --> A D[Reward r_t arrives] --> A E[Action a_t dispatched] --> B F[Next State s_t+1 observed] --> B ``` ## 4.2 High-Level Architecture of a Deep RL Application (e.g., DQN for Atari) This block-level schematic shows how the components of a typical deep RL application interact in practice — from raw sensor input to action output, with experience replay and a target network for stability. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PerceptionLayer["PERCEPTION LAYER"] sensor1[Raw Sensors
Pixels, LiDAR, IMU] end subgraph StateEncoder["STATE ENCODER"] encoder1[Feature Extractor
CNN or MLP] end subgraph AgentCore["AGENT CORE"] qnet1[Q-Network theta
Outputs Q s,a for all a] policy1[Policy Selector
epsilon-greedy or softmax] end subgraph MemoryBuffer["EXPERIENCE REPLAY BUFFER D"] buffer1[Store Transition
s, a, r, s prime, done] end subgraph TrainingLoop["TRAINING LOOP"] targetnet1[Target Network theta minus
Frozen periodic copy] lossfunc1[TD Loss
r + gamma max Q minus Q squared] optimizer1[Gradient Descent
Adam, RMSProp] end subgraph ActuatorLayer["ACTUATOR LAYER"] actuator1[Action Execution
Motor, Servo, Game Cmd] end sensor1 --> encoder1 encoder1 --> qnet1 qnet1 --> policy1 policy1 --> actuator1 actuator1 --> sensor1 policy1 --> buffer1 buffer1 --> lossfunc1 qnet1 --> lossfunc1 targetnet1 --> lossfunc1 lossfunc1 --> optimizer1 optimizer1 --> qnet1 optimizer1 -.periodic copy.-> targetnet1 ``` ## 4.3 Taxonomy of RL Applications (Tree View) This hierarchical diagram organizes the major RL application domains encountered in real engineering deployments. ```mermaid flowchart TB root[RL Applications] root --> games[Gaming and Board Games] root --> robotics[Robotics and Control] root --> vehicles[Autonomous Systems] root --> health[Healthcare] root --> finance[Finance and Trading] root --> nlp[NLP and Dialogue] root --> industry[Industrial Optimization] games --> chess1[Chess Go Shogi
AlphaZero MCTS] games --> atari1[Atari Arcade Games
DQN Rainbow] games --> videogames1[Modern Video Games
NPC Behavior RL] robotics --> loco1[Locomotion
PPO SAC] robotics --> manip1[Manipulation
HER DDPG] robotics --> arm1[Industrial Arms
Model Based RL] vehicles --> selfdrive1[Self Driving Cars
Waymo Tesla] vehicles --> drone1[Drone Navigation
Skydio DJI] vehicles --> traffic1[Traffic Control
MARL] health --> sepsis1[Sepsis Treatment
Batch RL] health --> drug1[Drug Dosage
Offline RL] finance --> trading1[Algorithmic Trading
PPO] finance --> portfolio1[Portfolio Optimization
Policy Gradient] nlp --> dialogue1[Dialogue Agents
RLHF PPO on LLM] nlp --> summarize1[Text Summarization
Reward Model RL] industry --> hvac1[Smart HVAC
DQN 40 percent savings] industry --> supply1[Supply Chain
Multi Agent RL] ``` ## 4.4 Diagram: RL Application Development Pipeline This diagram captures the *engineering workflow* when deploying an RL solution to a real-world problem — from problem formulation to production monitoring. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[1 Problem Formulation
Define MDP] --> P2[2 Simulator Design
Gym MuJoCo CARLA] P2 --> P3[3 Algorithm Selection
DQN PPO SAC] P3 --> P4[4 Training Loop
Millions of episodes] P4 --> P5[5 Sim to Real Transfer
Domain Randomization] P5 --> P6[6 Safety Wrappers
Constrained RL] P6 --> P7[7 Deployment and Monitoring
Online A B Testing] P7 -.feedback.-> P1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Differentiate between model-based and model-free Reinforcement Learning. Give one real-world application where each is preferred.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Model-Based RL | Model-Free RL | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | The agent first learns (or is given) a model $P(s' \mid s, a), R(s, a)$ of the environment, then plans using it. | The agent directly learns a policy $\pi$ or value function $Q(s,a)$ by trial-and-error, with no explicit model. | | **Sample Efficiency** | High — reuses the learned model for planning. | Low — requires many real environment samples. | | **Computation** | Heavy (planning, tree search). | Lighter per step. | | **Preferred Application** | **Chess / Go** (AlphaZero builds a world model via MCTS). | **Atari games / Robotic control** (no tractable model). | | **Risk** | Model errors compound (model bias). | Cannot reason about unseen states. | *(1 mark definition difference, 1 mark characteristic contrast, 1 mark example each — award 3 marks for complete answer.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **List any three real-world applications of Reinforcement Learning. For each, state the state, action, and reward components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Application | State $s$ | Action $a$ | Reward $R$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Self-Driving Car** | Camera + LiDAR + GPS pose | Steer / Accelerate / Brake | +1 safe progress, −100 collision | | 2 | **Atari Breakout** | 84×84×4 pixel frame | Move paddle left / right / stay | Game score change | | 3 | **Stock Trading Bot** | OHLC price window + indicators | Buy / Hold / Sell | Daily PnL change | | 4 | **Smart HVAC Control** (any valid 3rd) | Indoor/outdoor temp, occupancy | Setpoint ±2 °C | Energy cost − comfort penalty | *(1 mark per correct application row, 3 marks total.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Format) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Set 1]** **(a)** Explain the **Bellman Optimality Equation** in detail. Derive the Q-Learning update rule from the Bellman equation. Show that Q-Learning converges to the optimal Q-function under standard assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable block diagram, describe a **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** application for playing Atari games. Explain the role of *experience replay* and the *target network* in stabilizing training. Mention any two challenges of DQN and how they are addressed. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — State the Bellman Optimality Equation for $V^*$ (1 mark):** $$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} \left[ R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) V^*(s') \right]$$ **Step 2 — State the Q-form (1 mark):** $$Q^*(s, a) = R(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')$$ **Step 3 — Derive the Q-Learning update (2 marks):** The TD target is $Y_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$. The update that minimizes the squared TD error $\left( Y_t - Q(S_t, A_t) \right)^2$ via stochastic gradient descent (learning rate $\alpha$) is: $$Q(S_t, A_t) \leftarrow Q(S_t, A_t) + \alpha \left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a') - Q(S_t, A_t) \right]$$ **Step 4 — Convergence conditions (3 marks):** Q-Learning converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 if: - All state-action pairs are visited infinitely often (requires GLIE — Greedy in the Limit with Infinite Exploration, e.g., $\varepsilon$-decay). - Learning rate satisfies $\sum_t \alpha_t(s,a) = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2(s,a) < \infty$ (e.g., $\alpha_t = 1/t$). - Rewards are bounded: $\vert R(s,a) \vert \le R_{\max}$. - The Markov property holds. > *Award partial credit: 1 mark for any one condition, 2 marks for two, 3 for all three plus the theorem statement.* --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — DQN architecture description (2 marks):** DQN uses a deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that takes the **state** as a stack of 4 consecutive 84×84 grayscale Atari frames and outputs a Q-value for every discrete joystick action: $$\text{Input: } s_t = (frame_{t-3}, frame_{t-2}, frame_{t-1}, frame_t) \xrightarrow{\text{CNN}} Q(s_t, a; \theta) \quad \forall a \in A$$ **Step 2 — Experience Replay (2 marks):** - Transitions $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1}, \text{done}_t)$ are stored in a buffer $\mathcal{D}$ of capacity $\sim 10^6$. - Mini-batches are sampled uniformly at random to compute gradients. - **Why it works:** Breaks the temporal correlation between consecutive samples (satisfies the i.i.d. assumption of SGD), and lets rare experiences be reused many times → **sample efficiency**. **Step 3 — Target Network (1 mark):** - A second network with parameters $\theta^-$ is a periodic copy of the online network $\theta$. - It is held fixed for $C$ steps (typically $C = 10{,}000$) and used to compute the TD target: $$Y_t^{\text{DQN}} = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a'; \theta^-)$$ - This prevents the "moving target" problem and stabilizes training. **Step 4 — Two challenges and fixes (2 marks):** - **Challenge 1: Overestimation bias** (the $\max$ operator propagates any Q-noise upwards). - *Fix:* **Double DQN (DDQN)** — decouple action selection (online net) and action evaluation (target net). - **Challenge 2: Sample inefficiency** in complex games. - *Fix:* **Prioritized Experience Replay** — sample important transitions (high TD error) more frequently. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Set 1, Alternative]** **(a)** Discuss **any four major application domains** of Reinforcement Learning. For each, clearly state the state, action, reward, and the RL algorithm best suited for it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a 3×3 grid world, explain how an RL agent learns the optimal policy using the Q-Learning algorithm. Show **at least two iterations** of the update and explain the role of the exploration–exploitation trade-off using the $\varepsilon$-greedy strategy. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** | Domain | State $s$ | Action $a$ | Reward $R$ | Best Algorithm | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Game Playing (Chess/Go)** | Board configuration | Move a piece | +1 win, −1 loss, 0 draw | **AlphaZero** (MCTS + DNN) | Combinatorial depth, deterministic | | **Robotic Manipulation** | Object pose + gripper state | Joint torque deltas | +1 grasp success | **HER + DDPG** | Sparse rewards, continuous control | | **Autonomous Driving** | LiDAR + camera + GPS | Steer / accel / brake | +progress − risk | **PPO / SAC** | Continuous actions, safety-critical | | **Healthcare Dosing** | Patient vitals + labs | Drug dosage mg/hr | Survival proxy | **Off-policy Batch RL (FQI)** | Cannot explore online; use observational data | | **(Optional 5th) Dialogue Systems** | Conversation tokens | Next response tokens | User rating / task success | **RLHF (PPO on LLM)** | Human feedback as reward signal | *(1.5 marks per row × 4 rows ≈ 6 marks, + 1 mark for the justification column being sensible.)* --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Setup (1 mark):** Consider a 3×3 grid with start $S = (1,1)$, goal $G = (3,3)$ giving reward $+10$, and a "cliff" cell $(2,2)$ giving reward $-10$. All other transitions yield $-1$. The discount factor is $\gamma = 0.9$, learning rate $\alpha = 0.5$, and all Q-values are initialized to $0$. **Iteration 1 (1.5 marks):** Suppose the agent is at $s = (1,1)$, uses $\varepsilon$-greedy ($\varepsilon = 0.1$, i.e., 10% random), picks $a = \text{right}$ (random), reaches $s' = (1,2)$, receives $r = -1$. $$Q((1,1), \text{right}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \left[ -1 + 0.9 \times \max_a Q((1,2), a) - 0 \right] = 0 + 0.5 \times (-1 + 0) = -0.5$$ **Iteration 2 (1.5 marks):** Now at $s = (1,2)$, suppose it picks $a = \text{down}$ (greedy, since all Q-values are $-0.5$ — tie broken randomly), reaches $s' = (2,2)$ (the cliff), receives $r = -10$. $$Q((1,2), \text{down}) \leftarrow 0 + 0.5 \left[ -10 + 0.9 \times \max_a Q((2,2), a) - 0 \right] = 0 + 0.5 \times (-10 + 0) = -5.0$$ **Exploration–Exploitation Trade-off (2 marks):** - **Exploitation:** With probability $1 - \varepsilon$, the agent selects $\arg\max_a Q(s, a)$ — uses its current knowledge to maximize reward. - **Exploration:** With probability $\varepsilon$, the agent samples a random action uniformly — discovers potentially better long-term paths. - **Schedule:** A common scheme is to start with $\varepsilon_0 = 1.0$ (pure exploration) and decay geometrically to $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.01$: $$\varepsilon_t = \max\left(\varepsilon_{\min}, \varepsilon_0 \cdot \delta^{\,t}\right), \quad \delta \in (0, 1)$$ **Convergence note (1 mark):** Over thousands of episodes, the agent will repeatedly sample the cliff and learn that avoiding $(2,2)$ yields a much higher cumulative reward, eventually discovering the safe path $S \to (1,2) \to (1,3) \to (2,3) \to (3,3) = G$. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **DO NOT confuse SARSA with Q-Learning.** SARSA uses $Q(S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$ (on-policy), while Q-Learning uses $\max_{a'} Q(S_{t+1}, a')$ (off-policy). Marks are deducted heavily for this mix-up. > 2. **Always state the Markov Property assumption** when defining the MDP — it is the foundation of the entire derivation. Omitting it loses 1 mark. > 3. **When drawing the DQN block diagram, label BOTH networks** ($\theta$ and $\theta^-$) and indicate the *direction* of gradient flow. Examiners specifically check this. > 4. **In the grid-world numerical**, show ALL intermediate calculations. Students who write only the final Q-value without the TD target and TD error lose 2 of the 7 marks. > 5. **Mention the discount factor range** $0 \le \gamma < 1$ explicitly — examiners allocate 0.5 mark for noting convergence conditions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications of Reinforcement Learning (Module 4)** - **Core abstraction:** Every RL application is an **MDP** $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ plus an **agent** optimizing a **policy** $\pi$ to maximize $\mathbb{E}[G_t]$. - **Two cornerstone equations:** (i) Bellman Expectation $V^\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s,a) [R + \gamma V^\pi(s')]$, and (ii) Bellman Optimality $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s,a)$. - **Q-Learning update** is the most-asked equation: $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$. - **Six major application domains** (memorize with examples): (1) Gaming/Board games, (2) Robotics, (3) Autonomous vehicles, (4) Healthcare, (5) Finance, (6) NLP/Dialogue. - **Algorithm-to-application mapping:** DQN → discrete actions, PPO/SAC → continuous control, AlphaZero → perfect-info games, RLHF → language models. - **Stabilization tricks** in deep RL: **experience replay** (decorrelates samples), **target network** (stabilizes TD target), **Double DQN** (reduces overestimation). - **Exploration–Exploitation** is solved via $\varepsilon$-greedy, UCB, or Thompson sampling — never via pure greedy in the early phases. - **Markov Property** is non-negotiable: future $\perp$ history $\mid$ present state. - **Discount factor $\gamma$** close to 1 = long-sighted agent; close to 0 = myopic agent. Standard range is $0.9$ to $0.99$. - **Convergence of Q-Learning** requires (i) GLIE exploration, (ii) Robbins-Monro learning rate, (iii) bounded rewards. - **Real-world challenges** in RL applications: sample inefficiency, sim-to-real gap, safety constraints, reward shaping, partial observability (POMDPs). - **Sample example worth remembering:** Google DeepMind reduced HVAC energy cost by **40%** using DQN-based RL on a real data center.
